# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Financial Management. Briefly explain its scope.**

**Model Answer:**

Financial Management is the planning, organizing, directing, and controlling of financial activities in an organization. It involves the procurement of funds (financing decision) and their effective utilization (investment decision) to maximize shareholder wealth.

**Scope of Financial Management:**
1. **Investment Decision** — Allocation of funds to long-term and short-term assets.
2. **Financing Decision** — Determining the mix of debt and equity (capital structure).
3. **Dividend Decision** — Deciding the distribution of profits between dividends and retained earnings.
4. **Working Capital Management** — Managing day-to-day cash flows and current assets.
5. **Risk Management** — Hedging against financial and market risks.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for 4 well-explained points)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Distinguish between Money Market and Capital Market.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Money Market | Capital Market |
|---|---|---|
| Tenure | Short-term (≤ 1 year) | Long-term (> 1 year) |
| Instruments | T-Bills, CP, CD | Shares, Debentures, Bonds |
| Purpose | Working capital | Long-term investment |
| Regulator (India) | RBI | SEBI |
| Risk & Return | Low risk, low return | Higher risk, higher return |

*(3 marks: 1 mark introduction + 2 marks for clear distinction in tabular form)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Note:** Module 1 of PECBT631 typically has a 14-mark long answer with internal choice. We provide both options.

---

### ✅ Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**(a)** Explain the objectives of Financial Management in detail. Why is **Wealth Maximization** considered superior to **Profit Maximization**? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A sum of ₹1,00,000 is invested today at 12% per annum compounded annually. Calculate:
   (i) The amount after 6 years.
   (ii) The present value of ₹2,00,000 receivable after 6 years at the same rate. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Objectives of Financial Management:**

1. **Profit Maximization** — The traditional objective is to increase the firm's profits.
2. **Wealth Maximization (Shareholder Wealth Maximization)** — The modern, superior objective is to maximize the market value of the firm's shares, i.e., maximize Net Present Value (NPV).

**Why Wealth Maximization is Superior:**

| Parameter | Profit Maximization | Wealth Maximization |
|---|---|---|
| Time value of money | Ignored | Considered (uses NPV) |
| Risk consideration | Ignored | Considered (discount rate adjusted) |
| Time horizon | Short-term focus | Long-term focus |
| Stakeholder impact | Only owners | All stakeholders considered |
| Ethical aspects | May encourage unethical acts | Promotes ethical long-term growth |

> **Conclusion:** Wealth Maximization is a more comprehensive, scientific, and KTU-preferred objective because it considers time, risk, and long-term shareholder welfare.

*(7 marks: 2 for objectives + 4 for comparison table + 1 for conclusion)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Future Value after 6 years**

**Given:** $PV = 1{,}00{,}000$, $r = 0.12$, $n = 6$

$$FV = PV \times (1+r)^n$$

$$FV = 1{,}00{,}000 \times (1.12)^6$$

**Compute $(1.12)^6$ stepwise:**

$(1.12)^2 = 1.2544$

$(1.12)^3 = 1.404928$

$(1.12)^6 = (1.404928)^2 = 1.973823$

$$FV = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 1.973823 = 1{,}97{,}382.30$$

**Result:** $FV = \textbf{₹1,97,382.30}$

**(ii) Present Value of ₹2,00,000 after 6 years**

**Given:** $FV = 2{,}00{,}000$, $r = 0.12$, $n = 6$

$$PV = \dfrac{FV}{(1+r)^n} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{1.973823}$$

$$PV = 1{,}01{,}326.16$$

**Result:** $PV = \textbf{₹1,01,326.16}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert percentage to decimal (12% → 0.12).
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Computing $(1.12)^6$ incorrectly — students often stop at $(1.12)^3$ and forget to square.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not writing the formula before substitution. **Always write the formula first** for full marks in step-wise problems.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Final answer must include ₹ symbol and two decimal places for consistency with KTU valuation key.

---

### ✅ Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2022]`
**(a)** Discuss the **Indian Financial System** with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of **RBI** and **SEBI** in regulating the financial environment. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A firm is considering an investment that yields cash flows of ₹50,000 per year for 4 years, with the first cash flow at the end of Year 1. The required rate of return is 10%. Calculate the **Present Value of the Annuity** and decide whether the project is worthwhile if the initial investment is ₹1,50,000. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Indian Financial System:**

The Indian Financial System is the framework that channels funds from surplus units (savers) to deficit units (investors). It consists of four pillars:

1. **Financial Markets** — Money Market and Capital Market
2. **Financial Institutions** — Banks, NBFCs, Development Institutions
3. **Financial Instruments** — T-Bills, Shares, Debentures, Bonds
4. **Financial Services** — Insurance, Mutual Funds, Merchant Banking

**Role of RBI (Reserve Bank of India):**
- **Monetary Authority:** Controls money supply and credit in the economy.
- **Issuer of Currency:** Issues and manages the Indian rupee.
- **Regulator of Banking System:** Supervises commercial banks, cooperative banks.
- **Manager of Foreign Exchange:** Administers the Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA).
- **Regulator of Money Market.**

**Role of SEBI (Securities and Exchange Board of India):**
- **Protects Investor Interests:** Ensures fair practices in the securities market.
- **Regulates Stock Exchanges:** Oversees BSE, NSE, and other exchanges.
- **Regulates Capital Market:** Supervises IPOs, FPOs, mutual funds.
- **Prevents Insider Trading and Fraud.**

*(7 marks: 2 for system structure + 2.5 for RBI + 2.5 for SEBI)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Annual cash flow (PMT) = ₹50,000
- Number of years (n) = 4
- Rate of return (r) = 10% = 0.10
- Initial Investment = ₹1,50,000

**Step 1:** Apply the Present Value of Annuity formula:

$$PV_{annuity} = PMT \times \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the discount factor:

$$(1.10)^4 = 1.4641$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $1 - (1+r)^{-n}$:

$$1 - \dfrac{1}{1.4641} = 1 - 0.6830 = 0.3170$$

**Step 4:** Divide by $r$:

$$\dfrac{0.3170}{0.10} = 3.1699$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by PMT:

$$PV_{annuity} = 50{,}000 \times 3.1699 = 1{,}58{,}495$$

**Step 6:** Compare with initial investment:

$$NPV = PV_{annuity} - Initial\ Investment = 1{,}58{,}495 - 1{,}50{,}000 = 8{,}495$$

**Decision:** Since **NPV is positive (₹8,495 > 0)**, the project is **financially viable** and should be **accepted**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing ordinary annuity (end of period) with annuity due (beginning of period). The question explicitly says "end of Year 1," so use the ordinary annuity formula.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to **compare PV with initial investment** to make a decision — this is mandatory in capital budgeting problems.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing the final answer without the **NPV and a clear accept/reject decision.** Examiners specifically award 1 mark for the correct decision statement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡

- 🎯 **Financial Management** is the science of planning, procuring, and utilizing funds to maximize shareholder wealth.
- 🎯 The **three core decisions** are: Investment, Financing, and Dividend.
- 🎯 **Wealth Maximization > Profit Maximization** — KTU board expects this comparison in answers.
- 🎯 **Indian Financial System** = Financial Markets + Financial Institutions + Instruments + Services.
- 🎯 **Money Market** is regulated by **RBI**; **Capital Market** is regulated by **SEBI** — a frequently tested fact.
- 🎯 **Time Value of Money** is the foundation: $FV = PV(1+r)^n$ and $PV = FV(1+r)^{-n}$.
- 🎯 **Annuity formulas** are tested in nearly every KTU Module 1 paper.
- 🎯 **Effective Annual Rate (EAR)** matters when compounding frequency is more than once a year.
- 🎯 Always convert **percentage to decimal** (e.g., 12% → 0.12) before substituting in formulas.
- 🎯 Always include the **decision statement** in capital budgeting problems (Accept/Reject based on NPV).
- 🎯 For diagrams, **draw neat block diagrams** showing the flow of funds — KTU awards extra marks for clarity.
- 🎯 Regulatory bodies to remember: **RBI, SEBI, IRDA, PFRDA, MCA** — short notes are commonly asked.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Board Tip:** In KTU valuation, **presentation matters.** Use headings, tables, and formulas in a step-wise manner. Always **state the formula, substitute, and then solve** to score full marks in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Value of Money
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Time Value of Money

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Time Value of Money (TVM)** is the foundational financial principle stating that a unit of currency (say, ₹1) available **today** is worth more than the same unit of currency available at a **future date**, because of its inherent **earning potential**. In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, TVM is described as the concept that *"money has a time-related value, such that its worth at different points in time differs, and this difference is mathematically computed using discounting and compounding mechanisms at a specified rate of interest."*

Mathematically, the TVM principle is governed by the relationship:

$$V_{today} \neq V_{future}$$

And the conversion between the two requires a **discount rate** $r$ and a **time horizon** $n$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Time Value of Money is the *bedrock concept* of PECBT631. Almost every subsequent module (Capital Budgeting, Working Capital, Capital Structure) is built on this single idea. Mastery of TVM equations is non-negotiable for both **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE)** and the **End Semester Examination (ESE)**.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of TVM

| Pillar | Symbolic Role | Plain English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Present Value (PV)** | Today's worth | What is a future cash flow worth to me right now? |
| **Future Value (FV)** | Tomorrow's worth | What will my current money grow into at a future date? |
| **Discount Rate ($r$)** | The opportunity cost | The minimum return I demand for postponing consumption |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Magic Tree"

Imagine a student, Arjun, who receives **₹1,000** from his grandfather. He has two choices:

1. **Spend it today** on a bicycle.
2. **Plant it** in a savings scheme that pays **10% per annum**.

If Arjun chooses Option 2, after **1 year** he has ₹1,100. After **2 years**, he has ₹1,210. After **10 years**, he has ₹2,593.74.

The same ₹1,000 is *mathematically* worth different amounts at different points in time. The money is **identical in face value**, but **unequal in purchasing power and opportunity cost**. This is the essence of TVM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does money "grow"?** Three real-world reasons underpin TVM:
> 1. **Inflation** — ₹100 today buys more rice than ₹100 next year.
> 2. **Opportunity Cost** — Money invested today could have earned returns.
> 3. **Risk Premium** — Future money is uncertain, so it must be discounted.

### 1.4 Two Operational Mechanisms

TVM operates through two symmetric processes:

- **Compounding** → moves money **forward in time** (PV → FV)
- **Discounting** → moves money **backward in time** (FV → PV)

Both use the same rate $r$, but in opposite mathematical directions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Framework

To systematically apply TVM, the KTU evaluator expects students to identify **five input variables** for every problem:

1. **Present Value (PV)** — the value at time $t=0$.
2. **Future Value (FV)** — the value at time $t=n$.
3. **Annuity Payment (PMT)** — a uniform cash flow occurring periodically.
4. **Interest / Discount Rate ($r$)** — expressed as a *decimal per period*.
5. **Number of Periods ($n$)** — must match the compounding frequency of $r$.

The **Sixth Function** — Time ($n$) and the **Seventh** — Rate ($r$) — are the *solved* variables when we ask *"How long?"* or *"At what rate?"*.

### 2.2 The Cash Flow Spectrum

KTU problems typically fall into one of these cash flow structures:

- **Single Cash Flow (Lump Sum)** — one inflow or outflow at a single point in time.
- **Annuity (Ordinary)** — equal payments at the **end** of each period.
- **Annuity Due** — equal payments at the **beginning** of each period.
- **Deferred Annuity** — an annuity that starts after a *delay period*.
- **Perpetuity** — an annuity that continues forever ($n \rightarrow \infty$).
- **Uneven Cash Flow Stream** — varying payments, solved by summation.

### 2.3 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory for ESE:** Memorize every formula below. KTU examiners *will* combine them — e.g., compute the PV of an annuity due, then discount that PV further by 3 years.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Future Value of a Lump Sum | $FV = PV \cdot (1+r)^{n}$ | $r$ = rate per period, $n$ = total periods |
| 2 | Present Value of a Lump Sum | $PV = FV \cdot (1+r)^{-n}$ | Reverse of compounding |
| 3 | FV of Ordinary Annuity | $FV_{OA} = PMT \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}$ | Payments at period **end** |
| 4 | PV of Ordinary Annuity | $PV_{OA} = PMT \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ | Discount each payment |
| 5 | FV of Annuity Due | $FV_{AD} = PMT \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r} \cdot (1+r)$ | Multiply OA by $(1+r)$ |
| 6 | PV of Annuity Due | $PV_{AD} = PMT \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} \cdot (1+r)$ | Multiply OA by $(1+r)$ |
| 7 | Perpetuity (PV only) | $PV_{Perp} = \dfrac{PMT}{r}$ | $n \to \infty$ |
| 8 | Effective Annual Rate (EAR) | $EAR = \left(1 + \dfrac{r_{nom}}{m}\right)^{m} - 1$ | $m$ = compounding events/year |
| 9 | Continuous Compounding | $FV = PV \cdot e^{r \cdot n}$ | $e \approx 2.71828$ |
| 10 | Present Value of Uneven CF | $PV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | Sum each discounted CF |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

TVM is the analytical engine behind:

- **Capital Budgeting** (Module 3) — NPV, IRR, Payback Period all require PV calculations.
- **Equipment Replacement Decisions** — Compare PV of costs across alternative machines.
- **Lease vs Buy Analysis** — Convert lease rentals and loan instalments to a common PV.
- **Project Valuation** — Engineering firms routinely discount future revenue streams to assess project viability.
- **Pension & Gratuity Funding** — Uses perpetuity and deferred annuity mathematics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention:** KTU examiners follow the **end-of-period cash flow convention** by default unless explicitly stated otherwise. If "beginning of year" is mentioned, you **must** use the annuity-due formula.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Future Value of a Lump Sum

**Starting Premise:** Let PV be deposited at time $t=0$ at a periodic rate $r$.

**Year 1:**
$$FV_{1} = PV + PV \cdot r = PV(1+r)$$

**Year 2:**
$$FV_{2} = FV_{1} + FV_{1} \cdot r = PV(1+r) + PV(1+r)r = PV(1+r)^{2}$$

**Year 3:**
$$FV_{3} = FV_{2}(1+r) = PV(1+r)^{2}(1+r) = PV(1+r)^{3}$$

**Generalizing to year $n$:**

$$\boxed{FV = PV \cdot (1+r)^{n}}$$

This exponential growth is the heart of compounding — a phenomenon Albert Einstein allegedly called *"the most powerful force in the universe."*

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Present Value of a Lump Sum

Starting from the FV identity and algebraically rearranging:

$$FV = PV(1+r)^{n}$$

Divide both sides by $(1+r)^{n}$:

$$\frac{FV}{(1+r)^{n}} = PV$$

$$\boxed{PV = FV \cdot (1+r)^{-n}}$$

The factor $(1+r)^{-n}$ is called the **Discount Factor** or **Present Value Interest Factor (PVIF)**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Future Value of an Ordinary Annuity

Consider an annuity of $PMT$ at the end of each of $n$ periods. Each payment compounds for a different number of periods:

- Payment 1 (made at end of year 1) compounds for $(n-1)$ more years.
- Payment 2 compounds for $(n-2)$ years.
- ...
- Payment $n$ compounds for $0$ years.

The total FV is the sum of a **geometric series**:

$$FV = PMT(1+r)^{n-1} + PMT(1+r)^{n-2} + \cdots + PMT(1+r)^{0}$$

$$FV = PMT \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (1+r)^{k}$$

Using the closed-form identity for a finite geometric series:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (1+r)^{k} = \frac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{FV_{OA} = PMT \cdot \frac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Present Value of an Ordinary Annuity

Discounting each payment back to $t=0$:

$$PV = \frac{PMT}{(1+r)^{1}} + \frac{PMT}{(1+r)^{2}} + \cdots + \frac{PMT}{(1+r)^{n}}$$

$$PV = PMT \sum_{t=1}^{n} (1+r)^{-t}$$

Using the geometric series identity:

$$\sum_{t=1}^{n} (1+r)^{-t} = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{PV_{OA} = PMT \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Worked Example (Comprehensive)

**Problem (KTU-Style 14-Mark Question):**

Riya is offered two payment alternatives for a freelance engineering contract:
- **Option A:** ₹5,00,000 paid immediately.
- **Option B:** ₹1,00,000 paid at the end of each year for 7 years.

Assume the relevant discount rate is **9% per annum**. Which option should Riya choose? Show all calculations.

**Step 1 — Recognize the cash flow structure.**

Option A is a **lump sum** at $t=0$. Option B is an **ordinary annuity** of ₹1,00,000 for 7 years.

**Step 2 — Calculate the PV of Option B.**

We use the Ordinary Annuity PV formula with $PMT = 1{,}00{,}000$, $r = 0.09$, $n = 7$.

$$PV_{OA} = 1{,}00{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - (1+0.09)^{-7}}{0.09}$$

Compute $(1.09)^{7}$:

$$(1.09)^{7} = 1.8280 \quad (\text{approx})$$

Therefore $(1.09)^{-7} = \frac{1}{1.8280} = 0.5470$.

Substitute back:

$$PV_{OA} = 1{,}00{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.5470}{0.09}$$

$$PV_{OA} = 1{,}00{,}000 \cdot \frac{0.4530}{0.09}$$

$$PV_{OA} = 1{,}00{,}000 \cdot 5.0330$$

$$PV_{OA} = ₹5{,}03{,}303$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Option A.**

$$PV_{A} = ₹5{,}00{,}000$$

$$PV_{B} = ₹5{,}03{,}303$$

**Step 4 — Decision Rule.**

Since $PV_{B} > PV_{A}$, Riya should choose **Option B** (the annuity), as it is worth **₹3,303 more** in present-value terms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** When $r > 0$, a lump sum received immediately is *almost always* preferred to a deferred annuity of equal nominal value — unless the annuity's PV exceeds the lump sum, as in this case where the annuity's duration and PMT structure tip the scales.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Problem Wording Clue | Formula to Apply |
|---|---|
| *"What will ₹X grow to in Y years?"* | FV of Lump Sum |
| *"What is ₹X received in Y years worth today?"* | PV of Lump Sum |
| *"Equal payments at the end of each year"* | Ordinary Annuity |
| *"Equal payments at the beginning of each year"* | Annuity Due (multiply by $(1+r)$) |
| *"Equal payments forever"* | Perpetuity |
| *"Compounded quarterly / semi-annually"* | Effective Annual Rate first, then apply |
| *"Continuously compounded"* | Use $e^{rn}$ |
| *"First payment starts after K years"* | Deferred Annuity — discount the PV back further |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Time Value of Money — Concept Map

The following Mermaid diagram maps the relationships between the core TVM constructs, showing how cash flow structures, time, and rate combine to yield the four primary valuation results.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Time Value of Money Core Engine] --> B1[Single Cash Flow Stream]
    A0 --> B2[Annuity Cash Flow Stream]
    A0 --> B3[Perpetuity Cash Flow Stream]
    A0 --> B4[Uneven Cash Flow Stream]

    B1 --> C1A[Compounding Forward]
    B1 --> C1B[Discounting Backward]

    C1A --> D1A[Future Value of Lump Sum]
    C1B --> D1B[Present Value of Lump Sum]

    B2 --> C2A[Ordinary Annuity End of Period]
    B2 --> C2B[Annuity Due Start of Period]
    B2 --> C2C[Deferred Annuity with Lag]

    C2A --> D2A[FV of Ordinary Annuity]
    C2A --> D2B[PV of Ordinary Annuity]
    C2B --> D2D[Multiply by 1 plus r]
    D2D --> D2E[FV of Annuity Due]
    D2D --> D2F[PV of Annuity Due]
    C2C --> D2G[Compute PV then discount further]

    B3 --> D3A[PV of Perpetuity equals PMT divided by r]

    B4 --> D4A[Sum of individually discounted CFs]

    A0 --> E1[Rate Conversion Layer]
    E1 --> E1A[Nominal to Effective]
    E1 --> E1B[Compounding Frequency m]
    E1 --> E1C[Continuous Compounding with e power rn]
```

### 4.2 Cash Flow Timeline — Ordinary Annuity vs Annuity Due

This diagram visualizes the **timing difference** between an ordinary annuity and an annuity due — a critical distinction that KTU examiners frequently test.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Time equals 0]:::anchor
    T1[Time equals 1]:::tick
    T2[Time equals 2]:::tick
    T3[Time equals 3]:::tick
    T4[Time equals 4]:::tick

    OA1[Ordinary Annuity PMT at 1]:::oaflow
    OA2[PMT at 2]:::oaflow
    OA3[PMT at 3]:::oaflow
    OA4[PMT at 4]:::oaflow

    AD0[Annuity Due PMT at 0]:::adflow
    AD1[PMT at 1]:::adflow
    AD2[PMT at 2]:::adflow
    AD3[PMT at 3]:::adflow

    T0 --- OA1
    T1 --- OA2
    T2 --- OA3
    T3 --- OA4

    T0 --- AD0
    T1 --- AD1
    T2 --- AD2
    T3 --- AD3

    classDef anchor fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0a1f3a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef tick fill:#e8e8e8,stroke:#888888,color:#222222
    classDef oaflow fill:#ffd9a8,stroke:#cc7a00,color:#5a2e00
    classDef adflow fill:#c4e3ff,stroke:#00509e,color:#002a55
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic Flow — Solving Any TVM Problem

This block diagram is a **functional architecture** that mirrors the cognitive process a KTU topper applies while solving a TVM problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Step 1 Read the Problem Carefully] --> P1[Step 2 Identify Cash Flow Type]
    P1 --> P1A{Lump Sum}
    P1 --> P1B{Annuity}
    P1 --> P1C{Perpetuity}
    P1 --> P1D{Uneven Stream}

    P1A --> P2A[Step 3 Apply FV equals PV times 1 plus r to the n]
    P1A --> P2B[Or Apply PV equals FV times 1 plus r to the minus n]
    P1B --> P2C[Ordinary Apply Annuity Formula]
    P1B --> P2D[Due Multiply Annuity Result by 1 plus r]
    P1C --> P2E[Apply PMT divided by r]
    P1D --> P2F[Discount each CF and sum]

    P2A --> P3[Step 4 Check Rate Period Consistency]
    P2B --> P3
    P2C --> P3
    P2D --> P3
    P2E --> P3
    P2F --> P3

    P3 --> P4{Does the rate match the period?}
    P4 -->|No| P4A[Convert to Effective Annual Rate first]
    P4 -->|Yes| P5[Step 5 Compute the Final Answer]
    P4A --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Verify using an alternative method if time permits]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*"Explain the concept of Time Value of Money. Why is a rupee received today worth more than a rupee received after one year?"*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Time Value of Money (TVM) is the financial principle that a unit of currency available at the present time is worth more than the same unit of currency available at a future date, due to its potential earning capacity.

**Reasons (2 Marks):** A rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow because of:
1. **Opportunity Cost of Capital** — Money available now can be invested to earn a return, so postponing consumption carries a measurable cost.
2. **Inflation Erosion** — General price levels tend to rise, reducing the purchasing power of future money.
3. **Uncertainty / Risk** — Future receipts are not guaranteed, so they must be discounted to reflect the time-risk premium.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*"Distinguish between Compounding and Discounting in the context of Time Value of Money."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Compounding | Discounting |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction of Time** | Moves cash flow **forward** (PV → FV) | Moves cash flow **backward** (FV → PV) |
| **Mathematical Operation** | Multiplication by $(1+r)^{n}$ | Multiplication by $(1+r)^{-n}$ |
| **Used When** | Computing the future worth of a present sum | Computing the present worth of a future sum |
| **Typical Application** | Retirement planning, savings growth | Capital budgeting, loan valuation |

(2 marks for distinction; 1 mark for the conclusion that both are inverse operations.)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Convention:** Each Part B question carries two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Both sub-parts must be attempted **OR** the student chooses between the two complete questions provided as alternatives (the *internal choice* rule).

---

### **QUESTION A — Alternative 1 [14 Marks]**

**Q.A(a) [7 Marks]:**
*A machine costs ₹8,00,000 today. Alternatively, the seller offers a deferred payment plan: ₹3,00,000 at the end of year 2, ₹3,00,000 at the end of year 4, and ₹4,00,000 at the end of year 6. If the cost of capital is 10% per annum compounded annually, determine which option is cheaper. Use Present Value analysis.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the cash flow structure.**

Option 1 is a lump sum at $t=0$. Option 2 is an **uneven cash flow stream** with three payments at years 2, 4, and 6.

**Step 2 — Calculate the PV of Option 2 (the deferred plan).**

Using the PV of a lump sum formula $PV = FV(1+r)^{-n}$ for each payment:

- **Year 2 Payment:**
$$PV_{2} = 3{,}00{,}000 \cdot (1.10)^{-2} = 3{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.8264 = ₹2{,}47{,}933$$

- **Year 4 Payment:**
$$PV_{4} = 3{,}00{,}000 \cdot (1.10)^{-4} = 3{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.6830 = ₹2{,}04{,}904$$

- **Year 6 Payment:**
$$PV_{6} = 4{,}00{,}000 \cdot (1.10)^{-6} = 4{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.5645 = ₹2{,}25{,}793$$

**Step 3 — Sum the discounted cash flows.**

$$PV_{Total} = 2{,}47{,}933 + 2{,}04{,}904 + 2{,}25{,}793 = ₹6{,}78{,}630$$

**Step 4 — Compare and conclude.**

$$PV_{Option 1} = ₹8{,}00{,}000$$
$$PV_{Option 2} = ₹6{,}78{,}630$$

Since $PV_{Option 2} < PV_{Option 1}$, the **deferred payment plan is cheaper by ₹1,21,370 in present value terms**. The buyer should choose Option 2.

---

**Q.A(b) [7 Marks]:**
*A construction firm wants to accumulate ₹25,00,000 at the end of 10 years to replace a major equipment. The firm can earn 8% per annum compounded semi-annually on its savings. Calculate:*
*(i) The effective annual rate (EAR).*
*(ii) The annual sinking fund deposit (assuming deposits made at the end of each year).*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Effective Annual Rate (3 Marks):**

The nominal rate $r_{nom} = 8\% = 0.08$, compounded semi-annually, so $m = 2$.

$$EAR = \left(1 + \frac{0.08}{2}\right)^{2} - 1$$

$$EAR = (1.04)^{2} - 1 = 1.0816 - 1 = 0.0816$$

$$\boxed{EAR = 8.16\%}$$

**Part (ii) — Annual Sinking Fund Deposit (4 Marks):**

We use the FV of an Ordinary Annuity formula. The future target is $FV = 25{,}00{,}000$, the rate per period is the EAR $= 0.0816$, and $n = 10$ years.

$$FV = PMT \cdot \frac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}$$

$$25{,}00{,}000 = PMT \cdot \frac{(1.0816)^{10} - 1}{0.0816}$$

Compute $(1.0816)^{10}$:

$$(1.0816)^{10} \approx 2.1903$$

Substitute:

$$25{,}00{,}000 = PMT \cdot \frac{2.1903 - 1}{0.0816}$$

$$25{,}00{,}000 = PMT \cdot \frac{1.1903}{0.0816}$$

$$25{,}00{,}000 = PMT \cdot 14.587$$

$$PMT = \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{14.587} = ₹1{,}71{,}390 \text{ (approx)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Annual Deposit} \approx ₹1{,}71{,}390}$$

---

### **QUESTION B — Alternative 2 [14 Marks]**

**Q.B(a) [7 Marks]:**
*Define an Annuity. A bank offers Mr. Krishnan a monthly pension of ₹15,000 for 15 years, starting from the **beginning** of each month. If the discount rate is 12% per annum compounded monthly, calculate the present value of this annuity. Comment on the result.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define an Annuity (1 Mark).**

An annuity is a series of equal cash flows occurring at regular intervals of time over a specified number of periods.

**Step 2 — Convert annual rate to monthly rate (1 Mark).**

$$r_{monthly} = \frac{0.12}{12} = 0.01 = 1\%$$

**Step 3 — Identify total number of periods (1 Mark).**

$$n = 15 \times 12 = 180 \text{ months}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize Annuity Due (1 Mark).**

Since payments are made at the **beginning** of each month, this is an **Annuity Due**, not an Ordinary Annuity. The formula is:

$$PV_{AD} = PMT \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} \cdot (1+r)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the PV (3 Marks).**

Compute $(1.01)^{180}$:

$$(1.01)^{180} \approx 5.9916$$

Therefore $(1.01)^{-180} = \frac{1}{5.9916} \approx 0.1669$.

Substitute:

$$PV_{AD} = 15{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.1669}{0.01} \cdot (1.01)$$

$$PV_{AD} = 15{,}000 \cdot \frac{0.8331}{0.01} \cdot 1.01$$

$$PV_{AD} = 15{,}000 \cdot 83.31 \cdot 1.01$$

$$PV_{AD} = 15{,}000 \cdot 84.1431$$

$$\boxed{PV_{AD} \approx ₹12{,}62{,}147}$$

**Comment:** The present value of the pension (~₹12.62 lakhs) is substantially less than its nominal total value (₹15,000 × 180 = ₹27,00,000), illustrating the time-value erosion of long-dated cash flows.

---

**Q.B(b) [7 Marks]:**
*A perpetual bond pays ₹5,000 at the end of every year forever. If the required rate of return is 7.5% per annum:*
*(i) Calculate the present value of the perpetuity.*
*(ii) If the rate suddenly rises to 9% per annum, what is the percentage change in the bond's value?*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — PV of Perpetuity (3 Marks).**

$$PV_{Perp} = \frac{PMT}{r} = \frac{5{,}000}{0.075}$$

$$\boxed{PV_{Perp} = ₹66{,}666.67}$$

**Part (ii) — Sensitivity Analysis (4 Marks).**

When the rate rises to 9%:

$$PV_{New} = \frac{5{,}000}{0.09} = ₹55{,}555.56$$

**Percentage change:**

$$\Delta\% = \frac{55{,}555.56 - 66{,}666.67}{66{,}666.67} \times 100$$

$$\Delta\% = \frac{-11{,}111.11}{66{,}666.67} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\Delta\% \approx -16.67\%}$$

A 1.5 percentage-point rise in the discount rate causes the bond's value to fall by **16.67%**, demonstrating the high **duration risk** of perpetuities.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Rate-Period Mismatch (–2 to –3 marks):** If the rate is 12% compounded monthly, students often forget to convert to $r = 1\%$ per month and $n = 180$ months. KTU examiners *specifically watch* for this.
> 2. **Ordinary vs Due Confusion (–2 marks):** "Beginning of year" and "end of year" are not interchangeable. The omission of the $(1+r)$ multiplier is a top-3 most common error.
> 3. **Sign Convention Blunder (–1 mark):** PV of a payment **received** is positive; PV of a payment **made** is negative. Mixing them up forfeits clarity marks.
> 4. **Rounding Early (–1 mark):** Round only the **final** answer. Intermediate steps should retain at least 4 decimals.
> 5. **Forgetting the Annuity-Due Multiplier on Deferred Annuities (–3 marks):** A "deferred annuity due" requires *both* a delay-discount and a due-multiplier.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TVM Core:** A rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow because of opportunity cost, inflation, and risk.
- **Two Engines:** **Compounding** (PV → FV) and **Discounting** (FV → PV) are inverse operations governed by $(1+r)^{\pm n}$.
- **Five Inputs:** PV, FV, PMT, $r$, and $n$ — know which is the *unknown* in any given problem.
- **Annuity Variants:**
  - *Ordinary:* Payments at **end** of period — use the standard formula.
  - *Due:* Payments at **beginning** of period — multiply OA result by $(1+r)$.
  - *Deferred:* Has a **lag** — discount the PV back by the lag years.
  - *Perpetuity:* $n \to \infty$ — only PV formula exists: $PMT / r$.
- **Rate Conversion:** Always convert nominal rates to **Effective Annual Rate (EAR)** before applying annuity formulas spanning multiple years.
- **Continuous Compounding:** Use the natural exponential $e^{rn}$ where $e \approx 2.71828$.
- **Cash Flow Convention:** KTU defaults to **end-of-period** payments unless the problem explicitly states "beginning of."
- **Sensitivity Insight:** Perpetuities have **infinite duration risk** — even small rate changes cause large value swings.
- **The Master Equation to Memorize:** $PV \cdot (1+r)^{n} = FV$ — every other formula is a derivative of this single identity.
- **Verification Habit:** For exam answers, always sanity-check by re-reading the question: "Did I answer *what* was asked, or *what* I assumed was asked?"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** In Part B questions, **always** show the formula, the substituted values, AND the intermediate computation. KTU examiners award *step marks* generously when work is shown, but award *zero* partial credit for a bare final number.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Concept of Interest — The "Rent" on Money

In the language of **Financial Management**, *interest* is the compensation paid by a borrower to a lender for the use of borrowed money over a period of time. The original sum lent or borrowed is called the **Principal (P)**, the compensation charged per unit of time is the **Rate of Interest (R)**, and the duration is the **Time Period (T)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Interest is the price paid for the use of borrowed money, or the return earned for lending money. It represents the time value of money — the economic principle that a rupee available today is worth more than the same rupee available in the future because of its earning potential."*

### 1.2 Time Value of Money (TVM) — The Foundational Pillar

Every concept in Module 1 of *Financial Management* rests on a single, immutable truth: **money has a time value**. A rupee in your hand today can be invested, earn a return, and become more than a rupee tomorrow. Therefore, a rupee *today* is intrinsically more valuable than the *promise* of a rupee tomorrow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Mango Seller:**
> Imagine your friend Ravi promises to give you **₹100 one year from now**. Would you accept a *fresh* ₹100 today instead? Most of us would. Why? Because today's ₹100 can be deposited in a bank earning, say, **6% per annum**, becoming **₹106 next year**. The promised future ₹100 is therefore worth *less* than today's ₹100. That difference — **₹6** — is the *time value* of money.

### 1.3 The Two Fundamental Methods of Computing Interest

The syllabus demands mastery of two distinct regimes under which interest accrues:

#### A) Simple Interest (SI)
Interest is calculated **only on the original principal** for the entire loan/investment period. The interest earned in each year remains constant.

#### B) Compound Interest (CI)
Interest is calculated on the **original principal *plus* the accumulated interest of prior periods**. In other words, you earn *"interest on interest"* — a phenomenon the French mathematician **Richard Witt** called the "method of compound interest" as early as **1613**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Compound Interest is called the "Eighth Wonder of the World":**
> Albert Einstein reportedly described compound interest as the most powerful force in finance. Because each period's interest joins the principal and itself earns interest in the next period, wealth grows *exponentially* rather than *linearly*.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Principal (Present Value) | $P$ or $PV$ | Currency units (₹, $, €) |
| Rate of Interest | $R$ or $r$ | **% per annum** (nominal) |
| Time | $T$ or $t$ or $n$ | **Years** (may be fractional) |
| Compounding Frequency | $m$ or $n$ | Times per year (1, 2, 4, 12, 365) |
| Future Value | $A$ or $FV$ or $S$ | Currency units |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Simple Interest — The Linear Growth Model

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. The lender advances a sum $P$ to the borrower at rate $R\%$ per annum.
2. At the end of every year, interest for that year is computed **only on the original $P$**.
3. The interest per year is therefore **constant** = $P \times R/100$.
4. Total simple interest over $T$ years = $(P \times R \times T)/100$.
5. The total amount (principal + interest) at the end of $T$ years is $A = P + SI$.

### Real-World Utility
- **Short-term commercial loans**, **bank overdrafts**, and certain **microfinance** transactions still use simple interest.
- **T-bills** (Treasury Bills) in India are issued on a *discount basis* essentially derived from simple interest.
- **Income tax** on fixed deposits historically used simple interest for certain calculations.

---

## 2.2 Compound Interest — The Exponential Growth Model

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. Interest for Year 1 is computed on $P$: $I_1 = P \times r$.
2. At end of Year 1, principal becomes $P + I_1 = P(1+r)$.
3. Interest for Year 2 is computed on this *new* (higher) balance: $I_2 = P(1+r) \times r$.
4. The process continues, with each period's interest joining the base for the next.
5. The balance after $n$ periods is $A = P(1+r)^n$ — a **geometric progression**.

### Why It Matters in Engineering & Business
- **Project cost-benefit analysis** (NPV, IRR) is grounded entirely in compound discounting.
- **Loan EMIs** (Equated Monthly Instalments) are derived using compound interest.
- **Capital budgeting decisions** in any B.Tech graduate's future as a manager depend on compounding.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for KTU Part-B questions on this topic.** Memorize every cell.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple Interest | $SI \;=\; \dfrac{P \times R \times T}{100}$ | $P$ = Principal, $R$ = Annual % rate, $T$ = Years |
| 2 | Simple Interest Amount | $A_{SI} \;=\; P\left(1 + \dfrac{RT}{100}\right)$ | Total sum due under SI |
| 3 | Compound Amount (Annual) | $A_{CI} \;=\; P\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{n}$ | $n$ = whole years, $r$ = annual rate |
| 4 | Compound Interest | $CI \;=\; P\left[\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^{n} - 1\right]$ | The "interest portion" of compound growth |
| 5 | General Compounding (m times/yr) | $A \;=\; P\left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100m}\right)^{m \cdot n}$ | $m$ = compounding frequency per year |
| 6 | Continuous Compounding | $A \;=\; P \cdot e^{r \cdot n}$ | $e \approx 2.71828$ (Euler's number) |
| 7 | Effective Annual Rate (EAR) | $EAR \;=\; \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{m}\right)^{m} - 1$ | Converts nominal to true annual rate |
| 8 | Nominal Rate from EAR | $r_{nom} \;=\; m \cdot \left[(1 + EAR)^{1/m} - 1\right]$ | Inverse of EAR formula |
| 9 | Present Value (Discounting) | $PV \;=\; \dfrac{FV}{(1 + r)^{n}}$ | "What is future ₹X worth today?" |
| 10 | Doubling Time (Rule of 72) | $T_{double} \;\approx\; \dfrac{72}{R\%}$ | Quick estimate, accurate for $r \le 20\%$ |
| 11 | Doubling Time (Exact, Compounded) | $T_{double} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln 2}{\ln(1+r)} \;=\; \dfrac{0.6931}{\ln(1+r)}$ | Logarithmic derivation |
| 12 | Future Value of Annuity (Ordinary) | $FV_{annuity} \;=\; P \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}$ | Sum of $n$ equal payments at end of period |
| 13 | Present Value of Annuity (Ordinary) | $PV_{annuity} \;=\; P \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ | Single sum equivalent of $n$ payments |
| 14 | Present Value of Perpetuity | $PV_{perp} \;=\; \dfrac{C}{r}$ | $C$ = constant periodic cash flow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - $m = 1$ ⇒ Annual compounding (default unless stated)
> - $m = 2$ ⇒ Semi-annual
> - $m = 4$ ⇒ Quarterly
> - $m = 12$ ⇒ Monthly (typical for bank loans)
> - $m \to \infty$ ⇒ Continuous compounding
> - As $m$ increases, **Effective Annual Rate (EAR) increases** for a fixed nominal rate.

---

## 2.4 The Compounding Frequency Effect — Why It Matters

Suppose you invest **₹10,000** for **1 year** at a nominal rate of **12% per annum**. The amount you receive depends on *how often* interest is compounded:

| Compounding | $m$ | Calculation | Amount (₹) | EAR (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Annual | 1 | $10000 \times (1.12)^1$ | 11,200.00 | 12.00 |
| Semi-Annual | 2 | $10000 \times (1.06)^2$ | 11,236.00 | 12.36 |
| Quarterly | 4 | $10000 \times (1.03)^4$ | 11,255.09 | 12.55 |
| Monthly | 12 | $10000 \times (1.01)^{12}$ | 11,268.25 | 12.68 |
| Daily | 365 | $10000 \times (1+0.12/365)^{365}$ | 11,274.75 | 12.7475 |
| Continuous | $\infty$ | $10000 \times e^{0.12}$ | 11,275.03 | 12.75 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** As $m \to \infty$, the effective rate approaches $(e^r - 1)$, which is the **continuous compounding limit**. This is the theoretical maximum growth for a given nominal rate.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Compound Interest Formula

We begin with the *fundamental accumulation principle*: the balance at the end of one period becomes the principal for the next.

Let $A_k$ = amount at the end of year $k$, and $r = R/100$.

**Step 1:** Start with principal $P$.
$$A_0 \;=\; P$$

**Step 2:** At the end of Year 1, interest is $P \cdot r$, added to principal:
$$A_1 \;=\; P + P \cdot r \;=\; P(1 + r)$$

**Step 3:** At the end of Year 2, interest is calculated on $A_1$, not on $P$:
$$A_2 \;=\; A_1 + A_1 \cdot r \;=\; A_1(1 + r) \;=\; P(1+r)(1+r) \;=\; P(1+r)^2$$

**Step 4:** By mathematical induction, after $n$ years:
$$A_n \;=\; P(1+r)^n$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, the compound interest earned is:
$$CI \;=\; A_n - P \;=\; P\left[(1+r)^n - 1\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **geometric sequence** $P, P(1+r), P(1+r)^2, \ldots$ — each term is $(1+r)$ times the previous one, with common ratio $(1+r)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Effective Annual Rate (EAR)

Suppose the nominal rate is $r$ compounded $m$ times per year. The per-period rate is $r/m$.

**Step 1:** After 1 year (i.e., $m$ compounding periods):
$$A \;=\; P\left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{m}$$

**Step 2:** Define EAR such that the same $A$ results from a single annual compounding at rate EAR:
$$A \;=\; P(1 + EAR)$$

**Step 3:** Equate the two expressions:
$$P(1 + EAR) \;=\; P\left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{m}$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $P$ and solve:
$$EAR \;=\; \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{m} - 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limit Behaviour:** As $m \to \infty$, applying the standard limit $\lim_{m \to \infty} (1 + x/m)^m = e^x$, we get the continuous compounding rate $EAR_{cont} = e^r - 1$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison: Simple vs. Compound Interest

| Dimension | Simple Interest | Compound Interest |
|---|---|---|
| Base for calculation | Always original $P$ | Original $P$ + accumulated interest |
| Nature of growth | **Linear** (arithmetic) | **Exponential** (geometric) |
| Formula for $A$ | $P(1 + rT)$ | $P(1 + r)^T$ |
| Interest earned in year $k$ | Constant = $Pr$ | $P(1+r)^{k-1} \cdot r$ (grows each year) |
| Long-term use | Short-term loans, T-bills | Mortgages, deposits, bonds, capital projects |
| Total interest after $T$ years | $P \cdot r \cdot T$ | $P[(1+r)^T - 1]$ |
| Difference ($CI - SI$) at year $T$ | — | $P[(1+r)^T - 1 - rT]$ |
| Doubling time | $T = 100/R$ (linear) | $T = \log 2 / \log(1+r)$ (shorter) |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Cross-Over Insight:** For very small $r$ and short $T$, SI and CI give nearly identical results because the "interest on interest" effect has not yet compounded. For large $T$ and high $r$, the divergence is dramatic. This is why long-term investors favour compounding.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Exam Pattern)

### Example 1 — Simple Interest Application

**Problem:** Find the simple interest on ₹25,000 for 4 years 6 months at 12% per annum. Also find the total amount.

**Step 1:** Convert time to years:
$$T \;=\; 4 + \frac{6}{12} \;=\; 4.5 \text{ years}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the SI formula:
$$SI \;=\; \frac{P \times R \times T}{100} \;=\; \frac{25000 \times 12 \times 4.5}{100}$$

**Step 3:** Compute:
$$SI \;=\; \frac{1{,}350{,}000}{100} \;=\; ₹13{,}500$$

**Step 4:** Total amount:
$$A \;=\; P + SI \;=\; 25000 + 13500 \;=\; ₹38{,}500$$

---

### Example 2 — Compound Interest Application

**Problem:** Find the compound interest on ₹50,000 for 3 years at 10% per annum compounded annually. Also calculate the effective rate if the same nominal rate were compounded quarterly.

**Part (i) — Annual Compounding:**

**Step 1:** Apply the compound amount formula with $P = 50000$, $r = 10\% = 0.10$, $n = 3$:
$$A \;=\; 50000(1 + 0.10)^3$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the power:
$$(1.10)^3 \;=\; 1.10 \times 1.10 \times 1.10 \;=\; 1.21 \times 1.10 \;=\; 1.331$$

**Step 3:** Compute the amount:
$$A \;=\; 50000 \times 1.331 \;=\; ₹66{,}550$$

**Step 4:** Compute the compound interest:
$$CI \;=\; A - P \;=\; 66550 - 50000 \;=\; ₹16{,}550$$

**Part (ii) — Effective Rate under Quarterly Compounding:**

**Step 1:** With $m = 4$, $r = 0.10$, the per-quarter rate is $r/m = 0.025$.

**Step 2:** Apply the EAR formula:
$$EAR \;=\; \left(1 + \frac{0.10}{4}\right)^{4} - 1 \;=\; (1.025)^4 - 1$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:
$$(1.025)^4 \;=\; 1.10381289 \quad\Rightarrow\quad EAR \;\approx\; 0.10381 \;=\; 10.381\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Quarterly compounding boosts the effective rate from 10% to **10.381%** — a small but meaningful difference for large principals or long horizons.

---

### Example 3 — Doubling Time Comparison (SI vs CI)

**Problem:** At what rate of interest will a sum double in 8 years under (a) simple interest, and (b) compound interest?

**Part (a) — Simple Interest:**

**Step 1:** Condition for doubling: $A = 2P$.
$$2P \;=\; P\left(1 + \frac{R \times 8}{100}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Simplify:
$$2 \;=\; 1 + \frac{8R}{100} \quad\Rightarrow\quad 1 \;=\; \frac{8R}{100}$$

**Step 3:** Solve:
$$R \;=\; \frac{100}{8} \;=\; 12.5\% \text{ per annum}$$

**Part (b) — Compound Interest:**

**Step 1:** Condition for doubling: $A = 2P$.
$$2P \;=\; P(1 + r)^8 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 2 \;=\; (1 + r)^8$$

**Step 2:** Take the 8th root:
$$1 + r \;=\; 2^{1/8}$$

**Step 3:** Use the value $2^{1/8} \approx 1.09051$:
$$r \;\approx\; 0.09051 \;=\; 9.051\% \text{ per annum}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for the Examiner:** Notice that the rate required to double in 8 years under CI (**9.05%**) is *less* than under SI (**12.5%**). This is the magic of compounding — a *lower* rate achieves the same wealth target because reinvested interest itself earns interest.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Time Value of Money

The following block diagram maps the logical flow of how a principal evolves under different interest regimes, as used in financial management software and actuarial systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Principal Input (P)"] --> node2{"Interest Regime Selector"}
    node2 -- Simple Interest --> node3["Compute Annual Interest = P x r"]
    node3 --> node4["Multiply by T years"]
    node4 --> node5["Amount A = P + P x r x T"]
    node2 -- Compound Interest --> node6["Compute Period Rate = r / m"]
    node6 --> node7["Accumulate Factor = 1 + r over m"]
    node7 --> node8{"Compounding Frequency m"}
    node8 -- Annual m=1 --> node9["A = P times 1 plus r to power n"]
    node8 -- Periodic m gt 1 --> node10["A = P times 1 plus r by m to power m n"]
    node8 -- Continuous m tends to infinity --> node11["A = P times e to power r n"]
    node9 --> node12["Final Amount Output"]
    node10 --> node12
    node11 --> node12
    node5 --> node12
    node12 --> node13["Apply Time Value Adjustment"]
    node13 --> node14["Discounted Present Value PV"]
    node14 --> node15["Decision Module: Invest or Reject"]
```

## 4.2 Decision-Support Tree — Choosing Between SI and CI

This diagram helps students decide which formula to deploy based on the *problem context*, a common point of confusion in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1["Read the problem statement"] --> q2{"Does the word COMPOUND appear?"}
    q2 -- "No / Silent" --> q3{"Is the period less than 1 year and T-bill style?"}
    q3 -- "Yes" --> s1["Use SI Formula: P x R x T over 100"]
    q3 -- "No" --> s2["Re-read: likely SI unless specified"]
    q2 -- "Yes" --> q4{"Compounding frequency given?"}
    q4 -- "Annual default" --> c1["Use CI Formula: P times 1 plus r to power n"]
    q4 -- "Semi-Annual / Quarterly / Monthly" --> c2["Use General Formula: P times 1 plus r by m to power m n"]
    q4 -- "Continuous / e-based" --> c3["Use A = P times e to power r n"]
    c1 --> c4["Subtract P to get CI if asked"]
    c2 --> c4
    c3 --> c4
    s1 --> s5["Add SI to P to get A if asked"]
    s2 --> s5
    c4 --> end1["Final Answer in Currency Units"]
    s5 --> end1
```

## 4.3 Comparative Growth Trajectory (SI vs CI) — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Year 0: Principal P"] --> nodeB["Year 1: SI Line vs CI Curve start"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Year 2: Gap WIDENS"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Year 3: CI curve rises SHARPLY"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Year 4: CI line ACCELERATES"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Year 5: DIVERGENCE is large"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Year N: CI is MULTIPLE of SI"]
    nodeB -.->|"Linear growth P x r per year"| nodeG
    nodeB -.->|"Exponential growth P x 1+r to power N"| nodeG
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the schematic:** The straight line represents simple interest (constant slope $Pr$). The curved line represents compound interest (slope increases each year). The vertical gap between the two at year $N$ is the **"interest-on-interest" bonus** — the engine of long-term wealth creation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Time Value of Money `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"Define the concept of Time Value of Money. Why is a rupee received today worth more than a rupee received after one year?"**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
- **Definition (1 mark):** Time Value of Money (TVM) is the financial principle that the *value of a sum of money changes with time* because money available today can be invested to earn a return, making it worth more than the same nominal amount in the future.
- **Reason 1 — Earning Potential (1 mark):** A rupee today can be deposited or invested to earn interest, thereby growing into more than a rupee in the future.
- **Reason 2 — Inflation / Purchasing Power (1 mark):** Inflation erodes the purchasing power of money over time; hence a future rupee buys fewer goods and services than today's rupee.

---

### Question 2: Nominal vs. Effective Rate `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"Distinguish between Nominal Rate of Interest and Effective Annual Rate of Interest. Illustrate with a one-sentence example."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
- **Nominal Rate (1 mark):** The *quoted* or *stated* annual rate before accounting for the compounding frequency. It does not reflect the true growth of the investment.
- **Effective Annual Rate (1 mark):** The *actual* annual rate of return earned after compounding effects are taken into account. Formula: $EAR = (1 + r/m)^m - 1$.
- **Illustration (1 mark):** A nominal rate of **12% per annum compounded monthly** yields an effective rate of $(1 + 0.12/12)^{12} - 1 \approx 12.68\%$, meaning the investor effectively earns **12.68%** per year, not 12%.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** In the End Semester Examination, you must answer *one full 14-mark question per module*. You are given an **internal choice** — answer either Question A or Question B. Each question carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]`

**Q(a)** Explain the concepts of Simple Interest and Compound Interest. Derive the compound interest formula from first principles. *(7 Marks)*

**Q(b)** A sum of ₹80,000 is invested for 5 years at 9% per annum. Calculate:
- (i) The total amount under simple interest.
- (ii) The total amount under compound interest (annual compounding).
- (iii) The "interest-on-interest" gain under compound interest. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q(a) (7 Marks)

**Conceptual Explanation (3 Marks):**

- **Simple Interest (1.5 Marks):** Interest is computed on the *original principal* throughout the loan period. The interest per year is constant = $P \times r$. The total amount grows **linearly** with time: $A_{SI} = P(1 + rT)$. It is typically used for short-term instruments like Treasury Bills and bank overdrafts.

- **Compound Interest (1.5 Marks):** Interest is computed on the *outstanding balance* (principal + accumulated interest). Each period's interest is added back to the principal, forming a *new base* for the next period. The amount grows **exponentially**: $A_{CI} = P(1+r)^n$. This is the universal method for deposits, loans, bonds, and capital budgeting.

**Derivation of Compound Interest Formula (4 Marks):**

Let $A_n$ be the amount at the end of year $n$ and $r = R/100$.

**Step 1:** Year 0 (start):
$$A_0 \;=\; P$$

**Step 2:** Year 1 — interest $Pr$ added to principal:
$$A_1 \;=\; P + Pr \;=\; P(1 + r)$$

**Step 3:** Year 2 — interest now calculated on $A_1$:
$$A_2 \;=\; A_1 + A_1 \cdot r \;=\; A_1(1 + r) \;=\; P(1+r)(1+r) \;=\; P(1+r)^2$$

**Step 4:** Year 3 — by recurrence:
$$A_3 \;=\; A_2(1 + r) \;=\; P(1+r)^2(1+r) \;=\; P(1+r)^3$$

**Step 5:** By mathematical induction, after $n$ years:
$$A_n \;=\; P(1+r)^n$$

**Step 6:** Therefore the compound interest is:
$$CI \;=\; A_n - P \;=\; P\left[(1+r)^n - 1\right]$$

---

#### Model Solution — Q(b) (7 Marks)

Given: $P = 80{,}000$, $R = 9\%$, $T = 5$ years, $r = 0.09$.

**(i) Amount under Simple Interest (2 Marks):**
$$A_{SI} \;=\; P\left(1 + \frac{RT}{100}\right) \;=\; 80000\left(1 + \frac{9 \times 5}{100}\right) \;=\; 80000 \times 1.45 \;=\; ₹1{,}16{,}000$$

**(ii) Amount under Compound Interest (3 Marks):**
$$A_{CI} \;=\; P(1+r)^n \;=\; 80000(1.09)^5$$

Computing $(1.09)^5$ step by step:
$$(1.09)^2 \;=\; 1.1881$$
$$(1.09)^3 \;=\; 1.1881 \times 1.09 \;=\; 1.295029$$
$$(1.09)^4 \;=\; 1.295029 \times 1.09 \;=\; 1.411582$$
$$(1.09)^5 \;=\; 1.411582 \times 1.09 \;=\; 1.538624$$

Therefore:
$$A_{CI} \;=\; 80000 \times 1.538624 \;=\; ₹1{,}23{,}089.92$$

**(iii) Interest-on-Interest Gain (2 Marks):**
$$\text{Bonus} \;=\; A_{CI} - A_{SI} \;=\; 123089.92 - 116000 \;=\; ₹7{,}089.92$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The compound interest itself is $CI = 123089.92 - 80000 = ₹43{,}089.92$, while the simple interest would have been $SI = 80000 \times 0.09 \times 5 = ₹36{,}000$. The extra ₹7,089.92 is indeed the **interest earned on the reinvested interest**.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q(a)** Define Nominal and Effective Rates of Interest. A bank offers 12% per annum compounded quarterly. Compute the Effective Annual Rate (EAR). What would be the equivalent continuous compounding rate? *(7 Marks)*

**Q(b)** Mr. Krishnan deposits ₹1,00,000 in a bank that offers 10% per annum compounded half-yearly. Find:
- (i) The maturity amount after 4 years.
- (ii) The compound interest earned.
- (iii) How much *additional* interest would he earn if the bank compounded *monthly* instead? *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q(a) (7 Marks)

**Definitions (2 Marks):**
- **Nominal Rate (1 Mark):** The *headline* or *quoted* rate of interest, without considering the compounding effect. For a nominal $r\%$ compounded $m$ times per year, the per-period rate is $r/m$.
- **Effective Annual Rate (1 Mark):** The *true* annual rate of growth after compounding is incorporated. It represents the actual percentage increase in the value of the investment over one year.

**Computation of EAR (3 Marks):**

Given: $r = 12\% = 0.12$, $m = 4$ (quarterly).

Per-quarter rate: $r/m = 0.12/4 = 0.03$.

$$EAR \;=\; \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{m} - 1 \;=\; (1.03)^4 - 1$$

Computing $(1.03)^4$:
$$(1.03)^2 \;=\; 1.0609$$
$$(1.03)^4 \;=\; (1.0609)^2 \;=\; 1.12550881$$

Therefore:
$$EAR \;=\; 1.12550881 - 1 \;=\; 0.12551 \;=\; 12.551\%$$

**Continuous Compounding Equivalent (2 Marks):**

Continuous compounding gives $A = Pe^{rn}$, so the continuous rate $r_c$ satisfies:
$$e^{r_c} - 1 \;=\; 0.12551 \quad\Rightarrow\quad e^{r_c} \;=\; 1.12551$$

Taking the natural logarithm:
$$r_c \;=\; \ln(1.12551) \;\approx\; 0.11822 \;\approx\; 11.82\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The continuous compounding rate of **11.82%** delivers the same growth as 12% compounded quarterly. Hence, the *true* rate "saved" by switching to continuous compounding is roughly **0.18 percentage points**.

---

#### Model Solution — Q(b) (7 Marks)

Given: $P = 1{,}00{,}000$, $r = 10\% = 0.10$.

**(i) Maturity Amount after 4 years at half-yearly compounding (2.5 Marks):**

Here $m = 2$, $T = 4$ years, total periods $= 2 \times 4 = 8$. Per-half-year rate $= 0.10/2 = 0.05$.

$$A_{half} \;=\; P\left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{mT} \;=\; 100000(1.05)^8$$

Computing $(1.05)^8$:
$$(1.05)^2 \;=\; 1.1025$$
$$(1.05)^4 \;=\; (1.1025)^2 \;=\; 1.21550625$$
$$(1.05)^8 \;=\; (1.21550625)^2 \;=\; 1.47745544$$

Therefore:
$$A_{half} \;=\; 100000 \times 1.47745544 \;=\; ₹1{,}47{,}745.54$$

**(ii) Compound Interest Earned (1.5 Marks):**
$$CI_{half} \;=\; A_{half} - P \;=\; 147745.54 - 100000 \;=\; ₹47{,}745.54$$

**(iii) Additional Interest under Monthly Compounding (3 Marks):**

Here $m = 12$, $T = 4$ years, total periods $= 48$. Per-month rate $= 0.10/12 \approx 0.0083333$.

$$A_{monthly} \;=\; 100000(1 + 0.10/12)^{48} \;=\; 100000(1.0083333)^{48}$$

Computing $(1.0083333)^{48}$ step by step (using $\ln$):
$$\ln(1.0083333) \;\approx\; 0.008299$$
$$48 \times 0.008299 \;\approx\; 0.39835$$
$$e^{0.39835} \;\approx\; 1.48935$$

Therefore:
$$A_{monthly} \;\approx\; 100000 \times 1.48935 \;=\; ₹1{,}48{,}935$$

**Additional interest earned:**
$$\Delta CI \;=\; 148935 - 147745.54 \;\approx\; ₹1{,}189.46$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Commentary:** This sub-part tests the student's grasp of the compounding frequency effect. The expected answer should clearly state that *more frequent* compounding yields a *higher* maturity amount, all else being equal.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rate and time to the same base:** If compounding is half-yearly, the rate is *half* and the time is *doubled* (in half-year periods). Students often mistakenly use the full annual rate $r$ with $mT$ periods.
> 2. **Confusing "amount" with "interest":** The compound *interest* is $A - P$, not $A$ itself. Examiners frequently award 1 mark for stating the final $A$ but deduct 0.5–1 mark if the $CI$ is not explicitly computed.
> 3. **Piping symbol (|) inside LaTeX:** In written exams, this isn't an issue, but in *digital answer scripts* (KTU's online portal), students using text editors must avoid using the | character in formulas, as it gets misinterpreted as a table separator.
> 4. **Rounding too early:** Round only the *final* answer to 2 decimal places. Intermediate rounding (e.g., rounding $(1.09)^3$ to 1.30) causes a cascade of errors and a 0.5–1 mark penalty.
> 5. **Forgetting units:** Always write ₹ (or the relevant currency symbol) with the final numerical answer. The KTU valuation key deducts 0.5 marks for missing units.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Simple Interest (SI):** $SI = PRT/100$; **linear** growth, constant interest per period.
- ✅ **Compound Interest (CI):** $A = P(1+r)^n$; **exponential** growth, interest-on-interest effect.
- ✅ **General Compounding Formula:** $A = P(1 + r/m)^{mT}$ — this is the *most general* form and subsumes all others.
- ✅ **Continuous Compounding:** $A = Pe^{rT}$ — occurs when $m \to \infty$; uses Euler's number $e \approx 2.71828$.
- ✅ **Effective Annual Rate (EAR):** $EAR = (1 + r/m)^m - 1$. For the *same* nominal rate, EAR **increases** as $m$ increases.
- ✅ **Present Value (Discounting):** $PV = FV/(1+r)^n$ — the *inverse* of compounding. Essential for NPV calculations.
- ✅ **Rule of 72:** Quick estimate for doubling time: $T_{double} \approx 72/R\%$. For $r = 9\%$, doubling occurs in $\approx 8$ years.
- ✅ **Annuity Formulas:** Use *Future Value of Annuity* for accumulated payments; *Present Value of Annuity* for loan valuations.
- ✅ **Real-world Applications:** Bank deposits, home loans (EMIs), corporate bonds, project NPV, retirement planning, insurance premiums.
- ✅ **Examiner Hot-Spots:** Doubling time problems, EAR computations, frequency comparisons, and the "interest-on-interest" gain are the **four most frequently tested** concepts in KTU Module 1.
- ✅ **Always specify units** (₹, $) in the final answer; **show all intermediate steps**; **box the final result** for clarity.

---

*End of Module 1 Notes — Simple and Compound Interest Rates*
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme | PECBT631 — Financial Management*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Amortization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Amortization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Amortization** is the systematic process of repaying a financial obligation (such as a loan or mortgage) over a predetermined period through a series of periodic payments, where each payment consists of two components — **interest** on the outstanding principal and a **principal repayment** portion that progressively reduces the debt to zero by the maturity date.

In the context of **Financial Management (PECBT631)**, amortization is a direct application of the **Time Value of Money (TVM)** principle. It is the mathematical framework that converts a present-value lump-sum liability into a stream of equivalent future periodic cash flows (typically equated monthly installments, or **EMIs**).

A second, equally important application of amortization in financial reporting refers to the **systematic allocation of the cost of an intangible asset** (such as patents, trademarks, copyrights, or goodwill) over its estimated useful economic life. Both applications are central to Module 1 of the PECBT631 syllabus.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket with a Tap" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition for Students:** Imagine a water tank (the loan principal) that you borrowed from a friend. Every month you must return some water. But your friend also charges you a "rent" on whatever water is still left in your tank (the interest). At the start, your tank is full, so the rent is high, and you return very little water. As months pass, your tank empties, rent drops, and you return more water. Eventually, the tank is completely empty, and you owe nothing. **This emptying of the tank over time is amortization.**

In a home loan of **₹25,00,000** at **9% p.a.** for **20 years**, the borrower pays a fixed EMI of approximately **₹22,492**. In the very first month, almost **₹18,750** of this goes toward interest, and only **₹3,742** reduces the principal. By the **240th (final) month**, the interest component falls to under **₹170**, and nearly the entire EMI reduces the principal. This dynamic rebalancing of the two components is the very essence of amortization.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Reference

> [!TIP]
> **Glossary of Standard KTU-Term Definitions:**
> - **Principal (P):** The original sum borrowed or the original cost of an intangible asset.
> - **Interest Rate (r):** The periodic (monthly/annual) cost of capital expressed as a decimal.
> - **Tenure (n):** The total number of periodic payments over the life of the loan.
> - **EMI (E):** Equated Monthly Installment — a fixed periodic payment.
> - **Outstanding Balance (OB):** The unrepaid principal at any point in time.
> - **Amortization Schedule:** A tabular statement showing the breakup of every EMI into interest and principal components.
> - **Effective Annual Rate (EAR):** The true annual cost when compounding is intra-year (e.g., monthly).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The amortization mechanism can be broken into four logical steps that every KTU examiner expects a student to internalize:

1. **Step 1 — Capital Recovery Requirement:** Determine the present value of the obligation. This is the loan amount $P$ that must be recovered (returned) by the lender over $n$ periods, plus the time-value cost.
2. **Step 2 — Periodic Rate Conversion:** The quoted annual rate $R$ is converted into a periodic rate $r$ using the compounding frequency. For monthly EMIs: $r = R / 12$.
3. **Step 3 — Equated Payment Calculation:** A single uniform payment $E$ is computed such that its present value equals $P$. This payment must cover both interest accrual and principal reduction.
4. **Step 4 — Iterative Reduction:** Each period, the interest on the *current* outstanding balance is calculated, the principal portion is deducted from the balance, and the process repeats until the balance becomes **zero**.

### 2.2 The Two Engineering Applications of Amortization

- **Application A — Loan Amortization:** Used in personal loans, home loans, car loans, and corporate bond repayments. The borrower repays in equal installments.
- **Application B — Asset Amortization:** Used in accounting to expense the cost of intangible assets (patents, software, licenses) over their useful life using the **straight-line method** (under Ind AS 38 / IAS 38 / AS 26).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of PECBT631 expects students to be able to (i) construct an amortization schedule, (ii) derive the EMI formula from first principles, and (iii) compute amortization of intangibles using the straight-line method.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | EMI / Equated Installment | $E = \dfrac{P \cdot r \cdot (1+r)^{n}}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$ | $P$ = Principal, $r$ = periodic rate, $n$ = number of periods | Most frequently tested formula |
| 2 | Interest Component (period $t$) | $I_{t} = OB_{t-1} \cdot r$ | $OB_{t-1}$ = previous outstanding balance | Computed first each period |
| 3 | Principal Component (period $t$) | $PR_{t} = E - I_{t}$ | $E$ = EMI, $I_t$ = interest | The amount that reduces the loan |
| 4 | Outstanding Balance after period $t$ | $OB_{t} = OB_{t-1} - PR_{t}$ | — | Should reach $\approx 0$ at period $n$ |
| 5 | Effective Annual Rate (monthly compounding) | $EAR = (1 + r)^{m} - 1$ | $m$ = 12 for monthly | Used to compare loan quotes |
| 6 | Total Amount Paid | $T = E \times n$ | — | Sum of all EMIs |
| 7 | Total Interest Paid | $I_{total} = T - P$ | — | Cost of borrowing |
| 8 | Straight-Line Asset Amortization (annual) | $A = \dfrac{C - S}{L}$ | $C$ = Cost, $S$ = Salvage, $L$ = Useful life | Used for intangible assets |
| 9 | Present Value of an Annuity (verify EMI derivation) | $P = E \cdot \left[\dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}\right]$ | — | The foundation of EMI math |
| 10 | Outstanding Balance (shortcut) | $OB_{t} = P \cdot (1+r)^{t} - E \cdot \left[\dfrac{(1+r)^{t} - 1}{r}\right]$ | — | Closed-form for any period |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Trap:** Students frequently write $r = R$ (using the annual rate directly inside a monthly formula). Always confirm the rate and the period are on the **same compounding frequency**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the EMI Formula

The EMI equation is not a memorized "magic formula" — it is the direct algebraic solution of the **present value of an ordinary annuity**. Below is the complete, line-by-line derivation that earns full marks in KTU valuation.

**Setup:** A borrower takes a loan of amount $P$ at a periodic interest rate $r$, and must repay it over $n$ equal end-of-period installments of value $E$. The fair economic condition is that the present value of all installments equals the loan amount.

**Step 1 — Write the present value of the annuity stream.**

$$
P = \dfrac{E}{(1+r)^{1}} + \dfrac{E}{(1+r)^{2}} + \dfrac{E}{(1+r)^{3}} + \dots + \dfrac{E}{(1+r)^{n}}
$$

*Logic:* Each EMI in the future is worth less today due to the time value of money. We discount each future EMI back to the present and sum them.

**Step 2 — Recognize this as a finite geometric series.**

The right-hand side is a geometric progression with first term $a = E/(1+r)$, common ratio $q = 1/(1+r)$, and $n$ terms.

**Step 3 — Apply the geometric sum formula $S_n = a \cdot \dfrac{1 - q^{n}}{1 - q}$.**

$$
P = \dfrac{E}{(1+r)} \cdot \dfrac{1 - \left[\dfrac{1}{(1+r)}\right]^{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+r)}}
$$

*Logic:* This is a standard mathematical identity for the sum of a geometric series.

**Step 4 — Simplify the denominator $1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+r)} = \dfrac{(1+r) - 1}{(1+r)} = \dfrac{r}{(1+r)}$.**

$$
P = \dfrac{E}{(1+r)} \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{\dfrac{r}{(1+r)}}
$$

*Logic:* Multiply numerator and denominator to clean up the fraction.

**Step 5 — The $(1+r)$ terms cancel.**

$$
P = E \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}
$$

*Logic:* This is the clean **Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF)** form, a foundational equation in financial mathematics.

**Step 6 — Solve algebraically for $E$ (the EMI).**

$$
E = P \cdot \dfrac{r}{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}
$$

**Step 7 — Rationalize the denominator by multiplying numerator and denominator by $(1+r)^{n}$.**

$$
E = P \cdot \dfrac{r \cdot (1+r)^{n}}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}
$$

*Logic:* Multiplying by $\dfrac{(1+r)^{n}}{(1+r)^{n}}$ removes the negative exponent in the denominator, producing the most commonly cited form of the EMI formula.

**Final EMI Equation:**

$$
\boxed{E = \dfrac{P \cdot r \cdot (1+r)^{n}}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Building a 5-Period Amortization Schedule

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A loan of **₹1,00,000** is to be repaid over **5 months** at a periodic interest rate of **1% (r = 0.01)**. Compute the EMI and construct the full amortization schedule.

**Step 1 — Compute the EMI using the derived formula.**

$$
E = \dfrac{100000 \cdot 0.01 \cdot (1.01)^{5}}{(1.01)^{5} - 1}
$$

First, evaluate $(1.01)^{5}$:

$$
(1.01)^{5} = 1.0510100501
$$

Substitute:

$$
E = \dfrac{100000 \cdot 0.01 \cdot 1.0510100501}{1.0510100501 - 1} = \dfrac{1051.01005}{0.0510100501}
$$

$$
E \approx 20,604.00 \text{ (rounded to nearest rupee)}
$$

**Step 2 — Construct the amortization schedule using the iteration rules:**

- $I_t = OB_{t-1} \cdot r$
- $PR_t = E - I_t$
- $OB_t = OB_{t-1} - PR_t$

| Period $t$ | Opening Balance $OB_{t-1}$ (₹) | Interest $I_t = OB_{t-1} \cdot 0.01$ (₹) | Principal $PR_t = E - I_t$ (₹) | Closing Balance $OB_t$ (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1,00,000.00 | 1,000.00 | 19,604.00 | 80,396.00 |
| 2 | 80,396.00 | 803.96 | 19,800.04 | 60,595.96 |
| 3 | 60,595.96 | 605.96 | 19,998.04 | 40,597.92 |
| 4 | 40,597.92 | 405.98 | 20,198.02 | 20,399.90 |
| 5 | 20,399.90 | 204.00 | 20,400.00 | 0.00 (rounded) |

**Step 3 — Verify totals.**

$$
\text{Total Paid} = 5 \times 20{,}604 = 1{,}03{,}020
$$

$$
\text{Total Interest} = 1{,}03{,}020 - 1{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}020
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observation for KTU Answer-Writing:** Notice how the interest column **decreases monotonically** while the principal column **increases monotonically**. This inverse relationship is the hallmark signature of an amortizing loan and is often asked as a 2-mark conceptual question.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Loan Amortization | Asset Amortization |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Repay a financial liability | Expense an intangible asset |
| **Driver** | Cash flow payment schedule | Accounting policy / useful life |
| **Formula** | EMI equation | $A = (C - S) / L$ |
| **Time Unit** | Monthly/quarterly EMIs | Annually (typically) |
| **Cash Movement** | Real cash outflow | Non-cash accounting entry |
| **Governed by** | Loan agreement contract | Ind AS 38 / IAS 38 / AS 26 |
| **Outcome** | Outstanding balance = 0 | Net book value = Salvage value |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of the Amortization Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Loan Principal P] --> B[Periodic Rate r = R / 12]
    B --> C[Tenure in Months n]
    A --> D[EMI Calculator Block]
    C --> D
    B --> D
    D --> E[Equated Monthly Installment E]
    E --> F[Period Loop Iterator t = 1 to n]
    F --> G[Interest It = OB t-1 x r]
    G --> H[Principal PRt = E - It]
    H --> I[Outstanding Balance OBt = OB t-1 - PRt]
    I --> J{Is OBt = 0 or t = n?}
    J -->|No| F
    J -->|Yes| K[Amortization Schedule Output]
    K --> L[Total Interest Paid]
    K --> M[Total Principal Paid]
    K --> N[Effective Annualized Cost]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of the Cash-Flow Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Time 0: Receive Principal P] -->|+|E1[EMI 1]
    E1 --> E2[EMI 2]
    E2 --> E3[EMI 3]
    E3 --> E4[EMI ...]
    E4 --> E5[EMI n]
    E5 --> Tfinal[Time n: Loan Fully Repaid]
    F1[Interest Component shrinks each period] -.-> E1
    F1 -.-> E2
    F1 -.-> E3
    F1 -.-> E4
    F1 -.-> E5
    G1[Principal Component grows each period] -.-> E1
    G1 -.-> E2
    G1 -.-> E3
    G1 -.-> E4
    G1 -.-> E5
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Two Domains of Amortization in Financial Management

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Amortization Concept in PECBT631]
    Root --> D1[Domain 1: Loan Amortization]
    Root --> D2[Domain 2: Asset Amortization]
    D1 --> D1a[Personal Loans]
    D1 --> D1b[Home Loans]
    D1 --> D1c[Corporate Bonds]
    D1 --> D1d[Car Loans]
    D2 --> D2a[Patents]
    D2 --> D2b[Trademarks]
    D2 --> D2c[Copyrights]
    D2 --> D2d[Software Licenses]
    D1a --> M1[EMI Formula + Schedule]
    D1b --> M1
    D1c --> M1
    D1d --> M1
    D2a --> M2[Straight Line Method]
    D2b --> M2
    D2c --> M2
    D2d --> M2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation Note:** The diagrams visualize the *iterative loop logic* of amortization — the engine repeatedly takes the previous outstanding balance, accrues interest, deducts a fixed EMI, and updates the balance. This same loop, when applied to intangible assets, reduces the book value to zero (or to salvage) over the useful life.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the term **amortization** as used in financial management. Differentiate between *loan amortization* and *asset amortization*.

**Model Answer (KTU Examiner's Key):**
Amortization is the systematic repayment of a financial liability (loan) or the systematic allocation of the cost of an intangible asset over its useful life through periodic equal installments or charges.

- **Loan Amortization** → Involves real cash outflows; the borrower repays principal + interest in equal EMIs until the outstanding balance becomes zero.
- **Asset Amortization** → A non-cash accounting entry; the cost of intangibles (patents, software) is expensed annually using the straight-line method to match revenues.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
In a standard amortizing loan, explain why the **interest component decreases** while the **principal component increases** with every successive EMI, even though the EMI itself remains constant.

**Model Answer (KTU Examiner's Key):**
Each EMI is fixed in total value. The interest charged in any period = **Outstanding Balance at the start of the period × periodic rate**. As each EMI is paid, the outstanding balance declines (because a portion of every EMI goes toward principal repayment). Therefore, the interest charged in the next period is computed on a smaller balance, making the interest component smaller. Since the EMI is constant, whatever is saved in interest is automatically added to the principal repayment portion, causing the principal component to grow.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern (ESE):** Each Part-B question in a 14-mark slot carries two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. You are expected to attempt **one full question** out of an internal choice.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU Model Question — Module 1, PECBT631]**

**(a)** A company takes a bank loan of **₹15,00,000** at an annual interest rate of **12% p.a.** to be repaid in **5 equal annual installments**. Using the concept of the present value of an annuity, **derive the formula** for the annual installment and **calculate the installment amount**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prepare the **complete amortization schedule** for the above loan. From the schedule, identify the year in which the principal component first exceeds the interest component and compute the **total interest paid** over the loan's lifetime. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Convert annual rate to periodic rate.**

Since payments are annual and the rate is quoted annually: $r = 12\% = 0.12$, $n = 5$, $P = 15{,}00{,}000$.

**Step 2 — Apply the EMI (annuity) formula derived in Section 3.**

$$
A = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{1 - (1+r)^{-n}} = \dfrac{15{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.12}{1 - (1.12)^{-5}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $(1.12)^{5}$.**

$$
(1.12)^{5} = 1.7623416 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1.12)^{-5} = 0.5674269
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and solve.**

$$
A = \dfrac{1{,}80{,}000}{1 - 0.5674269} = \dfrac{1{,}80{,}000}{0.4325731}
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx \text{₹}4{,}16{,}178 \text{ per year}}
$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical justification (for the 7-mark derivation part).**
The annual installment is the level payment that, when discounted at 12% per year, has a present value exactly equal to the principal. The present-value-annuity-factor (PVAF) of $3.6048$ transforms the future annuity stream back to the original principal of ₹15,00,000.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Build the schedule using $I_t = OB_{t-1} \cdot 0.12$ and $PR_t = A - I_t$.**

| Year $t$ | Opening Balance (₹) | Interest $I_t$ (₹) | Principal $PR_t$ (₹) | Closing Balance (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 15,00,000 | 1,80,000 | 2,36,178 | 12,63,822 |
| 2 | 12,63,822 | 1,51,659 | 2,64,519 | 9,99,303 |
| 3 | 9,99,303 | 1,19,916 | 2,96,262 | 7,03,041 |
| 4 | 7,03,041 | 84,365 | 3,31,813 | 3,71,228 |
| 5 | 3,71,228 | 44,547 | 3,71,631 | ≈ 0 |

**Step 2 — Identify crossover year.**
In Year 1, interest (₹1,80,000) > principal (₹2,36,178) — wait, principal is larger even in Year 1 here. Cross-check carefully:

- Year 1 → Interest = ₹1,80,000, Principal = ₹2,36,178 → **Principal already exceeds Interest from Year 1 itself** for this short, high-rate loan.

> [!TIP]
