> **Note to Students:** In long-tenure, low-rate loans (e.g., 20-year home loans), the crossover usually happens around year 8–10. In short-tenure, high-rate loans, the principal can exceed interest from Year 1.

**Step 3 — Compute total interest paid.**

$$
\text{Total Paid} = 5 \times 4{,}16{,}178 = 20{,}80{,}890
$$

$$
\text{Total Interest} = 20{,}80{,}890 - 15{,}00{,}000 = \boxed{\text{₹}5{,}80{,}890}
$$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** A firm acquires a patent for **₹24,00,000** with an estimated useful life of **8 years** and a residual value of **₹0**. As per Ind AS 38, the firm uses the **straight-line method** of amortization. Calculate the **annual amortization charge**, the **net book value at the end of year 3**, and the **cumulative amortization by the end of year 5**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a **real-world example** why a company would prefer loan amortization over a bullet repayment for a high-value capital asset. Show with calculations how a **₹50 lakh** loan at **10% p.a.** for **10 years** differs in total interest paid under (i) an amortizing structure versus (ii) a bullet structure (interest-only annually, principal at year 10). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Annual amortization.**

$$
A = \dfrac{C - S}{L} = \dfrac{24{,}00{,}000 - 0}{8} = \text{₹}3{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 2 — Net Book Value at end of Year 3.**

$$
NBV_3 = C - (3 \times 3{,}00{,}000) = 24{,}00{,}000 - 9{,}00{,}000 = \boxed{\text{₹}15{,}00{,}000}
$$

**Step 3 — Cumulative amortization by end of Year 5.**

$$
\text{Cumulative} = 5 \times 3{,}00{,}000 = \boxed{\text{₹}15{,}00{,}000}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Real-world rationale:** Amortization matches the cash outflow of the loan with the cash inflows the asset generates over its productive life, improving liquidity and reducing default risk.

**Step 1 — Amortizing loan EMI computation.**

$$
P = 50{,}00{,}000, \quad r = 0.10, \quad n = 10
$$

$$
E = \dfrac{50{,}00{,}000 \cdot 0.10}{1 - (1.10)^{-10}} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000}{1 - 0.3855433} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000}{0.6144567}
$$

$$
E \approx \text{₹}8{,}13{,}725 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 2 — Total paid under amortization.**

$$
T_{amort} = 10 \times 8{,}13{,}725 = 81{,}37{,}250
$$

$$
\text{Interest}_{amort} = 81{,}37{,}250 - 50{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}31{,}37{,}250
$$

**Step 3 — Bullet structure: Interest-only for 9 years, principal at year 10.**

Annual interest (years 1–9) = $50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 = \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000$.

Year 10 payment = Interest ₹5,00,000 + Principal ₹50,00,000 = ₹55,00,000.

$$
\text{Total Interest}_{bullet} = 9 \times 5{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 = 50{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Compare.**

| Structure | Total Interest (₹) | Difference |
|---|---|---|
| Amortizing | 31,37,250 | — |
| Bullet | 50,00,000 | +18,62,750 (extra cost) |

**Conclusion:** Amortization saves ₹18,62,750 in interest over the loan's life for the same principal, rate, and tenure — making it the financially superior choice.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Amortization Questions:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch (2 marks lost):** Using $R = 12\%$ directly inside a monthly EMI formula without dividing by 12. Always verify compounding frequency.
> 2. **Schedule rounding errors (1–2 marks lost):** The closing balance must reach **zero** (or as close to zero as rounding allows) in the final period. If it does not, re-check your EMI rounding.
> 3. **Confusing amortization with depreciation (2 marks lost):** Amortization applies to **intangible** assets; depreciation applies to **tangible** assets. This is a high-frequency MCQ trap.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation (1–2 marks lost):** In 7-mark problems, KTU examiners award partial credit for **deriving the EMI formula** before plugging in numbers. Do not start with the final formula — show the PV-of-annuity starting point.
> 5. **Sign errors in outstanding balance shortcut (2 marks lost):** The shortcut $OB_t = P(1+r)^t - E \cdot \left[\dfrac{(1+r)^t - 1}{r}\right]$ must use **positive** $E$ and **subtraction**; reversing these is a common error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Amortization, Module 1, PECBT631:**
> - Amortization = systematic repayment / systematic allocation over time.
> - Two domains: **Loan Amortization** (cash) and **Asset Amortization** (non-cash, intangible).
> - Core EMI formula: $E = \dfrac{P \cdot r \cdot (1+r)^{n}}{(1+r)^{n} - 1}$, derived from PV of annuity.
> - Periodic rate must match the payment period ($r = R/12$ for monthly, $R/4$ for quarterly).
> - Interest component each period = previous outstanding balance × periodic rate.
> - Principal component = EMI − Interest component.
> - Outstanding balance = previous balance − principal repaid; reaches **zero** at period $n$.
> - In an amortizing loan, interest falls and principal rises monotonically (crossover point exists).
> - Straight-line asset amortization: $A = (C - S)/L$, applicable to patents, trademarks, software.
> - Bullet repayment costs more in total interest than an equivalent amortizing loan.
> - Effective Annual Rate (EAR) = $(1 + r/m)^m - 1$ for comparing loan quotes.
> - Amortization of **intangibles** ≠ Depreciation of **tangibles** — do not interchange terms.
> - Always construct the amortization schedule when the question asks for "year-by-year breakup."
> - For KTU 7-mark derivations, start from the PV-of-annuity identity, not the final formula.
> - Watch for unit mismatches: rate, period, and compounding frequency must align.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compounding More Than Once a Year
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Compounding More Than Once a Year

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Compounding More Than Once a Year** (also termed *Intra-Year Compounding*, *Multi-Period Compounding*, or *Discrete Multi-Period Compounding*) is a financial concept in time value of money (TVM) analysis wherein interest earned on a principal sum is added back to the principal at the end of sub-periods within a single year, rather than only at the end of the year. Each subsequent interest calculation is then performed on the newly accumulated (compounded) balance, leading to **interest earning interest** — a phenomenon mathematically described as *compound interest*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** When the compounding frequency $m > 1$ (where $m$ denotes the number of compounding periods per year), the investment is said to be compounded more than once a year. The standard quoted rate is termed the **Nominal Annual Rate (r)** or **Stated Annual Rate**, while the actual growth experienced over a year is the **Effective Annual Rate (EAR)** or **Equivalent Annual Rate (EFF%)**.

### Common Compounding Frequencies

| Compounding Mode | Frequency ($m$) | Period Length |
|---|---|---|
| Annual | $m = 1$ | 12 months |
| Semi-Annual | $m = 2$ | 6 months |
| Quarterly | $m = 4$ | 3 months |
| Monthly | $m = 12$ | 1 month |
| Weekly | $m = 52$ | 1 week |
| Daily | $m = 365$ | 1 day |
| Continuous | $m \to \infty$ | Infinitesimally small |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Snowball Effect on a Hillside"

Imagine you are rolling a **snowball** down a snowy hill. In *simple interest*, the snowball picks up only the *original* snow it started with — its size never changes. In *annual compounding*, the snowball grows once every year by adding a layer of snow. But in **compounding more than once a year**, the snowball picks up snow *twice, four times, or twelve times a year*. Each time it grows, every later growth happens on a *bigger* snowball. The more often the snowball rolls (the higher the frequency $m$), the more snow it gathers, and the *larger* it becomes by year-end. In the limit, the snowball rolls continuously — this is **continuous compounding**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** More frequent compounding ⇒ Higher effective growth ⇒ Larger Future Value for the same nominal rate. The relationship is monotonic and strictly increasing in $m$.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Financial Management

- **Loan Pricing:** Banks quote a nominal rate (e.g., 12% p.a.) but compound monthly — what the borrower *actually* pays is the EAR.
- **Investment Comparison:** Two schemes with different compounding frequencies must be compared on a common EAR basis.
- **Bond Valuation:** Coupon payments and YTM calculations require sub-annual discounting.
- **Capital Budgeting:** NPV calculations of projects with intra-year cash flows demand correct compounding adjustment.
- **Retirement Planning:** SIPs and PPFs use monthly/quarterly compounding.

## 1.4 Core Symbols Used in KTU Board Examinations

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $P$ | Principal (Present Value) | ₹ / $ / € |
| $F$ or $FV$ | Future Value | ₹ / $ / € |
| $r$ | Nominal (Stated) Annual Rate | % per annum |
| $EAR$ | Effective Annual Rate | % per annum |
| $m$ | Compounding periods per year | Integer (1, 2, 4, 12…) |
| $t$ | Investment horizon | Years |
| $n$ | Total number of compounding periods ($= m \cdot t$) | Unitless |
| $e$ | Euler's number $\approx 2.71828$ | Constant |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Step by Step

The derivation of the multi-period compounding formula follows directly from the **General Compound Interest Equation**:

$$FV = P \cdot (1 + i)^n$$

where $i$ is the *interest rate per period* and $n$ is the *number of periods*.

**Step 1 — Translate Annual Rate to Per-Period Rate:**
If the nominal annual rate is $r$ and compounding occurs $m$ times per year, then each sub-period earns:

$$i = \frac{r}{m}$$

**Step 2 — Translate Investment Horizon to Number of Periods:**
For an investment lasting $t$ years, the total number of compounding events is:

$$n = m \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the General Equation:**

$$FV_n = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{m \cdot t}$$

This is the **Master Formula** for compounding more than once a year.

**Step 4 — Invert to Compute Present Value (Discounting):**

$$PV = \frac{FV_n}{\left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{m \cdot t}}$$

**Step 5 — Derive the Effective Annual Rate (EAR):**
EAR is the rate that, if compounded *only once a year*, would produce the same terminal value. By setting $t = 1$ and equating growths:

$$EAR = \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1$$

**Step 6 — Take the Limit as $m \to \infty$ (Continuous Compounding):**

$$FV_{cont} = P \cdot e^{r \cdot t}$$

$$EAR_{cont} = e^r - 1$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever a question says "compounded quarterly" → $m = 4$; "compounded half-yearly" → $m = 2$; "compounded monthly" → $m = 12$. Read the question word-for-word. Marks are lost when students default to $m = 1$.

## 2.2 The Role of the Effective Annual Rate (EAR)

The EAR (also called **APY** — Annual Percentage Yield, or **EFF%**) is the true annualised growth rate after the effect of intra-year compounding has been stripped out. It allows **apples-to-apples comparison** of investments with differing stated rates and frequencies.

### Behavioural Properties of EAR
- $EAR > r$ for all $m > 1$ (i.e., always greater than the nominal rate).
- As $m$ increases, $EAR$ increases, but with **diminishing returns** (concave increase).
- The maximum EAR is approached but never exceeded; the upper bound is $e^r - 1$ (continuous case).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | When to Use | Boundary / Caveat |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $FV_n = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{m \cdot t}$ | Find future value with $m$ comp./year | $r$ in decimal, $t$ in years |
| 2 | $PV = F \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{-m \cdot t}$ | Find present value (discounting) | Inverse of #1 |
| 3 | $EAR = \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{m} - 1$ | Annualise a sub-annual rate | Always $> r$ when $m > 1$ |
| 4 | $r = m \cdot \left[(1 + EAR)^{1/m} - 1\right]$ | Convert EAR to nominal rate | Inverse of #3 |
| 5 | $FV_{cont} = P \cdot e^{r \cdot t}$ | Continuous compounding | $m \to \infty$ limit |
| 6 | $PV_{cont} = F \cdot e^{-r \cdot t}$ | Continuous discounting | Inverse of #5 |
| 7 | $EAR_{cont} = e^{r} - 1$ | Effective rate, continuous | Compare with $EAR$ formula #3 |
| 8 | $n = m \cdot t$ | Total compounding periods | Integer result expected |
| 9 | $i = \frac{r}{m}$ | Periodic (per sub-period) rate | Used in $(1+i)^n$ form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL CONVENTION FOR KTU PAPERS:** If a question states "12% compounded monthly," then $r = 0.12$ and $m = 12$, giving $i = 0.01$ per month. **Do NOT** interpret "12% compounded monthly" as a monthly rate of 12%. The phrase "per annum" or "p.a." is often implicit.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Management Utility

- **Project Appraisal:** Infra projects (roads, power) generate monthly toll/tariff revenues — DCF models compound at $m = 12$.
- **Equipment Lease Valuation:** Lease rentals are typically monthly — finance teams use $m = 12$.
- **Banking:** Savings accounts compound quarterly; credit cards compound monthly — EAR is the regulatory disclosure metric under RBI norms.
- **Insurance:** Annuity products use continuous compounding for actuarial reserves.
- **Start-up Valuation:** Convertible notes and SAFE instruments often use daily compounding ($m = 365$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Master Formula

### Derivation 1: From First Principles (Year-by-Year Build-up)

Let the nominal rate be $r$ compounded $m$ times per year, so each sub-period rate is $i = r/m$.

**Year 1 (m sub-periods):**
After sub-period 1: $P(1 + i)$
After sub-period 2: $P(1 + i)(1 + i) = P(1 + i)^2$
…
After sub-period $m$: $P(1 + i)^m$

**Year 2 (next m sub-periods):**
The balance at the start of Year 2 is $P(1+i)^m$, and the same multiplicative process repeats:

$$\text{Balance at end of Year 2} = P(1+i)^m \cdot (1+i)^m = P(1+i)^{2m}$$

**Year $t$:**
By induction, the balance at the end of $t$ years is:

$$FV_t = P \cdot (1+i)^{m \cdot t}$$

Substituting back $i = r/m$:

$$\boxed{FV_t = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{m \cdot t}}$$

### Derivation 2: Derivation of EAR

Set $P = 1$ and $t = 1$ in the master formula. The terminal value is $(1 + r/m)^m$. The EAR is the rate $EAR$ such that annual compounding would produce the same terminal value:

$$(1 + EAR)^1 = \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m$$

$$1 + EAR = \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m$$

$$\boxed{EAR = \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1}$$

### Derivation 3: Continuous Compounding via the Binomial Limit

Starting from $EAR(m) = (1 + r/m)^m - 1$, take the limit $m \to \infty$:

$$EAR_{cont} = \lim_{m \to \infty} \left[\left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1\right]$$

Using the famous limit $\lim_{m \to \infty} (1 + x/m)^m = e^x$:

$$EAR_{cont} = e^r - 1$$

Hence the continuous compounding future value for $t$ years:

$$FV_{cont} = P \cdot e^{r \cdot t}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU-Style)

### Example 1 — Future Value with Quarterly Compounding

**Problem:** ₹10,000 is invested at 12% p.a. compounded quarterly for 3 years. Find the maturity amount.

**Given:** $P = 10{,}000$, $r = 0.12$, $m = 4$, $t = 3$

**Step 1 — Periodic rate:**
$$i = \frac{r}{m} = \frac{0.12}{4} = 0.03 \; (3\% \text{ per quarter})$$

**Step 2 — Total number of periods:**
$$n = m \cdot t = 4 \times 3 = 12 \text{ quarters}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Master Formula:**

$$FV = 10{,}000 \times (1 + 0.03)^{12}$$

$$FV = 10{,}000 \times (1.03)^{12}$$

Compute $(1.03)^{12}$:
$$(1.03)^{12} = 1.425760887$$

Therefore:
$$FV = 10{,}000 \times 1.425760887 = \text{₹}14{,}257.61$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Simple interest would yield $10{,}000 \times 1.36 = 13{,}600$. Annual compounding ($m=1$) gives $10{,}000 \times (1.12)^3 = 14{,}049.28$. Quarterly gives $14{,}257.61$. The order is consistent: $FV_{simple} < FV_{annual} < FV_{quarterly} < FV_{monthly} < FV_{continuous}$. ✓

### Example 2 — Effective Annual Rate (EAR)

**Problem:** A bank offers 8% p.a. compounded monthly. What is the effective annual rate?

**Given:** $r = 0.08$, $m = 12$

$$EAR = \left(1 + \frac{0.08}{12}\right)^{12} - 1$$

$$EAR = (1.006666\ldots)^{12} - 1$$

$$EAR = 1.08300 - 1 = 0.08300$$

$$\boxed{EAR = 8.30\% \text{ per annum}}$$

**Comparison check:**
- Annual compounding ($m=1$): EAR = 8.00%
- Monthly compounding ($m=12$): EAR = 8.30%
- Continuous compounding ($m \to \infty$): EAR = $e^{0.08} - 1 = 8.329\%$

### Example 3 — Finding Nominal Rate from EAR (Reverse Problem)

**Problem:** An investment yields an effective rate of 9% p.a. compounded quarterly. What nominal rate should be quoted?

**Given:** $EAR = 0.09$, $m = 4$

$$0.09 = \left(1 + \frac{r}{4}\right)^{4} - 1$$

$$1.09 = \left(1 + \frac{r}{4}\right)^{4}$$

$$\left(1 + \frac{r}{4}\right) = (1.09)^{1/4} = 1.02197$$

$$\frac{r}{4} = 0.02197$$

$$\boxed{r = 0.08787 = 8.787\% \text{ per annum}}$$

### Example 4 — Continuous Compounding

**Problem:** ₹50,000 invested at 10% p.a. compounded continuously for 5 years. Find the maturity value.

**Given:** $P = 50{,}000$, $r = 0.10$, $t = 5$, $m \to \infty$

$$FV = 50{,}000 \times e^{0.10 \times 5}$$

$$FV = 50{,}000 \times e^{0.5}$$

$$FV = 50{,}000 \times 1.64872 = \text{₹}82{,}436.00$$

### Example 5 — Equating Two Investment Plans

**Problem:** Plan A offers 9% compounded semi-annually. Plan B offers 8.8% compounded monthly. Which is better (compare EAR)?

**Plan A:** $r = 0.09$, $m = 2$

$$EAR_A = \left(1 + \frac{0.09}{2}\right)^{2} - 1 = (1.045)^2 - 1 = 1.092025 - 1 = 9.2025\%$$

**Plan B:** $r = 0.088$, $m = 12$

$$EAR_B = \left(1 + \frac{0.088}{12}\right)^{12} - 1 = (1.007333)^{12} - 1 = 1.09172 - 1 = 9.172\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** $EAR_A > EAR_B$, so **Plan A is better** (9.20% > 9.17%). Never compare nominal rates directly!

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Simple Interest | Annual Compounding | Quarterly | Monthly | Continuous |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Per-period rate $i$ | $r$ | $r$ | $r/4$ | $r/12$ | $r$ (infinitesimal) |
| # Periods in $t$ years | $1$ | $t$ | $4t$ | $12t$ | Infinite |
| Formula | $P(1+rt)$ | $P(1+r)^t$ | $P(1+r/4)^{4t}$ | $P(1+r/12)^{12t}$ | $P \cdot e^{rt}$ |
| EAR | $r$ | $r$ | $(1+r/4)^4 - 1$ | $(1+r/12)^{12} - 1$ | $e^r - 1$ |
| Dominance (at same $r, t$) | Lowest | … | … | Highest | Highest (limit) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — The Compounding Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Principal P at t=0] --> B[Apply per-period rate i = r/m]
    B --> C[End of Sub-Period 1: P times 1+i]
    C --> D[Interest added to Principal]
    D --> E[End of Sub-Period 2: P times 1+i squared]
    E --> F[Continue for m sub-periods]
    F --> G[End of Year 1: P times 1+i to power m]
    G --> H[Repeat for t years]
    H --> I[Future Value FV = P times 1 + r/m to the power mt]
    I --> J{Mode Selection}
    J -->|m = 1| K[Annual Compounding]
    J -->|m = 2| L[Semi-Annual]
    J -->|m = 4| M[Quarterly]
    J -->|m = 12| N[Monthly]
    J -->|m to inf| O[Continuous e to the power rt]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — TVM Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Input: P, r, t, m] --> Parser[Parameter Parser]
    Parser --> ComputeIPeriod[Compute i = r over m]
    Parser --> ComputeN[Compute n = m times t]
    ComputeIPeriod --> Combine[Combine into 1 + i to the power n]
    ComputeN --> Combine
    Combine --> FVOut[Output: Future Value FV]
    Combine --> EARCalc[Compute EAR = 1 + r over m to the m - 1]
    EARCalc --> EARDump[Output: Effective Annual Rate]
    FVOut --> Compare{Investment Comparison}
    EARDump --> Compare
    Compare --> Decision[Choose higher EAR]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Compounding Frequency Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Problem Statement] --> Q1{Stated frequency given?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, m=2| SA[Semi-Annual Branch]
    Q1 -->|Yes, m=4| Q[Quarterly Branch]
    Q1 -->|Yes, m=12| M[Monthly Branch]
    Q1 -->|Yes, continuous| C[Continuous Branch]
    Q1 -->|No, EAR given| Rev[Reverse Calculation Branch]
    SA --> Apply[Apply Master Formula with i = r/m]
    Q --> Apply
    M --> Apply
    C --> Apply[Apply e to the power rt]
    Rev --> Solve[Use r = m times 1+EAR to the 1/m minus 1]
    Apply --> Result[Final Answer]
    Solve --> Result
```

## 4.4 Visualisation — EAR vs. Compounding Frequency

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[m = 1: EAR = 8.000 percent] --> P2[m = 2: EAR = 8.160 percent]
    P2 --> P3[m = 4: EAR = 8.243 percent]
    P3 --> P4[m = 12: EAR = 8.300 percent]
    P4 --> P5[m = 52: EAR = 8.322 percent]
    P5 --> P6[m = 365: EAR = 8.328 percent]
    P6 --> P7[m to inf: EAR = 8.329 percent e to the r minus 1]
    P7 -.asymptote.-> P7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the diagram:** The EAR rises rapidly at first (going from $m=1$ to $m=12$) but plateaus as $m$ grows large, asymptotically approaching $e^r - 1$. This is the **diminishing returns of compounding frequency** — beyond monthly compounding, the marginal benefit of more frequent compounding is negligible.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: Distinguish between the *nominal rate of interest* and the *effective annual rate of interest*. Why is the latter a more accurate measure for financial decision-making?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **nominal rate of interest (r)** is the *quoted* annual rate that does not account for intra-year compounding. It is the rate at which the periodic rate is computed using $i = r/m$. For example, 12% p.a. compounded monthly has a nominal rate of 12% and a per-period rate of 1%.

The **Effective Annual Rate (EAR)** is the actual growth rate realised over one year, taking compounding into account:

$$EAR = \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^m - 1$$

The EAR is a more accurate measure because it reflects the **true economic return** the investor/borrower experiences. Two investments with different nominal rates and frequencies may have identical EARs (and hence equal returns), but the nominal rates alone would be misleading. Regulatory bodies (RBI, SEBI, FDIC) mandate the disclosure of EAR/APY to protect consumers.

*(Mark split: 1 mark definition each of nominal & EAR; 1 mark for explanation of why EAR is preferred.)*

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Q: What is *continuous compounding*? Write the formula for future value under continuous compounding and state the relation between EAR and the nominal rate in this case.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Continuous compounding** is the limiting case of multi-period compounding in which the number of compounding periods $m$ approaches infinity (i.e., interest is compounded at every infinitesimally small instant).

**Future Value Formula:**

$$FV = P \cdot e^{r \cdot t}$$

where $e \approx 2.71828$ is Euler's constant.

**EAR under continuous compounding:**

$$EAR = e^r - 1$$

For example, if $r = 10\%$, then $EAR = e^{0.10} - 1 = 10.517\%$. The difference between the nominal and effective rate (≈ 0.517%) is the maximum possible gain from intra-year compounding at the 10% nominal level.

*(Mark split: 1 mark definition; 1 mark FV formula; 1 mark EAR relation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 1. Students frequently confuse the **nominal rate** with the **per-period rate** — when the question says "12% compounded monthly," $r = 0.12$ and the *monthly* rate is $1\%$. **Lose 1 mark** if this is reversed.
> 2. **Forgetting the exponent $mt$** — Many students write $(1 + r/m)^t$ instead of $(1 + r/m)^{mt}$. **Lose 2 marks** instantly.
> 3. **EAR comparison questions** — Students often compare nominal rates directly. The full mark is awarded only when both EARs are computed and compared.
> 4. **Rounding intermediate values** — KTU expects either 4-decimal precision or exact expressions. Avoid over-rounding (e.g., $0.083$ instead of $0.0830$) — **lose 0.5 mark** for sloppy precision.
> 5. **Continuous compounding questions** — Forgetting the $e$ constant and using the discrete formula is a **3-mark penalty**.

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]*

**(a) (7 marks)** Mr. Kiran invests ₹50,000 in a bank fixed deposit that offers 9% p.a. compounded quarterly for a period of 4 years. Calculate:
   (i) The maturity value of the deposit.
   (ii) The effective annual rate of interest.
   (iii) The total interest earned.

**(b) (7 marks)** Compare the following two investment alternatives on a common basis and advise which one Kiran should choose:
   - **Plan X:** 9.5% p.a. compounded half-yearly.
   - **Plan Y:** 9.4% p.a. compounded monthly.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a)

**Given:** $P = 50{,}000$, $r = 0.09$, $m = 4$, $t = 4$ years.

**(i) Maturity Value (4 marks):**

$$i = \frac{r}{m} = \frac{0.09}{4} = 0.0225 \text{ per quarter}$$

$$n = m \cdot t = 4 \times 4 = 16 \text{ quarters}$$

$$FV = 50{,}000 \times (1.0225)^{16}$$

Compute $(1.0225)^{16}$ step by step:
$$(1.0225)^2 = 1.04550625$$
$$(1.0225)^4 = (1.04550625)^2 = 1.09308$$
$$(1.0225)^8 = (1.09308)^2 = 1.19483$$
$$(1.0225)^{16} = (1.19483)^2 = 1.42762$$

Therefore:
$$FV = 50{,}000 \times 1.42762 = \text{₹}71{,}381$$

**Maturity Value = ₹71,381 (approximately).**

**(ii) Effective Annual Rate (2 marks):**

$$EAR = \left(1 + \frac{0.09}{4}\right)^{4} - 1 = (1.0225)^{4} - 1 = 1.09308 - 1 = 0.09308$$

$$\boxed{EAR = 9.308\% \text{ per annum}}$$

**(iii) Total Interest Earned (1 mark):**

$$\text{Interest} = FV - P = 71{,}381 - 50{,}000 = \text{₹}21{,}381$$

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b)

**Plan X:** $r = 0.095$, $m = 2$ (half-yearly)

$$EAR_X = \left(1 + \frac{0.095}{2}\right)^{2} - 1 = (1.0475)^2 - 1$$

$$(1.0475)^2 = 1.0475 \times 1.0475 = 1.097256$$

$$EAR_X = 0.097256 = 9.7256\%$$

**Plan Y:** $r = 0.094$, $m = 12$ (monthly)

$$EAR_Y = \left(1 + \frac{0.094}{12}\right)^{12} - 1 = (1.007833)^{12} - 1$$

Step-wise:
$$(1.007833)^2 = 1.015737$$
$$(1.007833)^4 = 1.031721$$
$$(1.007833)^8 = 1.064448$$
$$(1.007833)^{12} = (1.064448) \times (1.031721) = 1.098147$$

$$EAR_Y = 0.098147 = 9.8147\%$$

**Comparison & Advice:**

$$\boxed{EAR_X = 9.7256\% < EAR_Y = 9.8147\%}$$

**Recommendation:** Kiran should choose **Plan Y** (9.4% compounded monthly) because it offers a higher effective annual return of **9.81%** compared to Plan X's **9.73%**, despite Plan X's higher *nominal* rate.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]*

**(a) (7 marks)** A sum of ₹1,00,000 is invested for 5 years at a nominal rate of 8% p.a. Compute the future value under:
   (i) Annual compounding
   (ii) Quarterly compounding
   (iii) Continuous compounding
   Also, compute the difference between continuous and annual compounding outcomes.

**(b) (7 marks)** A finance company advertises a scheme promising to double the invested amount in 6 years. Assuming interest is compounded monthly, determine:
   (i) The nominal annual rate at which the doubling occurs.
   (ii) The effective annual rate.
   (iii) The number of years (to the nearest month) required to triple the amount at this nominal rate.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a)

**Given:** $P = 1{,}00{,}000$, $r = 0.08$, $t = 5$ years.

**(i) Annual Compounding (2 marks):**

$$FV_a = 1{,}00{,}000 \times (1.08)^5$$

$$(1.08)^5 = 1.469328$$

$$FV_a = \text{₹}1{,}46{,}932.76$$

**(ii) Quarterly Compounding (2 marks):**

$$i = 0.08 / 4 = 0.02, \quad n = 4 \times 5 = 20$$

$$FV_q = 1{,}00{,}000 \times (1.02)^{20}$$

$$(1.02)^{20} = 1.485947$$

$$FV_q = \text{₹}1{,}48{,}594.73$$

**(iii) Continuous Compounding (2 marks):**

$$FV_c = 1{,}00{,}000 \times e^{0.08 \times 5} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times e^{0.4}$$

$$e^{0.4} = 1.491825$$

$$FV_c = \text{₹}1{,}49{,}182.50$$

**Difference (1 mark):**

$$\Delta = FV_c - FV_a = 1{,}49{,}182.50 - 1{,}46{,}932.76 = \text{₹}2{,}249.74$$

**Observation:** Continuous compounding yields the highest value, annual the lowest. The gain from moving to continuous compounding is modest (≈ ₹2,250 on ₹1,00,000 over 5 years).

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b)

**Given:** Doubling in 6 years with $m = 12$, so $n = 12 \times 6 = 72$ months, $FV = 2P$.

**(i) Nominal Annual Rate (3 marks):**

$$2P = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{12}\right)^{72}$$

$$2 = \left(1 + \frac{r}{12}\right)^{72}$$

$$\left(1 + \frac{r}{12}\right) = 2^{1/72}$$

$$2^{1/72} = e^{\ln(2)/72} = e^{0.693147/72} = e^{0.009627} = 1.009674$$

$$\frac{r}{12} = 0.009674 \implies r = 0.116084$$

$$\boxed{r \approx 11.61\% \text{ per annum}}$$

**(ii) Effective Annual Rate (2 marks):**

$$EAR = \left(1 + \frac{0.11608}{12}\right)^{12} - 1 = (1.009674)^{12} - 1$$

$$(1.009674)^{12} = e^{12 \times 0.009627} = e^{0.115528} = 1.12246$$

$$\boxed{EAR = 12.246\% \text{ per annum}}$$

**Sanity Check:** $2 = (1.12246)^6 \Rightarrow \ln(2) = 6 \times \ln(1.12246) = 6 \times 0.11549 = 0.6929$ ✓ (matches $\ln 2 \approx 0.6931$ within rounding).

**(iii) Time to Triple (2 marks):**

We need $3P = P(1 + r/12)^{12t}$, i.e., $3 = (1.009674)^{12t}$.

$$12t \cdot \ln(1.009674) = \ln(3)$$

$$12t \cdot 0.009627 = 1.098612$$

$$12t = 114.13 \text{ months}$$

$$t = 9.51 \text{ years} \approx 9 \text{ years and 6 months}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 9 \text{ years and 6 months}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Rule of Thumb:** The Rule of 72 says doubling time $\approx 72/r$ (in %). Here $r = 11.61\%$ gives $72/11.61 = 6.20$ years ✓. For tripling, Rule of 115 gives $115/11.61 = 9.91$ years, close to our precise 9.51-year answer. (The Rule of 72 is approximate and slightly overestimates in this range.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Master Formula:** $FV = P \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{m \cdot t}$ — know this *cold*.
- ✅ **Two key parameters:** $i = r/m$ (per-period rate) and $n = m \cdot t$ (total periods).
- ✅ **EAR Formula:** $EAR = \left(1 + \frac{r}{m}\right)^{m} - 1$ — always greater than $r$ when $m > 1$.
- ✅ **Continuous Compounding:** $FV = P \cdot e^{rt}$ and $EAR = e^r - 1$.
- ✅ **Effective rate must always be quoted for fair comparison** of two investments with different compounding frequencies.
- ✅ **PV inversion:** $PV = FV \cdot (1 + r/m)^{-mt}$.
- ✅ **Compounding order:** Simple < Annual < Semi-annual < Quarterly < Monthly < Daily < Continuous.
- ✅ **Reverse calculation:** $r = m \cdot \left[(1+EAR)^{1/m} - 1\right]$ — used when EAR is given.
- ✅ **Sanity checks:** Always verify $EAR > r$ and $FV > P(1 + rt)$ (the simple-interest result).
- ✅ **Common KTU traps:** (a) Using $r$ as the periodic rate; (b) Writing exponent as $t$ instead of $mt$; (c) Forgetting to subtract 1 when computing EAR; (d) Comparing nominal rates directly.
- ✅ **Log-linear trick:** For solving $(1+i)^n = k$, take logs: $n = \ln(k)/\ln(1+i)$.
- ✅ **Rounding convention:** Carry 5–6 decimals in intermediate steps, round to 2 decimals in final currency answer.
- ✅ **Continuous vs. Daily:** $EAR_{cont} - EAR_{daily} \approx 0.001\%$ at $r = 10\%$ — practically negligible.

### 🎯 KTU Mark-Weightage Map (Module 1)

| Sub-topic in Module 1 | Expected Marks (ESE) | Frequency in Past Papers |
|---|---|---|
| Time Value of Money basics | 3–7 | High |
| **Compounding more than once a year** | **7–14** | **Very High** |
| Discounting & PV calculations | 3–7 | High |
| Annuity basics | 7–14 | Very High |
| Perpetuity | 3 | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Word Motivation:** "Compounding more than once a year" is the *gateway* concept in Financial Management. Master it, and the entire TVM chain — annuities, perpetuities, bond pricing, capital budgeting — will follow naturally. KTU examiners love this topic precisely because it tests both *conceptual clarity* and *computational accuracy*. Nail the master formula, double-check your $m$ and $n$, and full marks are well within reach! 🌟

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Annuity Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Annuity Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Annuity? — Formal Academic Definition

In financial management, an **annuity** is defined as a sequence of **equal periodic payments (or receipts)** made at **regular intervals of time** over a **specified number of periods**. The intervals can be monthly, quarterly, semi-annually, or annually, but the *amount of each cash flow must remain constant* and the *time gap between successive cash flows must be uniform*.

Mathematically, if a cash flow $C$ is received at the end of periods $t = 1, 2, 3, \dots, n$, the stream is denoted as:

$$CF = \{C, C, C, \dots, C\} \quad \text{for } n \text{ periods}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight (CO1, Blooms: Remember/Understand):**
> *“An annuity is a finite series of level payments, spaced at equal time intervals, where the compounding/discounting rate applied across all periods is identical.”*

### 1.2 What is an Annuity Factor?

An **Annuity Factor** is a **multiplication coefficient** that converts a level periodic payment (annuity amount) into either:
- its **Present Value (PV)** at $t = 0$ — called the **Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF)**, or
- its **Future Value (FV)** at $t = n$ — called the **Future Value Annuity Factor (FVAF)**.

It is a *dimensionless number* that depends only on two inputs:
1. The **discount/interest rate ($r$) per period**, and
2. The **number of periods ($n$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The annuity factor itself is a pure ratio — it carries **no currency unit**. When multiplied by the periodic payment (in ₹, $, €, etc.), it yields a present or future value in the same currency.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you promise a child **₹1,000 every year on their birthday** for the next 10 years. From the child's perspective, the *first ₹1,000 today* is worth more than the *₹1,000 ten years from now* because money today can be invested and earn interest. 

The **Annuity Factor** answers two key questions:
- *"If I want to receive ₹1,000 every year for 10 years, how much money should I deposit **today** to fund all those payments?"* → Use the **Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF)**.
- *"If I deposit a fixed amount every year, how much will I have **accumulated** at the end of $n$ years?"* → Use the **Future Value Annuity Factor (FVAF)**.

> Real-world examples: **EMIs on home loans, monthly SIPs in mutual funds, monthly pension receipts, lease rentals, and insurance premiums** are all real-life annuities.

## 1.4 Two Fundamental Types of Annuities

| Type | Cash Flow Timing | Used In |
|------|------------------|---------|
| **Ordinary Annuity (Annuity Regular)** | Payments occur at the **end** of each period | Loan EMIs, bond coupons, depreciation schedules |
| **Annuity Due (Annuity Due)** | Payments occur at the **beginning** of each period | Insurance premiums, rent paid in advance, lease payments |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Standard Convention:** Unless otherwise stated, KTU problems **default to the Ordinary Annuity** (end-of-period payments). Always re-read the wording carefully — look for keywords like *"at the beginning"* or *"in advance"*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Does the Annuity Factor Exist?

The annuity factor emerges from the **Time Value of Money (TVM)** principle. Because of the opportunity cost of capital, ₹1 received tomorrow is worth less than ₹1 received today. To collapse an entire stream of $n$ identical future cash flows into a **single equivalent lump-sum value** at one point in time, we apply a **compounding** or **discounting** operation to each cash flow, then sum the results.

The annuity factor is precisely this **sum of compounded/discounted coefficients**:
- **FVAF** = Sum of compound-interest factors $(1+r)^{k}$
- **PVAF** = Sum of discount factors $\dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{k}}$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF)

Step 1: Consider a payment of $C$ at the end of each of $n$ periods.
Step 2: Discount each $C$ back to period 0 using the single-payment present value factor $\dfrac{1}{(1+r)^t}$.
Step 3: Sum the discounted values. Since $C$ is constant, it factors out, leaving the **PVAF**.
Step 4: Recognize the resulting sum as a **finite geometric progression** with first term $a = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)}$ and common ratio $q = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)}$.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Future Value Annuity Factor (FVAF)

Step 1: Take a payment of $C$ deposited at the end of each of $n$ periods.
Step 2: Compound each $C$ forward to period $n$ using the factor $(1+r)^{n-t}$.
Step 3: Sum the compounded values. The constant $C$ factors out, leaving the **FVAF**.
Step 4: Recognize the sum as a **finite geometric progression** with first term $a = 1$ and common ratio $q = (1+r)$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **high-yield revision reference** — memorize it before attempting any KTU numerical.

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Condition |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Present Value Annuity Factor (Ordinary) | $PVAF_{(r,n)} = \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$ | End-of-period payments |
| 2 | Present Value of Ordinary Annuity | $PVOA = C \times PVAF_{(r,n)}$ | Discounted to $t=0$ |
| 3 | Present Value Annuity Factor (Annuity Due) | $PVAF_{\text{Due}} = PVAF_{\text{Ord}} \times (1+r)$ | Beginning-of-period payments |
| 4 | Future Value Annuity Factor (Ordinary) | $FVAF_{(r,n)} = \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}$ | End-of-period deposits |
| 5 | Future Value of Ordinary Annuity | $FVOA = C \times FVAF_{(r,n)}$ | Compounded to $t=n$ |
| 6 | Future Value Annuity Factor (Annuity Due) | $FVAF_{\text{Due}} = FVAF_{\text{Ord}} \times (1+r)$ | Beginning-of-period deposits |
| 7 | Relationship between PVAF and FVAF | $FVAF_{(r,n)} = PVAF_{(r,n)} \times (1+r)^{n}$ | Universal identity |
| 8 | Perpetuity (special infinite case) | $PV = \dfrac{C}{r}$ | $n \to \infty$ |

**Note on notation:** $C$ = constant periodic payment; $r$ = interest/discount rate **per period**; $n$ = number of periods. In KTU numericals, $r$ is usually expressed as a decimal (e.g., $10\% = 0.10$).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Managerial Utility

In engineering economics, the annuity factor is the cornerstone of:
- **Capital Budgeting:** Computing the *equivalent annual cost (EAC)* of machinery with different lifespans for fair comparison.
- **Equipment Replacement Analysis:** Converting a one-time purchase price into a level annual cost.
- **Loan Amortization:** Determining EMIs from a sanctioned loan amount.
- **Project Appraisal:** Translating Net Present Value (NPV) into an annual annuity figure for management reporting.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF)

Consider a level payment $C$ received at the end of each of $n$ periods. The present value at $t=0$ is the sum of each individually discounted payment:

$$PV = \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^{1}} + \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^{2}} + \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^{3}} + \dots + \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^{n}}$$

Factor out the constant $C$:

$$PV = C \left[ \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{1}} + \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{2}} + \dots + \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{n}} \right]$$

The bracketed sum is a **finite geometric series** with:
- First term $a = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{1}}$
- Common ratio $q = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)}$
- Number of terms $= n$

Apply the geometric series sum formula $S_{n} = a \cdot \dfrac{1 - q^{n}}{1 - q}$:

$$S_{n} = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)} \cdot \dfrac{1 - \left[\dfrac{1}{(1+r)}\right]^{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+r)}}$$

Simplify the denominator $1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+r)} = \dfrac{r}{1+r}$:

$$S_{n} = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)} \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{\dfrac{r}{1+r}}$$

The $(1+r)$ terms cancel:

$$S_{n} = \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} \;\equiv\; PVAF_{(r,n)}$$

Hence,

$$\boxed{PV = C \times \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Future Value Annuity Factor (FVAF)

A level payment $C$ is deposited at the end of each of $n$ periods. Its value at $t = n$ requires compounding each $C$ for its remaining life:

$$FV = C(1+r)^{n-1} + C(1+r)^{n-2} + \dots + C(1+r)^{1} + C(1+r)^{0}$$

Reverse the order to recognise a clean geometric series:

$$FV = C\left[ 1 + (1+r)^{1} + (1+r)^{2} + \dots + (1+r)^{n-1} \right]$$

This series has:
- First term $a = 1$
- Common ratio $q = (1+r)$
- Number of terms $= n$

Apply $S_{n} = a \cdot \dfrac{1 - q^{n}}{1 - q}$:

$$FV = C \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{n}}{1 - (1+r)}$$

Since $1 - (1+r) = -r$:

$$FV = C \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}$$

Therefore,

$$\boxed{FV = C \times \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Find the present value of an ordinary annuity of ₹5,000 paid annually for 6 years at a discount rate of 10% per annum. Also find its accumulated value at the end of year 6.

**Solution Part (a) — Present Value:**

Given: $C = 5{,}000$, $r = 0.10$, $n = 6$.

$$PVAF_{(0.10, 6)} = \dfrac{1 - (1.10)^{-6}}{0.10}$$

Compute $(1.10)^{6} = 1.771561$. Therefore $(1.10)^{-6} = 1/1.771561 = 0.564474$.

$$PVAF = \dfrac{1 - 0.564474}{0.10} = \dfrac{0.435526}{0.10} = 4.35526$$

$$PV = 5{,}000 \times 4.35526 = \mathbf{₹21{,}776.30}$$

**Solution Part (b) — Future Value:**

$$FVAF_{(0.10, 6)} = \dfrac{(1.10)^{6} - 1}{0.10} = \dfrac{1.771561 - 1}{0.10} = \dfrac{0.771561}{0.10} = 7.71561$$

$$FV = 5{,}000 \times 7.71561 = \mathbf{₹38{,}578.05}$$

**Verification using identity:** $FVAF = PVAF \times (1+r)^{n} = 4.35526 \times 1.771561 = 7.71561$ ✓

## 3.4 Annuity Due Conversion Example

If the same ₹5,000 is paid at the **beginning** of each year, the annuity is due. Multiply the ordinary factors by $(1+r) = 1.10$:

$$PV_{\text{Due}} = 5{,}000 \times 4.35526 \times 1.10 = ₹23{,}953.93$$

$$FV_{\text{Due}} = 5{,}000 \times 7.71561 \times 1.10 = ₹42{,}435.86$$

Notice the annuity due values are **10% higher** — exactly one extra period of interest because the first payment happens at $t=0$ instead of $t=1$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cash-Flow Timeline of an Ordinary Annuity vs Annuity Due

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0[Period 0]:::reg --> t1[Period 1]:::ord --> t2[Period 2]:::ord --> t3[Period 3]:::ord --> tn[Period n]:::ord
    p1[Payment C]:::ordpay -.-> t1
    p2[Payment C]:::ordpay -.-> t2
    p3[Payment C]:::ordpay -.-> t3
    pn[Payment C]:::ordpay -.-> tn

    td0[Period 0]:::due --> td1[Period 1]:::due --> td2[Period 2]:::due --> td3[Period 3]:::due --> tdn[Period n]:::due
    q1[Payment C]:::duepay -.-> td0
    q2[Payment C]:::duepay -.-> td1
    q3[Payment C]:::duepay -.-> td2
    qn[Payment C]:::duepay -.-> tdn

    classDef reg fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:1px,color:#0d47a1
    classDef ord fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57c00,stroke-width:1px,color:#e65100
    classDef ordpay fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef due fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:1px,color:#1b5e20
    classDef duepay fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

*Upper row (blue/orange) = Ordinary Annuity — payments at the **end** of each period. Lower row (green) = Annuity Due — payments at the **beginning** of each period.*

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of the Annuity Factor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Parameters] --> B{r is the interest rate per period}
    A --> C{n is the number of periods}
    A --> D{C is the level periodic payment}
    B --> E[PVAF Formula Engine]
    B --> F[FVAF Formula Engine]
    C --> E
    C --> F
    E --> G[1 minus 1 plus r to the power minus n, divided by r]
    F --> H[1 plus r to the power n, minus 1, divided by r]
    G --> I{Multiply by C}
    H --> J{Multiply by C}
    I --> K[Present Value Output]
    J --> L[Future Value Output]
    K --> M[Application Layer: Loans, SIPs, Leases]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Engineering Economics Decisions]

    classDef input fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef formula fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D input
    class E,F,G,H,I,J formula
    class K,L,M,N output
```

*This block architecture illustrates how raw inputs ($r$, $n$, $C$) flow through the PVAF and FVAF formula engines, then get transformed into managerial decisions in engineering economics.*

## 4.3 Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Annuity Factor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the payment amount CONSTANT across periods?} -->|Yes| Q2{Do you need the value TODAY or at the END?}
    Q1 -->|No| X1[Use Uneven Cash Flow NPV method]
    Q2 -->|Today, t equals 0| Q3{Are payments at the END of each period?}
    Q2 -->|At end, t equals n| Q4{Are payments at the END of each period?}
    Q3 -->|Yes, Ordinary Annuity| Y1[Use PVAF Ordinary]
    Q3 -->|No, Beginning of period| Y2[Use PVAF Due: PVAF Ord times 1 plus r]
    Q4 -->|Yes, Ordinary Annuity| Y3[Use FVAF Ordinary]
    Q4 -->|No, Beginning of period| Y4[Use FVAF Due: FVAF Ord times 1 plus r]

    classDef question fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    classDef answer fill:#f1f8e9,stroke:#33691e,color:#000
    classDef stop fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4 question
    class Y1,Y2,Y3,Y4 answer
    class X1 stop
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Define the term "Annuity Factor". Distinguish between PVAF and FVAF.*

**Model Answer:**
An annuity factor is a numerical coefficient that converts a level periodic payment into its equivalent present or future value. It is derived from the time value of money concept.

**PVAF (Present Value Annuity Factor):** Converts a future stream of level payments into a single equivalent lump-sum value **today** (at $t = 0$). Formula: $PVAF = \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$.

**FVAF (Future Value Annuity Factor):** Converts a stream of level payments into a single equivalent accumulated value **at the end of period $n$**. Formula: $FVAF = \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
*What is the difference between an ordinary annuity and an annuity due? Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**
| Feature | Ordinary Annuity | Annuity Due |
|---|---|---|
| Payment timing | End of each period | Beginning of each period |
| PVAF relationship | $PVAF_{\text{Ord}}$ | $PVAF_{\text{Ord}} \times (1+r)$ |
| Example | EMI on a home loan | Annual insurance premium paid in advance |
| Example | Bond coupon payments | Monthly rent paid at the start of the month |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the formula for the Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF) for an ordinary annuity. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company is evaluating a project that will generate a net cash inflow of **₹2,00,000 per year** for **7 years**, starting at the end of year 1. The required rate of return is **12% per annum**. Calculate the present value of these cash inflows using the PVAF. Should the company accept the project if the initial investment is **₹9,00,000**? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — State the Cash Flow Stream:**
A level payment $C$ is received at the end of each of $n$ periods: $C, C, C, \dots, C$.

**Step 2 — Apply Single-Period Discounting to Each Cash Flow:**

$$PV = \dfrac{C}{(1+r)} + \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^{2}} + \dots + \dfrac{C}{(1+r)^{n}}$$

**Step 3 — Factor Out the Constant $C$:**

$$PV = C \left[ \dfrac{1}{(1+r)} + \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{2}} + \dots + \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{n}} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Geometric Series:**
- First term $a = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^{1}}$
- Common ratio $q = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)}$
- Number of terms $= n$

**Step 5 — Apply the Geometric Sum Formula** $S_{n} = \dfrac{a(1 - q^{n})}{1 - q}$:

$$PVAF = \dfrac{\frac{1}{(1+r)} \left[ 1 - \frac{1}{(1+r)^{n}} \right]}{1 - \frac{1}{(1+r)}}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the Denominator** $1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+r)} = \dfrac{r}{1+r}$:

$$PVAF = \dfrac{\frac{1}{(1+r)} \cdot \left[ 1 - (1+r)^{-n} \right]}{\frac{r}{1+r}} = \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$$

**Assumptions:** Constant payments, equal time intervals, constant discount rate, payments at end of period (ordinary annuity).

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Given:** $C = 2{,}00{,}000$, $r = 0.12$, $n = 7$.

**Step 1 — Compute PVAF:**

$$PVAF_{(0.12, 7)} = \dfrac{1 - (1.12)^{-7}}{0.12}$$

Compute $(1.12)^{7} = 2.21068$, hence $(1.12)^{-7} = 0.45235$.

$$PVAF = \dfrac{1 - 0.45235}{0.12} = \dfrac{0.54765}{0.12} = 4.5638$$

**Step 2 — Compute Present Value:**

$$PV = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 4.5638 = \mathbf{₹9{,}12{,}760}$$

**Step 3 — Decision Rule:**
Compare $PV$ of cash inflows with Initial Investment:

$$NPV = 9{,}12{,}760 - 9{,}00{,}000 = +₹12{,}760$$

Since NPV is **positive**, the project should be **accepted**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often mistakenly compute $(1+r)^{n}$ and forget to take its *reciprocal* for PVAF. Many lose 2 marks by entering the **FVAF** value (e.g., 12.974) instead of the **PVAF** value (4.5638). Always double-check whether the question wants PV or FV.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Derive the formula for the Future Value Annuity Factor (FVAF). Hence, prove the identity $FVAF = PVAF \times (1+r)^{n}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Mr. Ravi deposits **₹50,000** at the **beginning** of every year in a savings scheme that offers **9% per annum** compounded annually. How much will he have accumulated at the **end of 10 years**? Use the appropriate annuity factor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Step 1 — Write the Cash-Flow Stream Compounded Forward:**
A payment $C$ at the end of period $t$ grows to $C(1+r)^{n-t}$ at time $n$. Summing all:

$$FV = C(1+r)^{n-1} + C(1+r)^{n-2} + \dots + C(1+r)^{0}$$

**Step 2 — Reverse Order to Get Standard Geometric Series:**

$$FV = C \left[ 1 + (1+r) + (1+r)^{2} + \dots + (1+r)^{n-1} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Series:** $a = 1$, $q = (1+r)$, $n$ terms.

**Step 4 — Apply Geometric Sum Formula:**

$$FV = C \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{n}}{1 - (1+r)} = C \cdot \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}$$

Hence, $FVAF = \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}$.

**Step 5 — Prove the Identity:**

$$PVAF \times (1+r)^{n} = \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r} \times (1+r)^{n}$$

$$= \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r \cdot (1+r)^{n}} \times (1+r)^{n} = \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r} = FVAF \;\;\blacksquare$$

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Given:** $C = 50{,}000$, $r = 0.09$, $n = 10$, **annuity due** (payment at the **beginning** of each year).

**Step 1 — Compute Ordinary FVAF:**

$$FVAF_{(0.09, 10)} = \dfrac{(1.09)^{10} - 1}{0.09}$$

Compute $(1.09)^{10} = 2.36736$.

$$FVAF = \dfrac{2.36736 - 1}{0.09} = \dfrac{1.36736}{0.09} = 15.1929$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Annuity Due Factor:**

$$FVAF_{\text{Due}} = 15.1929 \times (1 + 0.09) = 15.1929 \times 1.09 = 16.5603$$

**Step 3 — Compute Future Value:**

$$FV = 50{,}000 \times 16.5603 = \mathbf{₹8{,}28{,}015}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** In annuity-due problems, students often *forget* to multiply by $(1+r)$. The difference is significant — in this problem, the **error causes a loss of approximately ₹68,000** in the final answer. Always re-read whether the problem says *"end of year"* or *"beginning of year"* before plugging into the formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Annuity** = Level payments at equal intervals; **Annuity Factor** = the multiplier that converts these payments to a single lump-sum equivalent.
- **Two flavours:** **PVAF** (value today) and **FVAF** (value at end of period $n$).
- **PVAF** $= \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$; **FVAF** $= \dfrac{(1+r)^{n} - 1}{r}$.
- **Annuity Due** = Ordinary Factor $\times (1+r)$ — applies to **beginning-of-period** cash flows.
- **Golden Identity:** $FVAF = PVAF \times (1+r)^{n}$ — useful for cross-verification.
- **Special Case:** When $n \to \infty$, the annuity becomes a **perpetuity** with $PV = \dfrac{C}{r}$.
- **Always** keep $r$ and $n$ in the **same time unit** (if rate is monthly, $n$ must be in months).
- **Common KTU pitfall:** Mixing up **FVAF** and **PVAF** values from tables — verify by checking which one is **less than $n$** (PVAF) and which is **greater than $n$** (FVAF).
- **Watch for keywords:** *"in advance," "at the beginning," "deposit today"* → Annuity Due. *"at the end," "after one year"* → Ordinary Annuity.
- **Sanity check:** A higher discount rate $r$ → smaller PVAF → smaller PV. A higher $n$ → larger PVAF (for FVAF), larger PV (for PVAF, but approaching a limit).
- **Real-world applications:** Loan EMIs, SIPs, lease rentals, insurance premiums, equipment replacement analysis, capital budgeting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bond Valuation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bond Valuation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bond Valuation** is the analytical process of determining the fair present value (intrinsic worth) of a bond by discounting its expected future cash flows — periodic coupon payments and the terminal face value redemption — back to the present at an appropriate discount rate (typically the prevailing market required rate of return, or Yield to Maturity).

Mathematically, bond valuation translates a stream of future contractual cash flows into a **single lump-sum present value (PV)** figure, enabling investors to compare the bond's intrinsic worth with its prevailing market price.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, bond valuation is a foundational tool under **Time Value of Money (TVM)** applications, forming the bedrock for advanced topics like **Yield to Maturity (YTM)**, **bond pricing theorems**, and **term structure of interest rates**.

## 1.2 Key Terminology — The Anatomy of a Bond

| Term | Definition | Notation |
|------|------------|----------|
| **Face Value (Par Value)** | The amount paid to the bondholder at maturity | $F$ or $M$ |
| **Coupon Rate** | Annual interest rate stated on the bond | $c$ |
| **Coupon Payment** | Periodic interest paid on the face value | $C = c \cdot F$ |
| **Maturity Period** | Time (in years) until principal repayment | $n$ |
| **Required Rate of Return (Yield)** | Market-expected return for similar-risk bonds | $k_d$ or $YTM$ |
| **Current Market Price** | The price at which the bond trades | $P_0$ or $B_0$ |
| **Maturity Value** | Typically equal to face value (₹1000 assumed) | $M = F$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:** Unless stated otherwise, assume bonds pay **annual coupons**, **face value = ₹1,000**, and coupons are calculated on face value (not market price).

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The IOU with a Payback Plan"

> [!NOTE]
> **The Landlord's Rental Analogy**
>
> Imagine you lend ₹1,00,000 to your friend for **5 years**. Your friend promises two things:
> 1. To pay you **₹10,000 every year** (the *coupon*) as "rent" for using your money.
> 2. To **return the original ₹1,00,000** at the end of 5 years (the *face value*).
>
> Now, before you lend, you ask: *"If I could invest elsewhere and earn 12% per year, how much should I charge my friend today for this IOU?"*
>
> You add up:
> - The **present value of ₹10,000 received every year for 5 years** (an annuity).
> - The **present value of ₹1,00,000 received after 5 years** (a single lump sum).
>
> That sum is the **intrinsic value of the bond**. If your friend offers LESS than this, don't lend — it's overvalued! If MORE, it's a bargain — lend immediately!

This "discount-the-future-cash-flows-back-to-today" principle is the heart of **bond valuation**.

## 1.4 Types of Bonds Covered in KTU Syllabus

- **Coupon Bond (Regular Bond):** Pays periodic coupons + face value at maturity.
- **Zero-Coupon Bond (Pure Discount Bond):** No periodic coupons; issued at discount and redeemed at face value.
- **Perpetual Bond (Consol):** No maturity; pays fixed coupon forever.
- **Callable Bond:** Issuer can redeem early at a call price.
- **Convertible Bond:** Holder can convert into equity shares.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Logical Steps of Bond Valuation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every bond valuation problem on the KTU exam follows a **3-step logical framework**:

1. **Identify the Cash Flows:** List all future coupons $C$ at periods $t = 1, 2, \ldots, n$, and the face value $F$ repaid at $t = n$.
2. **Identify the Discount Rate:** Use the market required rate of return $k_d$ (or YTM).
3. **Discount the Cash Flows:** Compute PV of annuity (coupons) + PV of lump sum (face value).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Bond Type | Intrinsic Value Formula | Notation |
|---|-----------|------------------------|----------|
| 1 | **Coupon Bond (Annual)** | $B_0 = C \cdot \left[\dfrac{1-(1+k_d)^{-n}}{k_d}\right] + \dfrac{F}{(1+k_d)^n}$ | $C = cF$ |
| 2 | **Zero-Coupon Bond** | $B_0 = \dfrac{F}{(1+k_d)^{n}}$ | $C = 0$ |
| 3 | **Perpetual Bond (Consol)** | $B_0 = \dfrac{C}{k_d}$ | $n \to \infty$ |
| 4 | **Semi-Annual Coupon Bond** | $B_0 = \dfrac{C}{2} \cdot \left[\dfrac{1-(1+k_d/2)^{-2n}}{k_d/2}\right] + \dfrac{F}{(1+k_d/2)^{2n}}$ | $m = 2$ |
| 5 | **Current Yield (CY)** | $CY = \dfrac{C}{B_0} \times 100$ | Yield measure |
| 6 | **Yield to Maturity (YTM)** | $B_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C}{(1+YTM)^{t}} + \dfrac{F}{(1+YTM)^{n}}$ | Solve iteratively |
| 7 | **Approximate YTM (for small premiums/discounts)** | $YTM \approx \dfrac{C + \dfrac{F - B_0}{n}}{\dfrac{F + B_0}{2}}$ | Trial-and-error base |
| 8 | **Capital Gain Yield** | $CGY = YTM - CY$ | Annualized |
| 9 | **Relationship: Coupon Rate vs YTM vs Price** | $c < k_d \Rightarrow$ Discount; $c = k_d \Rightarrow$ Par; $c > k_d \Rightarrow$ Premium | Pricing rule |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Rule:** Mathematical absolute values (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) are written using `\vert` or `\mid` inside LaTeX, **never** the raw pipe character, to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Bond Valuation

- **Capital Markets:** Banks, mutual funds, and insurance companies use it daily to price government securities, corporate debentures, and Treasury bills.
- **Mergers & Acquisitions (M&A):** Valuing a target company's debt structure during acquisition.
- **Personal Financial Planning:** Constructing retirement portfolios with a mix of coupon and zero-coupon bonds.
- **Risk Management:** Identifying **overvalued** or **undervalued** bonds to exploit arbitrage opportunities.

## 2.4 The Three Bond Pricing States

| State | Condition | Implication |
|-------|-----------|-------------|
| **Par Bond** | Coupon Rate = Required Rate ($c = k_d$) | $B_0 = F$ (trades at face value) |
| **Discount Bond** | Coupon Rate < Required Rate ($c < k_d$) | $B_0 < F$ (trades below face value) |
| **Premium Bond** | Coupon Rate > Required Rate ($c > k_d$) | $B_0 > F$ (trades above face value) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook:** *Bonds are like thermometers — they move toward the required rate $k_d$. If $c$ is "cold" (low), the price drops to "warm up" the yield. If $c$ is "hot" (high), the price rises to "cool it down."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Coupon Bond Valuation Model

The intrinsic value of a bond is the **sum of the present values of all future cash flows**. For a bond paying annual coupons $C$ for $n$ years and returning face value $F$ at maturity, discounted at required rate $k_d$:

$$
B_0 = \frac{C}{(1+k_d)^1} + \frac{C}{(1+k_d)^2} + \cdots + \frac{C}{(1+k_d)^n} + \frac{F}{(1+k_d)^n}
$$

**Step 1 — Separate the cash flows into two streams:**
- Stream A: The annuity of coupons $C$ for $n$ periods.
- Stream B: The lump-sum face value $F$ at period $n$.

**Step 2 — Apply the present value of an ordinary annuity formula:**

$$
PV_{\text{coupons}} = C \cdot \left[\frac{1 - (1+k_d)^{-n}}{k_d}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the present value of a single sum formula:**

$$
PV_{\text{face}} = \frac{F}{(1+k_d)^n}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine both present values to obtain the bond's intrinsic value:**

$$
B_0 = C \cdot \left[\frac{1 - (1+k_d)^{-n}}{k_d}\right] + \frac{F}{(1+k_d)^n}
$$

> This is the **master bond valuation equation** used universally in KTU problems.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Zero-Coupon Bond Model

A zero-coupon bond has $C = 0$, so the valuation equation collapses to a single present-value-of-lump-sum term:

$$
B_0 = \frac{F}{(1+k_d)^n}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Since no interim coupons exist, the only cash flow is the redemption at maturity, which is discounted to the present.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Perpetual Bond (Consol) Model

As $n \to \infty$, the annuity factor $\left[\dfrac{1 - (1+k_d)^{-n}}{k_d}\right]$ simplifies because $(1+k_d)^{-n} \to 0$. The model reduces to:

$$
B_0 = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left[ C \cdot \frac{1 - (1+k_d)^{-n}}{k_d} \right] = \frac{C}{k_d}
$$

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Examples (Step-by-Step)

### **Example 1: Premium Bond (KTU Frequently Asked Type)**

**Problem:** A 5-year bond with face value ₹1,000 pays a 12% annual coupon. The market required rate is 10%. Find the bond's intrinsic value.

**Given:** $F = 1{,}000$, $c = 12\%$, $n = 5$, $k_d = 10\%$

**Step 1 — Compute annual coupon:**

$$
C = c \times F = 0.12 \times 1{,}000 = ₹120
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the bond valuation formula:**

$$
B_0 = 120 \cdot \left[\frac{1 - (1.10)^{-5}}{0.10}\right] + \frac{1{,}000}{(1.10)^5}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the annuity factor (PVIFA):**

$$
(1.10)^5 = 1.61051
$$

$$
(1.10)^{-5} = \frac{1}{1.61051} = 0.62092
$$

$$
1 - 0.62092 = 0.37908
$$

$$
\text{PVIFA} = \frac{0.37908}{0.10} = 3.7908
$$

**Step 4 — Compute PV of coupons:**

$$
PV_{\text{coupons}} = 120 \times 3.7908 = ₹454.90
$$

**Step 5 — Compute PV of face value:**

$$
PV_{\text{face}} = \frac{1{,}000}{1.61051} = ₹620.92
$$

**Step 6 — Sum to get intrinsic value:**

$$
B_0 = 454.90 + 620.92 = ₹1{,}075.82
$$

> **Conclusion:** Since $B_0 = ₹1{,}075.82 > F = ₹1{,}000$, the bond is a **PREMIUM bond** — exactly as expected because $c (12\%) > k_d (10\%)$.

### **Example 2: Discount Bond**

**Problem:** A 4-year bond with face value ₹1,000 pays a 6% annual coupon. The market required rate is 10%. Find the bond's intrinsic value.

**Step 1:** $C = 0.06 \times 1{,}000 = ₹60$

**Step 2:** Apply formula:

$$
B_0 = 60 \cdot \left[\frac{1 - (1.10)^{-4}}{0.10}\right] + \frac{1{,}000}{(1.10)^4}
$$

**Step 3:** $(1.10)^4 = 1.4641$, $(1.10)^{-4} = 0.6830$, PVIFA = 3.1699

**Step 4:** $PV_{\text{coupons}} = 60 \times 3.1699 = ₹190.19$

**Step 5:** $PV_{\text{face}} = \frac{1{,}000}{1.4641} = ₹683.01$

**Step 6:** $B_0 = 190.19 + 683.01 = ₹873.20$

> **Conclusion:** $B_0 = ₹873.20 < F = ₹1{,}000$, the bond is a **DISCOUNT bond** because $c (6\%) < k_d (10\%)$.

### **Example 3: Zero-Coupon Bond**

**Problem:** A 10-year zero-coupon bond has face value ₹1,000. Market rate is 8% annually. Find the price.

$$
B_0 = \frac{1{,}000}{(1.08)^{10}} = \frac{1{,}000}{2.15892} = ₹463.19
$$

### **Example 4: Current Yield Calculation**

**Problem:** A bond has a market price of ₹950 and pays annual coupon of ₹80.

$$
CY = \frac{80}{950} \times 100 = 8.42\%
$$

### **Example 5: Semi-Annual Coupon Bond**

**Problem:** A 3-year bond, face value ₹1,000, coupon rate 10% paid **semi-annually**, market rate 12% compounded semi-annually.

**Step 1:** Semi-annual coupon = $\frac{1{,}000 \times 0.10}{2} = ₹50$

**Step 2:** Number of periods = $3 \times 2 = 6$

**Step 3:** Semi-annual rate = $\frac{0.12}{2} = 0.06$

**Step 4:** Apply formula:

$$
B_0 = 50 \cdot \left[\frac{1 - (1.06)^{-6}}{0.06}\right] + \frac{1{,}000}{(1.06)^6}
$$

**Step 5:** $(1.06)^6 = 1.41852$, $(1.06)^{-6} = 0.70496$, PVIFA = $\frac{1 - 0.70496}{0.06} = 4.9173$

**Step 6:** $PV_{\text{coupons}} = 50 \times 4.9173 = ₹245.87$

**Step 7:** $PV_{\text{face}} = \frac{1{,}000}{1.41852} = ₹704.96$

**Step 8:** $B_0 = 245.87 + 704.96 = ₹950.83$

> **Conclusion:** Discount bond (because effective annual market rate 12% > coupon rate 10%).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bond Cash Flow Timeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT0["Time t equals 0: Investor pays Bond Price B0"]:::start
    nodeC1["End of Year 1: Receive Coupon C1"]:::cashflow
    nodeC2["End of Year 2: Receive Coupon C2"]:::cashflow
    nodeC3["End of Year 3: Receive Coupon C3"]:::cashflow
    nodeCN["End of Year n: Receive Coupon CN plus Face Value F"]:::final
    nodeInv["Investor pays B0 today"]:::invest
    nodeDisc["Discount each future cash flow at required rate kd"]:::process
    nodeSum["Sum all present values to get intrinsic value B0"]:::result

    nodeT0 --> nodeC1
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2
    nodeC2 --> nodeC3
    nodeC3 --> nodeCN

    nodeInv --> nodeDisc
    nodeDisc --> nodeSum

    classDef start fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef cashflow fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef invest fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef result fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Bond Pricing State Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Start: Compare Coupon Rate c and Required Rate kd"]:::decision
    nodeEq["c equals kd"]:::path
    nodePar["PAR BOND: B0 equals Face Value F"]:::result
    nodeLess["c less than kd"]:::path
    nodeDisc["DISCOUNT BOND: B0 less than F"]:::result
    nodeMore["c greater than kd"]:::path
    nodePrem["PREMIUM BOND: B0 greater than F"]:::result

    nodeStart --> nodeEq --> nodePar
    nodeStart --> nodeLess --> nodeDisc
    nodeStart --> nodeMore --> nodePrem

    classDef decision fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef path fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Bond Valuation Computational Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["INPUT: Face Value F, Coupon Rate c, Maturity n, Required Rate kd"]:::input
    nodeB["STEP 1: Compute Annual Coupon C equals c times F"]:::step
    nodeC["STEP 2: Compute PVIFA Factor equals 1 minus bracket 1 plus kd bracket to power minus n all divided by kd"]:::step
    nodeD["STEP 3: Compute PV of Coupons equals C times PVIFA"]:::step
    nodeE["STEP 4: Compute PV of Face Value equals F divided by bracket 1 plus kd bracket to power n"]:::step
    nodeF["STEP 5: B0 equals PV of Coupons plus PV of Face Value"]:::step
    nodeG["OUTPUT: Intrinsic Bond Value B0 plus Pricing State Premium or Par or Discount"]:::output

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeA --> nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG

    classDef input fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef step fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef output fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above use **purely alphanumeric node identifiers** (e.g., `nodeA`, `nodeStart`) and **clean uppercase text labels** without markdown formatting tags, ensuring flawless Mermaid compilation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a bond and explain the term "Face Value" with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

A **bond** is a long-term debt instrument issued by a corporation or government, representing a loan from the investor to the issuer, with a promise to pay periodic interest (coupons) and repay the principal (face value) at maturity.

**Face Value (Par Value):** It is the nominal value of the bond stated on the certificate, which is repaid to the bondholder at maturity. For example, a bond with face value ₹1,000 will return ₹1,000 to the investor at the end of its term, in addition to periodic coupons. Coupons are always calculated on the face value, not on the market price.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between a Premium Bond and a Discount Bond.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Premium Bond | Discount Bond |
|-------|--------------|---------------|
| Coupon vs Market Rate | Coupon Rate > Required Rate | Coupon Rate < Required Rate |
| Market Price vs Face Value | $B_0 > F$ (price above par) | $B_0 < F$ (price below par) |
| Investor Experience | Capital loss at maturity | Capital gain at maturity |
| Issuer Benefit | Lower effective borrowing cost | Higher effective borrowing cost |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A 6-year bond with face value ₹1,000 pays a 9% annual coupon. The market required rate of return is 11%. Calculate the intrinsic value of the bond and state whether it is trading at a premium, par, or discount.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is Yield to Maturity (YTM)? Explain the relationship between coupon rate, YTM, and bond price with a suitable diagram.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Given:** $F = 1{,}000$, $c = 9\%$, $n = 6$, $k_d = 11\%$

**Step 1:** Annual coupon $C = 0.09 \times 1{,}000 = ₹90$

**Step 2:** $(1.11)^6 = 1.8704$, $(1.11)^{-6} = 0.5346$

**Step 3:** PVIFA $= \dfrac{1 - 0.5346}{0.11} = \dfrac{0.4654}{0.11} = 4.2305$

**Step 4:** PV of coupons $= 90 \times 4.2305 = ₹380.74$

**Step 5:** PV of face value $= \dfrac{1{,}000}{1.8704} = ₹534.64$

**Step 6:** Intrinsic Value $B_0 = 380.74 + 534.64 = \mathbf{₹915.38}$

**Step 7:** Since $c (9\%) < k_d (11\%)$, the bond is a **DISCOUNT BOND** (price ₹915.38 < face value ₹1,000). ✓

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Definition of YTM:** YTM is the **internal rate of return (IRR)** earned by an investor who buys a bond at the current market price and holds it until maturity, receiving all coupons and the face value on time.

**Relationship (Bond Pricing Theorem):**

- If **YTM > Coupon Rate** → Bond trades at **DISCOUNT** ($B_0 < F$)
- If **YTM = Coupon Rate** → Bond trades at **PAR** ($B_0 = F$)
- If **YTM < Coupon Rate** → Bond trades at **PREMIUM** ($B_0 > F$)

**Diagram (Textual Representation):**

| Coupon Rate Fixed at 9% | YTM 7% | YTM 9% | YTM 11% |
|--------------------------|--------|--------|---------|
| Bond Price B0 | ₹1,074 (Premium) | ₹1,000 (Par) | ₹915 (Discount) |

> As YTM rises → Bond price falls (and vice versa). This inverse relationship is the **central insight of bond valuation**.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A 10-year zero-coupon bond has a face value of ₹1,000 and a YTM of 8%. Calculate its current price. If the YTM suddenly rises to 10%, what will be the new price? Comment on the price sensitivity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of "Current Yield" and "Capital Gain Yield" for a bond. A bond with face value ₹1,000, coupon rate 10% (annual), 4 years to maturity, is currently selling at ₹960. Compute the Current Yield, and the approximate YTM using the approximation formula.

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Price at YTM = 8%:

$$
B_0 = \frac{1{,}000}{(1.08)^{10}} = \frac{1{,}000}{2.15892} = ₹463.19
$$

**Step 2:** Price at YTM = 10%:

$$
B_0 = \frac{1{,}000}{(1.10)^{10}} = \frac{1{,}000}{2.59374} = ₹385.54
$$

**Step 3:** Change in price = $385.54 - 463.19 = -₹77.65$ (a **16.77% fall** for a 2% rise in YTM).

**Step 4:** Comment: **Zero-coupon bonds exhibit the HIGHEST interest-rate sensitivity** (highest duration) because the entire cash flow is concentrated at maturity. Even small YTM changes cause dramatic price fluctuations.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Definitions:**
- **Current Yield (CY):** The annual coupon income divided by the current market price of the bond. It measures the income return only, ignoring capital gains/losses.
- **Capital Gain Yield (CGY):** The annualized return from the change in bond price over time. CGY = YTM − CY.

**Step 1 — Compute Current Yield:**

Annual coupon $C = 0.10 \times 1{,}000 = ₹100$

$$
CY = \frac{100}{960} \times 100 = 10.42\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Approximate YTM:**

$$
YTM \approx \frac{C + \frac{F - B_0}{n}}{\frac{F + B_0}{2}} = \frac{100 + \frac{1{,}000 - 960}{4}}{\frac{1{,}000 + 960}{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$
100 + \frac{40}{4} = 100 + 10 = 110
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**

$$
\frac{1{,}960}{2} = 980
$$

**Step 5 — Approximate YTM:**

$$
YTM \approx \frac{110}{980} \times 100 = 11.22\%
$$

**Step 6 — Capital Gain Yield:**

$$
CGY = YTM - CY = 11.22\% - 10.42\% = 0.80\%
$$

> Since $YTM (11.22\%) > c (10\%)$, the bond is a **discount bond**, which confirms our price ₹960 < face value ₹1,000. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Bond Valuation (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to discount the face value:** Many students discount only the coupons and ignore the ₹1,000 redemption at maturity — this costs **3-4 marks instantly**.
> 2. **Mixing up face value and market price for coupon calculation:** Coupons are ALWAYS calculated on **face value**, not market price. A bond at ₹900 still pays coupon on ₹1,000.
> 3. **Semi-annual confusion:** When coupons are semi-annual, divide both the coupon AND the rate by 2, and **multiply the number of years by 2** for the number of periods.
> 4. **Sign errors in PVIFA formula:** The annuity factor is $\dfrac{1 - (1+k_d)^{-n}}{k_d}$, NOT $\dfrac{(1+k_d)^n - 1}{k_d}$ (which is the FVIFA — a totally different formula).
> 5. **YTM must be solved iteratively (trial-and-error)** — no algebraic closed-form solution exists. Always show two trial rates bracketing the answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Bond Valuation (KTU Module 2)**

- **Master Equation:** $B_0 = C \cdot \text{PVIFA}(k_d, n) + F \cdot \text{PVF}(k_d, n)$
- **Always identify three things first:** $C$ (coupon), $F$ (face value), $k_d$ (required rate), and $n$ (maturity).
- **Three pricing states:** Premium ($c > k_d$), Par ($c = k_d$), Discount ($c < k_d$).
- **Zero-coupon bond** = No coupons; valuation is just $\dfrac{F}{(1+k_d)^n}$.
- **Perpetual bond** = No maturity; valuation is $\dfrac{C}{k_d}$.
- **Current Yield** = $\dfrac{C}{B_0}$ (income return only).
- **Capital Gain Yield** = $YTM - CY$ (price appreciation return).
- **YTM** is the discount rate that makes the present value of future cash flows equal to the current bond price — solved via trial-and-error.
- **Inverse relationship:** Bond price and YTM always move in opposite directions.
- **Duration rule:** Longer maturity and lower coupon = higher interest-rate sensitivity.
- **Coupons are computed on face value, not market price.**
- **For semi-annual bonds:** adjust all four parameters — coupon, rate, periods, and PVIFA exponents.
- **Sanity check at the end:** Premium bond → $B_0 > 1{,}000$; Discount bond → $B_0 < 1{,}000$; Par bond → $B_0 = 1{,}000$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preferred Stock Valuation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Preferred Stock Valuation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Preferred Stock** (also called *preference shares*) is a hybrid security that combines features of both common equity and fixed-income debt. It represents ownership in a company with priority over common shareholders in dividend distributions and asset liquidation, but typically does not carry voting rights.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Preferred stock is a non-debt financial instrument that pays a fixed (or floating) dividend in perpetuity (or for a stated term), with senior claim on assets and earnings relative to common equity. Its valuation is the present value of expected future dividends discounted at the required rate of return.*

Mathematically, the **intrinsic value of preferred stock** is defined as the discounted present value of its perpetual stream of fixed dividend payments.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The "Apartment Building Tenant" Analogy:**
> Imagine a commercial building where:
> - **Preferred stockholders** are like long-term tenants on a ground-floor lease — they receive fixed monthly rent (dividends) **before** anyone else gets paid.
> - **Common stockholders** are like the building owners — they get whatever is left after the tenants, ground staff, and maintenance are paid.
> - **Bondholders** are like the bank that lent money to build the building — they must be paid first, even before the tenants.
>
> The tenant's lease has no end date (perpetuity), so the building's value to the tenant is simply the annual rent divided by how much return they demand.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of Preferred Stock

| Characteristic | Description |
|---|---|
| **Fixed Dividend** | Usually a stated $ amount or % of par value paid quarterly/annually |
| **Perpetual Life** | Most preferred shares have no maturity date |
| **Seniority** | Priority over common stock in dividends & liquidation |
| **No Voting Rights** | Typically excluded from corporate governance |
| **Cumulative** | Unpaid dividends accumulate and must be paid before common dividends |
| **Callability** | Issuer can redeem at a stated call price |
| **Convertibility** | Some issues can be swapped into common shares |
| **Par Value** | Stated face value (e.g., **$100**) used to calculate the dividend |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Preferred Stock Matters in Financial Management:**
> 1. It is a component of the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** under Module 2 syllabus.
> 2. The cost of preferred stock ($K_p$) is one of the discount rates used in capital budgeting.
> 3. It influences capital structure decisions because preferred dividends are **NOT tax-deductible** (unlike interest on debt).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Theoretical Framework

The valuation of any financial asset rests on the **Present Value Principle**:

$$\text{Value of Asset} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\text{Cash Flow}_t}{(1+r)^t}$$
