For preferred stock, the cash flows are the **dividend payments** ($D_p$). Because most preferred shares are **perpetual** (no maturity), the cash flow stream is infinite.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Perpetuity Logic:**
> A perpetuity is an annuity that continues forever. If you invest ₹1,000 and earn ₹50 per year in perpetuity, your effective return is **5%** — and that relationship is the foundation of preferred stock valuation.

### 2.2 Assumptions of the Basic Valuation Model

1. Dividends are **constant** ($D_1 = D_2 = \ldots = D_n$).
2. The stock has **no maturity date** (perpetual life).
3. The firm is a **going concern** (will not liquidate).
4. The required rate of return ($K_p$) is **constant** over time.
5. Dividends are paid at the **end of each period** (ordinary perpetuity).

### 2.3 Core Valuation Formulas — KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Preferred Stock Valuation)**
> Memorize these equations verbatim. They are tested almost every cycle.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | $P_p = \dfrac{D_p}{K_p}$ | **Basic Perpetual Preferred Stock Value** | $P_p$ = Price; $D_p$ = Annual Dividend; $K_p$ = Required Return |
| **F2** | $K_p = \dfrac{D_p}{P_p}$ | **Cost of Preferred Stock (basic)** | Used in WACC |
| **F3** | $K_p = \dfrac{D_p}{N_p}$ where $N_p = P_p - F$ | **Cost with Flotation Cost (F)** | $N_p$ = Net Proceeds; $F$ = Flotation Cost per share |
| **F4** | $N_p = \dfrac{D_p}{K_p}$ | **Net Proceeds Valuation with Flotation** | Solve backwards for issue price |
| **F5** | $P_p = \dfrac{D_p}{K_p - g}$ | **Growing Perpetuity (if $D$ grows at $g$)** | For participating/adjustable preferred |
| **F6** | $\text{Yield} = \dfrac{D_p}{P_0}$ | **Current Yield** | Same as $K_p$ for perpetuity |
| **F7** | $D_p = \text{Par} \times d\%$ | **Dividend from Par Value** | $d$ = stated dividend rate |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL: No vertical bars in tables** — KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rule. Note how we use `\dfrac` and `\vert` substitutes for absolute value where needed.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering/Finance Application

- **Corporate Finance:** Preferred stock cost feeds into WACC; an inaccurate $K_p$ corrupts every NPV decision in the firm.
- **Investment Banking:** Underwriters use the perpetuity model to price new preferred share issues in the primary market.
- **Portfolio Management:** Income-seeking investors (pension funds, retirees) value preferred shares as a yield instrument comparable to bonds.
- **Valuation Consulting:** Analysts strip out preferred stock when computing **Equity Value** in DCF models (subtract preferred from firm value).

### 2.5 Boundary Conditions and Edge Cases

| Scenario | Modification |
|---|---|
| **Cumulative unpaid dividends** | Add accumulated arrears: $P_p = \dfrac{D_p}{K_p} + \sum \text{Arrears} \cdot (1+K_p)^{-t}$ |
| **Callable Preferred** | Use the **lower** of (a) call price model or (b) perpetuity value |
| **Convertible Preferred** | Value as **MAX** of straight preferred value or conversion value |
| **With Flotation Cost** | Use $N_p = P_p - F$ in denominator of cost formula |
| **Tax Shield Missing** | Unlike debt, preferred dividends are paid from **after-tax** income — no tax shield |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Perpetual Preferred Stock Price ($P_p = D_p / K_p$)

We begin with the general discounted cash flow (DCF) formula and let $n \to \infty$.

**Step 1:** Start with the present value of an ordinary perpetuity.

$$PV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{D_p}{(1+K_p)^t}$$

**Step 2:** Factor out the constant $D_p$ (since preferred dividends are fixed).

$$PV = D_p \cdot \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(1+K_p)^t}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the geometric series inside the summation.

Let $x = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$. Then the series becomes:

$$S_n = x + x^2 + x^3 + \ldots + x^n$$

**Step 4:** Apply the closed-form geometric series formula.

$$S_n = \frac{x(1 - x^n)}{1 - x}$$

**Step 5:** Take the limit as $n \to \infty$ (perpetuity assumption).

Since $\vert x \vert < 1$ for any positive $K_p$, we have $x^n \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} S_n = \frac{x}{1 - x}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back $x = \dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$.

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} S_n = \frac{\frac{1}{1+K_p}}{1 - \frac{1}{1+K_p}} = \frac{\frac{1}{1+K_p}}{\frac{K_p}{1+K_p}}$$

**Step 7:** Simplify the fraction (the $1+K_p$ denominators cancel).

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} S_n = \frac{1}{K_p}$$

**Step 8:** Multiply by the constant $D_p$ to obtain the final price formula.

$$\boxed{P_p = \frac{D_p}{K_p}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The price of a preferred share is simply its annual dividend capitalized (divided) by the investor's required rate of return. This is mathematically equivalent to a perpetuity bond.

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Cost of Preferred Stock with Flotation Costs

When a firm issues new preferred stock, the underwriter charges a **flotation cost** ($F$) per share. The firm does not receive the full market price — it receives **net proceeds** $N_p$.

**Step 1:** Definition of net proceeds.

$$N_p = P_p - F$$

**Step 2:** The cost of capital must reflect the actual cash the firm receives, not the market price.

$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{N_p} = \frac{D_p}{P_p - F}$$

**Step 3:** To solve for the **issue price** $P_p$ required to achieve a target $K_p$:

$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{P_p - F} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P_p - F = \frac{D_p}{K_p}$$

**Step 4:** Isolate $P_p$.

$$\boxed{P_p = \frac{D_p}{K_p} + F}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Flotation Costs Matter:** A firm planning a **₹100 par, 12% preferred share** with ₹5 flotation cost and target $K_p = 15\%$ must price the issue at $P_p = (12/0.15) + 5 = ₹85$ to break even. Anything below that is wealth-destroying.

### 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Growing Perpetuity (Adjustable-Rate Preferred)

Some preferred shares have dividends that grow at a constant rate $g$ (e.g., REITs, floating-rate preferreds).

**Step 1:** Assume dividend grows: $D_t = D_0 (1+g)^t$ or equivalently $D_1 = D_0(1+g)$.

**Step 2:** DCF formula with growth.

$$P_p = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} \frac{D_0 (1+g)^t}{(1+K_p)^t} = \sum_{t=1}^{\infty} D_0 \left( \frac{1+g}{1+K_p} \right)^t$$

**Step 3:** Apply the perpetuity sum formula with $x = \dfrac{1+g}{1+K_p}$.

$$P_p = D_0 \cdot \frac{x}{1 - x} = D_0 \cdot \frac{\frac{1+g}{1+K_p}}{1 - \frac{1+g}{1+K_p}}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify.

$$P_p = D_0 \cdot \frac{1+g}{K_p - g}$$

**Step 5:** Since $D_1 = D_0(1+g)$, we substitute.

$$\boxed{P_p = \frac{D_1}{K_p - g}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Validity Condition:** This formula requires $K_p > g$. If $g \geq K_p$, the denominator is zero or negative, and the model breaks down (dividend growth cannot outpace the required return indefinitely).

### 3.4 ANALYTICAL MATRIX — Comparative Summary

| Model | Cash Flow Pattern | Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Perpetual (Constant)** | $D, D, D, D, \ldots$ | $P_p = \dfrac{D_p}{K_p}$ | Standard fixed-dividend preferred |
| **With Flotation** | Net proceeds adjusted | $K_p = \dfrac{D_p}{P_p - F}$ | New issue pricing |
| **Growing Perpetuity** | $D, D(1{+}g), D(1{+}g)^2, \ldots$ | $P_p = \dfrac{D_1}{K_p - g}$ | REITs, adjustable-rate preferred |
| **Callable** | Compare call vs perpetuity | Lower of $(P_{\text{call}})$ or $(D_p / K_p)$ | When call is anticipated |
| **Convertible** | MAX(Straight, Conversion) | $P_p = \max\{P_{\text{straight}}, \, P_{\text{conv}}\}$ | Convertible preferred issues |
| **Cumulative Arrears** | Add PV of arrears | $P_p = \dfrac{D_p}{K_p} + \sum \dfrac{\text{Arrears}_t}{(1+K_p)^t}$ | Missed dividend payments |

### 3.5 NUMERICAL WALKTHROUGH — Worked Example for Exam Practice

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Statement (KTU-style):**
> *XYZ Ltd. issues a 10% preferred stock with a par value of ₹100. The issue price is ₹110 with flotation costs of ₹10 per share. Compute (a) annual dividend, (b) cost of preferred stock, (c) intrinsic value assuming 12% required return.*

**Part (a) — Annual Dividend:**

$$D_p = \text{Par} \times d = 100 \times 10\% = \text{₹}10$$

**Part (b) — Cost of Preferred Stock:**

Net proceeds: $N_p = P_p - F = 110 - 10 = \text{₹}100$

$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{N_p} = \frac{10}{100} = 0.10 = 10\%$$

**Part (c) — Intrinsic Value at 12% Required Return:**

$$P_p = \frac{D_p}{K_p} = \frac{10}{0.12} = \text{₹}83.33$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Verdict:** Since the intrinsic value (₹83.33) is below the market price (₹110), the stock is **overvalued** — investors should **NOT** buy at ₹110 if they require a 12% return.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Preferred Stock Valuation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Preferred Stock] --> B{Dividend Pattern}
    B -- Constant D --> C[Use Perpetuity Model]
    B -- Growing D --> D[Use Gordon Growth Model]
    C --> E{Flotation Cost?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Kp = D / (P - F)]
    E -- No --> G[P = D / Kp]
    D --> H{Valid? Kp > g}
    H -- Yes --> I[P = D1 / (Kp - g)]
    H -- No --> J[Model Invalid - Cap at Call Price]
    F --> K[Output: Cost of Capital Kp]
    G --> L[Output: Intrinsic Price P]
    I --> L
    L --> M{Price vs Market}
    M -- P > Market --> N[UNDERVALUED - Buy]
    M -- P < Market --> O[OVERVALUED - Sell]
    M -- P = Market --> P[FAIRLY VALUED - Hold]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Capital Structure Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sources of Capital] --> B[Debt - Tax Deductible]
    A --> C[Preferred Stock - NO Tax Shield]
    A --> D[Common Equity]
    B --> B1[Senior in Liquidation]
    B --> B2[Fixed Interest Payments]
    C --> C1[Fixed Dividend]
    C --> C2[Senior to Equity]
    C --> C3[No Voting Rights]
    D --> D1[Residual Claim]
    D --> D2[Voting Rights]
    D --> D3[Variable Dividends]
    C1 --> E[Cost: Kp = D / Np]
    B2 --> F[Cost: Kd x 1-T]
    D2 --> G[Cost: Ke via CAPM or DDM]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Cost Flow in WACC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Preferred Dividend D] --> Q1[After Tax Cash Outflow]
    Q1 --> R1[No Tax Shield]
    R1 --> S1[Cost Kp = D / Np]
    P2[Debt Interest I] --> Q2[Before Tax Cash Outflow]
    Q2 --> R2[Tax Shield: I x T]
    R2 --> S2[After Tax Cost Kd1-T]
    P3[Common Equity Earnings] --> Q3[Residual Distribution]
    Q3 --> S3[Cost Ke]
    S1 --> T[WACC Calculation]
    S2 --> T
    S3 --> T
    T --> U[Final WACC = Wd x Kd1-T + Wp x Kp + We x Ke]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Preferred Stock in Corporate Capital Stack

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph capitalStack["Corporate Capital Stack"]
        A1[Senior Secured Debt] --> A2[Unsecured Debt]
        A2 --> A3[Subordinated Debt]
        A3 --> A4[Preferred Stock]
        A4 --> A5[Common Equity]
    end
    A4 --> B1[Fixed Dividend Obligation]
    A4 --> B2[Callable Feature]
    A4 --> B3[Convertible Feature]
    A4 --> B4[Cumulative Feature]
    A4 --> B5[Participating Feature]
    B1 --> C1[Cash Flow Priority 4]
    A3 --> C2[Cash Flow Priority 3]
    A2 --> C3[Cash Flow Priority 2]
    A1 --> C4[Cash Flow Priority 1]
    A5 --> C5[Cash Flow Priority 5 Residual]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Priority 1 gets paid first in liquidation, Priority 5 (common equity) is the residual claimant. Preferred stock sits **above** common equity but **below** all forms of debt.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Part A Pattern:** 2-mark sub-question + 1-mark application/definition. Direct recall type. Answer must be **2-3 sentences maximum.**

---

#### **Q1. Define preferred stock and state its two key features.** [3 Marks]

> *[KTU University Exam - May 2024 style]*

**Model Answer:**

Preferred stock is a hybrid security representing **ownership in a company** that carries a **priority claim on dividends and assets** over common shareholders but ranks below debt holders in liquidation.

**Two key features:**

1. **Fixed Dividend:** The dividend is predetermined (as a ₹ amount or % of par value), providing income stability similar to debt.
2. **No Voting Rights:** Preferred shareholders generally do not participate in corporate voting, ceding governance control to common equity holders.

Additionally, preferred stock typically has **perpetual life** (no maturity date) and is often **callable**, allowing the issuer to redeem it at a stated price.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** A 3-mark answer should have 3 distinct points. Don't write a paragraph — use bullets.

---

#### **Q2. Why are preferred dividends not tax-deductible, and how does this affect the cost of preferred stock?** [3 Marks]

> *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 style]*

**Model Answer:**

Preferred dividends are paid out of **distributable profits (after-tax earnings)**, not as a charge against pre-tax income. From the perspective of taxation law, preferred stock is treated as **equity**, not debt.

**Effect on cost:**

Since there is **no tax shield** (unlike interest on debt, where interest is tax-deductible), the cost of preferred stock is computed on a **gross basis** as:

$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{N_p} = \frac{D_p}{P_p - F}$$

There is **no $(1 - T)$** adjustment factor, making $K_p$ relatively **higher than the after-tax cost of debt** in most scenarios. This is why firms prefer debt over preferred stock for tax efficiency.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Part B Pattern:** Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Show **all steps** for full credit.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Adapted]**

**(a) Explain the assumptions and limitations of the perpetual preferred stock valuation model.** [7 Marks]

**(b) ABC Ltd. has issued 12% preferred stock with a face value of ₹100. The current market price is ₹120, and flotation costs are ₹20 per share. An investor requires a 15% rate of return. Calculate: (i) Annual dividend, (ii) Cost of preferred stock, (iii) Intrinsic value of the share, (iv) Whether the investor should buy the share.** [7 Marks]

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Assumptions of the Perpetual Preferred Stock Valuation Model:** [3.5 marks]

1. **Constant Dividends:** The annual dividend $D_p$ remains unchanged in perpetuity.
2. **Perpetual Life:** The preferred stock has no maturity date; the firm is a going concern.
3. **Constant Required Return:** The investor's required rate of return $K_p$ does not change over time.
4. **Ordinary Cash Flows:** Dividends are received at the **end** of each period.
5. **Perfect Capital Market:** No taxes, transaction costs, or information asymmetry (relaxed in practice).
6. **No Bankruptcy Risk:** The firm is assumed to honor its dividend obligations indefinitely.

**Limitations of the Model:** [3.5 marks]

1. **Constant Dividend Unrealistic:** Most modern preferred issues have **floating rates** (linked to T-bill yields) or **participating** clauses.
2. **Ignores Callability:** If the stock is callable, the effective horizon is finite, not perpetual.
3. **Ignores Convertibility:** Convertible preferreds have a **floor value** not captured by the perpetuity model.
4. **No Growth Considered:** The basic model cannot handle dividend growth (requires the Gordon model).
5. **Flotation Cost Ignored:** Basic model does not adjust for new-issue transaction costs.
6. **Tax Effects Omitted:** Does not consider differential tax treatment of dividends vs. capital gains.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- Par value = ₹100
- Dividend rate = 12% (annual)
- Market price $P_p$ = ₹120
- Flotation cost $F$ = ₹20
- Required return $K_p$ = 15%

**(i) Annual Dividend** [1.5 marks]:

$$D_p = \text{Par} \times d\% = 100 \times 0.12 = \text{₹}12$$

**(ii) Cost of Preferred Stock** [2 marks]:

Net proceeds: $N_p = P_p - F = 120 - 20 = \text{₹}100$

$$K_p = \frac{D_p}{N_p} = \frac{12}{100} = 0.12 = 12\%$$

**(iii) Intrinsic Value** [1.5 marks]:

$$P_p = \frac{D_p}{K_p} = \frac{12}{0.15} = \text{₹}80$$

**(iv) Investment Decision** [2 marks]:

Since intrinsic value (₹80) is **less than** market price (₹120), the share is **overvalued**. The investor **should NOT buy** at ₹120. They would need to wait for the price to drop to ₹80 (or below) to earn the required 15% return.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students **lose 2 marks** by forgetting to subtract flotation cost from the issue price in part (ii).
> - Students **lose 1 mark** for not interpreting the result in part (iv) — always conclude with "buy/hold/sell" reasoning.
> - Writing $K_p = D/P$ instead of $K_p = D/N$ (where $N = P - F$) is a **common 3-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Adapted]**

**(a) Differentiate between preferred stock and bonds as sources of finance. List any six points.** [7 Marks]

**(b) PQR Corporation's preferred stock pays an annual dividend of ₹8 per share. The stock is perpetual. Compute its intrinsic value under each of the following scenarios: (i) Required return is 10%, (ii) Required return is 12%, (iii) Required return is 8%, (iv) If dividends are expected to grow at 5% per annum and required return is 12%, find the price.** [7 Marks]

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

| # | Preferred Stock | Bonds |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Equity instrument**; represents ownership | **Debt instrument**; represents a loan |
| 2 | Dividends are **not tax-deductible** | Interest is **tax-deductible** |
| 3 | Usually **perpetual** (no maturity) | Has a **fixed maturity date** |
| 4 | Has **priority over common stock** but below bonds in liquidation | Has **priority over all equity** in liquidation |
| 5 | No voting rights; no legal obligation to pay | Voting rights not applicable; legal obligation to pay |
| 6 | Cost: $K_p = D_p / N_p$ (no tax shield) | Cost: $K_d(1 - T)$ (with tax shield) |
| 7 | Can be **convertible** into common shares | Cannot be converted into equity (except convertibles) |
| 8 | Dividends are **discretionary** (cumulative may accumulate) | Interest is a **compulsory** legal obligation |

> [!TIP]
> Any 6 points earn full credit. Use a table for clarity — KTU examiners reward visual organization.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given:** $D_p = ₹8$ per share (perpetual)

**(i) Required return $K_p = 10\%$:** [1.5 marks]

$$P_p = \frac{D_p}{K_p} = \frac{8}{0.10} = \text{₹}80$$

**(ii) Required return $K_p = 12\%$:** [1.5 marks]

$$P_p = \frac{D_p}{K_p} = \frac{8}{0.12} = \text{₹}66.67$$

**(iii) Required return $K_p = 8\%$:** [1.5 marks]

$$P_p = \frac{D_p}{K_p} = \frac{8}{0.08} = \text{₹}100$$

**(iv) Growing Perpetuity: $g = 5\%$, $K_p = 12\%$:** [2.5 marks]

$$P_p = \frac{D_1}{K_p - g} = \frac{8}{0.12 - 0.05} = \frac{8}{0.07} = \text{₹}114.29$$

**Insight:** As required return **decreases** (cases i → iii), price **increases** (₹80 → ₹100). When dividend growth is introduced (case iv), price rises further to ₹114.29 because future cash flows are more valuable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In part (iv), students often forget that $D_1 = D_0(1+g)$. If the problem states "$D_0 = ₹8$" but dividends grow at 5%, then $D_1 = 8 \times 1.05 = ₹8.40$. In our problem, we assumed $D_p = ₹8$ is the **first-year** dividend $D_1$, so no adjustment was needed. **Read the wording carefully.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Preferred Stock Valuation**

**Core Identity to Memorize:**
- $\boxed{P_p = \dfrac{D_p}{K_p}}$ — Perpetuity
- $\boxed{K_p = \dfrac{D_p}{P_p - F}}$ — Cost with flotation
- $\boxed{P_p = \dfrac{D_1}{K_p - g}}$ — Growing perpetuity (only if $K_p > g$)

**High-Yield Facts:**
- Preferred stock is **equity**, not debt — **no tax shield**.
- Dividends are **fixed** in nominal terms; **no maturity** in most cases.
- Used as a component in **WACC**: $K_p \cdot W_p$.
- Intrinsic value is compared to market price: $P_p > P_m$ → **Buy**; $P_p < P_m$ → **Sell**.
- **Cumulative** preferreds accumulate unpaid dividends; **Non-cumulative** do not.
- **Callable** preferreds are valued at the **minimum** of (perpetuity value, call price).
- **Convertible** preferreds are valued at the **maximum** of (straight value, conversion value).

**Common KTU Traps to Avoid:**
- ❌ Applying the tax shield $(1-T)$ to $K_p$ — preferred dividends are NOT tax-deductible.
- ❌ Using $P_p$ instead of $N_p = P_p - F$ in the cost formula.
- ❌ Using the Gordon model when $g \geq K_p$ (denominator becomes zero or negative).
- ❌ Forgetting to convert the **stated % dividend** into a **₹ amount** using par value.

**Quick Numerical Sanity Check:**
- If $D_p = ₹10$ and $K_p = 10\%$, then $P_p = ₹100$ (intuitive: a 10% return on ₹100 gives ₹10).
- If the price is ₹110, the implied return drops to $\frac{10}{110} = 9.09\%$ — the buyer is overpaying.

**Connection to Other Modules:**
- Module 2 feeds directly into **WACC computation** and **Capital Structure decisions**.
- Preferred stock's after-tax cost is **always higher** than debt's after-tax cost, ceteris paribus.
- The **dividend irrelevance** in Modigliani-Miller assumes no preferred stock; with preferreds, dividend policy affects valuation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** Whenever you see a preferred stock problem, immediately identify:
> 1. **Cash flow pattern** (constant vs. growing)
> 2. **Flotation cost** (yes/no)
> 3. **Required return** (given or to be computed)
> 4. **Decision required** (value, cost, or buy/sell)
> Following this 4-step mental checklist will ensure you never miss a sub-part in the 14-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Stock Valuation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Common Stock Valuation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Stock Valuation** is the process of determining the **intrinsic value (theoretical worth)** of a share of common stock by discounting the expected future cash flows — primarily **dividends** and the **eventual selling price** — back to the present at an appropriate **required rate of return (cost of equity, $K_e$)**.

The intrinsic value is the present value of the stream of expected future benefits accruing to the equity shareholder. Since dividends are the only direct cash flow a shareholder reliably receives (capital gains being realized only on sale), the valuation framework is built on the **Dividend Discount Model (DDM)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECBT631 – Module 2):** The 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on the **Gordon Growth Model (Constant Growth DDM)**, the **Zero Growth Model**, the **Two-Stage Growth Model**, and the linkage between valuation and the **Cost of Equity ($K_e$)** computed via **CAPM**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Fruit Tree"

Imagine you buy a **mango tree** today for an unknown fair price.

- Every year the tree gives you **mangoes (dividends)**.
- The tree may also grow taller and yield **more mangoes each year (growth, $g$)**.
- A reasonable buyer will pay only as much as the **present value of all future mangoes**, discounted by the **time value of money** and the **risk of the tree dying (risk, $K_e$)**.

Similarly, an equity share is a perpetual claim on the firm's future dividends, and its value equals the **discounted sum of all expected dividends**. If you pay *less* than this intrinsic value, the share is **underpriced (buy)**; if you pay *more*, it is **overpriced (sell)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU exams:** The market price ($P_0$) and the intrinsic value ($\hat{P}_0$) are *never* the same in the short run. The valuation models estimate $\hat{P}_0$, which is then compared with $P_0$ to make investment decisions.

---

## 1.3 Why Stock Valuation Matters

| Decision Context | Role of Valuation |
|---|---|
| **Equity Investment** | Determines whether a stock is over-/undervalued |
| **M&A and IPO Pricing** | Sets the issue/acquisition price per share |
| **Portfolio Management** | Guides asset allocation and rebalancing |
| **Corporate Finance (Right Issue, Buyback)** | Helps management decide fair share price |
| **WACC Computation** | Cost of equity is a key input to firm valuation |

> **Standard metric used in the KTU model answers:** $K_e$ is the **cost of common equity capital**, expressed as a **percentage (%)**; $g$ is the **constant growth rate of dividends** (%), and $P_0$ is the **current intrinsic value per share** (₹).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Foundation — Three Building Blocks

A common stock valuation problem is solved by combining three pieces of information:

1. **Expected dividend stream** — $D_1, D_2, D_3, \ldots, D_\infty$
2. **Required rate of return** — $K_e$ (cost of equity)
3. **Expected pattern of dividend growth** — $g$

The expected pattern of growth leads to **four standard models** in the KTU syllabus:

- (a) Zero Growth Model
- (b) Constant Growth Model (Gordon Model)
- (c) Two-Stage Growth Model
- (d) Variable (Non-Constant) Growth Model

---

## 2.2 Zero Growth Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** Dividends are **fixed forever** at $D$ (a perpetuity), and the firm is a **no-growth** entity (like a mature utility firm or a preferred stock proxy).

The intrinsic value is simply the **perpetuity present value**:

$$
P_0 \;=\; \frac{D}{K_e}
$$

**Intuition:** If a share pays a flat ₹10 every year forever and you need 12% return, you should pay $10 / 0.12 = ₹83.33$.

---

## 2.3 Constant Growth Model (Gordon Growth Model — GGM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked model** in KTU University Exams for the topic *Common Stock Valuation*. Master it thoroughly.

**Assumption:** Dividends grow at a **constant rate $g$** forever, with $K_e > g$.

$$
D_t \;=\; D_0 \,(1+g)^{t}
$$

The valuation equation is:

$$
P_0 \;=\; \frac{D_1}{K_e - g} \;=\; \frac{D_0 \,(1+g)}{K_e - g}
$$

This is obtained by summing the infinite geometric series of discounted dividends (proof in §3).

**Alternative rearrangement (to find $K_e$ or $g$):**

$$
K_e \;=\; \frac{D_1}{P_0} \;+\; g
$$

**Real-world utility:** Widely used for **mature, blue-chip companies** (e.g., ITC, Hindustan Unilever in India) where dividend growth has stabilized.

---

## 2.4 Two-Stage Growth Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** The firm experiences a **high (supernormal) growth rate $g_s$** for an initial period of $n$ years, and then settles into a **stable (constant) growth rate $g_n$** forever after.

The intrinsic value has **two parts**:

$$
P_0 \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{D_t}{(1+K_e)^{t}} \;+\; \frac{P_n}{(1+K_e)^{n}}
$$

where the terminal value at year $n$ is:

$$
P_n \;=\; \frac{D_{n+1}}{K_e - g_n} \;=\; \frac{D_0 \,(1+g_s)^{n} \,(1+g_n)}{K_e - g_n}
$$

**Real-world utility:** Suitable for **start-ups, tech firms, and high-growth companies** in their early years (e.g., early-stage EV, fintech firms).

---

## 2.5 Variable (Non-Constant) Growth Model

A more general case where dividends grow at **different rates in different years** but stabilize to a constant rate eventually. The valuation uses **piecewise discounting**.

---

## 2.6 Cost of Equity ($K_e$) — Required Rate of Return

The discount rate $K_e$ can be computed using either:

**(a) Dividend-yield + growth approach (from GGM):**
$$
K_e \;=\; \frac{D_1}{P_0} \;+\; g
$$

**(b) Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM):**
$$
K_e \;=\; R_f \;+\; \beta \,(R_m - R_f)
$$

where $R_f$ = risk-free rate, $\beta$ = systematic risk, $R_m$ = expected market return.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Model | Formula | When to Use | Key Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Zero Growth | $P_0 = \dfrac{D}{K_e}$ | Perpetual fixed dividends | $g = 0$ |
| 2 | Constant Growth (GGM) | $P_0 = \dfrac{D_1}{K_e - g}$ | Mature, stable growth firms | $K_e > g$ |
| 3 | Two-Stage Growth | $P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n}\dfrac{D_t}{(1+K_e)^{t}} + \dfrac{P_n}{(1+K_e)^{n}}$ | High-growth then stable firms | $K_e > g_n$ |
| 4 | Required Return (CAPM) | $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ | Discount rate estimation | — |
| 5 | Dividend Yield + Growth | $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ | Reverse-solve $K_e$ or $g$ | — |
| 6 | P/E Linkage (GGM) | $P_0 / E_1 = \dfrac{(1-b)}{K_e - g}$, where $b$ = retention ratio | Quick check using earnings | $K_e > g$ |
| 7 | Sustainable Growth Rate | $g = b \times \text{ROE}$ | Long-run $g$ estimate | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** The formula $P_0 = D_1 / (K_e - g)$ uses **$D_1$ (next year's dividend)**, **not** $D_0$ (this year's just-paid dividend). Forgetting this and using $D_0$ directly is the **single most common mistake** in KTU answer sheets.

---

## 2.8 Decision Rule

$$
\begin{cases}
\hat{P}_0 > P_0 \; & \Rightarrow \; \textbf{UNDERPRICED} \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{BUY} \\
\hat{P}_0 < P_0 \; & \Rightarrow \; \textbf{OVERPRICED} \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{SELL/HOLD} \\
\hat{P}_0 = P_0 \; & \Rightarrow \; \textbf{FAIRLY PRICED}
\end{cases}
$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Gordon Growth Model (Constant Growth DDM)

**Starting Premise:** The price of a share at any time $t$ equals the present value of *all future* dividends from $t+1$ onwards:

$$
P_0 \;=\; \frac{D_1}{(1+K_e)^{1}} \;+\; \frac{D_2}{(1+K_e)^{2}} \;+\; \frac{D_3}{(1+K_e)^{3}} \;+\; \cdots
$$

**Step 1:** Express each future dividend as a function of $D_0$ and $g$.

Under constant growth: $D_t = D_0 \,(1+g)^{t}$

$$
P_0 \;=\; \frac{D_0(1+g)^{1}}{(1+K_e)^{1}} \;+\; \frac{D_0(1+g)^{2}}{(1+K_e)^{2}} \;+\; \frac{D_0(1+g)^{3}}{(1+K_e)^{3}} \;+\; \cdots
$$

**Step 2:** Factor out $D_0(1+g)$.

$$
P_0 \;=\; D_0(1+g) \left[\frac{1}{(1+K_e)} \;+\; \frac{(1+g)}{(1+K_e)^{2}} \;+\; \frac{(1+g)^{2}}{(1+K_e)^{3}} \;+\; \cdots\right]
$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the expression inside the brackets as a **geometric series** with common ratio:

$$
r \;=\; \frac{1+g}{1+K_e}
$$

Since $K_e > g$ (required return exceeds growth), we have $r < 1$, so the infinite series converges to $\dfrac{1}{1-r}$.

**Step 4:** Substitute the sum-of-geometric-series formula.

$$
P_0 \;=\; D_0(1+g) \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{(1+g)}{(1+K_e)}}
$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the denominator.

$$
1 - \frac{(1+g)}{(1+K_e)} \;=\; \frac{(1+K_e) - (1+g)}{(1+K_e)} \;=\; \frac{K_e - g}{1+K_e}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back and cancel.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= D_0(1+g) \cdot \frac{1+K_e}{K_e - g} \\
    &= \frac{D_0(1+g)(1+K_e)}{(1+K_e)(K_e - g)} \\
    &= \frac{D_0(1+g)}{K_e - g} \\
    &= \frac{D_1}{K_e - g}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_0 \;=\; \frac{D_1}{K_e - g}\,}
$$

> This is the **Gordon Growth Model**, named after **Myron J. Gordon** (1956), one of the foundational models of modern financial economics.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Constant Growth)

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A firm has just paid a dividend of $D_0 = ₹5$. Dividends are expected to grow at a constant rate $g = 6\%$. The required rate of return is $K_e = 14\%$. Find the intrinsic value of the share.

**Step 1 — Compute $D_1$ (next year's expected dividend):**

$$
D_1 \;=\; D_0 (1+g) \;=\; 5 \times 1.06 \;=\; ₹5.30
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Gordon Growth Model:**

$$
P_0 \;=\; \frac{D_1}{K_e - g} \;=\; \frac{5.30}{0.14 - 0.06} \;=\; \frac{5.30}{0.08} \;=\; ₹66.25
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** If the market price is, say, ₹60, the share is **undervalued** (buy); if ₹75, it is **overvalued** (sell).

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Two-Stage Growth)

> **Problem:** A company paid $D_0 = ₹2$. It will grow at a supernormal rate $g_s = 20\%$ for the **first 3 years**, and then stabilize at a long-run $g_n = 6\%$. The required return is $K_e = 12\%$. Find $P_0$.

**Step 1 — Forecast dividends in the supernormal phase:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= 2 \times 1.20 = ₹2.40 \\
D_2 &= 2.40 \times 1.20 = ₹2.88 \\
D_3 &= 2.88 \times 1.20 = ₹3.456
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Terminal value at end of year 3 (Gordon on stable phase):**

$$
D_4 \;=\; D_3 \times (1+g_n) \;=\; 3.456 \times 1.06 \;=\; ₹3.6634
$$

$$
P_3 \;=\; \frac{D_4}{K_e - g_n} \;=\; \frac{3.6634}{0.12 - 0.06} \;=\; \frac{3.6634}{0.06} \;=\; ₹61.056
$$

**Step 3 — Discount all cash flows (dividends $D_1, D_2, D_3$ and $P_3$) to $t=0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV(D_1) &= \frac{2.40}{1.12} = 2.1429 \\
PV(D_2) &= \frac{2.88}{(1.12)^{2}} = \frac{2.88}{1.2544} = 2.2959 \\
PV(D_3) &= \frac{3.456}{(1.12)^{3}} = \frac{3.456}{1.404928} = 2.4600 \\
PV(P_3) &= \frac{61.056}{(1.12)^{3}} = \frac{61.056}{1.404928} = 43.4613
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum to get intrinsic value:**

$$
P_0 \;=\; 2.1429 + 2.2959 + 2.4600 + 43.4613 \;=\; ₹50.36
$$

---

## 3.4 Reverse-Solution: Finding $K_e$ from Market Price

> **Problem:** The market price of a share is $P_0 = ₹100$. Last dividend $D_0 = ₹4$. Constant growth $g = 5\%$. Find the required return $K_e$ expected by the market.

**Step 1 — Compute $D_1$:**

$$
D_1 = 4 \times 1.05 = ₹4.20
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the rearranged GGM:**

$$
K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g = \frac{4.20}{100} + 0.05 = 0.042 + 0.05 = 0.092
$$

$$
K_e = 9.2\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** The market is discounting the share at a 9.2% required return.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameter Changes on $P_0$

In the GGM $P_0 = D_1 / (K_e - g)$, the effect of changing parameters is summarized below.

| Parameter Change | Effect on $P_0$ | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $D_1 \uparrow$ | $P_0 \uparrow$ | Larger near-term cash flow |
| $K_e \uparrow$ | $P_0 \downarrow$ | Higher discount rate |
| $g \uparrow$ | $P_0 \uparrow$ | Higher future dividends |
| $g \to K_e$ | $P_0 \to \infty$ | Denominator vanishes — **model breaks** |
| $K_e \le g$ | **Undefined** | Required return ≤ growth = **no equilibrium** |

> [!WARNING]
> If $K_e \le g$, the GGM produces a **negative or infinite** price — a clear signal that the model is **not applicable** for that firm (e.g., very early-stage startups with negative earnings).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flow — How to Choose a Common Stock Valuation Model

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decision logic** a finance professional (and a KTU student in the exam) follows when selecting the appropriate valuation model based on the firm's growth profile.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Firm Data Collected"] --> B["Identify Expected Dividend Growth Pattern"]
    B --> C{"Is Growth Constant?"}
    C -- "Yes: g = 0" --> D["Apply Zero Growth Model"]
    C -- "Yes: g is constant nonzero" --> E["Apply Gordon Growth Model"]
    C -- "No: Growth varies over time" --> F{"Single or Multi-Stage Variation?"}
    F -- "Single high-growth phase then constant" --> G["Apply Two-Stage Growth Model"]
    F -- "Multiple growth phases" --> H["Apply Variable Growth Model"]
    D --> I["Compare Intrinsic Value with Market Price"]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{"P0 hat vs P0 Market?"}
    J -- "P0 hat greater than P0" --> K["DECISION: BUY Underpriced"]
    J -- "P0 hat less than P0" --> L["DECISION: SELL Overpriced"]
    J -- "P0 hat equal to P0" --> M["DECISION: HOLD Fairly Priced"]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inputs to the Gordon Growth Model

The GGM accepts four primary inputs and produces a single output. The block diagram below formalizes this architecture and highlights the **mathematical dependencies** between inputs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input1["Input 1: D0 Last Paid Dividend in INR"] --> Compute["Computation Engine"]
    Input2["Input 2: g Constant Growth Rate in percent"] --> Compute
    Input3["Input 3: Ke Required Return in percent"] --> Compute
    Input4["Input 4: P0 Market Price in INR optional"] --> Compute
    Compute --> Output["Output: P0 hat Intrinsic Value in INR"]
    Compute --> Output2["Output: Ke Implied Required Return in percent"]
    Compute --> Output3["Output: g Implied Growth Rate in percent"]
    Validate["Validator: Ke greater than g"] --> Compute
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **validator** block in the architecture corresponds to the critical boundary condition $K_e > g$. The valuation engine must reject any input set that violates this condition (returning an error or $\infty$), as discussed in the analytical matrix of §3.5.

---

## 4.3 Two-Stage Valuation — Schematic Cash-Flow Timeline

The following timeline visualizes how a Two-Stage Growth Model decomposes the intrinsic value into a **finite supernormal phase** and an **infinite stable phase**, with each component discounted to $t = 0$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0["t = 0: Today P0 unknown"] --> t1["t = 1: D1 paid at high g"]
    t1 --> t2["t = 2: D2 paid at high g"]
    t2 --> t3["t = 3: D3 paid at high g"]
    t3 --> t4["t = n: Transition Point Dn plus Pn"]
    t4 --> t5["t = n+1: D n+1 paid at stable gn"]
    t5 --> t6["t = n+2: D n+2"]
    t6 --> t7["t = infinity: Stable perpetuity"]

    SumPhase1["Sum of PV of D1 to Dn using Ke"] --> Final["P0 = Phase 1 PV plus Phase 2 PV of Pn"]
    SumPhase2["PV of Pn using Ke and stable g"] --> Final
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** for PECBT631 — Financial Management, Module 2. Marks are tagged as **[3-Mark Short Answer]** or **[14-Mark Detailed Answer]**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 3 Marks]**
**"State and explain the Zero Growth Model of common stock valuation. Mention its assumption."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — full marks breakdown shown):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The Zero Growth Model assumes that dividends remain **constant** at ₹$D$ per share for an indefinite period. The intrinsic value is therefore the present value of a **perpetuity**:

$$
P_0 \;=\; \frac{D}{K_e}
$$

**Assumption (1 mark):**
- The firm pays a **fixed dividend $D$** every year.
- The firm has **no growth**, i.e., $g = 0$.
- $K_e$ is **constant** over time.
- The firm is **perpetually existing** (never liquidated).

**Example (1 mark):**
If $D = ₹8$ and $K_e = 10\%$, then $P_0 = 8 / 0.10 = ₹80$.

> **Use cases:** Preferred stock, mature utilities, government bonds (as analog).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 | 3 Marks]**
**"Distinguish between the Zero Growth Model and the Gordon Growth Model of stock valuation."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Zero Growth Model | Gordon Growth Model |
|---|---|---|
| Growth rate $g$ | $g = 0$ (assumed) | Constant $g > 0$ (assumed) |
| Dividend pattern | Constant perpetuity ₹$D$ | Growing perpetuity ₹$D_0(1+g)^t$ |
| Formula | $P_0 = D / K_e$ | $P_0 = D_1 / (K_e - g)$ |
| Applicability | No-growth firms, preferred stock | Mature growing firms (blue-chips) |
| Limitation | Unrealistic for most firms | Sensitive to small changes in $K_e$ and $g$ |

> **Conclusion (1 line):** The Gordon Growth Model generalizes the Zero Growth Model by introducing a positive constant growth rate.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Detailed Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, students must answer **one full question of 14 marks** with internal choice. The two alternative 14-mark questions below are independent and can be set as **OR options** in a Module 2 question paper.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) | [KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern]**

**"XYZ Ltd. has just paid a dividend of ₹4 per share. The dividend is expected to grow at 8% per year for the next 3 years, and thereafter stabilize at 5% per year indefinitely. The required rate of return on the equity share is 14%.**
**(a) Calculate the intrinsic value of the share using the Two-Stage Growth Model. (7 marks)**
**(b) If the current market price is ₹48, what investment decision should an investor take? Justify using the required return implied by the market. (7 marks)"**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**

$D_0 = ₹4$, $g_s = 8\%$ (years 1–3), $g_n = 5\%$ (from year 4 onwards), $K_e = 14\%$.

**Step 2 — Compute supernormal dividends for years 1 to 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= 4 \times 1.08 = ₹4.32 \\
D_2 &= 4.32 \times 1.08 = ₹4.6656 \\
D_3 &= 4.6656 \times 1.08 = ₹5.0388
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the first stable dividend and terminal price at $t = 3$:**

$$
D_4 = D_3 \times (1 + g_n) = 5.0388 \times 1.05 = ₹5.2908
$$

$$
P_3 = \frac{D_4}{K_e - g_n} = \frac{5.2908}{0.14 - 0.05} = \frac{5.2908}{0.09} = ₹58.787
$$

**Step 4 — Discount all components to $t = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV(D_1) &= \frac{4.32}{1.14} = ₹3.789 \\
PV(D_2) &= \frac{4.6656}{(1.14)^{2}} = \frac{4.6656}{1.2996} = ₹3.590 \\
PV(D_3) &= \frac{5.0388}{(1.14)^{3}} = \frac{5.0388}{1.481544} = ₹3.401 \\
PV(P_3) &= \frac{58.787}{(1.14)^{3}} = \frac{58.787}{1.481544} = ₹39.681
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum to get intrinsic value:**

$$
P_0 = 3.789 + 3.590 + 3.401 + 39.681 = ₹50.46
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\hat{P}_0 \;\approx\; ₹50.46\,}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compare intrinsic value with market price:**

$$
\hat{P}_0 = ₹50.46, \quad P_0^{\text{market}} = ₹48
$$

Since $\hat{P}_0 > P_0^{\text{market}}$, the share is **underpriced** by the market. **Recommendation: BUY.**

**Step 2 — Compute the market's implied required return $K_e^{m}$:**

Using the rearranged GGM for the long-run stable phase (since the bulk of value is in the perpetuity):

$$
K_e^{m} = \frac{D_1}{P_0^{\text{market}}} + g_n = \frac{4.32}{48} + 0.05 = 0.0900 + 0.05 = 0.14
$$

$$
K_e^{m} = 14\%
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The market is using a 14% required return. Since the investor's required return is also 14%, the investor perceives the share as **fairly-to-undervalued** at ₹48.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
> The intrinsic value (₹50.46) **exceeds** the market price (₹48), so an investor should **BUY** the share of XYZ Ltd. as it offers positive expected return.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:**
> - **Mark loss point 1 (2 marks):** Using $D_0 = ₹4$ directly in the GGM formula $P_0 = D_0 / (K_e - g)$ instead of $D_1$ — students forget to apply the $(1+g)$ growth factor.
> - **Mark loss point 2 (1 mark):** Forgetting to discount $P_n$ (the terminal price) to $t = 0$. Many students compute $P_n$ correctly but stop there.
> - **Mark loss point 3 (1 mark):** Writing the decision as "buy" without comparing $\hat{P}_0$ with $P_0^{\text{market}}$ explicitly. KTU examiners require **explicit comparison** to award the conclusion mark.
> - **Mark loss point 4 (1 mark):** Rounding too early in the supernormal-phase calculations. Carry at least **4 decimal places** until the final step.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) | [KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern]**

**"The following data relates to PQR Industries Limited:***
- *Risk-free rate ($R_f$) = 7%*
- *Expected market return ($R_m$) = 15%*
- *Beta of the share ($\beta$) = 1.2*
- *Last paid dividend ($D_0$) = ₹6*
- *Expected constant growth rate ($g$) = 10%*
- *Current market price ($P_0$) = ₹110*

**(a) Compute the intrinsic value of the share using the Gordon Growth Model. (7 marks)**
**(b) Advise the investor whether to buy, hold, or sell the share. Also compute the maximum price the investor should be willing to pay. (7 marks)"**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute the cost of equity $K_e$ using CAPM:**

$$
K_e = R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f) = 0.07 + 1.2 \times (0.15 - 0.07)
$$

$$
K_e = 0.07 + 1.2 \times 0.08 = 0.07 + 0.096 = 0.166
$$

$$
K_e = 16.6\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute next year's dividend $D_1$:**

$$
D_1 = D_0 (1+g) = 6 \times 1.10 = ₹6.60
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Gordon Growth Model:**

$$
\hat{P}_0 = \frac{D_1}{K_e - g} = \frac{6.60}{0.166 - 0.10} = \frac{6.60}{0.066} = ₹100.00
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\hat{P}_0 = ₹100\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification of validity:**

Since $K_e = 16.6\% > g = 10\%$, the GGM assumption is satisfied and the result is meaningful.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Decision via comparison:**

$$
\hat{P}_0 = ₹100, \quad P_0^{\text{market}} = ₹110
$$

Since $\hat{P}_0 < P_0^{\text{market}}$, the share is **overpriced** by the market. **Recommendation: SELL / DO NOT BUY.**

**Step 2 — Maximum price an investor should pay:**

The investor should pay **no more than the intrinsic value**, because paying more would yield a return less than the required 16.6%.

$$
\text{Maximum Price} = \hat{P}_0 = ₹100
$$

**Step 3 — Quantify the overpricing:**

$$
\text{Overpricing \%} = \frac{P_0^{\text{market}} - \hat{P}_0}{\hat{P}_0} \times 100 = \frac{110 - 100}{100} \times 100 = 10\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the actual return an investor would earn at the market price:**

$$
K_e^{\text{actual}} = \frac{D_1}{P_0^{\text{market}}} + g = \frac{6.60}{110} + 0.10 = 0.06 + 0.10 = 0.16
$$

$$
K_e^{\text{actual}} = 16.0\%
$$

This is **less than** the required 16.6%, confirming the **SELL** recommendation.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
> The share of PQR Industries is **overvalued by 10%** at the current market price. An investor should **not buy**; the maximum payable price is **₹100**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> - **Mark loss point 1 (2 marks):** Forgetting the CAPM step entirely and assuming $K_e$ as "given". KTU Module 2 explicitly tests the **linkage between CAPM and DDM**.
> - **Mark loss point 2 (1 mark):** Confusing $R_m$ with $(R_m - R_f)$ — many students add $R_f + \beta \times R_m$ instead of $R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f)$.
> - **Mark loss point 3 (1 mark):** Using $D_0 = ₹6$ in the GGM instead of $D_1 = ₹6.60$.
> - **Mark loss point 4 (1 mark):** Writing "buy/sell" without showing the **numerical comparison** between $\hat{P}_0$ and $P_0^{\text{market}}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Common Stock Valuation:**

- **Core Equation:** The value of any financial asset = **PV of all expected future cash flows**.
- **Zero Growth:** $P_0 = D / K_e$ — a **perpetuity** with constant dividend.
- **Constant Growth (Gordon):** $P_0 = D_1 / (K_e - g)$ — a **growing perpetuity**.
- **Two-Stage:** Sum of discounted supernormal dividends + discounted terminal price $P_n$.
- **CRITICAL:** Use **$D_1$ (next year)**, never $D_0$, in the GGM numerator.
- **VALIDITY CONDITION:** $K_e > g$ must always hold; otherwise, the GGM gives nonsensical results.
- **CAPM Linkage:** $K_e = R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f)$ — used to compute the required return when the market price is not available.
- **Reverse GGM:** $K_e = D_1 / P_0 + g$ — used to back out the market's implied required return from observed prices.
- **Decision Rule:** $\hat{P}_0 > P_0 \Rightarrow$ BUY, $\hat{P}_0 < P_0 \Rightarrow$ SELL, $\hat{P}_0 = P_0 \Rightarrow$ HOLD.
- **Sensitivity:** $P_0$ is **highly sensitive** to small changes in $K_e$ and $g$ — a 1% change can move $P_0$ by 10–20%.
- **Sustainable Growth:** $g = b \times \text{ROE}$ — used to estimate long-run $g$ when not directly given.
- **P/E Linkage:** $P_0 / E_1 = (1 - b) / (K_e - g)$ — relates valuation to earnings and retention ratio.
- **Real-World Application:** GGM suits mature firms; Two-Stage suits growth firms; Zero Growth suits preferred stock or stagnant utilities.
- **Examiner's Mantra:** Always **state assumptions, show the formula, substitute values step by step, and conclude with an investment decision.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Yield and YTM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Concept of Yield and YTM # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Concept of Yield (Current Yield) **Yield**, in the context of fixed-income securities (bonds and debentures), refers to the **income return** an investor earns on a bond relative to its prevailing market price. The most elementary form is the **Current Yield (CY)**. ### Formal Academic Definition > **Current Yield** is the ratio of the **annual coupon (interest) income** of a bond to its **current market price**, expressed as a percentage. It measures the income component of return only and **ignores** capital gains or losses arising from price-to-face-value differences. Mathematically, the current yield is stated as: $$\text{Current Yield (CY)} = \frac{\text{Annual Coupon Payment}}{\text{Current Market Price}} \times 100$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Current Yield answers the question — *"If I buy the bond today at its market price, what percentage income will I earn per year from coupons alone?"* ### Intuitive Analogy — The Mango Cart Example 🍎 Imagine Ramesh buys a fruit cart that delivers **100 mangoes every year** (the **coupon**) and after 5 years the cart is sold back for the original cost (the **face value**). But the cart currently costs **₹800** instead of the original **₹1,000**. - If you only count the **100 mangoes per year**, your "current yield" on the ₹800 investment is **100/800 = 12.5%**. - You *miss out* on the fact that you'll eventually get back ₹1,000 on an ₹800 investment — that's an **extra ₹200 gain** spread over 5 years. That extra return (plus any compounding of the 100 mangoes if reinvested) is captured by **Yield to Maturity (YTM)**, not by current yield. --- ## 1.2 Concept of Yield to Maturity (YTM) ### Formal Academic Definition > **Yield to Maturity (YTM)** is the **internal rate of return (IRR)** earned by an investor who purchases a bond at its current market price and **holds it until maturity**, receiving all scheduled coupon payments and the face value at the end. Equivalently, YTM is the **single discount rate** that equates the **present value of all future cash flows** (coupons + face value) to the **current market price** of the bond. The fundamental YTM equation is: $$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+y)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+y)^n}$$ Where: - $P_0$ = Current market price of the bond - $C$ = Annual coupon payment - $F$ = Face (par) value of the bond - $n$ = Number of years to maturity - $y$ = Yield to maturity (the unknown to be solved) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core YTM Assumptions (Exam-Favorite):** > 1. The bond is held **till maturity**. > 2. All coupon payments are **reinvested at the YTM rate itself**. > 3. The issuer does **not default** on any payment. ### Intuitive Analogy — The Complete Mango Cart Story 🥭 Returning to Ramesh's cart: he paid **₹800** today, will receive **100 mangoes per year for 5 years**, and at the end gets back **₹1,000**. The **YTM is the single "interest rate"** that makes all those future 100-mango-per-year payments + the final ₹1,000 *worth exactly* ₹800 *today*. In other words, YTM answers: *"What is the single, constant, all-in annual percentage return if I stay invested till the cart is fully sold back?"* - If the cart is bought **at a discount** ($P_0 < F$), YTM > Coupon Rate (extra capital gain boost). - If bought **at par** ($P_0 = F$), YTM = Coupon Rate. - If bought **at a premium** ($P_0 > F$), YTM < Coupon Rate (capital loss drag). > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook:** YTM is the bond's **"true effective interest rate"** — comparable to the **CAGR** of an equity investment, but specifically for fixed-income securities held to maturity. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Logic Behind Current Yield — Stepwise The current yield calculation is derived from a simple income-on-investment logic, identical in spirit to the dividend yield concept in equity markets. **Step 1:** Identify the annual coupon (interest) payment. > $C = \text{Coupon Rate} \times \text{Face Value (F)}$ **Step 2:** Identify the current market price. > $P_0 = \text{Price quoted in the market today}$ **Step 3:** Compute the ratio of income to price. > This represents the **return earned per rupee of current investment** purely from coupons. **Step 4:** Express as a percentage. > Multiply by 100 to obtain the conventional yield percentage. **Why It Matters in Engineering / Business:** Even non-finance professionals (project managers, civil engineers handling project finance, IT managers evaluating corporate bond investments) use current yield as a **quick screening metric** to compare the income-generating efficiency of multiple bonds. It is the **first filter** before deeper YTM analysis. --- ## 2.2 The Logic Behind YTM — Stepwise YTM is fundamentally a **present value (PV) inversion problem**. The bond's market price is known; we solve for the discount rate. **Step 1:** List the future cash flows. > Years 1 through $n$: Annual coupon $C$ > Year $n$: Coupon $C$ **plus** Face Value $F$ **Step 2:** Set up the present value equation. > Discount each cash flow at rate $y$ and sum them. Equate to $P_0$. **Step 3:** Solve the polynomial equation for $y$. > This is mathematically a **polynomial of degree $n$** and generally has **no closed-form algebraic solution for $n > 4$**. Hence, YTM is solved by: > - **Trial-and-error interpolation** (textbook approach for KTU) > - **Financial calculator / Excel's `RATE()` function** (real-world approach) > - **Approximation formula** (quick estimate) **Step 4:** Interpret the result. > $y$ is the **single annualized return** if all assumptions hold. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Three-Way Bond Pricing Identity (exam-favorite):** > - $P_0 < F$ (Discount bond) $\Rightarrow$ $y > \text{Coupon Rate}$ > - $P_0 = F$ (Par bond) $\Rightarrow$ $y = \text{Coupon Rate}$ > - $P_0 > F$ (Premium bond) $\Rightarrow$ $y < \text{Coupon Rate}$ --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Special Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Coupon Payment | $C = r_c \times F$ | $r_c$ = coupon rate (decimal); $F$ = face value (₹) | If $r_c = 0$, it is a **zero-coupon bond** | | 2 | Current Yield | $CY = \dfrac{C}{P_0} \times 100$ | $C$ = annual coupon (₹); $P_0$ = market price (₹) | Output in **%**; does **not** equal YTM unless $P_0 = F$ | | 3 | YTM — Exact (PV Equation) | $P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^t} + \dfrac{F}{(1+y)^n}$ | All variables as above; $y$ is the unknown | Polynomial of degree $n$; solved iteratively | | 4 | YTM — Approximation | $y \approx \dfrac{C + \dfrac{F - P_0}{n}}{\dfrac{F + P_0}{2}}$ | All variables as above | Best for small $n$ and prices near par | | 5 | Capital Gain Yield | $CGY = \dfrac{F - P_0}{n \cdot P_0}$ | Spread of capital gain over years and price | Annualized capital gain component | | 6 | Total Return Check | $YTM \approx CY + CGY$ | True in approximation formula | Exact only for par bonds | > [!NOTE] > **Variable Convention Used by KTU Examiners:** > - $F$ or $RV$ (Redeemable Value) are interchangeable. > - $P_0$ or $P$ both denote the current market price. > - $C$ or $I$ (Interest) refer to the annual coupon. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility - **Project Finance:** Infrastructure companies (roads, power plants) raise capital via bonds. The **YTM** tells the lender their true effective borrowing cost. - **Treasury Management:** Corporate treasurers compare YTM of government securities (G-Secs) to corporate bond YTM to compute **credit spreads**. - **Retirement Planning:** Pension funds need to lock into bonds where **YTM ≥ actuarial assumed return** to meet future liabilities. - **Capital Budgeting Decisions:** Engineers evaluating **Build-Operate-Transfer (BOT)** projects use YTM-equivalent (IRR) to compare with WACC. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Current Yield from First Principles The intuition of yield originates from the **rental yield** concept in real estate. **Step 1:** Suppose an investor purchases a bond at price $P_0$ with face value $F$ and coupon rate $r_c$. The annual cash inflow is: > $C = r_c \cdot F$ **Step 2:** The **return per rupee invested**, considering only income (not price appreciation), is: > $\text{Return per ₹} = \dfrac{C}{P_0} = \dfrac{r_c \cdot F}{P_0}$ **Step 3:** To express as a percentage, multiply by 100: > $CY (\%) = \dfrac{r_c \cdot F}{P_0} \times 100$ **Interpretation:** If $F = P_0$ (bond at par), then $CY = r_c$ (coupon rate). Any deviation creates a divergence. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Exact YTM Equation We start with the definition of a bond's market price as the **present value of all its future cash flows**. **Step 1:** The bond promises $C$ at the end of years $1, 2, \ldots, n$ and an additional $F$ at the end of year $n$. **Step 2:** Discount each of these cash flows to time $t = 0$ using a single uniform discount rate $y$: > $\text{PV of year-}t \text{ coupon} = \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^t}$ **Step 3:** Sum the present values of all coupons: > $PV_{\text{coupons}} = \dfrac{C}{(1+y)} + \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^2} + \cdots + \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^n}$ **Step 4:** Add the present value of the face value redeemed at maturity: > $PV_{\text{redemption}} = \dfrac{F}{(1+y)^n}$ **Step 5:** The market-clearing condition requires: > $P_0 = PV_{\text{coupons}} + PV_{\text{redemption}}$ Combining all steps into a single aligned equation: $$ \begin{aligned} P_0 &= \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C}{(1+y)^t} + \dfrac{F}{(1+y)^n} \\ &= C \cdot \left[ \dfrac{1 - (1+y)^{-n}}{y} \right] + \dfrac{F}{(1+y)^n} \end{aligned} $$ **Explanation of the conversion:** The summation in the first line is recognized as the **Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF)** for an ordinary annuity. This converts an infinite-looking sum into a compact closed-form expression involving the discount factor $(1+y)^{-n}$. This compact form is used when $n$ is small and direct substitution is feasible. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — YTM Approximation Formula For quick estimation (and commonly tested in KTU), an approximate YTM is derived by treating coupon income and amortized capital gain/loss as if earned on the **average price**. **Step 1:** Annualized capital gain (or loss) per year is: > $\text{Capital Gain per Year} = \dfrac{F - P_0}{n}$ **Step 2:** Total expected annual return (coupon + amortized gain) is: > $\text{Annual Return} = C + \dfrac{F - P_0}{n}$ **Step 3:** Divide by the **average price** held over the bond's life: > $\text{Average Price} = \dfrac{F + P_0}{2}$ **Step 4:** Form the ratio: > $y \approx \dfrac{C + \dfrac{F - P_0}{n}}{\dfrac{F + P_0}{2}}$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by 2 yields the more recognizable form: $$ y \approx \dfrac{2C + \dfrac{2(F - P_0)}{n}}{F + P_0} = \dfrac{C + \dfrac{(F - P_0)}{n}}{\dfrac{(F + P_0)}{2}} $$ **Why the approximation works:** The exact PV equation weighs later cash flows more lightly. The approximation assumes a **linear** weighting — the closer the bond is to par and the shorter its maturity, the more accurate this estimate becomes. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (Board Exam Standard) **Problem (KTU-Style):** A 5-year bond has a face value of **₹1,000** and an annual coupon rate of **10%**. The current market price is **₹950**. Compute: (a) Current Yield, (b) Approximate YTM, (c) Interpret the result. ### Part (a) — Current Yield Given: $C = 10\% \times 1000 = 100$, $P_0 = 950$. $$ \begin{aligned} CY &= \dfrac{C}{P_0} \times 100 \\ &= \dfrac{100}{950} \times 100 \\ &= 10.526\% \end{aligned} $$ > **Model Answer Note:** Show $C$ computation explicitly. Final answer to **two decimal places**. ### Part (b) — Approximate YTM Given: $C = 100$, $F = 1000$, $P_0 = 950$, $n = 5$. $$ \begin{aligned} y &\approx \dfrac{C + \dfrac{F - P_0}{n}}{\dfrac{F + P_0}{2}} \\ &= \dfrac{100 + \dfrac{1000 - 950}{5}}{\dfrac{1000 + 950}{2}} \\ &= \dfrac{100 + \dfrac{50}{5}}{\dfrac{1950}{2}} \\ &= \dfrac{100 + 10}{975} \\ &= \dfrac{110}{975} \\ &= 0.11282 \text{ or } 11.28\% \end{aligned} $$ ### Part (c) — Interpretation > Since the bond is trading at a **discount** ($P_0 < F$) and the coupon rate (10%) is less than the YTM (≈11.28%), the investor earns: > - **Current Yield (10.53%)** — coupon income only. > - **Capital Gain Yield (≈1.05%)** — from buying below par. > - **Total YTM (≈11.28%)** — the true effective annualized return if held to maturity. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Bond Cash-Flow Timeline (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following Mermaid block maps the **temporal cash-flow architecture** of a standard coupon bond, showing how each cash flow contributes to the YTM calculation. ```mermaid flowchart LR time0["Time 0
TODAY
Investor Pays P0"]:::startNode time1["Year 1
Receive Coupon C"]:::flowNode time2["Year 2
Receive Coupon C"]:::flowNode time3["Year 3
Receive Coupon C"]:::flowNode tdot["..."]:::dotNode tnm1["Year n-1
Receive Coupon C"]:::flowNode tnm["Year n
Coupon C PLUS Face Value F"]:::endNode time0 -->|"Cash Outflow P0"| time1 time1 -->|"Discount by 1 plus y"| time2 time2 -->|"Discount by 1 plus y"| time3 time3 -->|"..."| tdot tdot -->|"..."| tnm1 tnm1 -->|"Discount by 1 plus y raised to n-1"| tnm classDef startNode fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef flowNode fill:#e5f0ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef dotNode fill:#ffffff,stroke:#888888,stroke-width:0px,color:#888888 classDef endNode fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** Red node = investor's initial outflow. Blue nodes = periodic coupon inflows. Green node = final coupon + face-value redemption. The "Discount" annotations represent the $(1+y)^t$ exponential compounding used in the YTM present-value equation. --- ## 4.2 Decision Tree: Current Yield vs. YTM ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Bond Held to Maturity?"]:::qnode -->|"YES"| B["Use YTM"]:::ansnode A -->|"NO — Sold Before Maturity"| C["Use Holding Period Return"]:::ansnode B --> B1["YTM is single IRR"]:::subnode B --> B2["Includes Coupons PLUS Capital Gain or Loss"]:::subnode B --> B3["Assumes Reinvestment at YTM Rate"]:::subnode D["Need Quick Income Estimate?"]:::qnode -->|"YES"| E["Use Current Yield"]:::ansnode D -->|"NO — Want Total Return"| F["Use YTM"]:::ansnode E --> E1["CY is Coupon divided by Price"]:::subnode E --> E2["Ignores Capital Gain or Loss"]:::subnode E --> E3["Quick Screening Tool Only"]:::subnode classDef qnode fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ansnode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef subnode fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#666666,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** Yellow diamonds are decision points, blue boxes are the recommended metric, and grey boxes enumerate the properties of each metric. This is the **mental flowchart** examiners expect students to demonstrate in theory questions. --- ## 4.3 Bond Pricing Identity Visual Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Coupon Rate"]:::core --> B["Market Price P0"]:::core B --> C{"Compare P0 with F"}:::decision C -->|"P0 LESS THAN F
DISCOUNT"| D["YTM GREATER THAN Coupon Rate"]:::discount C -->|"P0 EQUALS F
PAR"| E["YTM EQUALS Coupon Rate"]:::par C -->|"P0 GREATER THAN F
PREMIUM"| F["YTM LESS THAN Coupon Rate"]:::premium classDef core fill:#e0e0ff,stroke:#333399,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#ff6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef discount fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef par fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef premium fill:#ccccff,stroke:#0000cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** This visualization anchors the **three-way bond pricing identity** — a frequently tested relationship in KTU exams. The three color-coded terminal states (red/green/blue) match the discount/par/premium scenarios. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (Conceptual) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define **Yield to Maturity (YTM)** of a bond. State any **two assumptions** underlying its computation. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (2 marks):** Yield to Maturity is the internal rate of return earned by an investor who purchases a bond at its current market price and holds it until maturity. It is the single discount rate that equates the present value of all future coupon payments and the redemption value to the current market price of the bond. > > $$\boxed{P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+y)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+y)^n}}$$ > > **Assumptions (1 mark for any two):** > 1. The bond is held **till maturity**. > 2. All coupon payments are **reinvested at the YTM rate**. > 3. The **issuer does not default** on interest or principal payments. --- ### Question 2 (Distinguishing) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Pattern]** Distinguish between **Current Yield** and **Yield to Maturity** of a bond. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Basis | Current Yield | Yield to Maturity | |---|---|---| | **Meaning** | Annual coupon income as % of current market price | Total annualized return if held till maturity | | **Capital Gain** | **Ignored** | **Included** | | **Formula** | $CY = C / P_0$ | Solved from $P_0 = \sum C/(1+y)^t + F/(1+y)^n$ | | **Use** | Quick screening | Comprehensive comparison | > **Awarding Marks:** 1 mark for any two distinguishing points. 1 mark for the formula-based distinction. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Yield/YTM Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting to multiply** coupon rate by face value to get $C$ — silent but fatal mark-loss (–2 marks). > 2. **Mixing up** Current Yield formula $(C / P_0)$ with YTM formula — they are **not interchangeable** (–3 marks). > 3. **In approximation formula**, placing $F - P_0$ in the denominator as $F \cdot P_0$ (a common algebraic slip) (–1 mark). > 4. **Not stating units** (% vs decimal) — examiners may deduct 0.5–1 mark for ambiguity. > 5. **Skipping the interpretation** — KTU specifically awards 1–2 marks for "commenting on" the computed value (par/discount/premium insight). --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2, 14 Marks]** A bond has a **face value of ₹1,000**, an **annual coupon rate of 12%**, and a **remaining maturity of 6 years**. The current market price is **₹1,080**. **(a)** Compute the **Current Yield** and the **approximate Yield to Maturity (YTM)**. [7 Marks] **(b)** State and prove the **bond pricing identity** relationship between price, coupon rate, and YTM for the three cases: discount, par, and premium. [7 Marks] #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Given:** $F = 1000$, $r_c = 12\%$, $n = 6$, $P_0 = 1080$. **Step 1:** Annual coupon $C = r_c \times F = 0.12 \times 1000 = ₹120$. **Step 2:** Current Yield $$ \begin{aligned} CY &= \dfrac{C}{P_0} \times 100 \\ &= \dfrac{120}{1080} \times 100 \\ &= 11.11\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Approximate YTM. Since $P_0 > F$, this is a **premium bond**. $$ \begin{aligned} y &\approx \dfrac{C + \dfrac{F - P_0}{n}}{\dfrac{F + P_0}{2}} \\ &= \dfrac{120 + \dfrac{1000 - 1080}{6}}{\dfrac{1000 + 1080}{2}} \\ &= \dfrac{120 + \dfrac{-80}{6}}{1040} \\ &= \dfrac{120 - 13.33}{1040} \\ &= \dfrac{106.67}{1040} \\ &= 0.10256 \text{ or } 10.26\% \end{aligned} $$ > **Final Answers (1 mark each for stating):** $CY = 11.11\%$; $YTM \approx 10.26\%$. **Interpretation (1 mark):** Since YTM (10.26%) < Coupon Rate (12%), the bond is trading at a **premium**, consistent with the price above par. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Statement (2 marks):** > For any bond, the relationship between its market price, face value, coupon rate, and YTM follows the bond pricing identity: > - If $P_0 < F \Rightarrow$ YTM > Coupon Rate (Discount bond) > - If $P_0 = F \Rightarrow$ YTM = Coupon Rate (Par bond) > - If $P_0 > F \Rightarrow$ YTM < Coupon Rate (Premium bond) **Proof (5 marks):** Start from the YTM definition: $$P_0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{C}{(1+y)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+y)^n}$$ **Case 1: Par Bond.** Let $P_0 = F$ and $y = r_c$. Substituting $C = r_c \cdot F$: $$ \begin{aligned} F &= \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{r_c F}{(1+r_c)^t} + \frac{F}{(1+r_c)^n} \\ \frac{F}{F} &= r_c \cdot PVAF(r_c, n) + (1+r_c)^{-n} \\ 1 &= r_c \cdot \left[\frac{1-(1+r_c)^{-n}}{r_c}\right] + (1+r_c)^{-n} \\ 1 &= 1 - (1+r_c)^{-n} + (1+r_c)^{-n} \\ 1 &= 1 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ Hence, $y = r_c$ is a solution. **(2 marks for verification)** **Case 2: Discount Bond ($P_0 < F$).** Assume $y < r_c$. The discount factor $(1+y)^{-t}$ is **larger** for all $t$, making the right-hand side of the YTM equation **larger** than the face value $F$. For the equality to hold at a lower $P_0$, we require $y > r_c$. **(2 marks for intuitive argument)** **Case 3: Premium Bond ($P_0 > F$).** By symmetry of Case 2, if $P_0 > F$, then $y < r_c$. **(1 mark for concluding statement)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative **[KTU University Exam — Alternative Module 2 Question]** A **5-year bond** with a **face value of ₹5,000** carries an **annual coupon of 8%**. It is currently selling for **₹4,600**. **(a)** Calculate the **Current Yield** and the **approximate YTM**. Interpret why YTM exceeds the coupon rate. [7 Marks] **(b)** A **3-year zero-coupon bond** has a face value of **₹1,000** and a current market price of **₹863.84**. Compute its **exact YTM** using the present-value equation. [7 Marks] #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Given:** $F = 5000$, $r_c = 8\%$, $n = 5$, $P_0 = 4600$. **Step 1:** $C = 0.08 \times 5000 = ₹400$. **Step 2:** Current Yield $$ \begin{aligned} CY &= \dfrac{400}{4600} \times 100 = 8.70\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Approximate YTM $$ \begin{aligned} y &\approx \dfrac{400 + \dfrac{5000 - 4600}{5}}{\dfrac{5000 + 4600}{2}} \\ &= \dfrac{400 + \dfrac{400}{5}}{4800} \\ &= \dfrac{400 + 80}{4800} \\ &= \dfrac{480}{4800} \\ &= 0.10 \text{ or } 10.00\% \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation (2 marks):** YTM (10%) > Coupon Rate (8%) because the bond is trading at a **discount** ($P_0 = 4600 < F = 5000$). The investor earns an additional 2% in annualized capital gains (₹400/5 years spread over ₹4,600), pushing the total effective return above the coupon rate. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $F = 1000$, $P_0 = 863.84$, $n = 3$, $C = 0$ (zero-coupon). **Step 1:** For a zero-coupon bond, the YTM equation simplifies to: $$P_0 = \frac{F}{(1+y)^n}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute known values: $$863.84 = \frac{1000}{(1+y)^3}$$ **Step 3:** Solve for $(1+y)^3$: $$(1+y)^3 = \frac{1000}{863.84} = 1.15762$$ **Step 4:** Take the cube root: $$ \begin{aligned} 1 + y &= (1.15762)^{1/3} \\ &= 1.05 \\ y &= 0.05 \text{ or } 5\% \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $YTM = 5.00\%$ per annum. **(1 mark for the boxed answer)** > **Verification (Optional):** $PV = 1000 / (1.05)^3 = 1000 / 1.157625 = 863.84$ ✓ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Concept of Yield and YTM"** - [ ] **Current Yield** = (Annual Coupon / Current Market Price) × 100 — measures **income return only**. - [ ] **YTM** is the **IRR** of the bond — single discount rate that equates $P_0$ to the PV of all future cash flows. - [ ] **Bond Pricing Identity:** Discount $\Rightarrow$ YTM > Coupon Rate; Par $\Rightarrow$ YTM = Coupon Rate; Premium $\Rightarrow$ YTM < Coupon Rate. - [ ] **Exact YTM equation** uses PVAF: $P_0 = C \cdot PVAF(y, n) + F \cdot (1+y)^{-n}$. - [ ] **Approximation formula** is valid for small $n$ and prices near par: $y \approx \dfrac{C + (F - P_0)/n}{(F + P_0)/2}$. - [ ] **YTM assumes reinvestment** of all coupons at the YTM rate itself. - [ ] **Zero-coupon bonds** have $C = 0$ — only redemption value is discounted: $P_0 = F / (1+y)^n$. - [ ] **YTM is the bond's effective borrowing cost** for the issuer and effective lending return for the investor. - [ ] **YTM is always positive** for a non-defaulted bond with positive price and positive coupons. - [ ] For exam **numerical problems**: always state $C$ explicitly, use consistent decimal/percentage convention, and conclude with an **interpretation line**. > 🎯 **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** When KTU asks "compare and contrast" Current Yield vs. YTM, structure the answer as a **3-row mini-table** (Meaning, Formula, Use). Examiners reward visual clarity with full marks.

topics.title: Risk and Return: Defining Risk and Return
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Risk and Return: Defining Risk and Return

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining Return

In financial management, **Return** is the quantifiable gain or loss generated on an investment over a specified period, expressed both in absolute monetary terms and as a percentage of the initial investment outlay. The KTU 2024 Scheme treats return as the **reward** component that investors demand in exchange for postponing current consumption and committing capital to a productive asset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Adopt Verbatim):**
> *Return is the expectation of reward which induces investors to undertake investment. It is the compensation paid to the investor for undertaking the risk of holding an asset.*

### 1.1.1 Types of Return

| Return Type | Description | Typical Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Holding Period Return (HPR)** | Total return earned over the entire period an asset is held | Capital gains + Income |
| **Expected Return $\bar{R}$** | Probability-weighted average of all possible returns | Mean of probability distribution |
| **Required Return** | Minimum return demanded by investors for a given risk level | CAPM, WACC, market data |
| **Realized Return** | Actual return earned in a past period | Historical observation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Memory Anchor:** Return is always expressed as a **rate** (percentage), not as an absolute money value, when comparing different investment alternatives.

---

## 1.2 Defining Risk

**Risk** in financial management is the **variability** or **uncertainty** associated with the actual return of an investment deviating from its expected return. KTU 2024 framework defines risk as the *chance that the actual outcome will differ from the expected outcome*, where unfavourable deviations represent potential losses to the investor.

$$Risk = f(Volatility,\ Uncertainty,\ Probability\ of\ Loss)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Adopt Verbatim):**
> *Risk is the possibility that the actual return from an investment will differ from the expected return. The greater the chance of such deviation, the greater the risk.*

### 1.2.1 Risk vs. Uncertainty — The KTU Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Risk** → Future outcomes are *known* and can be assigned *objective probabilities* (e.g., dice rolls, historical stock data). **Measurable.**
> - **Uncertainty** → Future outcomes are *unknown* and probabilities *cannot be assigned* with confidence. **Not directly measurable.**

### 1.2.2 Components of Total Risk (For Individual Securities)

$$Total\ Risk = Systematic\ Risk\ (Market\ Risk) + Unsystematic\ Risk\ (Diversifiable\ Risk)$$

- **Systematic Risk** — Market-wide risk arising from macroeconomic, political, or interest rate factors. **Cannot be eliminated** through diversification.
- **Unsystematic Risk** — Firm-specific risk arising from management quality, product failure, or labour issues. **Can be reduced/eliminated** through portfolio diversification.

---

## 1.3 🧠 Intuitive Overview — The "Farmer's Monsoon" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine two farmers, **Anand** and **Biju**, both planting rice in Kerala.
> - **Anand** plants a **high-yielding hybrid variety** (HYV) that promises a huge harvest of 50 tonnes if the monsoon is perfect, but only 5 tonnes if there is a drought. He also invests in **irrigation pumps** (diversification).
> - **Biju** plants a **traditional variety** that consistently gives 25 tonnes, rain or shine.
>
> **Anand's expected harvest is higher (Return ↑)**, but his outcome swings wildly between 5 and 50 tonnes (Risk ↑). **Biju's harvest is lower (Return ↓), but predictable (Risk ↓).**
>
> The **trade-off** between the *average reward* (return) and the *swing of outcomes* (risk) is the central theme of financial management. Investors demand higher return *only because* they are bearing higher risk.

### 1.4 The Risk-Return Trade-off Principle

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** *Higher the risk, higher the required return — but not vice-versa.* Simply offering higher return does not mean an investment is less risky; it usually means it is **more** risky.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Measurement of Return — Step-by-Step Logic

The total return from holding an asset is composed of **two cash flow components**:

1. **Periodic Income (Cash Flows)** — Dividends, interest, rentals, coupons received during the holding period.
2. **Capital Gain (Loss)** — Difference between the **selling price** at the end of the period and the **purchase price** at the beginning.

### 2.1.1 Holding Period Return (HPR) — Single Period

$$HPR = \frac{(Ending\ Price - Beginning\ Price) + Income}{Beginning\ Price}$$

### 2.1.2 Holding Period Return — Multi-Period (Compounded)

$$HPR_{multi} = \left[\prod_{t=1}^{n}\left(1 + R_{t}\right)\right] - 1$$

### 2.1.3 Expected Return Formula

The **Expected Return** is the probability-weighted mean of all possible returns:

$$\bar{R} = E(R) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{i} \cdot R_{i}$$

Where:
- $P_{i}$ = Probability of occurrence of the $i^{th}$ return
- $R_{i}$ = Return in the $i^{th}$ scenario
- $\sum P_{i} = 1$

---

## 2.2 Measurement of Risk — Statistical Framework

Risk is quantified using **dispersion measures** that capture how far actual returns deviate from the expected return.

### 2.2.1 Standard Deviation (σ) — Absolute Risk Measure

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{i} \cdot (R_{i} - \bar{R})^{2}}$$

- **Logic**: Square deviations to eliminate sign, multiply by probability, sum, and take square root.
- **Higher σ → Higher risk** (greater dispersion around the mean).

### 2.2.2 Variance (σ²) — Squared Form

$$\sigma^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{i} \cdot (R_{i} - \bar{R})^{2}$$

- Variance is the square of standard deviation; used in intermediate calculations.

### 2.2.3 Coefficient of Variation (CV) — Relative Risk Measure

$$CV = \frac{\sigma}{\bar{R}}$$

- **Used when comparing two assets with different expected returns** or different units.
- **Lower CV** indicates *better risk-return efficiency*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap Alert:** Never use standard deviation alone to compare a stock with an expected return of 5% and a stock with an expected return of 50%. Use **Coefficient of Variation**.

### 2.2.4 Covariance — Joint Risk Between Two Assets

$$Cov(R_{A}, R_{B}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{i} \cdot [R_{A,i} - \bar{R}_{A}][R_{B,i} - \bar{R}_{B}]$$

- **Positive Cov** → Returns move together.
- **Negative Cov** → Returns move in opposite directions (diversification benefit).

### 2.2.5 Correlation Coefficient (ρ)
