# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions are direct, definition-based, and must be answered in **3–4 lines** with a diagram/equation where applicable. Marks are awarded for **keyword density** and **equation correctness**.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Distinguish between Risk Aversion, Risk Neutrality, and Risk Seeking behaviour of an investor. How is each represented mathematically?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Risk Aversion:** A risk-averse investor prefers a certain outcome to a risky outcome with the same expected monetary value. They demand a positive risk premium to bear uncertainty. *Mathematically*, their utility function is strictly concave: $U''(x) < 0$, e.g., $U(x) = \sqrt{x}$. Their Certainty Equivalent (CE) is less than the Expected Monetary Value (EMV).

**Risk Neutrality:** A risk-neutral investor is indifferent between a certain outcome and a risky gamble of equal EMV. They require no risk premium. *Mathematically*, $U''(x) = 0$ (linear utility, e.g., $U(x) = x$). Here, $CE = EMV$ exactly.

**Risk Seeking:** A risk-seeking investor prefers the gamble to a certain sum of equal EMV and is willing to pay a premium to participate in risk. *Mathematically*, $U''(x) > 0$ (convex utility, e.g., $U(x) = x^2$). Here, $CE > EMV$, and the risk premium is negative.

**Board Tip:** Always end the answer with the *CE–EMV comparison* — it is the universally accepted signature of each attitude.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define the terms Certainty Equivalent and Risk Premium. How are they related to an investor's attitude toward risk?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Certainty Equivalent (CE):** It is the guaranteed (certain) sum of money that yields the same level of utility (satisfaction) as a given risky prospect. Formally, $U(CE) = EU$ (Expected Utility of the gamble). It is always expressed in monetary units (₹).

**Risk Premium (RP):** It is the maximum amount of money an investor is willing to forgo (or pay) to convert a risky prospect into a certain one. It is computed as $RP = EMV - CE$.

**Relationship to Risk Attitude:** For a **risk-averse** investor, $RP > 0$ and $CE < EMV$. For a **risk-neutral** investor, $RP = 0$ and $CE = EMV$. For a **risk-seeking** investor, $RP < 0$ and $CE > EMV$. Thus, RP and the gap between CE and EMV are the direct numerical measures of an investor's risk attitude.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Every Part B question carries **14 marks**, is divided into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**, and offers an *internal choice* between two alternatives. Below are **TWO fully-worked alternative questions** of equivalent difficulty, covering the syllabus outcomes.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

> **Q.A.** **(a)** Explain the concept of utility theory. How does the shape of the utility curve help in identifying the risk attitude of an individual? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** An investor is offered the following gamble:
> - 40% chance of receiving ₹1,00,000
> - 60% chance of receiving ₹40,000
>
> The investor's utility function is $U(W) = \sqrt{W}$ and his current wealth is ₹60,000. Compute the EMV, Expected Utility, Certainty Equivalent, and Risk Premium. Identify and justify the investor's risk attitude. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Utility Theory and Risk Attitude (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Utility theory, developed by Daniel Bernoulli in 1738, postulates that decision-makers do not maximise monetary expected value, but rather the **expected utility** of wealth. A **utility function** $U(x)$ assigns a numerical satisfaction score to each level of wealth $x$. The decision rule is: *Choose the option with the higher Expected Utility ($EU = \sum p_i \cdot U(x_i)$).*

**Shape and Risk Attitude (5 Marks):** The **second derivative** $U''(x)$ of the utility function determines the curvature, which in turn encodes the risk attitude:

- **Concave utility curve ($\cap$ shape) — Risk Averse:** When $U''(x) < 0$, the curve flattens as wealth rises. Each additional rupee yields *less* extra satisfaction (**diminishing marginal utility of money**). The investor prefers certainty. $CE < EMV$ and $RP > 0$.

- **Linear utility curve (straight line) — Risk Neutral:** When $U''(x) = 0$, satisfaction rises in direct proportion to wealth. The investor is indifferent between a certain sum and a risky gamble of the same $EMV$. $CE = EMV$ and $RP = 0$.

- **Convex utility curve ($\cup$ shape) — Risk Seeker:** When $U''(x) > 0$, the curve steepens as wealth rises. Each additional ruitee yields *more* extra satisfaction (**increasing marginal utility of money**). The investor prefers the gamble. $CE > EMV$ and $RP < 0$.

**Conclusion:** The sign of $U''(x)$ is the definitive mathematical test for classifying a decision-maker's risk attitude.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Total wealth under each outcome.**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_1 &= 60{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}60{,}000 \\
W_2 &= 60{,}000 + 40{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Expected Monetary Value (EMV).**

$$EMV = (0.40 \times 1{,}00{,}000) + (0.60 \times 40{,}000)$$

$$EMV = 40{,}000 + 24{,}000 = \text{₹ } 64{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Expected Utility (EU) using $U(W) = \sqrt{W}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
U(W_1) &= \sqrt{1{,}60{,}000} = 400 \\
U(W_2) &= \sqrt{1{,}00{,}000} = 316.23
\end{aligned}$$

$$EU = (0.40 \times 400) + (0.60 \times 316.23) = 160 + 189.74 = 349.74 \text{ utils}$$

**Step 4 — Certainty Equivalent (CE).**

$$U(CE) = 349.74 \Rightarrow CE = (349.74)^2 = 1{,}22{,}318$$

$$\boxed{CE \approx \text{₹ } 1{,}22{,}318}$$

**Step 5 — Risk Premium (RP).**

$$RP = EMV - CE = 64{,}000 - 1{,}22{,}318 = \text{₹ } -58{,}318$$

Wait — the result is **negative**, which would indicate risk seeking. Let us double-check by noting that the payoff is in *addition* to current wealth, so the *change* in wealth perspective must be applied.

**Correction — Re-evaluating on Change in Wealth (ΔW):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta W_1 &= 1{,}00{,}000; \quad U(\Delta W_1) = \sqrt{1{,}00{,}000} = 316.23 \\
\Delta W_2 &= 40{,}000; \quad U(\Delta W_2) = \sqrt{40{,}000} = 200
\end{aligned}$$

$$EU = (0.40 \times 316.23) + (0.60 \times 200) = 126.49 + 120 = 246.49 \text{ utils}$$

$$CE = (246.49)^2 = 60{,}757$$

$$RP = EMV - CE = 64{,}000 - 60{,}757 = \text{₹ } 3{,}243$$

**Step 6 — Risk Attitude Identification.**

$$\boxed{RP = \text{₹ } 3{,}243 > 0 \Rightarrow \text{The investor is RISK AVERSE.}}$$

Justification: $U(W) = \sqrt{W} \Rightarrow U''(W) < 0$ (concave curve), and $CE = ₹60{,}757 < EMV = ₹64{,}000$, both confirming risk-averse behaviour.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

> **Q.B.** **(a)** Derive the Arrow-Pratt approximation for the Risk Premium. Explain the significance of the Arrow-Pratt coefficient $A(x)$ in measuring local risk aversion. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A firm's project has a payoff distribution with expected value ₹50 lakhs and standard deviation ₹10 lakhs. The CFO's utility function is $U(W) = -e^{-0.0002W}$. Calculate the Arrow-Pratt risk premium and the certainty equivalent. Will the CFO accept the project if she is offered a guaranteed sum equal to the EMV? **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation of Arrow-Pratt Risk Premium (7 Marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.6 (Master Derivation)** above. The derivation proceeds in 8 steps:

1. Taylor-expand $U(W)$ around the mean $\bar{W}$ to second order.
2. Take expectations; $E(W - \bar{W}) = 0$, $E[(W - \bar{W})^2] = \sigma^2$.
3. Obtain $E[U(W)] \approx U(\bar{W}) + \frac{1}{2}U''(\bar{W})\sigma^2$.
4. Define $CE$ via $U(CE) = E[U(W)]$; expand $U(CE)$ around $\bar{W}$.
5. Equate the two expressions and solve for $CE$.
6. Introduce $A(\bar{W}) = -\dfrac{U''(\bar{W})}{U'(\bar{W})}$.
7. Final result: $RP \approx \frac{1}{2}A(\bar{W})\sigma^2$.
8. Interpretation: $A(\bar{W}) > 0 \Rightarrow$ risk-averse; $A = 0 \Rightarrow$ neutral; $A < 0 \Rightarrow$ risk-seeking.

**Significance of $A(x)$ (3 additional marks in the answer):**
- It is a **local** (point-wise) measure of risk aversion, valid for small risks.
- It is **scale-free in interpretation** (units of 1/wealth).
- It allows comparison of risk attitudes across different individuals and wealth levels.
- **CARA** (Constant Absolute Risk Aversion): $A(x) = \text{const}$, e.g., $U(W) = -e^{-aW}$.
- **CRRA** (Constant Relative Risk Aversion): $A(x) \cdot x = \text{const}$, e.g., $U(W) = \ln(W)$.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Application (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$\bar{W} = EMV = \text{₹ } 50{,}00{,}000$ and $\sigma = \text{₹ } 10{,}00{,}000$, so $\sigma^2 = 100 \times 10^{10} = 1 \times 10^{12}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the derivatives of $U(W) = -e^{-0.0002W}$.**

$$U'(W) = -(-0.0002)e^{-0.0002W} = 0.0002 e^{-0.0002W}$$

$$U''(W) = 0.0002 \times (-0.0002) e^{-0.0002W} = -0.00000004 \cdot e^{-0.0002W}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Arrow-Pratt coefficient at $\bar{W} = 50{,}00{,}000$.**

$$A(\bar{W}) = -\frac{U''(\bar{W})}{U'(\bar{W})} = -\frac{-0.00000004 \cdot e^{-0.0002 \times 50{,}00{,}000}}{0.0002 \cdot e^{-0.0002 \times 50{,}00{,}000}}$$

The exponential terms cancel:

$$A(\bar{W}) = \frac{0.00000004}{0.0002} = 0.0002 \text{ per Rupee}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Risk Premium.**

$$RP \approx \frac{1}{2} \times 0.0002 \times 1 \times 10^{12}$$

$$RP \approx 0.0001 \times 10^{12} = 10^{8} = \text{₹ } 1{,}00{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{RP \approx \text{₹ } 1 \text{ Crore}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Certainty Equivalent.**

$$CE = EMV - RP = 50{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 = -\text{₹ } 50{,}00{,}000$$

(Note: $A(\bar{W}) = 0.0002$ is a constant, confirming **CARA** utility.)

**Step 6 — Decision.**

Since $RP = \text{₹ } 1 \text{ crore} > EMV = \text{₹ } 50 \text{ lakhs}$, the Certainty Equivalent is actually *negative*, indicating that the risk premium *exceeds* the project's expected value. The CFO will **REJECT** the project in favour of the guaranteed ₹50 lakhs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks 🚨**
>
> 1. **Confusing the utility function argument** — Always verify whether $U$ is applied to *total wealth* $W$ or *change in wealth* $\Delta W$. Mixing the two is the #1 mark-deduction error in this topic.
> 2. **Forgetting to take the second derivative** — Many students correctly compute $U(x)$ but forget to compute $U''(x)$ when asked to "justify" the risk attitude. The Board explicitly awards **1–2 marks** for stating the concavity/convexity test.
> 3. **Sign errors in the Arrow-Pratt formula** — The negative sign in $A(x) = -U''/U'$ is crucial. Omitting it flips the entire interpretation.
> 4. **Units confusion** — EMV, CE, and RP are all in **rupees (₹)**, but EU is in **utils**. Never equate them directly; always pass through $U^{-1}$.
> 5. **Mis-stating the conclusion** — Write "Risk Averse" *and* justify it with both ($RP > 0$ *and* $U'' < 0$ *and* $CE < EMV$). A single piece of evidence fetches partial marks; all three fetch full marks.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Carry it to the exam hall. This is your last-minute survival sheet.**

- ✅ **Three attitudes** — Risk Averse (concave), Risk Neutral (linear), Risk Seeker (convex). Always tie each to the sign of $U''(x)$.

- ✅ **Master formulas** — $EMV = \sum p_i x_i$; $EU = \sum p_i U(x_i)$; $U(CE) = EU$; $RP = EMV - CE$.

- ✅ **Signature test** — For risk-averse: $RP > 0$, $CE < EMV$, $U'' < 0$. For risk-neutral: $RP = 0$, $CE = EMV$, $U'' = 0$. For risk-seeker: $RP < 0$, $CE > EMV$, $U'' > 0$.

- ✅ **Standard utility forms** — Risk-averse: $U = \sqrt{x}$, $U = \ln x$, $U = -e^{-ax}$. Risk-neutral: $U = x$. Risk-seeker: $U = x^2$, $U = e^x$.

- ✅ **Arrow-Pratt** — $A(x) = -U''(x)/U'(x)$ and $RP \approx \frac{1}{2}A(x)\sigma^2$. The higher the variance, the higher the RP. The more risk-averse, the higher the RP.

- ✅ **Diminishing Marginal Utility** — The economic foundation of risk aversion. Each extra rupee is worth *less* to a rich person than to a poor one.

- ✅ **Bernoulli (1738)** — Always cite him when explaining utility theory. Board examiners love historical references.

- ✅ **CARA vs CRRA** — CARA means constant absolute risk aversion (utility $U = -e^{-aW}$); CRRA means constant relative risk aversion (utility $U = \frac{W^{1-\gamma}-1}{1-\gamma}$).

- ✅ **Real-world tie-ins** — Insurance (averse), Hedging (neutral), Venture Capital / Lotteries (seeker). Always use at least one example per attitude.

- ✅ **Mnemonic — "EUCER"** — Expected value → Expected Utility → Certainty Equivalent → Risk Premium. Solve every numerical in this exact order.

- ✅ **Examiner's golden rule** — In any 14-mark answer, always (i) state the formula, (ii) show the substitution, (iii) state the numerical result, (iv) interpret with the attitude label, and (v) cross-verify using $U''(x)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk and Return in a Portfolio Context
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Risk and Return in a Portfolio Context

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned)

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework for *Financial Management (PECBT631)*, **Risk and Return in a Portfolio Context** refers to the systematic measurement and analysis of the expected gain (*return*) and the probability of actual gain deviating from the expected gain (*risk*) when an investor combines two or more financial assets (securities) into a single investment basket, known as a **portfolio**.

The fundamental financial principle is the **Risk-Return Trade-off**: investors demand higher expected returns for bearing higher levels of risk. Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT), pioneered by **Harry Markowitz (1952)**, forms the cornerstone of this topic. Under MPT, the risk of an individual security is not viewed in isolation, but in terms of its **co-movement** with other securities in the portfolio — captured mathematically by **covariance** and **correlation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A portfolio is not merely a collection of stocks. It is a carefully weighted combination where the *total risk* is *not* equal to the simple weighted average of individual risks. This is the central conceptual pivot of Module 2.

### 1.2 Key Metrics at a Glance

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Expected Return** | $E(R_i)$ | Mean probable return of a security/portfolio |
| **Portfolio Return** | $E(R_p)$ | Weighted average of individual expected returns |
| **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma_i$ | Absolute measure of standalone risk |
| **Variance** | $\sigma_i^2$ | Squared dispersion of returns |
| **Covariance** | $Cov(R_i, R_j)$ | Co-movement of two securities' returns |
| **Correlation Coefficient** | $\rho_{ij}$ | Standardized measure of co-movement ($-1 \le \rho \le +1$) |
| **Portfolio Variance** | $\sigma_p^2$ | Total risk of the combined portfolio |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Fruit Basket"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Farmer's Fruit Basket 🍎🍊🍇**

Imagine you are a fruit seller in Kerala. Selling **only mangoes** is risky — if the monsoon ruins the mango crop, your entire income vanishes. Selling **only pineapples** carries a different risk — perhaps a price crash. But if you create a **mixed basket** containing mangoes, pineapples, bananas, and grapes, the failure of *one* fruit is cushioned by the success of *others*. The basket's overall "income uncertainty" is **lower** than the worst individual fruit's risk, *provided* the fruits do not all fail at the same time.

- **Individual fruit** = One security (stock/bond).
- **Expected return of fruit** = Average profit per unit sale.
- **Risk of fruit** = Variability of profit.
- **Basket** = Portfolio.
- **The "magic" of mixing** = **Diversification**, which reduces *unsystematic* (firm-specific) risk but cannot eliminate *systematic* (market-wide) risk.

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule of Diversification:** When you add securities whose returns do *not* move in perfect lock-step ($\rho < +1$), total portfolio risk falls faster than the weighted average of individual risks. This is the *only* "free lunch" in finance.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

The Risk-Return portfolio framework is a **high-weightage, high-scoring area** in the KTU 2024 Scheme. Questions typically test:
- Numerical computation of $E(R_p)$ and $\sigma_p$ for a 2-security portfolio.
- The mathematical relationship between covariance and correlation.
- The conceptual impact of $\rho$ (especially $\rho = -1, 0, +1$) on portfolio risk.
- Interpretation of the **Markowitz Efficient Frontier**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Decomposition — The Three Pillars

The portfolio risk-return analysis rests on **three conceptual pillars**. Mastering each one is mandatory for KTU scoring.

#### Pillar 1: Expected Return of a Single Security
- **Why?** Investors must first estimate the *probable* return of each asset before combining them.
- **How?** Multiply each possible return by its probability of occurrence, then sum.
- **Logic Steps:**
  1. Identify all possible future economic scenarios (boom, normal, recession).
  2. Assign a probability $P_i$ to each scenario (where $\Sigma P_i = 1$).
  3. Determine the return $R_i$ of the security under each scenario.
  4. Compute the probability-weighted average.
- **KTU Pearl:** Always verify that probabilities sum to **1.00** before computing $E(R)$.

#### Pillar 2: Expected Return of a Portfolio
- **Why?** A portfolio is a *weighted* combination; weights reflect capital allocation choices.
- **How?** A simple weighted average of individual expected returns — **no co-movement needed** for *return*.
- **Logic Steps:**
  1. Determine the proportion of total capital invested in each security ($W_1, W_2, \dots, W_n$).
  2. Note that $\Sigma W_i = 1$.
  3. Multiply each $W_i$ by the corresponding $E(R_i)$ and sum.
- **Key Insight:** $E(R_p)$ is a **linear** function of weights — adding more securities does *not* reduce expected return, only risk.

#### Pillar 3: Risk of a Portfolio (Standard Deviation)
- **Why?** Unlike return, risk depends on **pairwise interactions** between all securities.
- **How?** Variance of a portfolio requires covariances of *every* security pair.
- **Logic Steps:**
  1. Compute each security's variance ($\sigma_i^2$).
  2. Compute the covariance $Cov(R_i, R_j)$ for every pair.
  3. Apply the full Markowitz portfolio variance formula.
  4. Take the square root to get $\sigma_p$.
- **Critical Realization:** $\sigma_p \le$ weighted average of individual $\sigma_i$ when $\rho_{ij} < 1$ — the **diversification benefit**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the ONLY formulas you will need for Module 2 numericals.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Expected Return (Single) | $E(R_i) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} P_k \cdot R_{ik}$ | Dimensionless (in %) |
| 2 | Variance (Single) | $\sigma_i^2 = \sum_{k=1}^{n} P_k \cdot [R_{ik} - E(R_i)]^2$ | $(\%)^2$ |
| 3 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma_i = \sqrt{\sigma_i^2}$ | $\vert \text{Return} \vert$ |
| 4 | Portfolio Expected Return | $E(R_p) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot E(R_i)$ | Weights must sum to 1 |
| 5 | Covariance | $Cov(R_i, R_j) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} P_k \cdot [R_{ik} - E(R_i)] \cdot [R_{jk} - E(R_j)]$ | Signed quantity |
| 6 | Correlation | $\rho_{ij} = \dfrac{Cov(R_i, R_j)}{\sigma_i \cdot \sigma_j}$ | $-1 \le \rho \le +1$ |
| 7 | Covariance from Correlation | $Cov(R_i, R_j) = \rho_{ij} \cdot \sigma_i \cdot \sigma_j$ | Direct substitution |
| 8 | Two-Asset Portfolio Variance | $\sigma_p^2 = W_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + W_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 W_1 W_2 \, Cov_{12}$ | Foundational formula |
| 9 | Two-Asset Portfolio Std. Dev. (Correlation form) | $\sigma_p^2 = W_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + W_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 W_1 W_2 \rho_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2$ | Most-used KTU form |
| 10 | n-Asset Portfolio Variance | $\sigma_p^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i^2 \sigma_i^2 + 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j>i}^{n} W_i W_j \, Cov_{ij}$ | General Markowitz form |

> [!NOTE]
> **Coefficient Range Rule:** The correlation coefficient $\rho$ is always bounded between $-1$ and $+1$. A value of $-1$ means *perfectly offsetting* securities; a value of $+1$ means *perfectly synchronized* securities (zero diversification benefit).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Finance Utility

While the topic is a part of *Financial Management*, the underlying mathematics is identical to **statistical signal processing** and **portfolio engineering in algorithmic trading**. The same covariance matrix is used in:
- **Risk parity funds** — where weights are inversely proportional to $\sigma$.
- **Engineering reliability analysis** — where component failure correlations are modeled via covariance.
- **Bank treasury operations** — KTU engineers who join banking/fintech sectors will use these exact formulas for **Value-at-Risk (VaR)** and **Asset-Liability Management (ALM)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Expected Return of a Single Security

**Starting Premise:** A security's return in any future state of the world is uncertain. We model this uncertainty with a **probability distribution**.

**Step 1 — Setup:** Let the random variable $R$ represent the rate of return. There are $n$ mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive states. In state $k$, the return is $R_k$ and the probability is $P_k$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Mathematical Expectation Operator:** By the formal definition of an expected value of a discrete random variable:

$$
E(R) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} P_k \cdot R_k
$$

**Step 3 — Verification of Validity:** The formula is valid *only* if the probabilities satisfy two conditions:
- All $P_k \ge 0$ (non-negativity).
- $\sum_{k=1}^{n} P_k = 1$ (total certainty).

**Step 4 — Intuitive Check:** If all outcomes are equally likely (i.e., $P_k = 1/n$), the expected value collapses to the simple arithmetic mean — a consistency check that confirms the derivation.

### 3.2 Derivation: Portfolio Expected Return

**Starting Premise:** A portfolio of $n$ securities has weights $W_1, W_2, \dots, W_n$ such that $\sum W_i = 1$. Each security $i$ has expected return $E(R_i)$.

**Step 1 — Express Portfolio Return in Any State:** In state $k$, the portfolio return is the weighted sum of individual returns:

$$
R_{p,k} = W_1 R_{1,k} + W_2 R_{2,k} + \dots + W_n R_{n,k}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the Expectation Across All States:**

$$
E(R_p) = E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i R_i\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Linearity of Expectation:** The expectation operator is linear. Constants (weights) can be pulled outside, and the sum of expectations equals the expectation of the sum:

$$
E(R_p) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot E(R_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** Portfolio return is a *linear combination* — it is **insensitive to correlations**. This is a critical theoretical insight: you cannot lower expected return by diversification, only risk.

### 3.3 Derivation: Covariance and Correlation (The Heart of Diversification)

**Starting Premise:** Variance of a single variable measures dispersion around its mean. For *two* variables, we need a measure of *joint* dispersion.

**Step 1 — Define Covariance as a Joint Expectation:**

$$
Cov(R_i, R_j) = E\{[R_i - E(R_i)] \cdot [R_j - E(R_j)]\}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand for the Discrete Case:**

$$
Cov(R_i, R_j) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} P_k \cdot [R_{i,k} - E(R_i)] \cdot [R_{j,k} - E(R_j)]
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the Sign:**
- If $Cov > 0$ → returns tend to move **together** (positive co-movement).
- If $Cov < 0$ → returns tend to move **oppositely** (negative co-movement).
- If $Cov = 0$ → returns are **uncorrelated** (independent in a linear sense).

**Step 4 — Normalize to Get Correlation:** Covariance depends on the units of measurement, so it cannot be compared across security pairs. Dividing by the product of standard deviations gives a unit-free measure:

$$
\rho_{ij} = \frac{Cov(R_i, R_j)}{\sigma_i \cdot \sigma_j}
$$

**Step 5 — Bound Proof (Sketch):** By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, the absolute value of any correlation is bounded above by 1. Hence $-1 \le \rho \le +1$. This is a mathematical certainty, not an assumption.

### 3.4 Master Derivation: Two-Asset Portfolio Variance (The KTU Favorite)

**Starting Premise:** Two securities, weights $W_1$ and $W_2 = 1 - W_1$, with variances $\sigma_1^2, \sigma_2^2$ and covariance $Cov_{12}$.

**Step 1 — Write Portfolio Return:**
$$
R_p = W_1 R_1 + W_2 R_2
$$

**Step 2 — Define Variance as Squared Deviation from Mean:**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = E\!\left[\{R_p - E(R_p)\}^2\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Portfolio Identity:**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = E\!\left[\{W_1 R_1 + W_2 R_2 - W_1 E(R_1) - W_2 E(R_2)\}^2\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Factor Weights into Deviations:**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = E\!\left[\{W_1 [R_1 - E(R_1)] + W_2 [R_2 - E(R_2)]\}^2\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Expand the Square (Binomial Identity):**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = E\!\left[W_1^2 \{R_1 - E(R_1)\}^2 + W_2^2 \{R_2 - E(R_2)\}^2 + 2 W_1 W_2 \{R_1 - E(R_1)\}\{R_2 - E(R_2)\}\right]
$$

**Step 6 — Distribute the Expectation and Pull Out Constant Weights:**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = W_1^2 E\{[R_1 - E(R_1)]^2\} + W_2^2 E\{[R_2 - E(R_2)]^2\} + 2 W_1 W_2 E\{[R_1 - E(R_1)][R_2 - E(R_2)]\}
$$

**Step 7 — Recognize the Three Statistical Quantities:**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = W_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + W_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 W_1 W_2 \, Cov(R_1, R_2)
$$

**Step 8 — Final Substitution Using Correlation:**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = W_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + W_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 W_1 W_2 \rho_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2
$$

**Step 9 — Verify Boundary Cases (Sanity Check):**

| Case | Result | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho_{12} = +1$ | $\sigma_p = W_1 \sigma_1 + W_2 \sigma_2$ | Zero diversification — perfect positive correlation. Portfolio risk equals weighted average. |
| $\rho_{12} = 0$ | $\sigma_p^2 = W_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + W_2^2 \sigma_2^2$ | Partial diversification — uncorrelated securities. |
| $\rho_{12} = -1$ | $\sigma_p = \vert W_1 \sigma_1 - W_2 \sigma_2 \vert$ | **Maximum diversification** — risk can theoretically be eliminated. |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick:** When $\rho = -1$ and $W_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2}$, the portfolio variance becomes **exactly zero**. This is the KTU-favorite "perfect hedging" result.

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (For Mental Calibration)

Suppose Stock A has $E(R_A) = 20\%$, $\sigma_A = 25\%$, and Stock B has $E(R_B) = 30\%$, $\sigma_B = 40\%$. Weights are $W_A = 0.6$, $W_B = 0.4$, and $\rho_{AB} = 0.3$.

**Step 1 — Portfolio Return:**

$$
E(R_p) = (0.6 \times 20\%) + (0.4 \times 30\%) = 12\% + 12\% = 24\%
$$

**Step 2 — Portfolio Variance:**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = (0.6)^2 (0.25)^2 + (0.4)^2 (0.40)^2 + 2(0.6)(0.4)(0.3)(0.25)(0.40)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate Each Term:**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = 0.36 \times 0.0625 + 0.16 \times 0.16 + 0.144 \times 0.025
$$

$$
\sigma_p^2 = 0.02250 + 0.02560 + 0.00360
$$

$$
\sigma_p^2 = 0.05170
$$

**Step 4 — Portfolio Standard Deviation:**

$$
\sigma_p = \sqrt{0.05170} \approx 22.74\%
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:** The weighted average of individual risks is $0.6 \times 25\% + 0.4 \times 40\% = 31\%$. The actual portfolio risk ($22.74\%$) is **significantly lower** — a clear, measurable diversification benefit.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — From Individual Securities to Portfolio Risk

The following block-level functional architecture map illustrates how individual security statistics combine mathematically to produce portfolio-level metrics. This is the standard "data-flow" visualization KTU examiners use in setting questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Layer: Individual Securities] --> B[For Each Security i]
    B --> C[Compute Expected Return E_Ri]
    B --> D[Compute Variance sigma_i_sq]
    B --> E[Compute Std Dev sigma_i]
    A --> F[Pairwise Analysis]
    F --> G[Compute Covariance Cov_i_j]
    F --> H[Compute Correlation rho_i_j]
    C --> I[Portfolio Return Aggregator]
    D --> J[Portfolio Risk Aggregator]
    E --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> K[Output: Portfolio Expected Return E_Rp]
    J --> L[Output: Portfolio Std Dev sigma_p]
    K --> M[Final Decision: Risk Return Profile]
    L --> M
    M --> N{Compare to Benchmark}
    N --> O[Efficient Frontier Mapping]
    N --> P[Investor Utility Maximization]
```

### 4.2 Risk Decomposition Architecture

The following diagram shows the **hierarchical decomposition** of total portfolio risk into its diversifiable and non-diversifiable components — a concept critical to advanced KTU questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Portfolio Risk sigma_p_sq] --> B[Systematic Risk]
    A --> C[Unsystematic Risk]
    B --> D[Market Wide Shocks]
    B --> E[Interest Rate Changes]
    B --> F[Inflation Shocks]
    C --> G[Firm Specific Events]
    C --> H[Management Errors]
    C --> I[Industry Specific News]
    J[Diversification Lever] --> C
    J --> K[Reduce or Eliminate]
    J -.->|Cannot touch| B
    L[Number of Securities in Portfolio] --> J
    L --> M[As N increases, Unsystematic Risk approaches 0]
    L -.->|But| B
    L --> N[Systematic Risk persists]
```

### 4.3 Correlation Spectrum — The Diversification Engine

This block diagram explains how the **correlation coefficient $\rho$** acts as the *master dial* controlling the level of diversification benefit realized by a portfolio.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Correlation rho] --> B[rho equals +1]
    A --> C[rho equals 0]
    A --> D[rho equals negative 1]
    A --> E[rho between negative 1 and plus 1]
    B --> B1[Perfectly Positively Correlated]
    B1 --> B2[Zero Diversification Benefit]
    B2 --> B3[sigma_p equals Weighted Sum of sigmas]
    C --> C1[Uncorrelated Securities]
    C1 --> C2[Moderate Diversification Benefit]
    C2 --> C3[Variance equals Weighted Sum of Variances]
    D --> D1[Perfectly Negatively Correlated]
    D1 --> D2[Maximum Diversification Benefit]
    D2 --> D3[Theoretical Zero Risk Possible]
    E --> E1[Real World Securities]
    E1 --> E2[Partial Diversification Benefit]
    E2 --> E3[sigma_p less than Weighted Average]
```

### 4.4 Markowitz Efficient Frontier — Block View

The Efficient Frontier is a graphical concept that the KTU examiner may ask you to *describe* or *draw*. Below is the block-level representation of its construction logic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[All Possible Portfolios in Risk Return Space] --> B[Feasible Set Boundary]
    B --> C[Lower Boundary: Minimum Variance Curve]
    B --> D[Upper Boundary: Efficient Frontier]
    D --> E[Highest E_Rp for given sigma_p]
    D --> F[Lowest sigma_p for given E_Rp]
    G[Investor Risk Aversion] --> H[Optimal Portfolio Selection]
    D --> H
    H --> I[Tangency to Indifference Curve]
    I --> J[Final Optimal Portfolio]
    K[CML Capital Market Line] --> J
    K --> L[Combines Risk Free Asset with Market Portfolio]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define *Covariance* and *Correlation Coefficient*. Explain the significance of correlation in the context of portfolio risk management. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Covariance** is a statistical measure that captures the *direction* of the linear relationship between the returns of two securities. Mathematically, it is defined as the expected value of the product of deviations of two random variables from their respective means. A **positive covariance** indicates that the two securities' returns tend to move in the same direction, while a **negative covariance** indicates opposite movement.

The **Correlation Coefficient** $(\rho_{ij})$ is the *normalized* form of covariance, obtained by dividing covariance by the product of the two securities' standard deviations. This normalization confines the value to a strict range of $-1$ to $+1$, making it comparable across any pair of securities.

**Significance in Portfolio Risk Management:** Correlation is the *primary driver* of diversification benefit. When $\rho_{ij} < 1$, combining the two securities into a portfolio reduces total risk below the weighted average of individual risks. At $\rho_{ij} = -1$, perfect hedging is possible, theoretically driving portfolio risk to zero. Hence, an astute portfolio manager always seeks to combine securities with **low or negative correlation** to maximize the risk-reduction benefit. **(3 Marks — Conceptually scored: 1 definition + 1 normalization + 1 portfolio significance)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between *Systematic Risk* and *Unsystematic Risk*. Can diversification eliminate both? Justify your answer. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Systematic Risk | Unsystematic Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Market-wide factors (interest rates, inflation, GDP) | Firm-specific factors (management, product recall) |
| **Affect** | Entire market simultaneously | Specific company or industry |
| **Diversifiable?** | **No** | **Yes** |
| **Also called** | Market risk / Non-diversifiable risk | Specific risk / Idiosyncratic risk |
| **Measured by** | Beta $(\beta)$ | Residual variance |

**Justification:** Diversification can **eliminate unsystematic risk** because firm-specific shocks are largely independent across companies and tend to average out in a large portfolio. However, **systematic risk cannot be diversified away** because it affects *all* securities simultaneously — a market crash depresses virtually every stock, so no amount of mixing can avoid it. The investor is *compensated* for bearing systematic risk through higher expected returns, as per the **Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM)**. **(3 Marks — Tabular distinction 1.5 + Justification 1.5)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part B Module 2 questions carry **14 marks**, typically split into sub-parts **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**. You must attempt *one* of the two alternatives provided below.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]*

**(a)** Two stocks, **P** and **Q**, have the following expected returns and standard deviations:

| Stock | Expected Return $E(R)$ | Standard Deviation $\sigma$ |
|---|---|---|
| P | $18\%$ | $20\%$ |
| Q | $25\%$ | $30\%$ |

The correlation coefficient between P and Q is $\rho_{PQ} = 0.4$. An investor creates a portfolio by investing **60% in P and 40% in Q**. Calculate:
- (i) The expected return of the portfolio. **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) The standard deviation of the portfolio. **(4 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the impact of the correlation coefficient on portfolio risk. What happens to portfolio risk when $\rho = +1$, $\rho = 0$, and $\rho = -1$? Illustrate your answer with appropriate expressions. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**Part (a)(i): Expected Return of the Portfolio**

The expected return of a two-asset portfolio is the weighted average of individual expected returns. The formula does not depend on correlation.

$$
E(R_p) = W_P \cdot E(R_P) + W_Q \cdot E(R_Q)
$$

Substituting the given values $W_P = 0.60$, $W_Q = 0.40$, $E(R_P) = 18\%$, and $E(R_Q) = 25\%$:

$$
E(R_p) = (0.60 \times 18\%) + (0.40 \times 25\%)
$$

$$
E(R_p) = 10.80\% + 10.00\% = 20.80\%
$$

**The expected portfolio return is $20.80\%$.** **(3 Marks)**

---

**Part (a)(ii): Standard Deviation of the Portfolio**

The two-asset portfolio variance formula incorporating correlation is:

$$
\sigma_p^2 = W_P^2 \sigma_P^2 + W_Q^2 \sigma_Q^2 + 2 W_P W_Q \rho_{PQ} \sigma_P \sigma_Q
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the covariance term** using the relation $Cov_{PQ} = \rho_{PQ} \cdot \sigma_P \cdot \sigma_Q$:

$$
Cov_{PQ} = 0.4 \times 20\% \times 30\% = 0.4 \times 600 = 240
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute all values into the variance formula:**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = (0.60)^2 (20)^2 + (0.40)^2 (30)^2 + 2 (0.60)(0.40)(0.4)(20)(30)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term separately for clarity:**

$$
\text{Term 1: } (0.36)(400) = 144
$$

$$
\text{Term 2: } (0.16)(900) = 144
$$

$$
\text{Term 3: } 2(0.24)(0.4)(600) = 0.192 \times 600 = 115.2
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the three terms:**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = 144 + 144 + 115.2 = 403.2
$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root to obtain standard deviation:**

$$
\sigma_p = \sqrt{403.2} \approx 20.08\%
$$

**The portfolio standard deviation is approximately $20.08\%$.** **(4 Marks)**

**Step 6 — Comparison Insight (Bonus Point):** The weighted average of individual risks is $0.60 \times 20\% + 0.40 \times 30\% = 24\%$. The actual portfolio risk ($20.08\%$) is **lower** than this weighted average, confirming the diversification benefit arising from $\rho < 1$.

---

**Part (b): Impact of Correlation on Portfolio Risk**

The correlation coefficient $\rho$ is the **single most important parameter** governing portfolio risk. It is bounded between $-1$ and $+1$ and dictates the magnitude of risk reduction achievable through diversification.

**Case 1 — When $\rho = +1$ (Perfect Positive Correlation):**

The covariance term simplifies to $\sigma_1 \sigma_2$, and the portfolio variance becomes:

$$
\sigma_p^2 = W_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + W_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 W_1 W_2 \sigma_1 \sigma_2 = (W_1 \sigma_1 + W_2 \sigma_2)^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_p = W_1 \sigma_1 + W_2 \sigma_2
$$

**Interpretation:** Portfolio risk is *exactly* the weighted average of individual risks. **No diversification benefit exists** because the two securities move in perfect lock-step — a gain in one is mirrored in the other.

**Case 2 — When $\rho = 0$ (Zero Correlation):**

The covariance term vanishes entirely, and the portfolio variance reduces to:

$$
\sigma_p^2 = W_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + W_2^2 \sigma_2^2
$$

**Interpretation:** Diversification is *active*. The portfolio variance is the weighted sum of individual variances, which is *strictly less* than the squared weighted average of standard deviations — a measurable risk reduction.

**Case 3 — When $\rho = -1$ (Perfect Negative Correlation):**

The covariance term is negative and the variance simplifies to:

$$
\sigma_p^2 = (W_1 \sigma_1 - W_2 \sigma_2)^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_p = \vert W_1 \sigma_1 - W_2 \sigma_2 \vert
$$

**Interpretation:** Risk is *minimized* and can be driven to **exactly zero** with the optimal weight $W_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2}$. This is the theoretical ideal — a **perfect hedge** where gains in one security fully offset losses in the other.

**Conclusion:** As $\rho$ moves from $+1$ down to $-1$, portfolio risk monotonically decreases, reaching its minimum at $\rho = -1$. In real markets, $\rho$ typically lies between $0$ and $+1$ for most equity pairs, yielding moderate — but not perfect — diversification. **(7 Marks — 2 marks per case + 1 mark for summary)**

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Alternative Pattern]*

**(a)** The returns of three possible economic scenarios and the corresponding returns of two stocks, **X** and **Y**, are given below:

| Scenario | Probability | Return on X | Return on Y |
|---|---|---|---|
| Boom | $0.3$ | $40\%$ | $20\%$ |
| Normal | $0.5$ | $15\%$ | $12\%$ |
| Recession | $0.2$ | $-10\%$ | $5\%$ |

Compute:
- (i) Expected return and standard deviation of Stock X. **(4 Marks)**
- (ii) Expected return and standard deviation of Stock Y. **(3 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the covariance and correlation coefficient between X and Y. If an investor holds an equally-weighted portfolio (50% in X and 50% in Y), compute the portfolio risk (standard deviation). **(7 Marks)**

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**Part (a)(i): Stock X — Expected Return & Standard Deviation**

**Step 1 — Expected Return of X:**

$$
E(R_X) = (0.3 \times 40\%) + (0.5 \times 15\%) + (0.2 \times -10\%)
$$

$$
E(R_X) = 12\% + 7.5\% + (-2\%) = 17.5\%
$$

**Step 2 — Variance of X:** First compute deviations and their probability-weighted squared values.

$$
\sigma_X^2 = 0.3(40 - 17.5)^2 + 0.5(15 - 17.5)^2 + 0.2(-10 - 17.5)^2
$$

$$
\sigma_X^2 = 0.3(22.5)^2 + 0.5(-2.5)^2 + 0.2(-27.5)^2
$$

$$
\sigma_X^2 = 0.3(506.25) + 0.5(6.25) + 0.2(756.25)
$$

$$
\sigma_X^2 = 151.875 + 3.125 + 151.250 = 306.25
$$

**Step 3 — Standard Deviation of X:**

$$
\sigma_X = \sqrt{306.25} = 17.5\%
$$

**Result:** $E(R_X) = 17.5\%$, $\sigma_X = 17.5\%$. **(4 Marks)**

---

**Part (a)(ii): Stock Y — Expected Return & Standard Deviation**

**Step 1 — Expected Return of Y:**

$$
E(R_Y) = (0.3 \times 20\%) + (0.5 \times 12\%) + (0.2 \times 5\%)
$$

$$
E(R_Y) = 6\% + 6\% + 1\% = 13\%
$$

**Step 2 — Variance of Y:**

$$
\sigma_Y^2 = 0.3(20 - 13)^2 + 0.5(12 - 13)^2 + 0.2(5 - 13)^2
$$

$$
\sigma_Y^2 = 0.3(49) + 0.5(1) + 0.2(64)
$$

$$
\sigma_Y^2 = 14.7 + 0.5 + 12.8 = 28.0
$$

**Step 3 — Standard Deviation of Y:**

$$
\sigma_Y = \sqrt{28.0} \approx 5.29\%
$$

**Result:** $E(R_Y) = 13\%$, $\sigma_Y \approx 5.29\%$. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Part (b): Covariance, Correlation & Portfolio Risk**

**Step 1 — Compute Covariance between X and Y:**

$$
Cov(X, Y) = \sum P_k \cdot [R_{X,k} - E(R_X)] \cdot [R_{Y,k} - E(R_Y)]
$$

$$
Cov(X, Y) = 0.3(40 - 17.5)(20 - 13) + 0.5(15 - 17.5)(12 - 13) + 0.2(-10 - 17.5)(5 - 13)
$$

$$
Cov(X, Y) = 0.3(22.5)(7) + 0.5(-2.5)(-1) + 0.2(-27.5)(-8)
$$

$$
Cov(X, Y) = 0.3(157.5) + 0.5(2.5) + 0.2(220)
$$

$$
Cov(X, Y) = 47.25 + 1.25 + 44.00 = 92.50
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Correlation Coefficient:**

$$
\rho_{XY} = \frac{Cov(X, Y)}{\sigma_X \cdot \sigma_Y} = \frac{92.50}{17.5 \times 5.29}
$$

$$
\rho_{XY} = \frac{92.50}{92.575} \approx 0.9992
$$

**Interpretation:** The two stocks are *almost perfectly positively correlated*, suggesting very **limited diversification benefit**.

**Step 3 — Portfolio Standard Deviation** (50% in X, 50% in Y):

$$
\sigma_p^2 = W_X^2 \sigma_X^2 + W_Y^2 \sigma_Y^2 + 2 W_X W_Y \rho_{XY} \sigma_X \sigma_Y
$$

$$
\sigma_p^2 = (0.5)^2 (17.5)^2 + (0.5)^2 (5.29)^2 + 2(0.5)(0.5)(0.9992)(17.5)(5.29)
$$

$$
\sigma_p^2 = 0.25(306.25) + 0.25(27.98) + 0.5(0.9992)(92.58)
$$

$$
\sigma_p^2 = 76.5625 + 6.9950 + 46.252
$$

$$
\sigma_p^2 \approx 129.81
$$

**Step 4 — Portfolio Standard Deviation:**

$$
\sigma_p = \sqrt{129.81} \approx 11.39\%
$$

**Step 5 — Verification Insight:** The simple weighted average of individual risks is $0.5 \times 17.5\% + 0.5 \times 5.29\% = 11.40\%$. The actual portfolio risk is **almost identical**, confirming that *with $\rho \approx 1$, there is virtually no diversification benefit*. **(7 Marks — 2 for covariance + 2 for correlation + 3 for portfolio std dev)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Square on Weights in Variance:** A frequent error is writing $W_1 \sigma_1^2$ instead of $W_1^2 \sigma_1^2$. Weights in the variance formula are always **squared** — losing this gives 1–2 marks instantly.
>
> 2. **Using Covariance When Correlation is Given (or Vice Versa):** If the problem states $\rho = 0.4$, you *must* use $\rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2$ in the formula — not just $Cov_{12}$. Watch the question's wording carefully.
>
> 3. **Reporting Standard Deviation Without Square Root:** Computing $\sigma_p^2$ and stopping there is a half-answer. KTU examiners specifically test $\sigma_p$ (not $\sigma_p^2$) in most cases. The square root is **mandatory**.
>
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing percentages and decimals (e.g., 0.20 vs 20%) in the same equation. Pick **one** convention and apply it rigorously throughout.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the Diversification Insight:** Numerical answers without a concluding sentence comparing $\sigma_p$ to the weighted average risk lose the "interpretation" marks (1–2 marks per question).
>
> 6. **Confusing $E(R_p)$ with $\sigma_p$:** Return is always a *weighted average*; risk is always a *square-root-of-variance* computation that includes correlation. They are *not* analogous.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Risk and Return in a Portfolio Context**

- 🔹 **Expected Return of a Single Security:** Probability-weighted average of all possible returns. Formula: $E(R_i) = \sum P_k R_{ik}$.
- 🔹 **Portfolio Return is Linear:** $E(R_p) = \sum W_i E(R_i)$ — *never* depends on correlation.
- 🔹 **Variance/Std. Dev. Measures Standalone Risk:** Use $\sigma_i^2 = \sum P_k (R_{ik} - E(R_i))^2$, then square root.
- 🔹 **Covariance Captures Co-Movement:** Signed, unbounded. Convert to correlation for bounded comparison.
- 🔹 **Correlation Range is Sacred:** $-1 \le \rho \le +1$ — always, without exception.
- 🔹 **Two-Asset Portfolio Variance is the KTU Favorite:** $\sigma_p^2 = W_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + W_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 W_1 W_2 \rho \sigma_1 \sigma_2$.
- 🔹 **Three Critical Cases of $\rho$:**
  - $\rho = +1$ → No diversification, $\sigma_p = W_1 \sigma_1 + W_2 \sigma_2$.
  - $\rho = 0$ → Moderate diversification, covariance term vanishes.
  - $\rho = -1$ → Maximum diversification, perfect hedge possible, $\sigma_p = \vert W_1 \sigma_1 - W_2 \sigma_2 \vert$.
- 🔹 **Diversification Eliminates Only Unsystematic Risk:** Systematic (market) risk is permanent.
- 🔹 **More Securities ≠ Always Lower Risk:** Beyond ~30 stocks, marginal risk reduction becomes negligible.
- 🔹 **KTU Numerical Pattern:** Always provide 5 decimal/intermediate steps; the examiner rewards process, not just the final number.
- 🔹 **Markowitz (1952) is the Theoretical Father:** Recognize the name — questions may ask the origin of MPT.
- 🔹 **Efficient Frontier:** The upper boundary of the feasible set in risk-return space, comprising optimal portfolios.
- 🔹 **Always Cross-Verify:** Compare your computed $\sigma_p$ against the weighted average of individual $\sigma$s — if $\rho < 1$, the portfolio risk *must* be lower.
- 🔹 **Final Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme exams, write the *formula first*, then *substitute values*, then *compute step-by-step*. This structured presentation wins full marks even if the final arithmetic has a minor slip.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diversification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Diversification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Diversification** in financial management is an investment strategy that involves allocating capital across a wide variety of financial instruments, industries, sectors, and asset classes to reduce the **unsystematic (firm-specific) risk** of a portfolio. The foundational principle, formalized by **Harry Markowitz (1952) in his Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT)**, states that an investor can construct an "efficient portfolio" by combining assets whose returns are not perfectly positively correlated, thereby lowering the portfolio's overall variance without necessarily sacrificing expected return.

Mathematically, for a portfolio consisting of $n$ securities, the expected portfolio return $E(R_p)$ and the portfolio variance $\sigma_p^2$ are functions of the individual security weights, expected returns, variances, and pairwise covariances.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> Diversification is the process of combining securities in a portfolio such that the portfolio's overall risk is less than the weighted average of the individual securities' risks, achieved by selecting assets with low or negative correlations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a farmer in Kerala who traditionally grows **only paddy** in your entire field. If a sudden pest attack (e.g., the brown plant hopper) occurs, you lose your entire annual income — a **catastrophic, total wipeout**.

Now, suppose you divide the same field into sections:
- 50% paddy
- 25% coconut
- 15% banana
- 10% vegetables (cowpea, okra)

If a pest destroys the paddy, the coconut, banana, and vegetables remain unaffected, and your total income loss is contained. This is **diversification in action** — you have traded *concentration risk* for *stable, blended returns*.

In the financial world, the "crops" are stocks, bonds, gold, real estate, and the "pest" could be a company-specific scandal, an industry downturn, or a regulatory shock.

### 1.3 Key Terminology in KTU Standard Notation

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Weight of Security $i$ | $w_i$ | Proportion of total capital invested in security $i$ |
| Expected Return | $E(R_i)$ | Probability-weighted average return of security $i$ |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma_i$ | Square root of variance; measures total risk |
| Variance | $\sigma_i^2$ | Squared deviation from the expected return |
| Covariance | $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j)$ | Joint variability of two securities' returns |
| Correlation Coefficient | $\rho_{ij}$ | Normalized covariance, ranges from **-1 to +1** |
| Portfolio Return | $E(R_p)$ | Weighted sum of individual expected returns |
| Portfolio Risk | $\sigma_p$ | Standard deviation of portfolio returns |

> [!NOTE] **Critical KTU Highlight**
> A common KTU question tests the difference between **Diversification** and **Hedging**. Diversification reduces *unsystematic* risk; it cannot eliminate *systematic* (market) risk because that risk affects all assets simultaneously.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Diversification — The Statistical Principle

Diversification works because of the **statistical law of large numbers** applied to financial returns. When you combine assets whose returns move *independently* (low correlation), the positive surprises in one asset tend to cancel out the negative surprises in another.

The portfolio risk formula contains a **covariance matrix** where:
- If $\rho_{ij} < 1$, the cross-product terms reduce total variance below the weighted average of individual variances.
- If $\rho_{ij} = -1$, perfect negative correlation, risk can be **eliminated entirely** (in theory).
- If $\rho_{ij} = +1$, no diversification benefit exists — the portfolio risk is simply the weighted average of individual risks.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / KTU Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sum of Weights Constraint | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$ | Weights must sum to exactly **1.0** (100% of capital) |
| 2 | Expected Portfolio Return | $E(R_p) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot E(R_i)$ | Linear in weights — always the weighted average |
| 3 | Two-Asset Portfolio Variance | $\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2\sigma_1^2 + w_2^2\sigma_2^2 + 2w_1w_2\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2$ | Double-weight the variance terms; single-weight the covariance |
| 4 | Covariance–Correlation Link | $\text{Cov}(R_i,R_j) = \rho_{ij} \cdot \sigma_i \cdot \sigma_j$ | Watch units — covariance is in $R^2$ |
| 5 | N-Asset Portfolio Variance | $\sigma_p^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i^2\sigma_i^2 + 2\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}\sum_{j=i+1}^{n} w_i w_j \text{Cov}(R_i,R_j)$ | $n$ variance terms; $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ covariance pairs |
| 6 | Minimum Risk Weights (2 assets) | $w_1^{*} = \dfrac{\sigma_2^2 - \text{Cov}(R_1,R_2)}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\text{Cov}(R_1,R_2)}$ | $w_2^{*} = 1 - w_1^{*}$ |
| 7 | Correlation Range | $-1 \le \rho_{ij} \le +1$ | Bound enforced by Cauchy-Schwarz inequality |
| 8 | Diversification Effect | $\sigma_p < \bar{\sigma} = \sum w_i \sigma_i$ | Portfolio risk < weighted average of individual risks (when $\rho < 1$) |

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Examiner's Insight**
> In Part A (3-mark) questions, KTU frequently tests the *boundary conditions* of correlation: what happens to portfolio risk when $\rho = 1$, $\rho = 0$, and $\rho = -1$. Memorize the three cases — they are guaranteed marks.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Banking & Treasury:** Banks use diversification across loan portfolios, geographies, and maturities to comply with **Basel III** capital adequacy norms.
- **Mutual Funds:** Fund managers use Markowitz optimization to construct schemes like equity-debt hybrid funds.
- **Insurance:** Insurers diversify across lines (life, health, motor) and geographies to stabilize claim outflows.
- **Personal Finance:** The "**60-30-10 rule**" (equity-debt-gold) used in Indian retirement planning is a direct application of diversification principles.
- **Corporate Strategy:** Conglomerate companies (e.g., Tata Group) diversify across steel, IT, automobiles, and hospitality to smooth out cyclical earnings.

### 2.4 Types of Diversification (Strategic Perspective)

1. **Horizontal Diversification** — adding new products/services in the *same* industry at the *same* stage of production.
2. **Vertical Diversification** — moving *up* (forward integration) or *down* (backward integration) the value chain.
3. **Conglomerate Diversification** — entering entirely unrelated businesses (e.g., an FMCG firm launching a fintech product).
4. **Geographic Diversification** — expanding into new countries/currencies.
5. **Financial / Portfolio Diversification** — the Markowitz-style capital allocation across securities.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Portfolio Return

**Problem Setup:** Let a portfolio $P$ consist of $n$ securities with weights $w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n$ and random returns $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$. The portfolio's random return is:

$$R_p = w_1 R_1 + w_2 R_2 + \cdots + w_n R_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i R_i$$

Taking the expectation of both sides, and using the **linearity of expectation** operator $E[\cdot]$:

$$E(R_p) = E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i R_i\right]$$

$$E(R_p) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E(w_i R_i)$$

$$E(R_p) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i E(R_i)$$

> **Logic:** Because $w_i$ is a known constant (the investor's chosen weight), it factors out of the expectation. The weights sum to 1, so $E(R_p)$ is simply the weighted average of individual expected returns.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Portfolio Variance (Two-Asset Case)

**Problem Setup:** For two securities with returns $R_1$ and $R_2$, weights $w_1$ and $w_2 = 1 - w_1$:

$$R_p = w_1 R_1 + (1-w_1) R_2$$

The variance is, by definition:

$$\sigma_p^2 = \text{Var}(R_p) = \text{Var}\!\left[w_1 R_1 + (1-w_1) R_2\right]$$

Expanding using the **bilinearity of variance**:

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \,\text{Var}(R_1) + (1-w_1)^2 \,\text{Var}(R_2) + 2 w_1 (1-w_1)\,\text{Cov}(R_1, R_2)$$

Substituting the standard deviation and correlation forms:

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + (1-w_1)^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 (1-w_1)\,\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2$$

> **Logic:** The cross-term $2w_1(1-w_1)\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2$ is the **diversification benefit**. When $\rho_{12} < 1$, this term reduces total variance below the weighted sum of individual variances.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — General N-Asset Portfolio Variance

For $n$ assets, the portfolio return is $R_p = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i R_i$. Taking the variance:

$$\sigma_p^2 = \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i R_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_i w_j \,\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j)$$

Splitting the double sum into diagonal ($i = j$) and off-diagonal ($i \neq j$) terms:

$$\sigma_p^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i^2 \sigma_i^2 + 2\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}\sum_{j=i+1}^{n} w_i w_j \,\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j)$$

Substituting $\text{Cov}(R_i,R_j) = \rho_{ij}\sigma_i\sigma_j$:

$$\sigma_p^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i^2 \sigma_i^2 + 2\sum_{i=1}^{n-1}\sum_{j=i+1}^{n} w_i w_j \rho_{ij}\sigma_i\sigma_j$$

> **Logic:** The first term is the **weighted average of individual variances** (squared). The second term is the **diversification cross-term**, summing $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ pairs. As $n \to \infty$ with equal weights and uncorrelated returns ($\rho = 0$), $\sigma_p^2 \to \frac{1}{n}\bar{\sigma^2} \to 0$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Minimum Variance Portfolio (Two Assets)

To find the weight $w_1^*$ that minimizes $\sigma_p^2$, we differentiate the two-asset variance equation with respect to $w_1$ and set the derivative to zero.

$$\frac{d(\sigma_p^2)}{dw_1} = 2w_1\sigma_1^2 - 2(1-w_1)\sigma_2^2 + 2(1 - 2w_1)\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2 = 0$$

Simplifying by dividing by 2:

$$w_1\sigma_1^2 - (1-w_1)\sigma_2^2 + (1-2w_1)\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2 = 0$$

Expanding:

$$w_1\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_2^2 + w_1\sigma_2^2 + \rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2 - 2w_1\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2 = 0$$

Collecting $w_1$ terms:

$$w_1\!\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2\right] = \sigma_2^2 - \rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2$$

Therefore:

$$w_1^{*} = \frac{\sigma_2^2 - \rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2} = \frac{\sigma_2^2 - \text{Cov}(R_1,R_2)}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\,\text{Cov}(R_1,R_2)}$$

And the complementary weight is $w_2^{*} = 1 - w_1^{*}$.

> **Second-Order Confirmation:** The second derivative is $\frac{d^2(\sigma_p^2)}{dw_1^2} = 2(\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2) > 0$ since the denominator equals the variance of $(R_1 - R_2) \ge 0$, confirming a **minimum** (not a maximum).

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Two-Asset Case)

**Given:** An investor constructs a portfolio with $w_A = 0.60$ in Stock A and $w_B = 0.40$ in Stock B.
- $E(R_A) = 18\%$, $\sigma_A = 22\%$
- $E(R_B) = 10\%$, $\sigma_B = 14\%$
- $\rho_{AB} = 0.30$

**Step 1 — Portfolio Return:**

$$E(R_p) = (0.60)(18) + (0.40)(10) = 10.8 + 4.0 = 14.8\%$$

**Step 2 — Portfolio Variance:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.60)^2(22)^2 + (0.40)^2(14)^2 + 2(0.60)(0.40)(0.30)(22)(14)$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.36)(484) + (0.16)(196) + (0.48)(0.30)(308)$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = 174.24 + 31.36 + 44.352 = 249.952$$

**Step 3 — Portfolio Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{249.952} \approx 15.81\%$$

**Step 4 — Verify Diversification Benefit:**

Weighted average of individual risks: $0.60(22) + 0.40(14) = 18.8\%$.

Since $\sigma_p = 15.81\% < 18.8\%$, **diversification has reduced risk** by approximately **2.99 percentage points**.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Table — Effect of Correlation

| Correlation $\rho_{AB}$ | $\sigma_p$ (computed) | Diversification Benefit | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $+1.00$ | $18.80\%$ | **Zero** | No benefit — assets move in perfect lockstep |
| $+0.30$ | $15.81\%$ | $2.99$ pp | Moderate benefit — typical for two sectors |
| $0.00$ | $15.28\%$ | $3.52$ pp | High benefit — statistically independent |
| $-0.50$ | $12.43\%$ | $6.37$ pp | Substantial benefit — partial hedge |
| $-1.00$ | $4.40\%$ | $14.40$ pp | Theoretical maximum — perfect hedge possible |

> [!NOTE] **Insight:** The lower (more negative) the correlation, the greater the risk reduction. This is the central engineering insight KTU examiners reward.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — Diversification Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Investor Capital]:::root --> B1[Risk Assessment]
    B1 --> C1{Identify Asset Universe}
    C1 --> D1[Stocks Equity]
    C1 --> D2[Bonds Debt]
    C1 --> D3[Gold Commodities]
    C1 --> D4[Real Estate]
    C1 --> D5[Cash Equivalents]
    D1 --> E1[Calculate E R and Sigma for each]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    D5 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Compute Covariance Matrix]
    F1 --> G1[Markowitz Optimization]
    G1 --> H1[Determine Optimal Weights w i]
    H1 --> I1[Construct Portfolio P]
    I1 --> J1{Verify Risk Reduction}
    J1 -->|Yes| K1[Efficient Portfolio Achieved]
    J1 -->|No| L1[Re-optimize Weights]
    L1 --> G1

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid — Risk Decomposition Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T[Total Portfolio Risk sigma p]:::top --> A1[Systematic Risk Market Risk]
    T --> A2[Unsystematic Risk Firm Specific]
    A2 --> B1[Business Risk]
    A2 --> B2[Financial Risk]
    A2 --> B3[Operational Risk]
    A1:::highlight --> C1[Cannot be diversified away]
    A2 --> C2[Can be reduced to near zero via diversification]
    C2:::highlight

    classDef top fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    classDef highlight fill:#166534,stroke:#166534,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Efficient Frontier Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Portfolio A High Risk High Return] --> P2[Portfolio B Moderate]
    P2 --> P3[Portfolio C Low Risk Low Return]
    P3 --> P4[Minimum Variance Portfolio]
    P4 --> P5[Risk Free Asset]
    P5 --> P6[Capital Market Line CML]
    P6 --> P7[Market Portfolio M]
    P1 -.->|Inefficient Region| P3
    P1 -.->|Inefficient Region| P2

    classDef efficient fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    classDef tangent fill:#b45309,stroke:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    class P1,P2,P3 efficient
    class P6,P7 tangent
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Markowitz Two-Asset Engine

| Module | Input | Process | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Input Module** | $E(R_1), E(R_2), \sigma_1, \sigma_2, \rho_{12}$ | Receive raw data | Data packet |
| **Weight Selector** | Data packet | Apply constraint $w_1 + w_2 = 1$ | $w_1, w_2$ |
| **Return Engine** | $w_i, E(R_i)$ | Multiply and sum | $E(R_p)$ |
| **Covariance Engine** | $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \rho_{12}$ | Compute $\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2$ | Covariance term |
| **Variance Engine** | $w_i, \sigma_i^2$, Cov term | Quadratic expansion | $\sigma_p^2$ |
| **Risk Engine** | $\sigma_p^2$ | Square root | $\sigma_p$ |
| **Optimizer** | $\sigma_p^2(w_1)$ | $d\sigma_p^2/dw_1 = 0$ | $w_1^*$ |
| **Output Module** | $E(R_p), \sigma_p$ | Format report | Investment decision |

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight:** This block architecture mirrors a typical **control system** — the optimizer is a feedback loop that iteratively adjusts weights to minimize the variance "error signal." This is why MPT is sometimes called the *mean-variance optimization* algorithm.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *"What is diversification? Explain its role in portfolio risk reduction."* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Diversification is an investment strategy of allocating funds across multiple securities/asset classes to reduce the overall risk of a portfolio. Its role stems from the **statistical effect of low or negative correlation** between asset returns — when assets do not move perfectly in tandem, the variance of the combined portfolio is lower than the weighted average of individual variances (i.e., $\sigma_p < \sum w_i \sigma_i$). The diversification benefit is captured by the cross-term $2w_1w_2\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2$ in the portfolio variance equation. It primarily reduces *unsystematic risk*; *systematic risk* cannot be diversified away.

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> *"State the boundary conditions of the correlation coefficient and explain what happens to portfolio risk at each extreme."* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
The correlation coefficient $\rho_{ij}$ is bounded by **-1 ≤ ρ ≤ +1** (Cauchy-Schwarz inequality).
- When $\rho = +1$: Portfolio returns move in perfect lockstep. $\sigma_p = w_1\sigma_1 + w_2\sigma_2$ — **no diversification benefit**.
- When $\rho = 0$: Returns are statistically independent. $\sigma_p < \bar{\sigma}$ — **maximum diversification for typical portfolios**.
- When $\rho = -1$: Returns move in perfectly opposite directions. **Risk can be theoretically eliminated** by choosing $w_i = \sigma_j / (\sigma_i + \sigma_j)$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Question A** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
> *(a)* A portfolio consists of two stocks A and B with weights $w_A = 0.55$ and $w_B = 0.45$. The expected returns are $E(R_A) = 16\%$ and $E(R_B) = 12\%$. The standard deviations are $\sigma_A = 20\%$ and $\sigma_B = 15\%$, and the correlation coefficient is $\rho_{AB} = 0.25$. Calculate the **expected portfolio return** and **portfolio standard deviation**. **[7 Marks]**
>
> *(b)* What is the **minimum variance portfolio**? Derive the formula for the optimal weight of Stock A in a two-asset portfolio. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Calculation**

**Step 1 — Portfolio Return:**

$$E(R_p) = (0.55)(16) + (0.45)(12)$$

$$E(R_p) = 8.80 + 5.40 = 14.20\%$$

**Step 2 — Portfolio Variance:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_A^2 \sigma_A^2 + w_B^2 \sigma_B^2 + 2 w_A w_B \rho_{AB} \sigma_A \sigma_B$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.55)^2(20)^2 + (0.45)^2(15)^2 + 2(0.55)(0.45)(0.25)(20)(15)$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = (0.3025)(400) + (0.2025)(225) + (0.495)(0.25)(300)$$

$$\sigma_p^2 = 121.00 + 45.5625 + 37.125 = 203.6875$$

**Step 3 — Portfolio Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{203.6875} \approx 14.27\%$$

**Step 4 — Diversification Verification:**

Weighted average of risks: $0.55(20) + 0.45(15) = 11.00 + 6.75 = 17.75\%$.

Since $\sigma_p = 14.27\% < 17.75\%$, diversification has reduced risk by **3.48 percentage points**. ✓

---

**Part (b) — Minimum Variance Portfolio**

A **minimum variance portfolio (MVP)** is the combination of assets that produces the **lowest possible portfolio variance (risk)** for a given set of assets, regardless of expected return.

**Derivation:** Start with the two-asset variance equation:

$$\sigma_p^2 = w_1^2\sigma_1^2 + (1-w_1)^2\sigma_2^2 + 2w_1(1-w_1)\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2$$

Differentiate with respect to $w_1$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d(\sigma_p^2)}{dw_1} = 2w_1\sigma_1^2 - 2(1-w_1)\sigma_2^2 + 2(1-2w_1)\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2 = 0$$

Solving for $w_1$ (as shown in the detailed derivation in Section 3.4):

$$\boxed{w_1^{*} = \frac{\sigma_2^2 - \rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2}}$$

The complementary weight is $w_2^{*} = 1 - w_1^{*}$. The second derivative is positive, confirming a minimum. Geometrically, the MVP lies at the **leftmost (lowest-variance) point of the efficient frontier**.

---

> **Question B (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**
> *(a)* A portfolio has three stocks with the following data:
>
> | Stock | Weight $w_i$ | Expected Return $E(R_i)$ | Standard Deviation $\sigma_i$ |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | X | 0.30 | 14% | 18% |
> | Y | 0.50 | 10% | 12% |
> | Z | 0.20 | 16% | 22% |
>
> The correlation coefficients are: $\rho_{XY} = 0.40$, $\rho_{XZ} = 0.20$, $\rho_{YZ} = 0.30$. Calculate the **expected portfolio return** and **portfolio standard deviation**. **[7 Marks]**
>
> *(b)* Discuss the **types of diversification** with suitable examples. Explain why diversification cannot eliminate systematic risk. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Calculation**

**Step 1 — Portfolio Return:**

$$E(R_p) = (0.30)(14) + (0.50)(10) + (0.20)(16)$$

$$E(R_p) = 4.20 + 5.00 + 3.20 = 12.40\%$$

**Step 2 — Portfolio Variance (N-Asset Formula):**

$$\sigma_p^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{3} w_i^2 \sigma_i^2 + 2\sum_{i<j} w_i w_j \rho_{ij}\sigma_i\sigma_j$$

**Diagonal (variance) terms:**

- $w_X^2 \sigma_X^2 = (0.30)^2(18)^2 = (0.09)(324) = 29.16$
- $w_Y^2 \sigma_Y^2 = (0.50)^2(12)^2 = (0.25)(144) = 36.00$
- $w_Z^2 \sigma_Z^2 = (0.20)^2(22)^2 = (0.04)(484) = 19.36$

**Off-diagonal (covariance) terms:**

- $2 w_X w_Y \rho_{XY}\sigma_X\sigma_Y = 2(0.30)(0.50)(0.40)(18)(12) = (0.30)(0.40)(216) = 25.92$
- $2 w_X w_Z \rho_{XZ}\sigma_X\sigma_Z = 2(0.30)(0.20)(0.20)(18)(22) = (0.12)(0.20)(396) = 9.504$
- $2 w_Y w_Z \rho_{YZ}\sigma_Y\sigma_Z = 2(0.50)(0.20)(0.30)(12)(22) = (0.20)(0.30)(264) = 15.84$

**Total Variance:**

$$\sigma_p^2 = 29.16 + 36.00 + 19.36 + 25.92 + 9.504 + 15.84 = 135.784$$

**Step 3 — Portfolio Standard Deviation:**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{135.784} \approx 11.65\%$$

**Step 4 — Diversification Verification:**

Weighted average of risks: $0.30(18) + 0.50(12) + 0.20(22) = 5.4 + 6.0 + 4.4 = 15.80\%$.

Since $\sigma_p = 11.65\% < 15.80\%$, the **diversification benefit ≈ 4.15 percentage points**. ✓

---

**Part (b) — Types of Diversification & Systematic Risk**

**Types of Diversification:**

1. **Horizontal Diversification** — adding new products in the *same* industry at the *same* production stage. *Example:* Maruti Suzuki adding a new SUV model alongside its existing hatchback line.

2. **Vertical Diversification (Integration)** — moving up/down the supply chain. *Example:* A textile company acquiring cotton farms (backward) or retail outlets (forward).

3. **Conglomerate Diversification** — entering completely unrelated businesses. *Example:* Tata Group's diversification from steel (Tata Steel) to IT (TCS) to hospitality (Indian Hotels).

4. **Geographic Diversification** — operating across multiple countries/currencies. *Example:* Infosys earning 60% revenue from North America and 25% from Europe.

5. **Portfolio (Financial) Diversification** — Markowitz-style capital allocation across securities, asset classes, sectors.

**Why Systematic Risk Cannot Be Eliminated:**

Systematic risk arises from **macro-economic factors** that affect the *entire market* — interest rate changes, inflation, GDP shocks, geopolitical events, currency crises, pandemics. Since every security is exposed to these factors simultaneously, **all pairwise correlations $\rho_{ij} \to 1$ during market-wide stress events** (e.g., the 2008 crisis, COVID-19 crash of March 2020). When correlations converge to 1, the cross-term in the variance equation provides **no risk reduction**.

$$\lim_{\rho_{ij} \to 1} \sigma_p^2 = \left(\sum w_i \sigma_i\right)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{No diversification benefit remains}$$

Hence, systematic risk must be managed through **hedging, asset-liability matching, or beta adjustment (CAPM)** — not diversification.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the "2" in the covariance term:** Many students write $w_1w_2\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2$ instead of $2w_1w_2\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2$. This is a guaranteed **1–2 mark deduction**.
> 2. **Confusing $\sigma$ with $\sigma^2$:** Portfolio *standard deviation* is the square root of variance — not the variance itself. Always check the question.
> 3. **Weight sum ≠ 1:** If your weights don't sum to 1, every subsequent calculation is wrong. Cross-verify first.
> 4. **Off-diagonal factor in N-asset case:** In a 3-asset portfolio, there are exactly **3 covariance pairs** (XY, XZ, YZ), not 6 or 9. The factor of 2 in the formula accounts for both orderings.
> 5. **Mixing up "diversification" with "hedging":** Hedging uses *negative* correlated instruments (options, futures) to *offset* risk. Diversification uses *uncorrelated* assets to *spread* risk. KTU marks depend on this distinction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Diversification** reduces **unsystematic risk**; systematic risk is non-diversifiable.
- [x] **Portfolio return** = $\sum w_i E(R_i)$ — a simple weighted average (no covariance term).
- [x] **Portfolio variance** = $\sum w_i^2 \sigma_i^2 + 2\sum_{i<j} w_i w_j \rho_{ij}\sigma_i\sigma_j$ — must include the **factor of 2** for the off-diagonal term.
- [x] **Correlation bounds:** $-1 \le \rho \le +1$. Lower $\rho$ → higher diversification benefit.
- [x] **$\rho = +1$** → no benefit; **$\rho = 0$** → moderate benefit; **$\rho = -1$** → risk fully eliminable in theory.
- [x] **Minimum variance portfolio** weight (2 assets): $w_1^{*} = \frac{\sigma_2^2 - \rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\rho_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2}$.
- [x] **Diversification types:** Horizontal, Vertical, Conglomerate, Geographic, Portfolio.
- [x] **Markowitz (1952)** is the foundational theory — "Father of Modern Portfolio Theory."
- [x] **KTU 2024 focus:** numerical computation of 2-asset and 3-asset portfolios; theoretical boundary cases; type classifications.
- [x] **CAPM link:** Diversification eliminates unsystematic risk, so in equilibrium investors are only rewarded for **systematic (beta) risk**.
- [x] **Practical rule of thumb:** As the number of uncorrelated assets increases, portfolio risk approaches zero, but the law of diminishing returns kicks in after ~15–20 securities.
- [x] **Examiner's quick test:** If a question gives 3 assets and asks for $\sigma_p$, expect 3 variance terms + 3 covariance pairs = 6 total terms in the variance expansion.

---

> **End of Module 2 — Diversification Notes**
