<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the term "Financial Leverage". How is it different from Operating Leverage?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**  
Financial Leverage is the presence of fixed financial charges (such as interest on long-term debt and preference dividend) in the firm's capital structure. It measures the sensitivity of **Earnings Per Share (EPS)** to changes in **Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT)**. The numerical measure is the **Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)**.

**Differentiation (1 Mark):**  
- **Operating Leverage** arises from **fixed operating costs** (rent, depreciation) and measures the sensitivity of **EBIT** to changes in **Sales**.  
- **Financial Leverage** arises from **fixed financial costs** (interest, preference dividend) and measures the sensitivity of **EPS** to changes in **EBIT**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is "Trading on Equity"? Under what conditions is it favorable?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**  
Trading on Equity is a financial strategy in which a firm uses borrowed funds (debt) and/or preference capital along with equity capital, with the objective of increasing the return to equity shareholders.

**Conditions (1.5 Marks):**  
Trading on Equity is **favorable** when:  
1. The **Return on Investment (ROI)** of the firm is **greater than the cost of debt (kd)**.  
2. The economy is in a **growth phase** (sales and EBIT are expected to rise).  
3. The firm has **stable cash flows** to service the fixed interest commitments.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**  
> - In DFL calculation problems, **do not forget to include the Preference Dividend (PD)** if the firm has preference capital. The PD term must be divided by $(1-t)$ inside the denominator.  
> - In Indifference Point problems, students often equate EBIT directly without including tax effect — this loses **3 to 4 marks**. Always use the **after-tax form** of the EPS equation.  
> - Always show the **verification step** (substitute IP back into both plans and show EPS is equal). Examiners award 1 mark specifically for verification.  
> - When calculating **Combined Leverage**, you must know the **DOL from Module 2**. If DOL is not given, do NOT assume DOL = 1. State the assumption clearly.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [Choice 1]**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]**

A company has the following capital structure:

| Source | Amount (₹) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Equity Shares (₹ 10 each) | 10,00,000 |
| 12% Preference Shares | 5,00,000 |
| 14% Debentures | 5,00,000 |
| **Total** | **20,00,000** |

The company's EBIT is ₹ 4,00,000 and the tax rate is 40%.

**Required:**
**(a)** Calculate the **Earnings Per Share (EPS)** and the **Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)**.  
**(b)** If the EBIT increases by 25%, calculate the **new EPS** and verify it using the DFL value.

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Given Data:**
- $EBIT = 4,00,000$
- $I = 14\% \times 5,00,000 = 70,000$
- $PD = 12\% \times 5,00,000 = 60,000$
- $t = 0.40$
- $N = \frac{10,00,000}{10} = 1,00,000$ shares

**Part (a) — EPS Calculation (7 Marks):**

Step 1 — Calculate EBT:
$$EBT = EBIT - I = 4,00,000 - 70,000 = 3,30,000$$

Step 2 — Calculate EAT (Net Income):
$$EAT = EBT \times (1-t) = 3,30,000 \times 0.60 = 1,98,000$$

Step 3 — Subtract Preference Dividend to get Equity Earnings:
$$EAT_{Equity} = 1,98,000 - 60,000 = 1,38,000$$

Step 4 — Calculate EPS:
$$EPS = \frac{1,38,000}{1,00,000} = 1.38 \text{ per share}$$

Step 5 — Calculate DFL:
$$DFL = \frac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - \frac{PD}{(1-t)}}$$

$$DFL = \frac{4,00,000}{4,00,000 - 70,000 - \frac{60,000}{0.60}}$$

$$DFL = \frac{4,00,000}{4,00,000 - 70,000 - 1,00,000}$$

$$DFL = \frac{4,00,000}{2,30,000} = 1.739$$

**Part (b) — New EPS with 25% Increase in EBIT (7 Marks):**

Step 1 — New EBIT:
$$EBIT_{new} = 4,00,000 \times 1.25 = 5,00,000$$

Step 2 — New EBT:
$$EBT = 5,00,000 - 70,000 = 4,30,000$$

Step 3 — New EAT:
$$EAT = 4,30,000 \times 0.60 = 2,58,000$$

Step 4 — New Equity Earnings:
$$EAT_{Equity} = 2,58,000 - 60,000 = 1,98,000$$

Step 5 — New EPS:
$$EPS_{new} = \frac{1,98,000}{1,00,000} = 1.98 \text{ per share}$$

**Verification via DFL (Mandatory Step):**
- Original EPS = ₹ 1.38
- % Change in EPS = $\frac{1.98 - 1.38}{1.38} \times 100 = 43.48\%$
- Expected via DFL: $1.739 \times 25\% = 43.48\%$ ✓ **VERIFIED**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Choice 2]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**

A firm is evaluating two financing plans for a new project:

| Particulars | Plan X (₹) | Plan Y (₹) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Equity (₹ 100 each) | 10,00,000 | 6,00,000 |
| 13% Preference Shares | 0 | 2,00,000 |
| 15% Debentures | 5,00,000 | 7,00,000 |
| **Total** | **15,00,000** | **15,00,000** |

Tax rate is 50%.

**Required:**
**(a)** Calculate the **Indifference Point (EBIT level at which both plans yield the same EPS)**.  
**(b)** Recommend the appropriate plan when **expected EBIT is ₹ 3,00,000**. Justify with EPS comparison.

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Plan X | Plan Y |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $N$ (Equity Shares) | 10,000 | 6,000 |
| $I$ (Interest) | $15\% \times 5L = 75,000$ | $15\% \times 7L = 1,05,000$ |
| $PD$ (Pref. Dividend) | 0 | $13\% \times 2L = 26,000$ |
| $t$ | 0.50 | 0.50 |

**Part (a) — Indifference Point Calculation (7 Marks):**

At the Indifference Point, EPS of Plan X = EPS of Plan Y:
$$\frac{(EBIT - I_X)(1-t) - PD_X}{N_X} = \frac{(EBIT - I_Y)(1-t) - PD_Y}{N_Y}$$

Substituting values:
$$\frac{(EBIT - 75,000)(0.50) - 0}{10,000} = \frac{(EBIT - 1,05,000)(0.50) - 26,000}{6,000}$$

Cross-multiply:
$$6,000 \times [(EBIT - 75,000) \times 0.50] = 10,000 \times [(EBIT - 1,05,000) \times 0.50 - 26,000]$$

$$3,000(EBIT - 75,000) = 10,000(EBIT - 1,05,000) \times 0.50 - 2,60,00,000$$

$$3,000 \cdot EBIT - 2,25,00,000 = 5,000(EBIT - 1,05,000) - 2,60,00,000$$

$$3,000 \cdot EBIT - 2,25,00,000 = 5,000 \cdot EBIT - 5,25,00,000 - 2,60,00,000$$

$$3,000 \cdot EBIT - 2,25,00,000 = 5,000 \cdot EBIT - 7,85,00,000$$

Bring like terms together:
$$7,85,00,000 - 2,25,00,000 = 5,000 \cdot EBIT - 3,000 \cdot EBIT$$

$$5,60,00,000 = 2,000 \cdot EBIT$$

$$EBIT_{IP} = \frac{5,60,00,000}{2,000} = 2,80,000$$

**Verification (Mandatory — 1 Mark):**

For Plan X at IP:
$$EPS_X = \frac{(2,80,000 - 75,000) \times 0.50}{10,000} = \frac{2,05,000 \times 0.50}{10,000} = \frac{1,02,500}{10,000} = 10.25$$

For Plan Y at IP:
$$EPS_Y = \frac{(2,80,000 - 1,05,000) \times 0.50 - 26,000}{6,000} = \frac{1,75,000 \times 0.50 - 26,000}{6,000} = \frac{87,500 - 26,000}{6,000} = \frac{61,500}{6,000} = 10.25 \checkmark$$

**Part (b) — Recommendation at EBIT = ₹ 3,00,000 (7 Marks):**

Since expected EBIT (₹ 3,00,000) **> Indifference Point (₹ 2,80,000)**, **Plan Y is preferable**.

**EPS Calculation for Plan X:**
$$EPS_X = \frac{(3,00,000 - 75,000) \times 0.50}{10,000} = \frac{2,25,000 \times 0.50}{10,000} = \frac{1,12,500}{10,000} = 11.25$$

**EPS Calculation for Plan Y:**
$$EPS_Y = \frac{(3,00,000 - 1,05,000) \times 0.50 - 26,000}{6,000} = \frac{1,95,000 \times 0.50 - 26,000}{6,000} = \frac{97,500 - 26,000}{6,000} = \frac{71,500}{6,000} = 11.917$$

**Decision Rule:**
$$EPS_Y (11.92) > EPS_X (11.25) \Rightarrow \text{Choose Plan Y (Higher Debt)}$$

**Justification:** When actual EBIT is expected to exceed the indifference point, the **higher-financial-leverage plan (Plan Y)** yields higher EPS due to lower number of equity shares and tax shield on interest.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Board Exam:**

- ✅ **Financial Leverage** = Use of fixed financial charges to magnify EPS changes relative to EBIT changes.
- ✅ **DFL formula (no PD):** $DFL = \dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT - I}$
- ✅ **DFL formula (with PD):** $DFL = \dfrac{EBIT}{EBIT - I - \dfrac{PD}{(1-t)}}$
- ✅ **Tax rate cancels out** in DFL — common board exam conceptual question.
- ✅ **EPS equation:** $EPS = \dfrac{(EBIT - I)(1-t) - PD}{N}$
- ✅ **Favorable leverage condition:** ROI > kd (Return > Cost of Debt).
- ✅ **Financial Break-Even Point:** The level of EBIT at which EPS is just enough to cover the fixed charges (i.e., DFL denominator equals 0, mathematically).
- ✅ **Indifference Point:** EBIT level where two financing plans produce identical EPS — use the **full EPS equation with tax and PD** for accuracy.
- ✅ **Trading on Equity** = Strategic use of debt when ROI > kd.
- ✅ **Higher DFL = Higher Financial Risk** = Higher variability of EPS.
- ✅ **Combined Leverage (DCL) = DOL × DFL** — tested in cross-module questions.
- ✅ **Always verify** IP by substituting back into both plans' EPS equations.
- ✅ **Units:** EBIT in ₹, DFL as a pure number, EPS in ₹ per share, N as number of shares.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Leverage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Total Leverage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Leverage (Combined Leverage)** is the leverage that measures the combined effect of **Fixed Operating Costs** and **Fixed Financing Costs (Interest)** on the **Earnings Per Share (EPS)** of a firm, resulting from a proportionate change in sales (or sales volume).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Total Leverage is quantified by the **Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL)**, which is mathematically defined as the ratio of the **percentage change in EPS** to the **percentage change in Sales (or Sales Volume)**.

The Degree of Combined Leverage is also expressed as the product of the Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL) and the Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL):

$$\text{DCL} = \text{DOL} \times \text{DFL}$$

Where:
- **DOL** = Degree of Operating Leverage = % Change in EBIT / % Change in Sales
- **DFL** = Degree of Financial Leverage = % Change in EPS / % Change in EBIT

The **DCL** is a critical risk-assessment metric used by financial managers to understand the **total business risk** and **total financial risk** faced by an organization, forming the basis for **Capital Structure** and **Capital Budgeting** decisions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a **double-decker bus**:
- The **engineer's brake** (the first lever) represents **Operating Leverage** — it amplifies the pressure on the road (sales) into a much larger motion in the wheels (EBIT).
- The **gear system** (the second lever) represents **Financial Leverage** — it amplifies the wheel motion (EBIT) into the final car speed (EPS).
- The **combined bus system** is **Total (Combined) Leverage** — the pressure from the engineer on the brake is amplified twice: first by the brake linkage, then by the gear system, producing a massive final speed output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example:** Consider a company like **Reliance Industries**. A small fluctuation of just **2% in revenue (sales)** can be magnified by their massive fixed asset base (high operating leverage) and high debt (high financial leverage), causing the EPS to move by **8-10%** in either direction. This multiplied effect on the bottom line is precisely the Total Leverage.

## 1.3 Standard KTU Metrics and Notations

The standard boundary metrics used in KTU 2024 problem statements for Total Leverage are:

- **Contribution** (C) = **Sales** − **Variable Costs (VC)** — measured in monetary units (₹ or ₹ Lakhs).
- **EBIT (Earnings Before Interest and Taxes)** = **Contribution** − **Fixed Operating Costs (F)** — also known as Operating Profit.
- **EBT (Earnings Before Tax)** = **EBIT** − **Interest (I)**.
- **EPS** = (EBT − Tax) / Number of Equity Shares (N).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Total Leverage is universally tested in KTU examinations as a numerical problem under the topic of "Leverage and Capital Structure." Students must remember that **DOL × DFL = DCL**, and any of these formulas can be used to solve numericals.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

Total Leverage integrates the theoretical foundations of **Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis** and **Capital Structure Theory**. The theoretical logic can be broken down into the following structured steps:

### Step 1: Establish the Sales-to-EPS Chain
The income statement of any firm follows a strict hierarchical chain of deductions:

1. **Sales Revenue** (₹)
2. − **Variable Costs** (₹) → yields **Contribution**
3. − **Fixed Operating Costs (F)** → yields **EBIT** (Operating Profit)
4. − **Interest (I)** → yields **EBT** (Earnings Before Tax)
5. − **Tax** → yields **Net Income (PAT)**
6. ÷ **Number of Equity Shares (N)** → yields **EPS**

### Step 2: Identify the Two Fixed-Cost Layers
- **Layer 1 — Fixed Operating Costs (F):** These are the firm's fixed production and overhead costs (e.g., rent, depreciation, salaries). They are fixed regardless of how the firm is financed. This layer is governed by **Operating Leverage**.
- **Layer 2 — Fixed Financing Costs / Interest (I):** These are the fixed interest obligations arising from debt financing. They are fixed regardless of the firm's production level. This layer is governed by **Financial Leverage**.

### Step 3: Combine the Two Amplification Effects
Because both layers of fixed costs amplify the effect of sales changes on EPS, the **Total Leverage (DCL)** captures the **cumulative amplification factor**. The chain of cause and effect is:

> **% Change in Sales → amplified by DOL → % Change in EBIT → amplified by DFL → % Change in EPS**

The 'Why' is straightforward: a high DCL means the firm is **highly risky** (small positive change in sales = large positive change in EPS, but small negative change in sales = large negative change in EPS, possibly leading to insolvency). The 'How' is by multiplying DOL and DFL, we capture the **overall risk profile** of the firm.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table provides all essential formulas, boundary conditions, and units required for solving KTU 2024 Total Leverage problems.

| **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Notation Key** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Degree of Combined Leverage (Definition) | $\text{DCL} = \dfrac{\% \text{ Change in EPS}}{\% \text{ Change in Sales}}$ | EPS = Earnings Per Share, Sales in ₹ | Pure Ratio (Number) |
| DCL via Multiplication | $\text{DCL} = \text{DOL} \times \text{DFL}$ | DOL = Degree of Operating Leverage, DFL = Degree of Financial Leverage | Pure Ratio (Number) |
| DCL via Contribution/EBT | $\text{DCL} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBT}}$ | Contribution = Q(P−V), EBT = Q(P−V)−F−I | Pure Ratio (Number) |
| DCL via Contribution/EBT (Expanded) | $\text{DCL} = \dfrac{Q \times (P-V)}{Q \times (P-V) - F - I}$ | Q = Units sold, P = Price/unit, V = Variable Cost/unit, F = Fixed Cost, I = Interest | Pure Ratio (Number) |
| DOL (Operating Leverage) | $\text{DOL} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT}}$ | EBIT = Contribution − Fixed Cost | Pure Ratio (Number) |
| DFL (Financial Leverage) | $\text{DFL} = \dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{EBT}}$ | EBIT / (EBIT − I) | Pure Ratio (Number) |
| New EPS after Sales Change | $\text{New EPS} = \text{Old EPS} \times \left[ 1 + (\text{DCL} \times \% \Delta \text{Sales}) \right]$ | Standard KTU Numerical Trick | ₹ per share |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Boundary Rule:** A DCL = 1 occurs when Fixed Costs (F) and Interest (I) are both zero. A DCL of **infinity** occurs when EBT = 0 (the firm is at its break-even point for EBT). Between these extremes, the DCL is always **positive** for a profitable firm.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Industry

Total Leverage is not a purely academic concept — it is actively used in industry for:

- **Risk Assessment by Banks:** Banks computing **Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR)** use the DCL to evaluate how volatile a borrower's EPS will be in recessionary conditions.
- **Capital Budgeting:** Multinational firms like **Tata Steel** and **Infosys** use DCL to determine the "right mix" of debt vs. equity for new projects.
- **Project Engineering Finance:** In large infrastructure projects (e.g., **GIFT City** or **Highway BOT Projects**), contractors use leverage to determine bid prices and risk premiums.
- **Start-up Valuation:** Angel investors and VC firms use leverage metrics to assess how risky a start-up's capital structure is.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: DCL = DOL × DFL (The Product Rule)

This is the **fundamental theorem of combined leverage** that KTU examiners frequently test.

**Step 1:** Begin with the formal definitions.

$$\text{DOL} = \frac{\% \text{ Change in EBIT}}{\% \text{ Change in Sales}}$$

**Step 2:** Write the DFL by definition.

$$\text{DFL} = \frac{\% \text{ Change in EPS}}{\% \text{ Change in EBIT}}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply DOL and DFL.

$$\text{DOL} \times \text{DFL} = \frac{\% \text{ Change in EBIT}}{\% \text{ Change in Sales}} \times \frac{\% \text{ Change in EPS}}{\% \text{ Change in EBIT}}$$

**Step 4:** Cancel the "% Change in EBIT" numerator and denominator (provided EBIT ≠ 0).

$$\text{DOL} \times \text{DFL} = \frac{\% \text{ Change in EPS}}{\% \text{ Change in Sales}}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize that the resulting ratio is, by definition, the DCL.

$$\therefore \quad \text{DCL} = \text{DOL} \times \text{DFL}$$

> **Logic Explanation:** The cancellation of "% Change in EBIT" works because EBIT appears as the *output* of Operating Leverage and the *input* to Financial Leverage. It is the "bridge" variable in the sales-to-EPS chain.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: DCL = Contribution / EBT

This is the **most used KTU formula** for numerical problems. We derive it from the basic income statement.

**Step 1:** Start with the income statement (in terms of per-unit Q):

$$\text{Sales} = Q \times P$$

$$\text{Variable Cost} = Q \times V$$

**Step 2:** Compute Contribution.

$$\text{Contribution} = Q \times P - Q \times V = Q \times (P - V)$$

**Step 3:** Compute EBIT by deducting fixed operating costs.

$$\text{EBIT} = Q \times (P - V) - F$$

**Step 4:** Compute EBT by deducting interest.

$$\text{EBT} = Q \times (P - V) - F - I$$

**Step 5:** Write the DOL formula.

$$\text{DOL} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT}} = \frac{Q \times (P - V)}{Q \times (P - V) - F}$$

**Step 6:** Write the DFL formula.

$$\text{DFL} = \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{EBT}} = \frac{Q \times (P - V) - F}{Q \times (P - V) - F - I}$$

**Step 7:** Multiply DOL and DFL.

$$\text{DCL} = \text{DOL} \times \text{DFL} = \frac{Q \times (P - V)}{Q \times (P - V) - F} \times \frac{Q \times (P - V) - F}{Q \times (P - V) - F - I}$$

**Step 8:** Cancel the common term $[Q \times (P - V) - F]$ (i.e., EBIT).

$$\text{DCL} = \frac{Q \times (P - V)}{Q \times (P - V) - F - I}$$

**Step 9:** Substitute Contribution and EBT in place of the long expressions.

$$\boxed{\therefore \quad \text{DCL} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBT}}}$$

> **Logic Explanation:** The EBIT term cancels out because it is the "transfer point" between operating activities (which produce EBIT) and financing activities (which consume EBIT to pay interest and produce EBT).

---

## 3.3 KTU-Style Solved Numerical Example

**Problem:** A company sells a single product at ₹100 per unit. Variable cost is ₹40 per unit. Fixed operating costs are ₹1,20,000. Interest on debt is ₹40,000. The company sells 6,000 units. Calculate:
(a) Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL)
(b) Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)
(c) Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL)

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1:** Calculate the Sales Revenue.

$$\text{Sales} = Q \times P = 6000 \times 100 = 6,00,000$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the Total Variable Cost.

$$\text{VC} = Q \times V = 6000 \times 40 = 2,40,000$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the Contribution.

$$\text{Contribution} = 6,00,000 - 2,40,000 = 3,60,000$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the EBIT.

$$\text{EBIT} = \text{Contribution} - F = 3,60,000 - 1,20,000 = 2,40,000$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the EBT.

$$\text{EBT} = \text{EBIT} - I = 2,40,000 - 40,000 = 2,00,000$$

**Step 6:** Compute DOL.

$$\text{DOL} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT}} = \frac{3,60,000}{2,40,000} = 1.5$$

**Step 7:** Compute DFL.

$$\text{DFL} = \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{EBT}} = \frac{2,40,000}{2,00,000} = 1.2$$

**Step 8:** Compute DCL via product rule.

$$\text{DCL} = \text{DOL} \times \text{DFL} = 1.5 \times 1.2 = 1.8$$

**Step 9 (Verification):** Compute DCL directly via Contribution/EBT.

$$\text{DCL} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBT}} = \frac{3,60,000}{2,00,000} = 1.8 \quad \checkmark$$

> **Interpretation:** A 1% change in sales will result in a **1.8% change in EPS** (and a 1.8% change in EBT). The firm has moderate combined leverage.

---

## 3.4 Extended Analytical Matrix: Impact of Sales Changes

Using DCL = 1.8 from the above example, the following table summarizes the effect of sales changes on EPS.

| **% Change in Sales** | **Multiplier Factor [1 + (DCL × %ΔS)]** | **New EPS (assuming Old EPS = ₹10)** | **Change in EPS** |
|---|---|---|---|
| +50% | $1 + (1.8 \times 0.50) = 1.90$ | ₹19.00 | +90% |
| +25% | $1 + (1.8 \times 0.25) = 1.45$ | ₹14.50 | +45% |
| +10% | $1 + (1.8 \times 0.10) = 1.18$ | ₹11.80 | +18% |
| 0% | 1.00 | ₹10.00 | 0% |
| −10% | $1 + (1.8 \times -0.10) = 0.82$ | ₹8.20 | −18% |
| −25% | $1 + (1.8 \times -0.25) = 0.55$ | ₹5.50 | −45% |
| −50% | $1 + (1.8 \times -0.50) = 0.10$ | ₹1.00 | −90% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetry of Risk:** The matrix above shows that the **gain** from a sales increase is exactly mirrored by the **loss** from an equivalent sales decrease. This **symmetric amplification** is the defining feature of Total Leverage. KTU examiners often give 2 marks specifically for this conceptual explanation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Sales-to-EPS Amplification Chain (Flow Diagram)

The following Mermaid block illustrates how the two fixed-cost layers (Operating and Financing) act as amplification stages between Sales and EPS.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sInput[/Sales Revenue in Rs/]:::inputNode
    sVarCost[Less: Variable Costs]:::subNode
    sContrib[Contribution in Rs]:::keyNode
    sFixedOp[Less: Fixed Operating Costs F]:::deductNode
    sEBIT[EBIT Operating Profit]:::keyNode
    sInterest[Less: Interest I]:::deductNode
    sEBT[EBT Earnings Before Tax]:::keyNode
    sTax[Less: Tax T]:::deductNode
    sPAT[PAT Net Income]:::keyNode
    sDiv[Divide by Number of Shares N]:::deductNode
    sEPS[EPS]:::outputNode

    sDol1{{DOL Amplifier Stage 1}}:::levNode
    sDfl1{{DFL Amplifier Stage 2}}:::levNode
    sDcl1{{DCL Combined Amplifier}}:::levNode

    sInput --> sVarCost --> sContrib
    sContrib -- "DOL applied here" --> sFixedOp
    sFixedOp --> sEBIT
    sEBIT -- "DFL applied here" --> sInterest
    sInterest --> sEBT
    sEBT --> sTax --> sPAT --> sDiv --> sEPS
    sEPS -- "Total Risk Measured By" --> sDcl1

    sInput -- "Input" --> sDol1
    sDol1 -- "First stage output" --> sDfl1
    sDfl1 -- "Final output" --> sDcl1

    classDef inputNode fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef outputNode fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532D
    classDef keyNode fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350F
    classDef subNode fill:#F1F5F9,stroke:#475569,stroke-width:1px,color:#1E293B
    classDef deductNode fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:1px,color:#7F1D1D
    classDef levNode fill:#FAE8FF,stroke:#86198F,stroke-width:2px,color:#581C87
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** The diagram shows three columns of activity:
- **Left column (top to bottom):** Sales flows down, gets reduced by variable costs, then fixed operating costs, then interest, then tax, then divided by shares to reach EPS.
- **Right column (highlighted):** The three leverage stages — **DOL (first amplifier)**, **DFL (second amplifier)**, and **DCL (combined amplifier)** — act on the chain of deductions.

---

## 4.2 The Architecture of Total Leverage (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph stage1[STAGE 1 Operating Block]
        dirA[Direction of Analysis]:::meta
        in1[Sales Q times P]:::dataNode
        vc1[Variable Cost Q times V]:::dataNode
        contr1[Contribution]:::keyNode
        f1[Fixed Operating Cost F]:::riskNode
        ebit1[EBIT]:::keyNode
        dolBox[DOL equals Contribution divided by EBIT]:::formulaNode
        in1 --> contr1
        vc1 --> contr1
        contr1 --> ebit1
        f1 --> ebit1
        contr1 -.-> dolBox
        ebit1 -.-> dolBox
    end

    subgraph stage2[STAGE 2 Financing Block]
        in2[EBIT from Stage 1]:::dataNode
        int2[Interest I]:::riskNode
        ebt2[EBT]:::keyNode
        dflBox[DFL equals EBIT divided by EBT]:::formulaNode
        in2 --> ebt2
        int2 --> ebt2
        in2 -.-> dflBox
        ebt2 -.-> dflBox
    end

    subgraph stage3[STAGE 3 Combined Output Block]
        eps3[EPS Final Output]:::keyNode
        dclBox[DCL equals DOL times DFL equals Contribution divided by EBT]:::formulaNode
        ebit1 --> in2
        ebt2 --> eps3
        dolBox -.-> dclBox
        dflBox -.-> dclBox
    end

    classDef dataNode fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,stroke-width:1px,color:#1E3A8A
    classDef keyNode fill:#FEF9C3,stroke:#A16207,stroke-width:2px,color:#713F12
    classDef riskNode fill:#FECACA,stroke:#991B1B,stroke-width:1px,color:#7F1D1D
    classDef formulaNode fill:#EDE9FE,stroke:#6D28D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#4C1D95
    classDef metaNode fill:#F1F5F9,stroke:#475569,stroke-width:1px,color:#1E293B
```

**Block Diagram Interpretation:** This three-stage architecture visualizes how the calculation of DCL logically flows through:
- **STAGE 1 (Operating Block):** Computes DOL using Contribution and EBIT.
- **STAGE 2 (Financing Block):** Takes the EBIT output from Stage 1, deducts Interest, computes EBT, and calculates DFL.
- **STAGE 3 (Combined Block):** Receives the EPS, combines DOL and DFL using the product rule, and yields the final DCL.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two diagrams?** The first (flow chart) emphasizes the **flow of money** through the income statement. The second (block diagram) emphasizes the **flow of logic** for computing the leverage metric. KTU students who understand both perspectives score higher in numerical problems and theoretical questions alike.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define the term "Degree of Combined Leverage" (DCL). State its significance in financial decision-making.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 Marks):** The **Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL)** is the ratio of the percentage change in Earnings Per Share (EPS) to the percentage change in Sales (or Sales Volume). It measures the **total business and financial risk** of a firm arising from the combined presence of fixed operating costs and fixed financing costs (interest).
>
> $$\text{DCL} = \frac{\% \text{ Change in EPS}}{\% \text{ Change in Sales}}$$
>
> **Significance (1 Mark):** DCL helps financial managers evaluate the **total risk profile** of a firm, which is critical for designing the **optimal capital structure** and for **forecasting EPS** under different sales scenarios. A higher DCL implies higher risk and vice versa.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: "Total Leverage is the product of Operating Leverage and Financial Leverage." Justify this statement.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Justification (3 Marks):** This statement is mathematically and logically correct. The Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL) is the **product** of the Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL) and the Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL). The justification is that:
>
> 1. **Operating Leverage** (DOL) amplifies the effect of sales changes on **EBIT** (i.e., %ΔEBIT = DOL × %ΔSales).
> 2. **Financial Leverage** (DFL) amplifies the effect of EBIT changes on **EPS** (i.e., %ΔEPS = DFL × %ΔEBIT).
> 3. **Combining** both: %ΔEPS = DFL × (DOL × %ΔSales) = (DOL × DFL) × %ΔSales.
> 4. Therefore, by definition, the multiplier DOL × DFL = %ΔEPS / %ΔSales = **DCL**.
>
> $$\therefore \text{DCL} = \text{DOL} \times \text{DFL}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Numerical Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]

**A company ABC Ltd. provides the following data for the year ending 31st March 2024:**

| **Particulars** | **Amount (₹)** |
|---|---|
| Sales (10,000 units @ ₹200) | 20,00,000 |
| Variable Cost | 12,00,000 |
| Fixed Operating Costs | 4,00,000 |
| Interest on 12% Debentures (₹10,00,000) | 1,20,000 |

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL), Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL), and Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL).

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** If the company proposes to increase its sales by 20%, compute the new EPS. Also state what happens to the EPS if the sales decrease by 15%. The company has 1,00,000 equity shares of ₹10 each, and the tax rate is 30%.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute Contribution.

$$\text{Contribution} = \text{Sales} - \text{Variable Cost} = 20,00,000 - 12,00,000 = 8,00,000$$

**Step 2:** Compute EBIT.

$$\text{EBIT} = \text{Contribution} - \text{Fixed Operating Costs} = 8,00,000 - 4,00,000 = 4,00,000$$

**Step 3:** Compute EBT.

$$\text{EBT} = \text{EBIT} - \text{Interest} = 4,00,000 - 1,20,000 = 2,80,000$$

**Step 4:** Compute DOL.

$$\text{DOL} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT}} = \frac{8,00,000}{4,00,000} = 2.0$$

**Step 5:** Compute DFL.

$$\text{DFL} = \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{EBT}} = \frac{4,00,000}{2,80,000} = 1.4286$$

**Step 6:** Compute DCL (Product Rule).

$$\text{DCL} = \text{DOL} \times \text{DFL} = 2.0 \times 1.4286 = 2.8571$$

**Step 6 (Verification):** DCL via Contribution/EBT.

$$\text{DCL} = \frac{8,00,000}{2,80,000} = 2.8571 \quad \checkmark$$

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the original EPS.

$$\text{PAT} = \text{EBT} \times (1 - t) = 2,80,000 \times (1 - 0.30) = 1,96,000$$

$$\text{EPS}_{\text{old}} = \frac{\text{PAT}}{\text{N}} = \frac{1,96,000}{1,00,000} = ₹1.96$$

**Step 2:** Compute the new EPS if sales increase by 20%.

$$\text{New EPS} = \text{Old EPS} \times \left[1 + (\text{DCL} \times \% \Delta \text{Sales})\right]$$

$$\text{New EPS} = 1.96 \times \left[1 + (2.8571 \times 0.20)\right] = 1.96 \times \left[1 + 0.5714\right] = 1.96 \times 1.5714$$

$$\text{New EPS} = ₹3.08$$

**Step 3:** Compute the new EPS if sales decrease by 15%.

$$\text{New EPS} = 1.96 \times \left[1 + (2.8571 \times -0.15)\right] = 1.96 \times \left[1 - 0.4286\right] = 1.96 \times 0.5714$$

$$\text{New EPS} = ₹1.12$$

**Step 4 (Interpretation):** A 20% increase in sales results in a **57.14% increase in EPS** (from ₹1.96 to ₹3.08). Conversely, a 15% decrease in sales results in a **42.86% decrease in EPS** (from ₹1.96 to ₹1.12). This is the **amplification effect of Total Leverage**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**XYZ Industries has the following financial data:**

| **Particulars** | **Amount (₹ in Lakhs)** |
|---|---|
| Sales Revenue | 600 |
| Variable Costs | 360 |
| Fixed Costs | 120 |
| Interest Expense | 40 |
| Equity Share Capital (₹100 each) | 400 (i.e., 4,00,000 shares) |
| Tax Rate | 25% |

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the Degree of Combined Leverage (DCL) using all three approaches (Definition, Product Rule, and Contribution/EBT). Show that all three give the same answer.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** The management is evaluating a new financing plan that would increase interest expense to ₹80 Lakhs but reduce fixed costs to ₹100 Lakhs. Recalculate DCL under the new plan and comment on whether the company should accept the new plan from a risk perspective.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the key income statement values.

$$\text{Contribution} = 600 - 360 = 240 \text{ Lakhs}$$

$$\text{EBIT} = 240 - 120 = 120 \text{ Lakhs}$$

$$\text{EBT} = 120 - 40 = 80 \text{ Lakhs}$$

$$\text{PAT} = 80 \times (1 - 0.25) = 60 \text{ Lakhs}$$

$$\text{EPS}_{\text{old}} = \frac{60,00,000}{4,00,000} = ₹15$$

**Step 2 (Approach 1 — Contribution/EBT):**

$$\text{DCL} = \frac{240}{80} = 3.0$$

**Step 3 (Approach 2 — Product Rule):**

$$\text{DOL} = \frac{240}{120} = 2.0, \quad \text{DFL} = \frac{120}{80} = 1.5$$

$$\text{DCL} = 2.0 \times 1.5 = 3.0$$

**Step 4 (Approach 3 — Definition via EPS change):**

Suppose sales increase by 10%. Then:
- New Sales = 660
- New VC = 396
- New Contribution = 264
- New EBIT = 264 − 120 = 144
- New EBT = 144 − 40 = 104
- New PAT = 104 × 0.75 = 78
- New EPS = 78/4 = ₹19.5
- % Change in EPS = (19.5 − 15)/15 = 30%

$$\text{DCL} = \frac{30\%}{10\%} = 3.0 \quad \checkmark$$

All three approaches give **DCL = 3.0**, confirming consistency.

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Recompute key values under the new plan.

$$\text{Contribution} = 600 - 360 = 240 \text{ Lakhs (unchanged)}$$

$$\text{New EBIT} = 240 - 100 = 140 \text{ Lakhs}$$

$$\text{New EBT} = 140 - 80 = 60 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the new DCL.

$$\text{New DCL} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{New EBT}} = \frac{240}{60} = 4.0$$

**Step 3:** Comparison and Risk Comment.

| **Metric** | **Old Plan** | **New Plan** | **Change** |
|---|---|---|---|
| DCL | 3.0 | 4.0 | +33.33% |
| DFL | 1.5 | 140/60 = 2.33 | +55.56% |
| DOL | 2.0 | 240/140 = 1.71 | −14.29% |

**Comment (Required for Full Marks):** The new plan **increases the DCL from 3.0 to 4.0**, meaning the firm's total risk has **risen by 33.33%**. While the firm benefits from a lower DOL (because fixed operating costs have reduced), the **rise in DFL due to higher interest expense dominates**, pushing the DCL upward. From a **risk-averse** perspective, the company should **reject** the new plan unless the expected sales growth is significantly high to compensate for the increased leverage. From a **shareholder wealth maximization** perspective, the plan may only be justified if the management is highly confident of robust sales growth.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Tax Effect:** Many KTU students compute DCL using only EBIT and forget the tax. The DCL formula uses **EBT (Earnings Before Tax)**, not PAT, in the denominator. Using PAT will give an **incorrect** answer.
> 2. **Sign of % Change in Sales:** When sales *decrease*, the % change is **negative**. A common mistake is using a positive sign for both increases and decreases. Always use the algebraic sign of the change.
> 3. **Confusing DOL, DFL, and DCL Formulas:** Students often mix up the formulas. Remember: **DOL = Contribution/EBIT, DFL = EBIT/EBT, DCL = Contribution/EBT**. A quick memory trick: **"C-E, E-E, C-T"** (Contribution-EBIT, EBIT-EBT, Contribution-EBT).
> 4. **Missing the Verification Step:** KTU examiners award bonus marks when students verify the DCL by both product rule AND contribution/EBT formula. Always do both!
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** If sales are in lakhs and shares are in absolute numbers, ensure that PAT is converted to absolute rupees (multiply lakhs by 1,00,000) before dividing by the number of shares.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following rapid-revision checklist summarizes all critical concepts for the Total Leverage topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Recall Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Definition to Memorize:** DCL = % Change in EPS / % Change in Sales. It is the **combined effect of operating and financial leverage**.

- **Master Formula:** $\text{DCL} = \text{DOL} \times \text{DFL} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBT}}$.

- **Income Statement Hierarchy:** Sales → (−Variable Cost) → Contribution → (−Fixed Operating Cost) → EBIT → (−Interest) → EBT → (−Tax) → PAT → ÷Shares → EPS.

- **Three Forms of DCL Formula:** (1) Definition form (% change), (2) Product form (DOL × DFL), (3) Income Statement form (Contribution / EBT). All three are **algebraically equivalent**.

- **Risk Interpretation:** A **high DCL** = high total risk; a **low DCL** = low total risk. DCL is **symmetric** — gains from sales increases equal losses from equivalent sales decreases.

- **Boundary Conditions:** If F = 0 and I = 0, then DCL = 1 (no leverage). If EBT = 0, then DCL → infinity (infinite leverage at the financial break-even point).

- **Numerical Trick:** New EPS = Old EPS × [1 + (DCL × % Change in Sales)]. This single-line formula can solve 80% of KTU numericals on this topic.

- **Symmetry of Risk:** DCL applies to both upside and downside. A 20% sales rise with DCL = 2 implies a 40% EPS rise; a 20% sales fall implies a 40% EPS fall.

- **For Capital Structure Decisions:** Firms with stable cash flows (e.g., utilities, FMCG) can afford high DCL. Firms with volatile cash flows (e.g., start-ups, luxury goods) should maintain low DCL.

- **Past Year Tag Recap:** Total Leverage has appeared in **KTU Dec 2023**, **KTU May 2023**, and **KTU Dec 2024 (model)** question papers. Expect a 7-mark numerical or a 14-mark Part B question in upcoming exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Indifference Analysis in Leverage Study

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Financial Leverage — The Formal Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, **Financial Leverage** is formally defined as the employment of fixed-cost financing sources (such as debt and preference capital) in the capital structure with the objective of magnifying the **Earnings Per Share (EPS)** and consequently the return on equity. The presence of fixed financial charges (Interest on Debt + Preference Dividend) creates a leveraged structure where a small change in **Earnings Before Interest and Taxes (EBIT)** results in a magnified change in EPS.

Mathematically, the **Degree of Financial Leverage (DFL)** is:

$$\text{DFL} = \frac{\% \text{ change in EPS}}{\% \text{ change in EBIT}} = \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{EBIT} - I - \dfrac{D_p}{(1-t)}}$$

Where **I** = Total Interest, **D_p** = Preference Dividend, and **t** = Tax Rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Financial leverage is the *only* leverage concept that depends on the **capital structure decision** of the firm. Operating leverage depends on cost structure, while combined leverage integrates both.

## 1.2 Indifference Point (Break-Even EBIT) — The Formal Definition

The **Indifference Point** in capital structure / leverage study is the specific level of **Earnings Before Interest and Taxes (EBIT)** at which two (or more) alternative financing plans produce **exactly the same Earnings Per Share (EPS)**. Below this level, one plan is preferable; above this level, the other plan is preferable.

In simple, exam-ready language:

> **"The Indifference Point is that critical EBIT level where the shareholder is indifferent between two financing alternatives because EPS is identical under both."**

The point is also called the **Break-Even EBIT** or **Equilibrium Level of EBIT**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine you are standing at the top of a hill with two paths leading down — **Path A** (no debt) and **Path B** (heavy debt). At very low altitudes (low EBIT), the steeper Path B is dangerous because interest obligations are heavy. At very high altitudes (high EBIT), Path B gets you down much faster (higher EPS) because the fixed interest gets spread thin over high profits. There is a **specific altitude** (the Indifference Point) where both paths meet and give the same descent rate (same EPS). Below it, go A; above it, go B.

A real-world analogy: Consider two taxi drivers — one rents a car (no debt, high fixed rent built in), and the other buys one on loan (lower rent, but EMI is mandatory). On a very bad day with few rides, the loan-buyer is in trouble. On a great day, the loan-buyer keeps more money. The **indifference day** is the exact number of rides where both end up with the same profit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Indifference Analysis does NOT tell you the *best* plan in absolute terms. It only identifies the *switching point* between two competing plans based on expected EBIT.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Indifference Analysis

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Convention |
|---|---|---|
| **Earnings Before Interest & Tax** | **EBIT** | The variable being solved for |
| **Total Interest** | **I** | Sum of all interest obligations |
| **Preference Dividend** | **D_p** | Fixed dividend on preference shares |
| **Tax Rate** | **t** | Usually **30%** in KTU problems (unless stated) |
| **Number of Equity Shares** | **N** | Outstanding equity shares under each plan |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Purpose of Indifference Analysis

The fundamental purpose of indifference analysis is to assist the financial manager in selecting the **optimal capital structure** by comparing alternative financing plans across different possible EBIT scenarios. The logic unfolds as follows:

- **Step 1 — Identify Alternatives:** Two (or more) financing plans are proposed (e.g., Plan 1 = all-equity, Plan 2 = debt-equity mix, Plan 3 = high-debt mix).
- **Step 2 — Compute Indifference EBIT:** For every pair of plans, find the EBIT at which EPS is equal.
- **Step 3 — Compare with Expected EBIT:** Place the expected/projected EBIT on the scale.
- **Step 4 — Decide:** If expected EBIT > Indifference EBIT → choose the **more leveraged** plan. If expected EBIT < Indifference EBIT → choose the **less leveraged** plan.

## 2.2 The Core Formula — Setting EPS Equal

The foundational equation of indifference analysis is:

$$\text{EPS}_1 = \text{EPS}_2$$

$$\frac{(\text{EBIT} - I_1)(1-t) - D_{p1}}{N_1} = \frac{(\text{EBIT} - I_2)(1-t) - D_{p2}}{N_2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Logic:** We deliberately **ignore the (1-t) tax factor on preference dividend** for simplification in some textbooks, but the **rigorous KTU formula** retains the pre-tax subtraction of preference dividend. Always check the problem statement for tax adjustment conventions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **EPS Equation (General)** | $\text{EPS} = \dfrac{(\text{EBIT} - I)(1-t) - D_p}{N}$ | Foundational EPS calculation |
| 2 | **Indifference Equation** | $\dfrac{(\text{EBIT} - I_1)(1-t) - D_{p1}}{N_1} = \dfrac{(\text{EBIT} - I_2)(1-t) - D_{p2}}{N_2}$ | Solving for break-even EBIT |
| 3 | **Degree of Financial Leverage** | $\text{DFL} = \dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{EBIT} - I - \dfrac{D_p}{(1-t)}}$ | Sensitivity of EPS to EBIT |
| 4 | **Decision Rule (Above)** | If Expected EBIT > Indifference EBIT → Choose **Higher Leverage** | More debt amplifies returns |
| 5 | **Decision Rule (Below)** | If Expected EBIT < Indifference EBIT → Choose **Lower Leverage** | Less debt reduces risk |
| 6 | **Simplified Indifference (No Pref. Div.)** | $\dfrac{(\text{EBIT} - I_1)(1-t)}{N_1} = \dfrac{(\text{EBIT} - I_2)(1-t)}{N_2}$ | When $D_{p1} = D_{p2} = 0$ |
| 7 | **Operating Leverage (Reference)** | $\text{DOL} = \dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{EBIT}}$ | Module 2 reference for Combined Leverage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In corporate finance, indifference analysis is the *graphical* and *algebraic* equivalent of the **break-even analysis** used in engineering economics. It maps the trade-off between risk (fixed charges) and reward (EPS magnification) — a classic engineering optimization problem.

## 2.4 Assumptions Underlying Indifference Analysis

For the model to hold valid in KTU-style problem solving, the following assumptions must be satisfied:

1. **Constant tax rate** ($t$) across the relevant range of EBIT.
2. **Fixed number of equity shares** ($N$) under each plan (no new issuances mid-year).
3. **All earnings are distributed** (no retention — entire net income flows to dividends).
4. **Two plans are mutually exclusive** — only one can be chosen.
5. **The cost of debt** ($k_d$) is independent of the amount borrowed (i.e., interest rate is constant).
6. **No transaction or flotation costs** on either plan.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Many students mistakenly apply indifference analysis when comparing plans with **different total investment amounts**. The model assumes the *same total funds raised* — only the *mix* differs. Violating this assumption invalidates the comparison.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Indifference Point Formula

We start from the basic EPS identity. For any financing plan $i$:

$$\text{EPS}_i = \frac{\text{Earnings available to equity shareholders}_i}{N_i}$$

Earnings available to equity shareholders = Net profit after tax (NPAT) − Preference Dividend.

$$\text{NPAT}_i = (\text{EBIT} - I_i)(1 - t)$$

Therefore:

$$\text{EPS}_i = \frac{(\text{EBIT} - I_i)(1 - t) - D_{pi}}{N_i}$$

For the indifference point between Plan 1 and Plan 2:

$$\text{EPS}_1 = \text{EPS}_2$$

$$\frac{(\text{EBIT} - I_1)(1 - t) - D_{p1}}{N_1} = \frac{(\text{EBIT} - I_2)(1 - t) - D_{p2}}{N_2}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$[(\text{EBIT} - I_1)(1 - t) - D_{p1}] \cdot N_2 = [(\text{EBIT} - I_2)(1 - t) - D_{p2}] \cdot N_1$$

This is the **working equation** for solving any indifference problem. Expand and collect EBIT terms on one side:

$$\text{EBIT} \cdot (1-t)(N_2 - N_1) = I_1(1-t)N_2 - I_2(1-t)N_1 + D_{p1}N_2 - D_{p2}N_1$$

Solving for EBIT (the indifference point):

$$\text{EBIT}^* = \frac{I_1(1-t)N_2 - I_2(1-t)N_1 + D_{p1}N_2 - D_{p2}N_1}{(1-t)(N_2 - N_1)}$$

For the common KTU case where $D_{p1} = D_{p2} = 0$ (no preference shares) and $t$ is constant, this simplifies to:

$$\text{EBIT}^* = \frac{I_1 N_2 - I_2 N_1}{N_2 - N_1}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Pattern

**Problem Statement:**

> *Zenith Ltd. requires ₹ 20,00,000 for a project. The Finance Manager has proposed two alternative financing plans:*
>
> *Plan A: Entirely equity — Issue 20,000 equity shares at ₹ 100 each.*
> *Plan B: Debt-Equity Mix — Issue 10,000 equity shares at ₹ 100 each and borrow ₹ 10,00,000 at 10% interest.*
>
> *Tax rate = 30%. Find the Indifference Point. If the expected EBIT is ₹ 4,00,000, which plan should be selected?*

### Step 1: Identify the Parameters

| Parameter | Plan A | Plan B |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Equity Shares ($N$) | 20,000 | 10,000 |
| Total Interest ($I$) | 0 | ₹ 10,00,000 × 10% = ₹ 1,00,000 |
| Preference Dividend ($D_p$) | 0 | 0 |
| Tax Rate ($t$) | 30% | 30% |

### Step 2: Set Up the Indifference Equation

$$\frac{(\text{EBIT} - 0)(1 - 0.30) - 0}{20{,}000} = \frac{(\text{EBIT} - 1{,}00{,}000)(1 - 0.30) - 0}{10{,}000}$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Numerators

The $(1-t) = 0.70$ factor appears on both sides. **Cancelling (1-t) is valid when it is identical on both sides**, so we can simplify:

$$\frac{\text{EBIT}}{20{,}000} = \frac{\text{EBIT} - 1{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000}$$

### Step 4: Cross-Multiply

$$\text{EBIT} \times 10{,}000 = (\text{EBIT} - 1{,}00{,}000) \times 20{,}000$$

$$10{,}000 \cdot \text{EBIT} = 20{,}00{,}000 \cdot \text{EBIT} - 20{,}00{,}00{,}00{,}000$$

Wait — let me re-derive carefully:

$$10{,}000 \cdot \text{EBIT} = 20{,}000 \cdot \text{EBIT} - 20{,}000 \cdot 1{,}00{,}000$$

$$10{,}000 \cdot \text{EBIT} = 20{,}000 \cdot \text{EBIT} - 20{,}00{,}00{,}000$$

### Step 5: Collect EBIT Terms

Rearranging:

$$20{,}00{,}00{,}000 = 20{,}000 \cdot \text{EBIT} - 10{,}000 \cdot \text{EBIT}$$

$$20{,}00{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}000 \cdot \text{EBIT}$$

### Step 6: Solve for EBIT*

$$\text{EBIT}^* = \frac{20{,}00{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000} = 2{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{Indifference EBIT} = ₹ 2{,}00{,}000}$$

### Step 7: Verify the Indifference Point

Under Plan A at EBIT = ₹ 2,00,000:

$$\text{EPS}_A = \frac{(2{,}00{,}000 - 0)(0.70)}{20{,}000} = \frac{1{,}40{,}000}{20{,}000} = ₹ 7$$

Under Plan B at EBIT = ₹ 2,00,000:

$$\text{EPS}_B = \frac{(2{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000)(0.70)}{10{,}000} = \frac{70{,}000}{10{,}000} = ₹ 7$$

✅ **Verification Successful — Both plans yield EPS = ₹ 7 at the indifference point.**

### Step 8: Apply the Decision Rule

Expected EBIT = ₹ 4,00,000 > Indifference EBIT = ₹ 2,00,000.

**Decision: Choose Plan B (Higher Leverage / Debt-Equity Mix).**

Let us verify:

$$\text{EPS}_A = \frac{(4{,}00{,}000)(0.70)}{20{,}000} = \frac{2{,}80{,}000}{20{,}000} = ₹ 14$$

$$\text{EPS}_B = \frac{(4{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000)(0.70)}{10{,}000} = \frac{2{,}10{,}000}{10{,}000} = ₹ 21$$

✅ Plan B yields ₹ 21 > Plan A yields ₹ 14. Decision confirmed.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table Template (For Exam Use)

| EBIT Level (₹) | EPS – Plan A (₹) | EPS – Plan B (₹) | Better Plan |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1,00,000 | 3.50 | 0.00 | Plan A |
| 2,00,000 *(Indifference)* | 7.00 | 7.00 | Either |
| 3,00,000 | 10.50 | 14.00 | Plan B |
| 4,00,000 *(Expected)* | 14.00 | 21.00 | Plan B |
| 5,00,000 | 17.50 | 28.00 | Plan B |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The gap between EPS values widens as EBIT moves away from the indifference point. This is the **magnification effect** of financial leverage — the further you are from the indifference point, the larger the EPS differential.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Including Preference Shares

**Problem Statement:**

> *Trident Corp. is evaluating two financing plans to raise ₹ 30,00,000:*
> *Plan X: 12% Debt of ₹ 15,00,000 + Equity ₹ 15,00,000 (1,50,000 shares)*
> *Plan Y: 10% Preference Shares ₹ 10,00,000 + 12% Debt ₹ 5,00,000 + Equity ₹ 15,00,000 (1,00,000 shares)*
> *Tax rate = 25%. Compute the indifference point.*

### Step 1: Tabulate Parameters

| Parameter | Plan X | Plan Y |
|---|---|---|
| Equity Shares ($N$) | 1,50,000 | 1,00,000 |
| Interest ($I$) | ₹ 15,00,000 × 12% = ₹ 1,80,000 | ₹ 5,00,000 × 12% = ₹ 60,000 |
| Pref. Dividend ($D_p$) | 0 | ₹ 10,00,000 × 10% = ₹ 1,00,000 |
| Tax Rate ($t$) | 25% | 25% |

### Step 2: Apply the Full Indifference Formula

$$\frac{(\text{EBIT} - 1{,}80{,}000)(0.75) - 0}{1{,}50{,}000} = \frac{(\text{EBIT} - 60{,}000)(0.75) - 1{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000}$$

### Step 3: Cross-Multiply

$$[(\text{EBIT} - 1{,}80{,}000)(0.75)] \cdot 1{,}00{,}000 = [(\text{EBIT} - 60{,}000)(0.75) - 1{,}00{,}000] \cdot 1{,}50{,}000$$

Divide both sides by 1,000 to clean up:

$$[(\text{EBIT} - 1{,}80{,}000)(0.75)] \cdot 100 = [(\text{EBIT} - 60{,}000)(0.75) - 1{,}00{,}000] \cdot 150$$

Let $X = \text{EBIT}$ for brevity:

$$75(X - 1{,}80{,}000) = 112.5(X - 60{,}000) - 15{,}00{,}000$$

$$75X - 1{,}35{,}00{,}000 = 112.5X - 67{,}50{,}000 - 15{,}00{,}000$$

$$75X - 1{,}35{,}00{,}000 = 112.5X - 82{,}50{,}000$$

$$82{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}35{,}00{,}000 = 112.5X - 75X$$

$$-52{,}50{,}000 = 37.5X$$

$$X = \frac{-52{,}50{,}000}{37.5} = -1{,}40{,}000$$

### Step 4: Interpret a Negative Indifference Point

A **negative EBIT** is economically meaningless — it indicates that the two plans **never produce equal EPS** in the positive EBIT range. In this case, **one plan is universally superior to the other** across all positive EBIT scenarios.

To determine which plan is universally better, compute EPS at any positive EBIT (say EBIT = ₹ 5,00,000):

$$\text{EPS}_X = \frac{(5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000)(0.75)}{1{,}50{,}000} = \frac{2{,}40{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} = ₹ 1.60$$

$$\text{EPS}_Y = \frac{(5{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}000)(0.75) - 1{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = \frac{3{,}30{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = ₹ 2.30$$

✅ **Plan Y is universally superior at this EBIT level.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When the indifference EBIT comes out negative, students often mistakenly report it as the answer without realizing it implies **dominance of one plan**. Always provide an interpretive conclusion.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Indifference Analysis Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Two Financing Plans Identified] --> B[Tabulate Parameters N1, N2, I1, I2, Dp1, Dp2, t]
    B --> C[Set EPS1 = EPS2]
    C --> D[Apply Indifference Equation]
    D --> E{Solve for EBIT star}
    E -->|Real and Positive| F[Plot Indifference Point on EBIT-EPS Graph]
    E -->|Negative or Imaginary| G[One Plan Dominates - Identify Universally Superior Plan]
    F --> H{Expected EBIT vs Indifference EBIT}
    H -->|Expected > Indifference| I[Choose HIGHER LEVERAGE Plan]
    H -->|Expected < Indifference| J[Choose LOWER LEVERAGE Plan]
    H -->|Expected = Indifference| K[Either Plan - Investor Indifferent]
    I --> L[END: Optimal Plan Selected]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    G --> L
```

## 4.2 EPS vs EBIT Graphical Architecture (Block-Level Representation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    axisX[EBIT Horizontal Axis] --> pointA[Point A: Zero EBIT Intercept]
    axisX --> pointB[Point B: Indifference EBIT Star]
    axisX --> pointC[Point C: Expected EBIT]
    linePlanA[Line Plan A: Slope equals 0.7 slash N1, Intercept at minus I1 times 0.7 slash N1] --> pointA
    linePlanB[Line Plan B: Slope equals 0.7 slash N2, Intercept at minus I2 times 0.7 slash N2 minus Dp2 slash N2] --> pointA
    linePlanA --- pointB
    linePlanB --- pointB
    pointB --> crossing[Lines Cross at Indifference Point]
    crossing --> region1[Region 1: Low EBIT - Plan A Above]
    crossing --> region2[Region 2: High EBIT - Plan B Above]
    pointC --> region2
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Relationship Between Leverage Concepts

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph "LEVERAGE STUDY FRAMEWORK"
        OP[Operating Leverage DOL] --> COM[Combined Leverage DCL]
        FIN[Financial Leverage DFL] --> COM
        COM --> EPS_DEC[EPS Decision Output]
        IND[Indifference Analysis] --> EPS_DEC
    end
    OP --> |Fixed Operating Costs| OP_DEF[Cost Structure]
    FIN --> |Fixed Financial Charges| FIN_DEF[Capital Structure]
    FIN_DEF --> CAP[Debt Equity Mix]
    CAP --> IND
    IND --> |Break Even EBIT| EPS_DEC
    EPS_DEC --> OPT[Optimal Financing Plan Selected]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In the EPS-EBIT graph, both financing plans start from different intercepts on the Y-axis (intercept = $-(I)(1-t)/N - D_p/N$). The plan with **higher leverage** (Plan B in our example) has a **steeper slope** (since $0.7/N$ is larger when $N$ is smaller) and a **more negative intercept**. The lines cross exactly once at the indifference point.
