<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)** is defined as the **weighted average of the after-tax cost of each category of capital (equity, preference share, debentures, term loans, retained earnings, etc.)**, where the weights represent the proportion of each source of capital in the firm's total capital structure.

Mathematically, the standard KTU-accepted representation is:

$$
K_{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_{i} \cdot W_{i}
$$

Where:
- $K_{w}$ = Overall (weighted average) cost of capital
- $K_{i}$ = After-tax cost of the $i^{th}$ source of capital
- $W_{i}$ = Proportion (weight) of the $i^{th}$ source in the total capital structure
- $n$ = Total number of distinct capital sources

**Boundary Condition (Fundamental Identity):**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_{i} = 1.0 \quad \text{(i.e., 100\%)}
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Capital Smoothie" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of WACC like making a **fruit smoothie** at a café. The final smoothie price (WACC) is NOT simply the average price of each fruit. It is weighted by how much of each fruit you pour into the blender. If you use 70% mango and 30% banana, the smoothie price is **(0.70 × Mango Price) + (0.30 × Banana Price)**. Each fruit's "contribution" determines the final blended cost.

A firm raises money from **multiple sources** (equity, debt, preference capital). Each source has its **own cost** (interest rate for lenders, dividend expectation for shareholders). The firm cannot simply take the arithmetic mean because the proportions of each source matter. The **true blended cost** of all financing is the **WACC**.

## 1.3 Why WACC is a KTU-Critical Concept

| Strategic Role | Explanation |
|----------------|-------------|
| **Discount Rate** in DCF | Used to discount future cash flows in NPV and IRR analysis |
| **Accept–Reject Benchmark** | Projects with return > WACC add value; below WACC destroy value |
| **Capital Structure Tool** | Helps decide the optimal debt-equity mix |
| **Performance Hurdle** | Acts as the minimum acceptable rate of return for the firm |
| **Valuation Anchor** | Used in firm valuation models like FCFE / FCFF discounting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric in KTU Problems:** WACC is generally expressed as a **percentage (%)** and **after-tax**, because interest on debt is a tax-deductible expense under corporate tax laws, while dividend payments are not. The **corporate tax rate** is typically denoted as **$t$** or **$T$** in KTU question papers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of WACC Computation

A firm typically raises capital from three primary sources. Each has a **distinct cost computation method**, which is the most heavily tested area in KTU Module 3.

### Pillar 1 — Cost of Equity Capital ($K_e$)

Equity has no contractual obligation (unlike debt), so its cost is **implied** by investor expectations.

**(a) Dividend Growth Model (Gordon's Model)** — *Most frequently used in KTU:*

$$
K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g
$$

Where:
- $D_1$ = Expected dividend at the end of year 1
- $P_0$ = Current market price of equity share
- $g$ = Constant annual growth rate of dividends

**(b) Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM):**

$$
K_e = R_f + \beta \left( R_m - R_f \right)
$$

Where:
- $R_f$ = Risk-free rate of return
- $\beta$ = Beta coefficient (systematic risk measure)
- $R_m$ = Expected return on the market portfolio
- $(R_m - R_f)$ = Market risk premium

**(c) Earnings Yield Method (when growth $g$ is not given):**

$$
K_e = \frac{E_1}{P_0}
$$

### Pillar 2 — Cost of Debt Capital ($K_d$)

Debt has a **contractual interest obligation**, so its cost is more straightforward — but it must be **adjusted for tax** because interest is tax-deductible.

**Before-tax cost of debt:**

$$
K_d^{\text{before}} = \frac{I}{NP}
$$

Where:
- $I$ = Annual interest payment (in rupees)
- $NP$ = Net proceeds from issue of debt instrument

**After-tax cost of debt (the relevant figure for WACC):**

$$
K_d = K_d^{\text{before}} \times (1 - t)
$$

Where $t$ = corporate tax rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **If a debt instrument is issued at par**, then $NP = \text{Face Value}$, and the pre-tax cost is simply the **coupon rate of interest**.

### Pillar 3 — Cost of Preference Share Capital ($K_p$)

Preference shares carry a **fixed dividend** but it is **not tax-deductible** (unlike debt interest).

$$
K_p = \frac{D_p}{NP_p} = \frac{\text{Preference Dividend per share}}{\text{Net Proceeds per share}}
$$

If issued at par:

$$
K_p = \frac{\text{Fixed Rate of Dividend} \times \text{Face Value}}{\text{Face Value}} = \text{Fixed Dividend Rate}
$$

## 2.2 The Two Critical Weightage Philosophies (KTU Hot Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of weights **changes the WACC value**. KTU questions frequently test this distinction.

| Weight Type | Definition | Formula | KTU Preference |
|-------------|-----------|---------|----------------|
| **Book Value Weights** | Weights based on values shown in the **Balance Sheet** | $W_i = \frac{\text{Book Value of Source}_i}{\text{Total Book Value of Capital}}$ | Easy to compute; **used in academic problems** |
| **Market Value Weights** | Weights based on **current market prices** of securities | $W_i = \frac{\text{Market Value of Source}_i}{\text{Total Market Value of Capital}}$ | Theoretically superior; **preferred in real practice** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | WACC (General) | $K_w = \sum K_i W_i$ | Dimensionless (output in %) |
| 2 | WACC (3 sources) | $K_w = K_e W_e + K_d W_d + K_p W_p$ | Most common KTU variant |
| 3 | Cost of Equity (Gordon) | $K_e = D_1/P_0 + g$ | $g$ in decimal (e.g., 0.05 for 5%) |
| 4 | Cost of Equity (CAPM) | $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ | $\beta$ is dimensionless |
| 5 | After-tax Cost of Debt | $K_d = I(1 - t) / NP$ | $t$ is tax rate |
| 6 | Cost of Preference Share | $K_p = D_p / NP_p$ | No tax adjustment |
| 7 | Marginal WACC | $K_{w}^{\text{marginal}}$ | Used for **one additional unit** of capital |
| 8 | Weight Sum Identity | $\sum W_i = 1$ | Always verify in solutions |
| 9 | Book Value Weight | $W_i = BV_i / \sum BV_i$ | From Balance Sheet |
| 10 | Market Value Weight | $W_i = MV_i / \sum MV_i$ | From Stock Exchange |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Corporate Utility

In a corporate engineering firm (e.g., L&T, Siemens India, Tata Projects), WACC is the **discount rate** used in:
- **Capital Budgeting Decisions** — to evaluate new plant/equipment investments
- **M&A Analysis** — to discount expected synergies
- **Working Capital Decisions** — to choose between short-term and long-term financing
- **Performance Appraisal** — Economic Value Added (EVA) = NOPAT − (WACC × Invested Capital)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the WACC Formula — The Logical Foundation

Consider a firm that raises capital from **$n$** sources. Let the total capital be $C$ (rupees), and the amount raised from source $i$ be $C_i$.

**Step 1:** The weight of the $i^{th}$ source is the **fractional proportion** in total capital:

$$
W_i = \frac{C_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i} = \frac{C_i}{C}
$$

**Step 2:** The total cost paid by the firm to source $i$ is $K_i \cdot C_i$ (cost rate × capital amount).

**Step 3:** Summing costs across all sources gives the **total annual cost of capital**:

$$
\text{Total Cost} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i C_i
$$

**Step 4:** The **average cost per rupee** of capital is total cost divided by total capital:

$$
K_w = \frac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Total Capital}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i C_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i}
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $C_i = W_i \cdot C$ (from Step 1):

$$
K_w = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i (W_i \cdot C)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (W_i \cdot C)} = \frac{C \cdot \sum K_i W_i}{C \cdot \sum W_i} = \frac{\sum K_i W_i}{1}
$$

**Final Derivation Result:**

$$
K_w = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i W_i
$$

This is the **theoretically pure WACC formula** that the KTU board expects students to derive and state clearly in Part B answers.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Full Numerical Computation (KTU Pattern)

### Problem Statement
XYZ Engineering Ltd. has the following capital structure:
- **Equity Share Capital:** ₹10,00,000 (1,00,000 shares of ₹10 each, market price ₹15)
- **9% Debentures:** ₹4,00,000 (market value = book value)
- **8% Preference Shares:** ₹2,00,000 (market value = book value)
- **Expected dividend next year (D₁):** ₹1.50 per share
- **Dividend growth rate (g):** 5%
- **Tax rate (t):** 30%

**Compute the WACC using market value weights.**

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Compute Market Value of Each Source**

$$
MV_e = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 15 = \text{₹}15{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
MV_d = \text{₹}4{,}00{,}000 \quad (\text{given market = book})
$$

$$
MV_p = \text{₹}2{,}00{,}000 \quad (\text{given market = book})
$$

**Step 2: Total Market Value of Capital**

$$
MV_{\text{total}} = 15{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}21{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Compute Weights**

$$
W_e = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{21{,}00{,}000} = 0.7143 \quad (71.43\%)
$$

$$
W_d = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{21{,}00{,}000} = 0.1905 \quad (19.05\%)
$$

$$
W_p = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{21{,}00{,}000} = 0.0952 \quad (9.52\%)
$$

**Verification (must equal 1.0):**

$$
0.7143 + 0.1905 + 0.0952 = 1.0000 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4: Compute Individual Component Costs**

Cost of equity (Gordon's Model):

$$
K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g = \frac{1.50}{15} + 0.05 = 0.10 + 0.05 = 0.15 = 15\%
$$

After-tax cost of debt:

$$
K_d = \frac{I}{NP} \times (1 - t) = 0.09 \times (1 - 0.30) = 0.09 \times 0.70 = 0.063 = 6.30\%
$$

Cost of preference share (issued at par, so $K_p$ = dividend rate):

$$
K_p = 8\% = 0.08
$$

**Step 5: Apply the WACC Formula**

$$
K_w = K_e W_e + K_d W_d + K_p W_p
$$

$$
K_w = (0.15 \times 0.7143) + (0.063 \times 0.1905) + (0.08 \times 0.0952)
$$

**Step 6: Evaluate Each Term**

$$
K_e W_e = 0.15 \times 0.7143 = 0.10714
$$

$$
K_d W_d = 0.063 \times 0.1905 = 0.01200
$$

$$
K_p W_p = 0.08 \times 0.0952 = 0.00762
$$

**Step 7: Sum All Components**

$$
K_w = 0.10714 + 0.01200 + 0.00762 = 0.12676
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{K_w = 12.68\% \text{ (approximately)}}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Interpreting WACC Values

| WACC Range | Interpretation | Strategic Implication |
|------------|---------------|----------------------|
| < 8% | Very low (blue-chip, AAA-rated firms) | Strong credit profile; high debt capacity |
| 8% – 12% | Moderate (typical manufacturing firm) | Balanced capital structure |
| 12% – 16% | High (risk or growth firms) | Costly capital; selective investments |
| > 16% | Very high (distressed or start-ups) | Restricted investment universe |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — WACC Computation Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of computing WACC from raw financial data:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Firm Capital Structure Data] --> B{Identify Capital Sources}
    B --> C1[Equity Capital: Shares + Retained Earnings]
    B --> C2[Debt Capital: Debentures + Term Loans]
    B --> C3[Preference Share Capital]
    C1 --> D1[Compute Cost of Equity Ke]
    C2 --> D2[Compute Cost of Debt Kd]
    C3 --> D3[Compute Cost of Preference Kp]
    D1 --> E1[Ke via Gordon / CAPM / Earnings Yield]
    D2 --> E2[Kd = Coupon Rate x 1 - t]
    D3 --> E3[Kp = Dividend / Net Proceeds]
    E1 --> F[Apply Tax Shield on Debt only]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Determine Weights: Book vs Market Value]
    G --> H[Compute W = Source Value / Total Capital]
    H --> I[Final Aggregation: Kw = Sum of Ki x Wi]
    I --> J{Output: WACC Percentage}
    J --> K[Use as Discount Rate in NPV / IRR]
    J --> L[Compare with Project Return for Accept / Reject]
    K --> M[Strategic Decision Output]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Capital Structure Hierarchy (Weights Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TotalCap[Total Capital of Firm] --> E1[Equity Component]
    TotalCap --> E2[Debt Component]
    TotalCap --> E3[Preference Component]
    E1 --> W1[Weight We = Equity MV / Total MV]
    E2 --> W2[Weight Wd = Debt MV / Total MV]
    E3 --> W3[Weight Wp = Preference MV / Total MV]
    W1 --> Result[Combined into WACC]
    W2 --> Result
    W3 --> Result
    Result --> Decision[Project Evaluation]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cost Component Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Cost Computation Method] --> Q1{Is Growth Rate g Given?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| P1[Use Gordon Model: Ke = D1/P0 + g]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is Beta Given?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| P2[Use CAPM: Ke = Rf + Beta x Market Risk Premium]
    Q2 -->|No| P3[Use Earnings Yield: Ke = E1 / P0]
    Start --> Debt[Debt Cost Kd: Apply Tax Shield]
    Start --> Pref[Preference Cost Kp: No Tax Shield]
    P1 --> Combine[Aggregate into WACC]
    P2 --> Combine
    P3 --> Combine
    Debt --> Combine
    Pref --> Combine
```

## 4.4 Tabular Schematic — Relationship Between Risk, Source, and Cost

| Capital Source | Risk Level | Cost Type | Tax-Deductible? | Relative Cost Magnitude |
|----------------|-----------|-----------|-----------------|-------------------------|
| Equity (Common Shares) | Highest | Implied / Expected | No | Highest |
| Preference Shares | Medium-High | Fixed Dividend | No | Medium-High |
| Debentures (Debt) | Lowest | Contractual Interest | **Yes** | Lowest (after-tax) |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define Weighted Average Cost of Capital. Why is it called a "weighted average" and not a simple average?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **WACC** is the **weighted average** of the after-tax cost of various sources of capital (equity, preference, debt) where weights are the proportion of each source in the total capital structure. (1 mark)

> The term "weighted" is used because the firm does not raise capital equally from all sources — the proportion (weight) of each source differs. A simple average would give equal importance to all sources, which is incorrect. For example, if equity forms 70% of capital, its cost must contribute 70% to the final average. (2 marks)

**Formula:**

$$
K_w = \sum K_i W_i
$$

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Distinguish between **Book Value** and **Market Value** weights. Which one is theoretically superior and why?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Book Value Weights | Market Value Weights |
|--------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Source of Data** | Balance Sheet | Current stock exchange prices |
| **Computation Ease** | Easy | Requires market data |
| **Theoretical Soundness** | Ignores current investor expectations | Reflects current investor expectations |
| **Superiority** | — | **Theoretically superior** |

**Theoretical Superiority (1 mark):** Market value weights are superior because the cost of capital is determined by the **expected returns of current investors**, who buy and sell securities at market prices. Book values reflect historical, sunk-cost data and may bear no relation to current investor expectations.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule (2024 Scheme):** Module 3 carries an **internal choice**. Students must answer **EITHER Question A OR Question B** (both from the same module, same marks). Each long question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `Option 1`

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]`**

A company has the following capital structure:

| Source | Amount (₹) | Details |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| Equity Shares (10 each) | 10,00,000 | 1,00,000 shares; Current market price ₹20; Expected dividend ₹2 per share for next year; Growth rate 6% |
| 12% Debentures | 4,00,000 | Net proceeds = Face Value; Tax rate 30% |
| 10% Preference Shares | 2,00,000 | Issued at par; Tax rate 30% |

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the **component cost of capital** for each source.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the **WACC using book value weights** and interpret the result.

#### SOLUTION — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Cost of Equity ($K_e$) — Gordon's Model**

$$
K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g = \frac{2}{20} + 0.06 = 0.10 + 0.06 = 0.16 = \mathbf{16\%}
$$

**Step 2: After-tax Cost of Debt ($K_d$)**

Pre-tax cost (issued at par) = Coupon rate = **12%**

After-tax cost:

$$
K_d = 0.12 \times (1 - 0.30) = 0.12 \times 0.70 = 0.084 = \mathbf{8.4\%}
$$

**Step 3: Cost of Preference Share ($K_p$)** — issued at par, so:

$$
K_p = \text{Dividend Rate} = \mathbf{10\%}
$$

**Summary Table for Part (a):**

| Source | Cost |
|--------|------|
| Equity ($K_e$) | 16% |
| Debt ($K_d$) | 8.4% |
| Preference ($K_p$) | 10% |

#### SOLUTION — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Compute Book Value Weights**

Total capital = 10,00,000 + 4,00,000 + 2,00,000 = **₹16,00,000**

$$
W_e = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{16{,}00{,}000} = 0.625
$$

$$
W_d = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{16{,}00{,}000} = 0.250
$$

$$
W_p = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{16{,}00{,}000} = 0.125
$$

**Verification:** $0.625 + 0.250 + 0.125 = 1.000$ ✓

**Step 2: Apply the WACC Formula**

$$
K_w = (K_e \times W_e) + (K_d \times W_d) + (K_p \times W_p)
$$

$$
K_w = (0.16 \times 0.625) + (0.084 \times 0.250) + (0.10 \times 0.125)
$$

$$
K_w = 0.1000 + 0.0210 + 0.0125
$$

$$
K_w = 0.1335
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{K_w = 13.35\%}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The firm's blended cost of capital is **13.35%**. Any new project must generate a return **greater than 13.35%** to be acceptable. If the firm's cost of capital rises above this threshold (e.g., due to higher interest rates), some previously profitable projects may become unviable.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `Option 2`

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model Paper]`**

PQR Industries is evaluating its capital structure. The following data is provided:

- **Equity:** 50,000 shares of ₹100 each, currently trading at ₹120. Last dividend paid was ₹8 per share. Expected growth rate = 7%.
- **Debentures:** ₹5,00,000, 10% coupon, issued at a discount of 10% (Face Value ₹100 each), redeemable at par. Tax rate = 25%.
- **Retained Earnings:** ₹3,00,000. Cost is same as cost of equity.

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the **cost of equity** and **cost of debentures** (after tax).

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** If the firm wants to raise an **additional ₹10,00,000** in a debt-equity ratio of **1:1** (i.e., ₹5,00,000 in equity at ₹120 per share and ₹5,00,000 in debentures at the same terms), compute the **marginal WACC**.

#### SOLUTION — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Cost of Equity ($K_e$) — Gordon's Model**

Expected dividend next year:

$$
D_1 = D_0 (1 + g) = 8 \times 1.07 = \text{₹}8.56
$$

$$
K_e = \frac{D_1}{P_0} + g = \frac{8.56}{120} + 0.07
$$

$$
K_e = 0.0713 + 0.07 = 0.1413 = \mathbf{14.13\%}
$$

**Cost of Debentures — Approximate Method (Irredeemable / Perpetual)**

Net Proceeds (NP) per debenture = ₹100 − 10% discount = **₹90**

Pre-tax cost:

$$
K_d^{\text{before}} = \frac{I}{NP} = \frac{10}{90} = 0.1111 = 11.11\%
$$

After-tax cost:

$$
K_d = 0.1111 \times (1 - 0.25) = 0.1111 \times 0.75 = 0.0833 = \mathbf{8.33\%}
$$

#### SOLUTION — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Compute Weights of the New Capital**

Total new capital = ₹10,00,000 (₹5,00,000 equity + ₹5,00,000 debentures)

$$
W_e^{\text{new}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} = 0.50
$$

$$
W_d^{\text{new}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} = 0.50
$$

**Step 2: Apply Marginal WACC Formula**

$$
K_w^{\text{marginal}} = (K_e \times W_e^{\text{new}}) + (K_d \times W_d^{\text{new}})
$$

$$
K_w^{\text{marginal}} = (0.1413 \times 0.50) + (0.0833 \times 0.50)
$$

$$
K_w^{\text{marginal}} = 0.07065 + 0.04165 = 0.1123
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{K_w^{\text{marginal}} = 11.23\%}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The **marginal WACC (11.23%)** is lower than the typical historical WACC, indicating that the firm can raise incremental capital at a cheaper blended cost. This is favorable for funding new projects — any project yielding more than 11.23% creates value for shareholders.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in WACC Problems (Module 3):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Tax Adjustment on Debt** — Students often compute $K_d$ as 12% (the coupon rate) instead of 12% × (1 − 0.30) = 8.4%. **Loss: up to 2 marks.**
> 2. **Mixing Up Book Value and Market Value Weights** — If the question says "market price ₹15 per share" and the student uses ₹10 (face value) for the weight, the WACC changes drastically. **Loss: up to 3 marks.**
> 3. **Not Verifying the Weight Sum** — KTU examiners check $\sum W_i = 1$ as a sanity check. If your weights sum to 1.05 or 0.95, you have an arithmetic error. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Using $D_0$ instead of $D_1$ in Gordon's Model** — Gordon's formula uses **next year's expected dividend** ($D_1$), not the just-paid dividend ($D_0$). **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 5. **Ignoring the Difference Between Specific Cost and Composite Cost** — $K_e$ is the *specific* cost of equity; WACC is the *composite* (overall) cost. Don't label them interchangeably.
> 6. **Reporting WACC as a Decimal** — Always report as a percentage (e.g., 13.35%), not 0.1335. Examiners may deduct for this.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Weighted Average Cost of Capital (Module 3)**
>
> - [x] **WACC = Σ (Cost of Source × Weight of Source)**
> - [x] **Three Core Sources:** Equity ($K_e$), Debt ($K_d$), Preference ($K_p$)
> - [x] **Cost of Equity Methods:** Gordon's ($D_1/P_0 + g$), CAPM ($R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$), Earnings Yield ($E_1/P_0$)
> - [x] **Cost of Debt Formula:** $K_d = I(1 - t)/NP$ — **always apply tax shield**
> - [x] **Cost of Preference Share Formula:** $K_p = D_p/NP_p$ — **no tax adjustment**
> - [x] **Weight Constraint:** $\sum W_i = 1$ (always verify)
> - [x] **Book Value vs Market Value Weights:** Market value is theoretically superior
> - [x] **Marginal WACC:** Used for incremental capital decisions; recomputed for the new mix
> - [x] **WACC as Discount Rate:** Use in NPV calculation; accept project only if IRR > WACC
> - [x] **Final Output:** Always express WACC in **percentage form** (multiplied by 100)
> - [x] **Formula Sheet is Your Best Friend:** Memorize all 10 formulas in the cheat sheet
> - [x] **Examiner's Traps:** Watch for tax adjustments, $D_1$ vs $D_0$, and weight sum verification

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Cost of Capital
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Cost of Capital

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of Capital (CoC)** is the **minimum rate of return** that a firm must earn on its investment projects to maintain the market value of its shares and satisfy the expectations of its providers of capital (equity shareholders, debenture holders, preference shareholders, and other lenders). In other words, it is the **weighted average cost of all long-term sources of finance** employed by a firm, where each source is weighted in proportion to its share in the total capital structure.

The **factors affecting cost of capital** are those internal (firm-specific) and external (macro-economic and market-driven) variables that cause a change — upward or downward — in the firm's composite weighted average cost of capital (WACC).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECBT631 / Module 3):** Students must be able to **list, classify, and critically explain** the determinants of cost of capital. The Board expects students to **link each factor to WACC** and to appreciate the **directional impact** (raises or lowers WACC).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Apartment Block Investor"

Imagine Mr. Rajan builds a 10-floor apartment block. He borrows **₹50 lakh from a bank @ 9% interest** and contributes **₹50 lakh of his own money** (for which he expects at least 12% — because he could earn 12% elsewhere in mutual funds with similar risk).

- The **bank's expectation (9%)** = Cost of Debt
- **Rajan's expectation (12%)** = Cost of Equity
- The **overall return Rajan must earn on the building project** = Cost of Capital = (50% × 9%) + (50% × 12%) = **10.5%**

Now suppose:
- The **Reserve Bank raises interest rates** (External factor) → Bank demands **10%** instead of 9% → Cost of Capital rises to 11%.
- Rajan **becomes very profitable** and stable (Internal factor) → Investors are willing to accept **11%** instead of 12% → Cost of Capital falls to 10%.
- **Government increases corporate tax** (External factor) → After-tax cost of debt falls (interest saves tax), but risk perception rises, raising equity cost → mixed effect.

> **The Intuition:** Any variable that changes the *risk* or *required return* of any one source of capital will *shift* the WACC. Factors are simply the **levers** that move the individual component costs.

---

## 1.3 Classification — The Two Big Buckets

| Bucket | Nature | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal / Firm-Specific Factors** | Within management's control (partially) | Capital structure, dividend policy, operating risk, size, profitability |
| **External / General / Environmental Factors** | Outside management's control | Interest rates, inflation, market conditions, taxation policy, macro-economy |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** When answering in KTU exams, always begin by stating *"Cost of capital is influenced by internal and external factors"*, then list **at least 5 factors from each category** with one-line explanations. This fetches **full 7 marks** in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do These Factors Matter?

The cost of capital is a **composite measure**. Any change in:
- the **cost of any one source** (debt, equity, preference, retained earnings), OR
- the **proportion (weight) of any one source** in the capital structure

will alter the WACC. Therefore, the factors affecting cost of capital are best understood as those that:
1. Change the **component cost** of each source of finance, OR
2. Change the **weights** of those sources in the capital structure.

> [!NOTE]
> The cost of capital is **not a single number** — it is a **function of risk, time, financing mix, and market perception**.

---

## 2.2 Internal (Firm-Specific) Factors

### A. Capital Structure (Debt-Equity Mix)
- A firm with **higher debt** generally has a **lower WACC** *up to an optimal point*, because debt is cheaper (interest is tax-deductible) and fixed.
- However, beyond the **optimal debt level**, financial risk rises sharply → cost of equity rises faster than the savings from cheaper debt → WACC **turns upward**.
- Refer to the **Traditional Trade-off Theory** and **Modigliani-Miller** propositions.

### B. Dividend Policy
- A **stable or rising dividend** signals financial health → lowers perceived risk → lowers cost of equity.
- A firm retaining more earnings (low payout) provides **internal accruals**, which have a *flotation cost advantage* → may lower WACC.
- However, an *inconsistent* dividend policy increases uncertainty → raises cost of capital.

### C. Operating Efficiency & Profitability
- High operating efficiency → stable cash flows → **lower business risk** → lower required return from investors → **lower cost of capital**.
- Loss-making or volatile firms face **higher risk premium**, raising WACC.

### D. Size of the Firm
- **Larger firms** → better diversification, easier access to capital markets, better credit rating → **lower cost of capital**.
- **Smaller firms** → higher information asymmetry, less analyst coverage → **higher risk premium** → higher cost of capital.

### E. Interest Coverage Ratio (ICR) and Credit Rating
- A **high ICR** (EBIT / Interest) means the firm can comfortably service debt → **better credit rating** → lenders charge lower interest → lower cost of debt.
- A **low ICR** signals default risk → credit downgrade → cost of debt **spikes up**.

### F. Business and Financial Risk
- **Business Risk** (variability of operating income) ↑ → Cost of Equity ↑ → WACC ↑.
- **Financial Risk** (fixed financing costs like interest & preference dividend) ↑ → Cost of Equity ↑ → WACC ↑.

### G. Tax Planning
- Since **interest is tax-deductible**, the **after-tax cost of debt = Rd × (1 − t)**, where `t` = tax rate.
- A higher tax rate → larger tax shield → **lower after-tax cost of debt** → WACC reduces.
- However, tax changes also affect investor after-tax returns → complex effect.

### H. Age and Maturity of the Firm
- **Mature, established firms** → predictable cash flows → lower cost of capital.
- **Young/start-up firms** → uncertainty, negative cash flows → higher cost of capital.

---

## 2.3 External (General / Environmental) Factors

### A. Market Conditions (Bull vs. Bear)
- **Bull market** → investors accept lower returns, share prices high → cost of equity **falls** → WACC falls.
- **Bear market** → risk aversion rises, share prices fall → cost of equity **rises** → WACC rises.

### B. Interest Rate Levels (Monetary Policy)
- Cost of debt is directly tied to **prevailing market interest rates** set by RBI/market forces.
- **RBI repo rate hike** → bank lending rates rise → Rd ↑ → WACC ↑.
- **Repo rate cut** → Rd ↓ → WACC ↓.

### C. Inflation Rate
- High inflation → investors demand **inflation premium** → both cost of debt and cost of equity rise.
- Inflation also erodes real value of interest payments → lenders raise nominal rates.
- **Formula implication:** Nominal rate = Real rate + Inflation premium + Risk premium.

### D. Tax Rates and Government Fiscal Policy
- Changes in **corporate tax rate** alter the tax shield on debt.
- **Dividend distribution tax** changes affect the cost of equity.
- Government subsidies, tax holidays, SEZ benefits reduce effective cost.

### E. Economic Conditions (Boom / Recession)
- **Boom** → high demand for funds → cost of capital rises.
- **Recession** → reduced investment demand, risk aversion, lower WACC (sometimes) but equity costs rise due to fear.

### F. Political and Regulatory Environment
- Political instability → **country risk premium** rises → cost of equity rises → WACC rises.
- Stable governance, SEBI regulations, contract enforceability → lower risk → lower WACC.

### G. Cost of Capital in Other Countries (Global Markets)
- MNCs tap global markets; a **lower foreign interest rate** can lower their blended cost.
- However, **exchange rate risk** and **country risk** modify this.

### H. Marketability and Floatation Costs
- Securities traded in **active markets** (large-cap, listed) have **lower floatation costs** → lower cost of capital.
- Unlisted, illiquid securities → higher floatation + risk premium → higher cost of capital.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **WACC (3 sources)** | $K_o = \left(\dfrac{E}{V}\right)K_e + \left(\dfrac{P}{V}\right)K_p + \left(\dfrac{D}{V}\right)K_d(1-t)$ | $E, P, D$ = market values; $V = E+P+D$; $K_e, K_p$ = after-tax cost of equity/preference; $K_d$ = before-tax cost of debt; $t$ = tax rate |
| 2 | **After-Tax Cost of Debt** | $K_d(\text{after-tax}) = K_d \times (1 - t)$ | $K_d$ before-tax; $t$ = tax rate |
| 3 | **Cost of Equity (CAPM)** | $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$ | $R_f$ = risk-free rate; $\beta$ = systematic risk; $(R_m - R_f)$ = market risk premium |
| 4 | **Cost of Equity (Dividend Model)** | $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ | $D_1$ = expected dividend; $P_0$ = current price; $g$ = growth rate |
| 5 | **Cost of Retained Earnings** | $K_r = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0} + g$ | Same as $K_e$ *only if* no floatation/adjustment cost |
| 6 | **Cost of Preference Capital** | $K_p = \dfrac{D_p}{P_0}$ (perpetual) | $D_p$ = fixed preference dividend |
| 7 | **Nominal vs. Real Rate** | $(1 + R_n) = (1 + R_r)(1 + h)$ | $R_n$ nominal; $R_r$ real; $h$ inflation |
| 8 | **Weighted Marginal Cost of Capital** | $WMCC = \sum w_i \cdot MC_i$ | $w_i$ = weight of source $i$; $MC_i$ = marginal cost of source $i$ |
| 9 | **Floatation Cost Adjustment** | $K_e = \dfrac{D_1}{P_0(1 - f)} + g$ | $f$ = floatation cost % |
| 10 | **Levered Beta (βL)** | $\beta_L = \beta_U \left[1 + (1 - t)\dfrac{D}{E}\right]$ | $\beta_U$ = unlevered beta; $D/E$ = debt-equity ratio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** When a question asks *"How does factor X affect cost of capital?"*, the model answer must **derive or invoke WACC** and show the *directional effect* on either a component cost (K_e, K_d, K_p) or a weight, and hence on WACC.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

| Engineering / Business Field | Use of Cost of Capital Concepts |
|---|---|
| **Capital Budgeting** | Discount rate for NPV, IRR, PI calculations |
| **Project Finance** | Bidding & viability of large infra projects |
| **Valuation & M&A** | DCF valuations of target firms |
| **Working Capital** | Short-term funding cost decisions |
| **Corporate Strategy** | Choosing optimal capital structure |
| **Public-Private Partnerships** | Quoting concession period & tariff |
| **Start-up Funding** | Determining how much dilution to accept |
| **Sustainability/ESG Finance** | Green bonds pricing |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU Board often asks for a **proof or numerical illustration** of how a specific factor shifts WACC. Below is a complete worked derivation showing the *mechanism* of change, followed by an analytical matrix mapping each factor → component → direction.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: How a Change in Interest Rate (External Factor) Alters WACC

**Given:**
- Existing capital structure: $E$ = ₹60 lakh, $D$ = ₹40 lakh, $V = E + D = ₹100$ lakh.
- Existing cost of equity, $K_e = 14\%$
- Existing before-tax cost of debt, $K_d = 10\%$
- Tax rate, $t = 30\%$

**Step 1: Compute initial WACC**

$$
K_{o,\text{old}} = \left(\dfrac{E}{V}\right)K_e + \left(\dfrac{D}{V}\right)K_d(1-t)
$$

$$
K_{o,\text{old}} = \left(\dfrac{60}{100}\right)(0.14) + \left(\dfrac{40}{100}\right)(0.10)(1-0.30)
$$

$$
K_{o,\text{old}} = (0.60)(0.14) + (0.40)(0.10)(0.70)
$$

$$
K_{o,\text{old}} = 0.084 + 0.028
$$

$$
\boxed{K_{o,\text{old}} = 0.112 \text{ or } 11.20\%}
$$

**Step 2: RBI raises repo rate → market interest rate rises to 12% (before tax). New $K_d = 12\%$.**

Capital structure unchanged initially: $E = 60$, $D = 40$, $V = 100$.

$$
K_{o,\text{new}} = (0.60)(0.14) + (0.40)(0.12)(1-0.30)
$$

$$
K_{o,\text{new}} = 0.084 + (0.40)(0.12)(0.70)
$$

$$
K_{o,\text{new}} = 0.084 + 0.0336
$$

$$
\boxed{K_{o,\text{new}} = 0.1176 \text{ or } 11.76\%}
$$

**Step 3: Effect on WACC**

$$
\Delta K_o = K_{o,\text{new}} - K_{o,\text{old}} = 11.76\% - 11.20\% = +0.56\%
$$

**Conclusion:** A 200 basis-point rise in pre-tax cost of debt (10% → 12%) raised WACC by **56 basis points (0.56%)** *after the tax shield is applied*. This confirms that **interest rate levels are a powerful external driver of WACC.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: How a Change in Capital Structure (Internal Factor) Alters WACC

**Given (same firm):**
- Cost of equity, $K_e = 14\%$ at current $D/E = 40/60$
- Cost of debt, $K_d = 10\%$, $t = 30\%$
- Suppose firm increases debt to $D = 60$, equity falls to $E = 40$ (so $D/E$ rises). Financial risk rises → cost of equity rises to $K_e^{\text{new}} = 15.5\%$.

**Step 1: Compute new WACC with $E = 40$, $D = 60$, $V = 100$**

$$
K_{o,\text{new}} = \left(\dfrac{40}{100}\right)(0.155) + \left(\dfrac{60}{100}\right)(0.10)(1-0.30)
$$

$$
K_{o,\text{new}} = 0.062 + 0.042
$$

$$
\boxed{K_{o,\text{new}} = 0.104 \text{ or } 10.40\%}
$$

**Step 2: Compare**

$$
\Delta K_o = 10.40\% - 11.20\% = -0.80\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Increasing debt from 40% to 60% **lowered WACC by 80 bps**, despite the cost of equity rising by 150 bps. This is because the **cheaper debt and its tax shield outweighed the rise in equity cost** *up to this point*. Beyond a certain debt level, the rise in $K_e$ would dominate, and WACC would rise — illustrating the **optimal capital structure**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Factor → Mechanism → Direction on WACC

| # | Factor | Type | Component / Mechanism Affected | Direction of WACC (typically) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rise in market interest rates | External | $K_d \uparrow$ | **↑ WACC** |
| 2 | Rise in inflation | External | All nominal costs $\uparrow$ via inflation premium | **↑ WACC** |
| 3 | Corporate tax rate $\uparrow$ | External | $K_d(1-t) \downarrow$ (tax shield $\uparrow$) | **↓ WACC** (debt-cheapening) |
| 4 | Bull market (share prices $\uparrow$) | External | $K_e = D_1/P_0 + g \downarrow$ | **↓ WACC** |
| 5 | Country risk premium $\uparrow$ | External | $K_e \uparrow$ | **↑ WACC** |
| 6 | Increase in debt proportion (up to optimal) | Internal | Weight of $D \uparrow$, weight of $E \downarrow$ | **↓ WACC** |
| 7 | Increase in debt proportion (beyond optimal) | Internal | $K_e \uparrow$ dominates | **↑ WACC** |
| 8 | Improved operating efficiency | Internal | Business risk $\downarrow$ → $K_e \downarrow$ | **↓ WACC** |
| 9 | Stable dividend policy | Internal | Perceived risk $\downarrow$ → $K_e \downarrow$ | **↓ WACC** |
| 10 | Larger firm size | Internal | Diversification, credit rating $\uparrow$ | **↓ WACC** |
| 11 | Better credit rating / higher ICR | Internal | $K_d \downarrow$ | **↓ WACC** |
| 12 | Rise in floatation costs | Internal | Net proceeds $\downarrow$ → $K_e, K_d \uparrow$ | **↑ WACC** |
| 13 | Political instability | External | Country risk premium $\uparrow$ | **↑ WACC** |
| 14 | Recession / low demand for funds | External | $K_e, K_d \downarrow$ | **↓ WACC** |
| 15 | Government subsidy / tax holiday | External | Effective tax $\downarrow$ → $K_d$ net cost $\downarrow$ | **↓ WACC** |

---

## 3.4 Sensitivity Insight — "Operating Leverage Effect"

Using CAPM, $K_e = R_f + \beta (R_m - R_f)$. The levered beta formula is:

$$
\beta_L = \beta_U\left[1 + (1 - t)\dfrac{D}{E}\right]
$$

**Interpretation:**
- If $\dfrac{D}{E}$ rises (more debt), $\beta_L$ rises.
- Higher $\beta_L$ → higher $K_e$ → higher WACC **at high debt levels**.
- This is the *quantitative bridge* between capital structure (internal) and cost of capital — a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**.

**Numerical illustration:** Take $\beta_U = 0.8$, $t = 0.30$, $R_f = 6\%$, $R_m - R_f = 7\%$.

**Case 1: $D/E = 0.5$**

$$
\beta_L = 0.8 \times [1 + 0.7 \times 0.5] = 0.8 \times 1.35 = 1.08
$$

$$
K_e = 6\% + 1.08 \times 7\% = 6\% + 7.56\% = 13.56\%
$$

**Case 2: $D/E = 1.0$**

$$
\beta_L = 0.8 \times [1 + 0.7 \times 1.0] = 0.8 \times 1.70 = 1.36
$$

$$
K_e = 6\% + 1.36 \times 7\% = 6\% + 9.52\% = 15.52\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Doubling leverage from 0.5 to 1.0 raised $K_e$ by nearly **2 percentage points** — a clear quantification of how capital structure affects cost of capital.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by listing factors **without linking them to WACC**. The Board expects you to state **which component cost** ($K_e$, $K_d$, $K_p$) is affected and the **direction of change** in WACC.
> - Many students **confuse "interest rate" with "inflation"** in the external factors. Keep them separate: interest rate = monetary policy; inflation = purchasing power erosion.
> - In numerical questions, **always show the WACC formula and substitution**, then conclude with the **change in basis points**. Do not write the final answer in percentage only.
> - **Don't write "cost of capital increases"** without specifying *which source's cost* (equity / debt / preference) increased.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** State any three external factors that affect the cost of capital of a firm.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The external (environmental) factors affecting cost of capital are:

1. **Interest Rate Levels** — A rise in market interest rates, determined by monetary policy (RBI repo rate), increases the cost of debt $K_d$, which in turn raises the firm's WACC.
2. **Inflation Rate** — Higher inflation increases the inflation premium demanded by both lenders and equity investors, raising $K_d$ and $K_e$ and hence the WACC.
3. **Market Conditions** — In a bull market, share prices are high, lowering $K_e = D_1/P_0 + g$, thereby reducing WACC. In a bear market, the opposite occurs.

*(1 mark each for clear identification and explanation; 3 marks total.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between internal and external factors affecting cost of capital with two examples each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Internal Factors | External Factors |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Arise within the firm; partly controllable by management | Arise in the macro-environment; beyond firm's control |
| **Example 1** | **Capital Structure** — choice of debt-equity mix changes WACC | **Interest Rates** — RBI policy changes market interest rates |
| **Example 2** | **Operating Efficiency** — affects business risk and cost of equity | **Inflation** — affects nominal cost of all sources |

*(2 marks for clear distinction + 1 mark for examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Either attempt Q-A or Q-B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **internal factors** that affect the cost of capital of a firm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** XYZ Ltd. has the following capital structure:
- Equity: ₹80 lakh (Cost of equity, $K_e = 15\%$)
- Debt: ₹40 lakh (Cost of debt, $K_d = 12\%$, before tax)
- Tax rate: $t = 30\%$

Calculate the **WACC**. If RBI raises interest rates such that the new pre-tax cost of debt becomes **14%**, what is the **new WACC** and the **change in basis points**? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Internal Factors (7 marks):**

1. **Capital Structure (2 marks):** The relative proportion of debt and equity influences WACC. Debt is cheaper due to tax shield; equity is costlier due to higher risk. Up to an optimal point, increasing debt lowers WACC; beyond it, rising financial risk raises $K_e$ faster than the saving from cheaper debt, raising WACC.

2. **Dividend Policy (1 mark):** A consistent and growing dividend policy reduces perceived risk, lowering $K_e$. Conversely, erratic dividends raise $K_e$ and WACC. Retained earnings also have a lower floatation cost than fresh equity.

3. **Operating Efficiency & Profitability (1 mark):** Higher operating efficiency → stable cash flows → lower business risk → lower $K_e$ and WACC.

4. **Size of the Firm (1 mark):** Larger firms enjoy better credit ratings, lower floatation costs, and easier market access, leading to a lower cost of capital. Smaller firms face higher risk premiums.

5. **Credit Rating and Interest Coverage Ratio (1 mark):** A higher ICR (EBIT/Interest) → better credit rating → lower $K_d$ → lower WACC.

6. **Tax Planning (1 mark):** Since interest is tax-deductible, firms that can fully utilise the tax shield have a lower after-tax cost of debt and a lower WACC.

---

**(b) WACC Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Initial WACC**

Total capital $V = E + D = 80 + 40 = ₹120$ lakh.

$$
K_{o,\text{old}} = \left(\dfrac{E}{V}\right)K_e + \left(\dfrac{D}{V}\right)K_d(1-t)
$$

$$
K_{o,\text{old}} = \left(\dfrac{80}{120}\right)(0.15) + \left(\dfrac{40}{120}\right)(0.12)(1-0.30)
$$

$$
K_{o,\text{old}} = (0.6667)(0.15) + (0.3333)(0.12)(0.70)
$$

$$
K_{o,\text{old}} = 0.1000 + 0.0280
$$

$$
\boxed{K_{o,\text{old}} = 0.1280 \text{ or } 12.80\%}
$$

**Step 2: New WACC with $K_d^{\text{new}} = 14\%$**

$$
K_{o,\text{new}} = (0.6667)(0.15) + (0.3333)(0.14)(0.70)
$$

$$
K_{o,\text{new}} = 0.1000 + 0.03267
$$

$$
\boxed{K_{o,\text{new}} = 0.13267 \text{ or } 13.27\%}
$$

**Step 3: Change in WACC**

$$
\Delta K_o = 13.27\% - 12.80\% = +0.47\% = +47 \text{ basis points}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 200 bps rise in pre-tax cost of debt (12% → 14%) led to a **47 bps rise in WACC** because of the 30% tax shield. Higher WACC means **fewer investment projects will clear the NPV hurdle** — directly affecting the firm's capital budgeting decisions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Retest]**

**(a)** Discuss the **external (general) factors** that influence the cost of capital. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A firm's unlevered beta is $\beta_U = 0.9$, the risk-free rate is $R_f = 6\%$, the market risk premium is $(R_m - R_f) = 7\%$, and the corporate tax rate is $t = 25\%$. The firm currently has a debt-to-equity ratio of $D/E = 0.6$. **(7 marks)**
- (i) Calculate the levered beta $\beta_L$.
- (ii) Calculate the cost of equity $K_e$ using CAPM.
- (iii) If the firm increases its leverage to $D/E = 1.2$, what is the new cost of equity? Comment on the implication for WACC.

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) External Factors (7 marks):**

1. **Interest Rate Levels (2 marks):** Determined by the central bank's (RBI) monetary policy. Higher rates raise the cost of debt $K_d$, raising WACC.

2. **Inflation Rate (1.5 marks):** Lenders demand an inflation premium; thus $K_d$ and $K_e$ rise with inflation, raising WACC.

3. **Market Conditions (1 mark):** In bull markets, share prices are high, reducing $K_e = D_1/P_0 + g$, lowering WACC. Bear markets do the opposite.

4. **Taxation Policy (1 mark):** Changes in corporate tax rate alter the value of the interest tax shield and after-tax returns to investors, affecting WACC.

5. **Economic Conditions (Boom/Recession) (0.75 mark):** Recessions reduce demand for funds and may lower WACC; booms increase it.

6. **Political & Country Risk (0.75 mark):** Political instability raises the country risk premium, raising $K_e$ and WACC.

---

**(b) CAPM-Based Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Levered Beta at $D/E = 0.6$:**

$$
\beta_L = \beta_U \left[1 + (1 - t)\dfrac{D}{E}\right]
$$

$$
\beta_L = 0.9 \times \left[1 + (1 - 0.25)(0.6)\right]
$$

$$
\beta_L = 0.9 \times \left[1 + (0.75)(0.6)\right] = 0.9 \times [1 + 0.45]
$$

$$
\boxed{\beta_L = 0.9 \times 1.45 = 1.305}
$$

**(ii) Cost of Equity at $D/E = 0.6$:**

$$
K_e = R_f + \beta_L (R_m - R_f) = 6\% + 1.305 \times 7\%
$$

$$
K_e = 6\% + 9.135\% = \boxed{15.135\%}
$$

**(iii) New Levered Beta at $D/E = 1.2$:**

$$
\beta_L^{\text{new}} = 0.9 \times \left[1 + (0.75)(1.2)\right] = 0.9 \times [1 + 0.90]
$$

$$
\beta_L^{\text{new}} = 0.9 \times 1.90 = 1.71
$$

$$
K_e^{\text{new}} = 6\% + 1.71 \times 7\% = 6\% + 11.97\% = \boxed{17.97\%}
$$

**Change in $K_e$:**

$$
\Delta K_e = 17.97\% - 15.135\% = +2.835\%
$$

**Comment:** Doubling the debt-equity ratio from 0.6 to 1.2 raised the levered beta from 1.305 to 1.71, which in turn raised the cost of equity by **2.83 percentage points**. This illustrates the **financial risk effect**: as the firm takes on more debt, equity becomes riskier, and investors demand higher returns. **Above the optimal capital structure, this rise in $K_e$ will outweigh the savings from cheaper debt, raising WACC.** The firm should compute the *full* WACC at both leverage levels to confirm the optimal point.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and revise 5 minutes before the exam.**

### ✅ Conceptual Must-Knows
- Cost of capital = **minimum required return** on investments to keep market value of firm intact.
- Affected by **internal** (controllable) and **external** (macro) factors.
- Each factor ultimately affects **either a component cost ($K_e, K_d, K_p$) or a weight, or both**.

### ✅ Internal Factors — 8 to Remember
1. **Capital structure** (debt-equity mix) → optimal structure minimises WACC.
2. **Dividend policy** → stable payouts lower $K_e$.
3. **Operating efficiency / profitability** → lower business risk → lower $K_e$.
4. **Size of the firm** → bigger = cheaper capital.
5. **Credit rating / ICR** → better rating = lower $K_d$.
6. **Business and financial risk** → both raise $K_e$.
7. **Tax planning** → maximise interest tax shield.
8. **Floatation costs** → higher $f$ → higher $K_e$ and $K_d$.

### ✅ External Factors — 8 to Remember
1. **Interest rates** (RBI repo rate) → direct effect on $K_d$.
2. **Inflation rate** → raises all nominal costs.
3. **Market conditions** (bull/bear) → affects $K_e$ via $P_0$.
4. **Tax policy** (corporate tax, DDT) → affects net cost.
5. **Economic cycle** (boom/recession).
6. **Political stability / country risk premium**.
7. **Global capital markets** (for MNCs).
8. **Regulatory environment** (SEBI, RBI, FEMA).

### ✅ Formulas You Must Memorise
- $K_o = (E/V)K_e + (D/V)K_d(1-t) + (P/V)K_p$
- $K_e = R_f + \beta(R_m - R_f)$
- $K_e = D_1/P_0 + g$
- $K_d(\text{after-tax}) = K_d(1 - t)$
- $\beta_L = \beta_U[1 + (1-t)D/E]$

### ✅ One-Line Examiner's Favourites
- *"Capital structure affects WACC through both weights and component costs."*
- *"Tax shield is the primary reason debt is cheaper than equity."*
- *"Beyond the optimal capital structure, WACC rises."*
- *"Inflation and interest rates are NOT the same — they affect cost of capital through different channels."*
- *"CAPM directly links systematic risk (beta) to cost of equity, hence to WACC."*

### ✅ Common Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Forgetting the **(1 − t)** tax shield term in WACC.
- ❌ Mixing up **$D$ as book value vs. market value** — KTU prefers **market value weights**.
- ❌ Saying *"interest rate is a factor"* without specifying **which** type (short-term/long-term, real/nominal).
- ❌ Ignoring the **directional effect** (up vs. down) on WACC.
- ❌ Writing $K_e = D_1/P_0$ (forgetting the **+ g** growth term).

> **Final Mantra for the Exam Hall:**
> *Identify factor → Identify component ($K_e, K_d, K_p$) affected → Specify direction → State effect on WACC.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capital Budgeting: The Capital Budgeting Concept and Process - An Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Capital Budgeting: The Capital Budgeting Concept and Process - An Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Capital Budgeting** is the systematic process by which a firm evaluates and selects long-term investment projects that are expected to generate cash flows over multiple periods. It involves the planning, evaluation, and control of capital expenditures (expenditures generating benefits beyond one accounting year).

The term *Capital* refers to **long-term funds** committed to a project, and *Budgeting* refers to the **planning of expenditure** over a specific period. Together, **Capital Budgeting** is the financial planning mechanism for deploying scarce long-term resources into profitable investment opportunities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capital Budget** is the total amount of funds appropriated for investment in long-term assets during a given fiscal year, while a **Capital Expenditure (CapEx)** is any expenditure made to acquire, upgrade, or maintain physical or intangible long-term assets.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a young entrepreneur, *Rahul*, who has ₹50,00,000 saved. He has three options:
1. **Buy a new pizza oven** for his restaurant (a productive asset).
2. **Purchase a delivery bike fleet** (a logistical asset).
3. **Open a cloud kitchen branch** in another city (a strategic expansion).

Each option costs ₹50,00,000, but the **returns, risks, and life spans** are different. Rahul cannot do all three with his limited capital. He must carefully **evaluate**, **compare**, and **choose** the option that maximizes long-term wealth. This act of *planning, evaluating, and selecting long-term investment proposals* is precisely what **Capital Budgeting** is in a corporate context.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Capital budgeting answers a single fundamental question — *"Given limited capital, which long-term investment(s) should the firm undertake to maximize shareholder wealth?"*

### 1.3 Why Capital Budgeting Matters (Significance)

The significance of capital budgeting in KTU 2024 Scheme terms is rooted in its **strategic, financial, and risk-management** impact:

- **Long-term Commitment:** Capital decisions typically lock up funds for **5 to 20 years**, making reversibility costly or impossible.
- **Magnitude of Funds:** Capital budgets of large firms often run into **hundreds of crores**, making allocation errors catastrophic.
- **Wealth Maximization Goal:** Properly evaluated projects align with the firm's primary objective of **maximizing the market value of shares**.
- **Coordination of Strategy & Finance:** Capital budgeting translates **strategic vision** (growth, diversification, replacement) into measurable financial decisions.
- **Risk Containment:** Since future cash flows are uncertain, structured evaluation reduces **subjectivity** and **cognitive bias** in decision-making.

### 1.4 Key Terminology (Board-Examiner Favourites)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Definitions for KTU Board Examination:**
> - **Capital Rationing:** A situation where a firm has a **fixed capital budget ceiling** and must select the optimal mix of projects within that constraint.
> - **Mutually Exclusive Projects:** Two or more projects where the **acceptance of one automatically rejects** the others (e.g., choosing between Land or Building for the same facility).
> - **Independent Projects:** Projects whose acceptance or rejection does **not affect** the cash flows of other projects.
> - **Sunk Cost:** A **past, irrecoverable** cost that is irrelevant to the current decision (e.g., market survey cost of a rejected proposal).
> - **Opportunity Cost:** The **forgone benefit** from the next-best alternative use of the capital.
> - **Incremental Cash Flow:** The **additional cash flow** generated by a project, ignoring sunk costs and including opportunity costs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Features / Characteristics of Capital Budgeting Decisions

Capital budgeting decisions exhibit the following distinguishing features that students must commit to memory:

- **Long-term horizon:** Benefits accrue over **multiple years**, not months.
- **Large capital outlay:** Involves **substantial initial expenditure** of funds.
- **Irreversibility:** Most capital decisions are **difficult or impossible to reverse** without significant loss.
- **High risk & uncertainty:** Future cash flows are **probabilistic estimates**, not certainties.
- **Strategic impact:** Affects the **competitive position**, capacity, and future direction of the firm.
- **Impact on cost structure:** Decisions determine **fixed costs** and operating leverage for years.

### 2.2 Types of Capital Budgeting Decisions

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies capital budgeting decisions into the following categories:

| **Decision Type** | **Meaning** | **Real-World Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **Accept-Reject Decision** | A project is either accepted (if it passes the evaluation criteria) or rejected. | Whether to install a new ERP system or not. |
| **Mutually Exclusive Decision** | Choosing one project excludes all others. | Choosing between two factory locations: Kochi or Calicut. |
| **Capital Rationing Decision** | Total capital available is limited; ranking & selection of best subset is required. | ₹10 crore budget; selecting projects totalling up to that limit. |
| **Independent Decision** | Acceptance of one project does not affect another. | Buying a delivery van and a new server simultaneously. |
| **Contingent / Dependent Decision** | Project B can be undertaken **only if** Project A is accepted. | Launching a coffee chain (B) only after a central roastery (A) is built. |

### 2.3 The Three Pillars of Capital Cash Flows

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every capital budgeting problem in KTU examinations is built on three cash flow components:**

**Pillar 1: Initial Investment (Cash Outflow at t = 0)**
- Cost of the asset
- Installation, freight, and trial-run costs
- Increase in Working Capital
- Less: Sale proceeds of any old asset replaced

**Pillar 2: Operating Cash Flows (Annual Inflows during 1 to n)**
- Revenue generated by the project
- Less: Operating costs (cash) and depreciation (non-cash)
- Less: Taxes on the taxable income
- Add back: Non-cash depreciation (since it is a tax shield, not an outflow)

**Pillar 3: Terminal Cash Flow (at end of year n)**
- Salvage value of the asset
- Recovery of working capital
- Less: Tax on gain/loss on sale of asset

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these expressions. Every KTU numerical problem on capital budgeting is solved by plugging into one or more of these formulas.**

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Investment | $\text{Initial Inv.} = C + I + W - S_{\text{old}} - T_{\text{old}}$ | All values in ₹; $C$ = asset cost, $I$ = installation, $W$ = working capital, $S_{\text{old}}$ = sale of replaced asset, $T_{\text{old}}$ = tax saved on old sale |
| Straight-Line Depreciation | $D = \dfrac{C_{\text{dep}} - S}{n}$ | $C_{\textdep}$ = depreciable cost, $S$ = salvage, $n$ = life in years |
| Operating Cash Flow (Method 1) | $\text{OCF} = (\text{Revenue} - \text{Operating Cost})(1 - t) + D \cdot t$ | Tax-shield method; widely used in KTU papers |
| Operating Cash Flow (Method 2) | $\text{OCF} = \text{EBIT}(1 - t) + D$ | $t$ = tax rate, $D$ = depreciation |
| After-Tax Salvage Value | $S_{\text{AT}} = S - (S - \text{BV}) \cdot t$ | $\text{BV}$ = book value at end of year $n$ |
| Terminal Cash Flow | $\text{TCF} = S_{\text{AT}} + W_{\text{recovered}}$ | Working capital assumed to be fully recovered |
| Tax Shield of Depreciation | $\text{Tax Shield} = D \cdot t$ | A **non-cash inflow** that lowers tax outflow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions / Assumptions (KTU Standard):**
> 1. All cash flows are treated as occurring at the **end of each year** (end-of-period convention).
> 2. The **tax rate is constant** throughout the project life.
> 3. Working capital is **fully recovered** at project termination unless otherwise stated.
> 4. Sunk costs are **excluded**; only incremental cash flows are counted.
> 5. Inflation is either **assumed zero** or already embedded in the given cash flows.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Capital Budgeting Process — Step-by-Step

The capital budgeting process is a **logical sequence of 7 stages**, each feeding into the next. KTU examiners frequently test the order, so memorize it in the exact sequence below:

**Step 1: Identification of Investment Opportunities**
The firm scans its environment for profitable projects arising from:
- **Expansion** (new products/markets)
- **Replacement** (outdated machinery)
- **Diversification** (new business lines)
- **Research & Development** outputs
- **Regulatory or statutory** mandates (pollution control, safety)

**Step 2: Collection of Relevant Data**
Gather data on **investment cost, expected revenues, operating costs, project life, salvage value, working capital needs, and tax implications.** Inaccurate data here is the **single largest source of project failure**.

**Step 3: Estimation of Cash Flows**
Apply the formula for each year's net cash flow. The **Operating Cash Flow derivation** is shown below:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Revenue} &= R \\
\text{Operating Cost (cash)} &= C_{\text{op}} \\
\text{Depreciation (non-cash)} &= D \\
\text{Earnings Before Interest \& Taxes (EBIT)} &= R - C_{\text{op}} - D \\
\text{Tax} &= \text{EBIT} \cdot t \\
\text{Net Income} &= \text{EBIT} \cdot (1 - t) \\
\text{Operating Cash Flow} &= \text{Net Income} + D \\
\text{OCF} &= \text{EBIT} \cdot (1 - t) + D \\
\text{OCF} &= (R - C_{\text{op}} - D)(1 - t) + D \\
\text{OCF} &= (R - C_{\text{op}})(1 - t) + D \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

The final simplified form, **$\text{OCF} = (R - C_{\text{op}})(1 - t) + D \cdot t$**, is the **most-tested expression** in KTU examinations.

**Step 4: Evaluation of Project Viability**
Apply capital budgeting techniques (covered in detail in later modules):
- **Non-discounted:** Payback Period, Accounting Rate of Return
- **Discounted:** Net Present Value (NPV), Internal Rate of Return (IRR), Profitability Index (PI)

**Step 5: Decision Making**
- If **NPV > 0** → Accept
- If **NPV < 0** → Reject
- For mutually exclusive projects → Choose the one with the **highest NPV**
- For capital rationing → Use **Profitability Index ranking** within the budget constraint

**Step 6: Implementation**
Acquire the asset, install, recruit/train staff, and commence operations. **Implementation gaps** (cost overruns, time delays) frequently erode the projected NPV — a topic KTU examines under **post-completion audit**.

**Step 7: Performance Review (Post-Completion Audit)**
Compare **actual** vs. **projected** cash flows to:
- Identify forecasting errors
- Improve future project appraisal
- Hold managers accountable

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Deriving All Three Cash Flow Pillars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (Boilerplate KTU Style):** *ABC Ltd. is evaluating a proposal to install a new machine.*
> - Cost of machine: **₹5,00,000**
> - Installation & freight: **₹50,000**
> - Working capital required: **₹1,00,000**
> - Annual incremental revenue: **₹6,00,000**
> - Annual incremental cash operating cost: **₹2,00,000**
> - Project life: **5 years**
> - Salvage value at end of year 5: **₹50,000**
> - Tax rate: **30%**
> - Depreciation: **Straight-Line on the depreciable cost of ₹5,50,000**

**Step A — Initial Investment (t = 0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial Investment} &= C_{\text{asset}} + \text{Installation} + \text{Working Capital} \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 6{,}50{,}000 \text{ rupees}
\end{aligned}
$$

This ₹6,50,000 is the **Year 0 cash outflow**.

**Step B — Annual Depreciation Charge:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{C_{\text{dep}} - S}{n} = \frac{5{,}50{,}000 - 50{,}000}{5} \\
&= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{5} \\
&= 1{,}00{,}000 \text{ rupees per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Annual Operating Cash Flow (Years 1 to 5):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OCF} &= (R - C_{\text{op}})(1 - t) + D \cdot t \\
&= (6{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000)(1 - 0.30) + 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30 \\
&= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.70 + 30{,}000 \\
&= 2{,}80{,}000 + 30{,}000 \\
&= 3{,}10{,}000 \text{ rupees per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Book Value at End of Year 5:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BV}_{5} &= C_{\text{dep}} - (D \times 5) = 5{,}50{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 50{,}000 \text{ rupees}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step E — After-Tax Salvage Value:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gain on Sale} &= S - \text{BV}_{5} = 50{,}000 - 50{,}000 = 0 \\
S_{\text{AT}} &= S - \text{Gain} \cdot t = 50{,}000 - 0 = 50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

(No tax is payable because the salvage equals the book value — a **fully depreciated** asset sale at book value.)

**Step F — Terminal Cash Flow (End of Year 5):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCF} &= S_{\text{AT}} + W_{\text{recovered}} \\
&= 50{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ rupees}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Computed Cash Flows

| **Year** | **Cash Flow Type** | **Component Breakdown** | **Amount (₹)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Year 0 | Initial Investment | $5{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000$ | $-6{,}50{,}000$ |
| Years 1 to 5 | Operating Cash Flow | $(R - C_{\text{op}})(1 - t) + D \cdot t$ | $+3{,}10{,}000$ each |
| Year 5 (additional) | Terminal Cash Flow | $S_{\text{AT}} + W_{\text{recovered}}$ | $+1{,}50{,}000$ |
| **Year 5 (Net)** | — | $3{,}10{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000$ | **$+4{,}60{,}000$** |

> [!TIP]
> **The KTU examination trick:** Notice how Year 5 has **two inflows** — the operating cash flow AND the terminal cash flow. Students commonly forget the terminal flow, leading to a 7-mark deduction in numerical questions. **Always sum both at year n.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Capital Budgeting Process

The following block-level architecture diagram captures the **sequential logic** of the capital budgeting process, with explicit decision gates for accept/reject and feedback loops for performance review.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Identify Investment Opportunities"] --> node2["Collect Relevant Data and Estimates"]
    node2 --> node3["Estimate Initial Investment"]
    node3 --> node4["Project Operating Cash Flows Year 1 to n"]
    node4 --> node5["Estimate Terminal Cash Flow at Year n"]
    node5 --> node6["Apply Evaluation Techniques NPV IRR PI"]
    node6 --> node7{"Decision Gate"}
    node7 -->|Accept| node8["Approve and Allocate Capital"]
    node7 -->|Reject| node9["Drop or Defer Project"]
    node8 --> node10["Implementation and Monitoring"]
    node10 --> node11["Post Completion Audit and Review"]
    node11 -.->|Feedback Loop| node2
    node9 -.->|Feedback Loop| node2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The dashed feedback arrows represent the **learning loop** — insights from the post-completion audit are fed back into future data-collection stages, enabling continuous improvement in capital budgeting accuracy. This is a high-yield KTU theory point.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Types of Capital Budgeting Decisions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rootA["Capital Budgeting Decision Types"] --> type1["Accept Reject Decisions"]
    rootA --> type2["Mutually Exclusive Decisions"]
    rootA --> type3["Independent Decisions"]
    rootA --> type4["Capital Rationing Decisions"]
    rootA --> type5["Contingent or Dependent Decisions"]
    type1 --> desc1["Pass fail test for viability"]
    type2 --> desc2["Pick one from a set of alternatives"]
    type3 --> desc3["Standalone projects no interaction"]
    type4 --> desc4["Limited capital choose best subset"]
    type5 --> desc5["Sequential or linked projects"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Three Cash Flow Pillars

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pillar0["Project Cash Flow Stream"] --> p1["Pillar 1 Initial Investment at t=0"]
    pillar0 --> p2["Pillar 2 Operating Cash Flows Years 1 to n"]
    pillar0 --> p3["Pillar 3 Terminal Cash Flow at Year n"]
    p1 --> c1["Asset cost plus installation plus working capital minus old asset proceeds"]
    p2 --> c2["Revenue minus operating cost times one minus t plus depreciation times t"]
    p3 --> c3["After tax salvage value plus working capital recovery"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Visual Cue:** When a KTU question asks you to "describe the capital budgeting process," reproducing any one of the above diagrams (correctly labeled) in your answer booklet typically fetches **2 to 3 extra marks** as visual evidence of conceptual clarity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`** Define capital budgeting. State any **two** features that distinguish it from operating budgeting.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Capital budgeting is the process of planning, evaluating, and selecting long-term investment projects whose benefits accrue over multiple years. It is concerned with the allocation of the firm's scarce long-term capital to projects that maximize shareholder wealth.
>
> **Any Two Distinguishing Features (1.5 Marks — 0.75 each):**
> 1. **Time Horizon:** Capital budgeting focuses on long-term projects (typically 5–20 years), whereas operating budgeting deals with the **day-to-day revenue and expenditure** of one fiscal year.
> 2. **Reversibility:** Operating decisions can be **reversed easily**, while capital decisions, once implemented, are largely **irreversible** and involve heavy sunk costs.
> 3. **Risk Profile:** Capital budgeting involves **greater uncertainty** and risk because future cash flows must be estimated over a long horizon.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`** Distinguish between **mutually exclusive** and **independent** capital budgeting decisions with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Mutually Exclusive (1.5 Marks):** These are alternative projects where the acceptance of one project **automatically rejects** the others. The firm must select the single best alternative. *Example:* Choosing between a petrol car and a diesel car for company use — only one can be purchased.
>
> **Independent (1.5 Marks):** These are projects whose acceptance or rejection **does not affect** the cash flows of any other project. Each can be evaluated separately. *Example:* A company simultaneously buying a new printer for the office and a new oven for the canteen — these are unrelated investments.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) at **7 marks** and (b) at **7 marks**. Module internal choice means the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B, not both.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a) Explain the various types of capital budgeting decisions with suitable examples.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Capital budgeting decisions are classified into the following types:

1. **Accept-Reject Decisions (1.5 Marks):** A binary "go/no-go" decision for each independent project. If the project clears the evaluation criterion (e.g., NPV > 0), it is accepted; otherwise rejected. *Example:* Whether to install solar panels on the factory roof.

2. **Mutually Exclusive Decisions (1.5 Marks):** A choice between two or more alternatives serving the same objective. The firm chooses the project with the **highest NPV** (or other suitable metric). *Example:* Choosing between a heavy-duty press machine from Supplier X (₹20 lakh, life 10 years) and Supplier Y (₹18 lakh, life 8 years).

3. **Capital Rationing Decisions (2 Marks):** A situation where the firm has a **fixed capital budget** (e.g., ₹10 crore) that is insufficient to finance all acceptable projects. The firm must select the **optimal combination of projects** that maximizes total NPV within the budget. The **Profitability Index (PI)** is typically used for ranking. *Example:* A startup with a ₹1 crore fund choosing between Project P (PI = 1.4, cost ₹60 lakh) and Project Q (PI = 1.3, cost ₹50 lakh) — both can be selected within budget.

4. **Independent and Contingent Decisions (2 Marks):** Independent projects do not interact; contingent projects are linked (B can be done only if A is done). *Example of contingent:* Opening a bottling plant (B) only after the mineral water plant (A) is established.

**(b) Discuss the steps involved in the capital budgeting process in detail.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The capital budgeting process consists of the following **seven sequential steps**:

| **Step** | **Description** | **Marks** |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Identification of Opportunities | Scanning for profitable investment proposals arising from expansion, replacement, modernization, R&D, or compliance needs. | 1 |
| 2. Data Collection | Gathering cost, revenue, life, salvage, and tax data. | 1 |
| 3. Cash Flow Estimation | Computing the initial investment, annual operating cash flows, and terminal cash flow. | 1.5 |
| 4. Project Evaluation | Applying techniques like NPV, IRR, PI, and Payback Period. | 1 |
| 5. Decision Making | Accept if NPV > 0; reject if NPV < 0; choose highest NPV among mutually exclusive; use PI for rationing. | 1 |
| 6. Implementation | Acquiring, installing, and starting the project; managing cost overruns and timelines. | 0.5 |
| 7. Post-Completion Audit | Comparing actual vs. projected cash flows; learning loop for future projects. | 1 |

**Conclusion:** A rigorous, sequential capital budgeting process minimizes **subjective bias**, contains **risk**, and aligns project selection with the **wealth-maximization** objective of the firm.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`**

**(a) Explain the concept of cash flows in capital budgeting. Describe the components of the initial investment with an example.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Cash Flows (3.5 Marks):**
In capital budgeting, decisions are based on **incremental cash flows**, not accounting profits. Cash flows represent the actual movement of money into and out of the firm attributable to the project, measured **after tax** and **excluding sunk costs**. Three principles govern cash flow estimation:
1. Only **incremental** cash flows are considered.
2. **Sunk costs** are ignored.
3. **Opportunity costs** are included.

**Components of Initial Investment (3.5 Marks):**
The initial investment is the total cash outflow at the **beginning (t = 0)** of the project. It includes:
1. **Cost of the asset** (purchase price).
2. **Installation, freight, and trial-run expenses.**
3. **Increase in working capital** (inventory, receivables, less payables).
4. **Less: Sale proceeds of any asset replaced** and the corresponding tax effect.

**Example:** If a firm buys a new machine for ₹6,00,000, spends ₹40,000 on installation, and invests ₹1,20,000 in additional working capital, while selling an old machine for ₹50,000 (book value ₹30,000, tax rate 30%):
- After-tax proceeds of old asset = 50,000 − (50,000 − 30,000)(0.30) = 50,000 − 6,000 = ₹44,000
- **Initial Investment = 6,00,000 + 40,000 + 1,20,000 − 44,000 = ₹7,16,000**

**(b) A company is evaluating a project with the following data. Compute the Initial Investment, Annual Operating Cash Flow, and Terminal Cash Flow. Assume a 30% tax rate and straight-line depreciation.** *(7 marks)*

> - Cost of equipment: **₹4,00,000**
> - Installation: **₹30,000**
> - Working capital: **₹70,000**
> - Annual revenue: **₹3,50,000**
> - Annual operating cost: **₹1,50,000**
> - Project life: **5 years**
> - Salvage value: **₹40,000**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initial Investment (2 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial Inv.} &= 4{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 70{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000 \text{ rupees}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Depreciation per Year (1 Mark):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{(4{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}000) - 40{,}000}{5} = \frac{3{,}90{,}000}{5} = 78{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual Operating Cash Flow (2.5 Marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OCF} &= (R - C_{\text{op}})(1 - t) + D \cdot t \\
&= (3{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000)(1 - 0.30) + 78{,}000 \times 0.30 \\
&= 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.70 + 23{,}400 \\
&= 1{,}40{,}000 + 23{,}400 = 1{,}63{,}400 \text{ rupees}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Terminal Cash Flow (1.5 Marks):**
Book value at end of Year 5 = 4,30,000 − (78,000 × 5) = 4,30,000 − 3,90,000 = ₹40,000. Since salvage = book value, there is **no tax on gain**.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TCF} &= S_{\text{AT}} + W_{\text{recovered}} = 40{,}000 + 70{,}000 = 1{,}10{,}000 \text{ rupees}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
| **Component** | **Value** |
|---|---|
| Initial Investment | ₹5,00,000 |
| Annual Operating Cash Flow | ₹1,63,400 |
| Terminal Cash Flow (Year 5) | ₹1,10,000 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Terminal Cash Flow:** Students frequently stop at the operating cash flow and forget to add the salvage + working capital recovery at year n. This is the **#1 cause** of 7-mark deductions in numerical problems.
> 2. **Conflating Depreciation with Cash:** Depreciation is a **non-cash expense**; it must be added back to Net Income. Students sometimes subtract it again, **double-counting**.
> 3. **Sunk Cost Trap:** Any pre-feasibility study cost or past R&D expense is a **sunk cost** and must be excluded from the initial investment.
> 4. **Tax Shield Mis-computation:** The tax shield is **$D \cdot t$**, not $D$. A 1-mark deduction follows if students use the full depreciation figure.
> 5. **Year of Terminal Flow:** The terminal cash flow occurs **at the end of year n**, *in addition to* the operating cash flow of year n — not in year n+1.
> 6. **Book Value vs. Salvage Mismatch:** Many students assume book value is zero. Always compute BV = Depreciable Cost − (D × n) explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Every KTU Exam:**

- **Capital Budgeting** = Long-term investment planning and selection. The goal is **shareholder wealth maximization**.
- **Three Pillars of Cash Flows:** Initial Investment (t = 0), Operating Cash Flow (Years 1 to n), Terminal Cash Flow (Year n).
- **Operating Cash Flow Formula (master this):** $\text{OCF} = (R - C_{\text{op}})(1 - t) + D \cdot t$.
- **Initial Investment Includes:** Asset cost + Installation + Working Capital − After-tax proceeds of any replaced asset.
- **Terminal Cash Flow Includes:** After-tax salvage + Recovery of working capital.
- **Tax Shield on Depreciation** = $D \cdot t$ — it is a real cash inflow, even though depreciation is non-cash.
- **Sunk Costs → Exclude. Opportunity Costs → Include.**
- **Mutually Exclusive Projects** → Choose the one with the **highest NPV** (or IRR if NPV is unavailable, but NPV takes precedence).
- **Capital Rationing** → Rank by **Profitability Index (PI)**, not NPV. Then select the optimal combination within the budget.
- **Straight-Line Depreciation** = $\dfrac{C_{\text{dep}} - S}{n}$.
- **After-Tax Salvage** = $S - (S - \text{BV}) \cdot t$. If $S = \text{BV}$, no tax arises.
- **7 Steps of the Process:** Identification → Data → Cash Flow Estimation → Evaluation → Decision → Implementation → Post-Completion Audit.
- **The Process is Cyclical:** The post-completion audit feeds back into future data-collection and estimation stages.
- **End-of-Period Convention:** All annual cash flows are assumed to occur at the **end of the year**, not the beginning.
- **Tax Rate is Constant:** KTU problems assume a **uniform tax rate** throughout the project life unless specified otherwise.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Capital Budgeting: Concept and Process Overview**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generating Investment Project Proposals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Generating Investment Project Proposals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
