> 2. **Payback vs. Discounted Payback confusion:** Do not apply undiscounted cumulative logic in DPBP. Always recompute the cumulative table using present values.
> 3. **Missing "and/or" word in mutually exclusive NPV-IRR questions:** If ranking conflicts, you must explicitly state *why* NPV is preferred (reinvestment assumption, size disparity, etc.) to secure full marks.
> 4. **Unit mistakes:** Always show NPV in ₹ (rupees) and IRR/ARR in % — confusing the two invites mark deduction.
> 5. **Interpolation precision:** Use the linear interpolation formula correctly: $IRR = r_L + \dfrac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \times (r_H - r_L)$. Reversing the sign of NPV is the most common error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Capital Budgeting Techniques**

- [x] **Definition:** Capital budgeting is the evaluation of long-term investment projects using quantitative techniques.
- [x] **Two main categories:** Non-Discounted (Payback, ARR) and Discounted (NPV, IRR, PI).
- [x] **NPV formula:** $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+k)^t}$ — Accept if NPV $\geq 0$.
- [x] **IRR formula:** Rate $r$ that makes NPV = 0 — Accept if IRR $\geq k$.
- [x] **PI formula:** $PI = \dfrac{PV \text{ of inflows}}{Initial Investment}$ — Accept if PI $\geq 1$.
- [x] **Payback (constant inflows):** $PBP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$.
- [x] **Payback (variable inflows):** Year before full recovery + (Unrecovered at start of year / Cash flow in that year).
- [x] **ARR formula:** $ARR = \dfrac{\text{Average Annual Profit}}{\text{Average Investment}} \times 100$.
- [x] **PVAF shortcut:** Use $PVAF = \dfrac{1 - (1+k)^{-n}}{k}$ when cash flows are constant.
- [x] **IRR interpolation:** $IRR = r_L + \dfrac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \times (r_H - r_L)$.
- [x] **NPV is the gold standard** — it directly measures wealth creation; NPV and IRR may conflict for mutually exclusive projects with different scales or timing patterns.
- [x] **Mutually exclusive projects:** Always prefer the project with the **higher NPV**, not the higher IRR.
- [x] **Capital rationing:** Use **PI** (or NPV per unit of investment) to rank projects.
- [x] **Discount rate** = Cost of capital (WACC) — typically the weighted average cost of equity and debt.
- [x] **Cash flows** must be *incremental* (after-tax) and include *terminal/salvage value* in the final year.
- [x] **Depreciation** is a non-cash charge — excluded from NPV/IRR but deducted while computing accounting profit for ARR.
- [x] **KTU 2024 weightage tip:** Part B questions on NPV-IRR comparison (with conflict resolution) appear almost every semester — practice interpolation and crossover rate identification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Evaluation and Selection: Alternative Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Project Evaluation and Selection: Alternative Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Project Evaluation and Selection** is the systematic, quantitative, and qualitative process by which a firm identifies, analyzes, and chooses among competing long-term investment proposals (capital budgeting projects) to maximize shareholder wealth. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this evaluation is treated as the core of the *Investment Decision* function of financial management, in which a firm must commit present funds expecting future benefits (cash inflows) over multiple periods.

The decision criteria mathematically compare the **incremental cash flows** of a project, the **time value of money (TVM)**, and the **cost of capital (K_d, K_e, or WACC)** to arrive at a single accept/reject/reject-indifference signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):**
> Students must be able to:
> 1. Compute **NPV, IRR, PI, Discounted Payback, Payback, and ARR** for single and mutually exclusive projects.
> 2. Apply **NPV vs. IRR conflict** resolution and the **Incremental IRR** technique.
> 3. Handle **Capital Rationing** and **Ranking Disagreement** problems.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "House Purchase" Analogy

Imagine you are choosing between two ways to spend **₹50,00,000** today:

- **Project A (Flat):** Pays you steady rent of ₹8,00,000 every year for 10 years, then gives the flat back.
- **Project B (Shop):** Pays low rent for 3 years, but after that yields ₹30,00,000 per year for the rest of the period.

At first glance, Project B looks "flashier." But money received **later is worth less** today (a rupee in 2025 is not equal to a rupee in 2034). Therefore, a *Discounting Method* (NPV) will give you the *true* answer, whereas a *Non-Discounting Method* (Payback) may trick you into picking the wrong asset.

**Project Evaluation Methods are the financial "lenses" that translate future uncertain cash flows into present-day rupee decisions.** Some lenses are simple (Payback), some are accounting-based (ARR), and some are academically rigorous (NPV, IRR).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary Anchors**
> - **Cash Flow (CF_t):** Net inflow/outflow at the end of year *t*.
> - **Initial Outlay (I_0):** Investment at time *t = 0*. Treated as negative cash flow.
> - **Cost of Capital (r or k):** Minimum required rate of return (often the firm's WACC).
> - **Accept / Reject:** Binary decision based on a numerical threshold.
> - **Mutually Exclusive Projects:** Picking one means rejecting the others (e.g., buying either a CNC machine OR a manual lathe).
> - **Independent Projects:** Each can be accepted/rejected independently based on its own merit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Tree of Capital Budgeting Techniques

The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly categorizes project evaluation methods into the following hierarchy:

#### A. Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Methods — Time Value Considered
1. **Net Present Value (NPV)**
2. **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**
3. **Profitability Index (PI)**
4. **Discounted Payback Period**

#### B. Non-Discounted Cash Flow (Non-DCF) Methods — Time Value Ignored
1. **Payback Period (PBP)**
2. **Accounting Rate of Return (ARR)**

### 2.2 The Core Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanism

**Why discounting matters (The Why):**
A rupee today can be invested at the cost of capital to become more than a rupee tomorrow. Therefore, future cash flows must be "shrunk" to their present equivalent before comparison.

**How each method works (The How):**

1. **NPV** sums all *discounted* future cash flows and subtracts the initial outlay. It directly measures wealth creation in today's rupees.
2. **IRR** solves for the discount rate that makes NPV exactly zero. It expresses the project's intrinsic rate of return as a percentage, which is then compared to the cost of capital.
3. **PI** divides the present value of future cash inflows by the initial investment, indicating "rupees of present value per rupee invested."
4. **Payback Period** finds the year in which cumulative *undiscounted* cash inflows first equal the initial investment.
5. **Discounted Payback** is identical to Payback but uses *discounted* cumulative inflows.
6. **ARR** uses accounting profits (not cash flows) divided by average investment — a non-cash, non-DCF yardstick.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All symbols below are KTU-board-standard. Memorize the table exactly.**

| # | Method | Mathematical Formula | Decision Rule (Accept if...) | Key Unit / Variable |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $$NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$$ | $NPV \geq 0$ | Rupees (₹) |
| 2 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $$0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} - I_0$$ | $IRR \geq r$ (cost of capital) | Percentage (%) |
| 3 | **Profitability Index (PI)** | $$PI = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}}{I_0}$$ | $PI \geq 1$ (or $\geq 1$ for ranking) | Ratio (pure number) |
| 4 | **Discounted Payback Period** | $$\text{Find } t^{*} \text{ where } \sum_{t=1}^{t^{*}} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} = I_0$$ | $t^{*} \leq \text{Target Period}$ | Years |
| 5 | **Payback Period (Uniform CFs)** | $$\text{Payback} = \frac{I_0}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$$ | $t \leq \text{Target Period}$ | Years |
| 6 | **Payback Period (Non-Uniform CFs)** | $$\text{Payback} = \text{Last full year} + \frac{\text{Unrecovered amount}}{\text{Next year CF}}$$ | $t \leq \text{Target Period}$ | Years |
| 7 | **Accounting Rate of Return (ARR)** | $$ARR = \frac{\text{Average Annual Accounting Profit}}{\text{Average Investment}} \times 100$$ | $ARR \geq \text{Target Rate}$ | Percentage (%) |
| 8 | **Average Investment** | $$\text{Avg. Investment} = \frac{I_0 + \text{Salvage Value}}{2}$$ | Used in ARR denominator | Rupees (₹) |
| 9 | **Incremental IRR (ΔIRR)** | $$0 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\Delta CF_t}{(1+\Delta IRR)^t}$$ | Accept larger project if $\Delta IRR \geq r$ | Percentage (%) |
| 10 | **Equivalent Annual Annuity (EAA)** | $$EAA = \frac{NPV \times r}{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}$$ | Higher EAA wins (unequal lives) | Rupees per year |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL ESCAPE HATCH:** When the pipe symbol $\vert$ is used for absolute value in exam answers, KTU valuators accept $PV = CF/(1+r)^t$ notation. In your own formula sheets, write $\vert I_0 \vert$ as \$\vert I\_0 \vert\$ only when truly needed.

### 2.4 Why These Methods Matter in Real Engineering Projects

For a Kerala-based B.Tech graduate managing a plant or construction firm, these methods are not academic:

- **Buying a CNC machine (₹30 lakh)** vs. **a 3D printer (₹15 lakh):** NPV tells you which one genuinely creates shareholder wealth.
- **Solar plant installation (25-year life)** vs. **Diesel generator (10-year life):** Equivalent Annual Annuity (EAA) gives a fair comparison.
- **Tender selection for highway contracts:** Profitability Index identifies the project that returns the most value *per rupee of scarce capital* — vital in capital rationing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: NPV from First Principles (Discounting Logic)

The fundamental premise of the time value of money is that:

$$PV_0 = \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

This is the **Present Value (PV)** of a future cash flow $CF_t$ receivable after $t$ years, discounted at rate $r$.

**Step 1.** The Net Present Value is the algebraic sum of all discounted cash flows, *including* the initial outflow at $t=0$ (which is already at present value, so no discounting needed).

$$NPV = -I_0 + \frac{CF_1}{(1+r)^1} + \frac{CF_2}{(1+r)^2} + \cdots + \frac{CF_n}{(1+r)^n} + \frac{S_n}{(1+r)^n}$$

**Step 2.** In compact summation form:

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

where $CF_0 = -I_0$.

**Step 3.** Decision rule derivation:
- If $NPV > 0$: The project earns *more* than the cost of capital → **Accept** (wealth created).
- If $NPV < 0$: The project earns *less* than the cost of capital → **Reject** (wealth destroyed).
- If $NPV = 0$: Indifference; project's IRR exactly equals the discount rate.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: IRR as the Root of the NPV Function

**Step 1.** Set NPV equal to zero:

$$0 = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t}$$

**Step 2.** Rearrange to isolate IRR — this is a *polynomial* of degree $n$ in $(1+IRR)$, so it cannot be solved algebraically beyond $n=2$.

**Step 3.** Use the **Interpolation (Trial-and-Error) Formula** for KTU board exams:

$$IRR = R_L + \frac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \times (R_H - R_L)$$

where:
- $R_L$ = Lower discount rate (giving positive NPV)
- $R_H$ = Higher discount rate (giving negative NPV)
- $NPV_L$ = NPV at $R_L$
- $NPV_H$ = NPV at $R_H$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** The interpolation formula assumes a *linear* NPV profile between $R_L$ and $R_H$. State this assumption explicitly in the answer to earn full marks.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Payback Period (Non-Uniform Cash Flows)

**Step 1.** Construct a cumulative cash flow column.

**Step 2.** Identify the *last year* in which cumulative cash flow is still negative (or, in the discounted version, still less than $I_0$).

**Step 3.** Apply the fractional formula:

$$\text{Payback} = A + \frac{B}{C}$$

where:
- $A$ = Last year with negative cumulative cash flow
- $B$ = Absolute unrecovered amount at the end of year $A$
- $C$ = Cash inflow during the year *after* $A$

### 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem Statement:**
A firm evaluates two mutually exclusive projects, A and B, with a cost of capital of **10%**. The data is:

| Year | Project A CF (₹) | Project B CF (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | -1,00,000 | -1,00,000 |
| 1 | 40,000 | 10,000 |
| 2 | 40,000 | 20,000 |
| 3 | 40,000 | 30,000 |
| 4 | 20,000 | 60,000 |
| 5 | — | 80,000 |

Required: Compute NPV, IRR (by interpolation), PI, and Payback for each project and recommend.

---

#### Step A — Compute NPV of Project A (r = 10%)

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV_A &= -1{,}00{,}000 + \frac{40{,}000}{1.10^1} + \frac{40{,}000}{1.10^2} + \frac{40{,}000}{1.10^3} + \frac{20{,}000}{1.10^4} \\
&= -1{,}00{,}000 + 36{,}363.64 + 33{,}057.85 + 30{,}052.59 + 13{,}660.27 \\
&= +13{,}134.35 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}$$

**Decision (NPV):** Since $NPV_A > 0$, **Project A is acceptable** on a stand-alone basis.

---

#### Step B — Compute NPV of Project B (r = 10%)

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV_B &= -1{,}00{,}000 + \frac{10{,}000}{1.10^1} + \frac{20{,}000}{1.10^2} + \frac{30{,}000}{1.10^3} + \frac{60{,}000}{1.10^4} + \frac{80{,}000}{1.10^5} \\
&= -1{,}00{,}000 + 9{,}090.91 + 16{,}528.93 + 22{,}539.44 + 40{,}980.80 + 49{,}670.99 \\
&= +38{,}811.07 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}$$

**Decision (NPV):** Since $NPV_B > NPV_A$, **Project B is preferred** for mutually exclusive choice.

---

#### Step C — Compute IRR of Project A (Interpolation)

Test at $r = 15\%$:

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV_A(15\%) &= -1{,}00{,}000 + \frac{40{,}000}{1.15} + \frac{40{,}000}{1.15^2} + \frac{40{,}000}{1.15^3} + \frac{20{,}000}{1.15^4} \\
&= -1{,}00{,}000 + 34{,}782.61 + 30{,}245.75 + 26{,}300.65 + 11{,}435.07 \\
&= +2{,}764.08 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}$$

Test at $r = 18\%$:

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV_A(18\%) &= -1{,}00{,}000 + 33{,}898.31 + 28{,}727.38 + 24{,}345.24 + 10{,}322.99 \\
&= -2{,}706.08 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}$$

Apply the interpolation formula:

$$IRR_A = 15\% + \frac{2{,}764.08}{2{,}764.08 - (-2{,}706.08)} \times (18\% - 15\%)$$

$$IRR_A = 15\% + \frac{2{,}764.08}{5{,}470.16} \times 3\% = 15\% + 1.52\% = 16.52\%$$

**Decision (IRR):** $IRR_A = 16.52\% > 10\%$ (cost of capital) → **Project A is acceptable**.

---

#### Step D — Compute IRR of Project B (Interpolation)

Test at $r = 18\%$:

$$NPV_B(18\%) = -1{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}474.58 + 14{,}363.69 + 18{,}892.99 + 35{,}153.43 + 39{,}435.45 = +16{,}320.14 \text{ ₹}$$

Test at $r = 25\%$:

$$NPV_B(25\%) = -1{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 12{,}800 + 15{,}360 + 24{,}576 + 26{,}214.40 = -13{,}049.60 \text{ ₹}$$

Apply interpolation:

$$IRR_B = 18\% + \frac{16{,}320.14}{16{,}320.14 + 13{,}049.60} \times (25\% - 18\%)$$

$$IRR_B = 18\% + \frac{16{,}320.14}{29{,}369.74} \times 7\% = 18\% + 3.89\% = 21.89\%$$

**Decision (IRR):** $IRR_B = 21.89\% > 10\%$ → **Project B is acceptable**; also $IRR_B > IRR_A$ → Project B is preferred on IRR basis *for this data set*.

---

#### Step E — Profitability Index

$$\begin{aligned}
PI_A &= \frac{PV \text{ of inflows of A}}{I_0} = \frac{1{,}13{,}134.35}{1{,}00{,}000} = 1.131 \\
PI_B &= \frac{PV \text{ of inflows of B}}{I_0} = \frac{1{,}38{,}811.07}{1{,}00{,}000} = 1.388
\end{aligned}$$

**Decision (PI):** $PI_B > PI_A$ → **Project B is preferred** (consistent with NPV here).

---

#### Step F — Payback Period (Undiscounted)

| Year | Cum. CF A | Cum. CF B |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 40,000 | 10,000 |
| 2 | 80,000 | 30,000 |
| 3 | 1,20,000 | 60,000 |
| 4 | 1,40,000 | 1,20,000 |
| 5 | — | 2,00,000 |

**Project A:** Reaches ₹1,00,000 between Year 2 and Year 3.

$$\text{Payback}_A = 2 + \frac{1{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000}{40{,}000} = 2 + 0.5 = 2.5 \text{ years}$$

**Project B:** Reaches ₹1,00,000 between Year 4 and Year 5.

$$\text{Payback}_B = 4 + \frac{1{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000}{80{,}000} = 4 + 0.25 = 4.25 \text{ years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conflict Scenario Triggered:** Project A has the *shorter* Payback Period, but Project B has the *higher* NPV, IRR, and PI. This is the classic **NPV vs. Payback Conflict** — KTU students must always defer to NPV.

### 3.5 The NPV vs. IRR Conflict — Incremental IRR Solution

When **NPV and IRR give opposite rankings** (or when the two projects are of *different scale*), KTU mandates the **Incremental IRR (ΔIRR)** test:

**Step 1.** Order projects by *increasing* initial investment (here both are ₹1,00,000, but assume B requires ₹1,20,000 for the incremental test).

**Step 2.** Compute the incremental cash flows ($\Delta CF_t = CF_{B,t} - CF_{A,t}$).

**Step 3.** Compute $\Delta IRR$ from the incremental cash flow series.

**Step 4.** If $\Delta IRR \geq r$, accept the **larger** project (B); else stick with the smaller project (A).

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$\Delta NPV(r) = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\Delta CF_t}{(1+r)^t} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{find } \Delta IRR$$

This is the *only* academically correct way to resolve a scale difference. Pure IRR comparison is misleading when project sizes differ.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Decision Tree — Project Evaluation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Cash Flow Forecast] --> B{Method Category}
    B -- DCF --> C1[NPV Calculation]
    B -- DCF --> C2[IRR Calculation]
    B -- DCF --> C3[PI Calculation]
    B -- DCF --> C4[Discounted Payback]
    B -- Non-DCF --> D1[Simple Payback]
    B -- Non-DCF --> D2[ARR]
    C1 --> E1{NPV Decision}
    E1 -- NPV greater than 0 --> F1[Accept Project]
    E1 -- NPV less than 0 --> F2[Reject Project]
    E1 -- NPV equals 0 --> F3[Indifference]
    C2 --> E2{IRR vs Cost of Capital}
    E2 -- IRR greater than r --> F1
    E2 -- IRR less than r --> F2
    E2 -- IRR equals r --> F3
    C3 --> E3{PI Decision}
    E3 -- PI greater than 1 --> F1
    E3 -- PI less than 1 --> F2
    C4 --> E4{Period vs Target}
    E4 -- Within Target --> F1
    E4 -- Beyond Target --> F2
    D1 --> G1{Period vs Target}
    G1 -- Within Target --> F1
    G1 -- Beyond Target --> F2
    D2 --> H1{ARR vs Target}
    H1 -- Greater --> F1
    H1 -- Less --> F2
    F1 --> I[Project Selected for Implementation]
    F2 --> J[Project Rejected]
    F3 --> K[Re-evaluate Assumptions]
```

### 4.2 NPV Profile — Visualizing the NPV vs. IRR Conflict

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Discount Rate 0 percent] --> A2[NPV Profile Curve]
    A2 --> A3[Crosses X axis at IRR]
    A3 --> A4[At r equals IRR, NPV equals 0]
    A4 --> A5[For r less than IRR, NPV greater than 0]
    A5 --> A6[For r greater than IRR, NPV less than 0]
    subgraph S1[Key Insight]
        direction TB
        K1[Project A: High early CFs, steep NPV decline]
        K2[Project B: Back-loaded CFs, gentle NPV decline]
        K3[NPV profiles may cross at Fisher Rate]
    end
    A6 --> S1
    K3 --> K4[Below Fisher rate: NPV rule picks one]
    K4 --> K5[Above Fisher rate: opposite project wins]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Method Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph S1[Input Stage]
        I1[Initial Investment I0]
        I2[Cash Flow Series CF1 to CFn]
        I3[Cost of Capital r]
        I4[Project Life n]
    end
    subgraph S2[Processing Stage]
        P1[Discounting Engine 1 over 1 plus r to power t]
        P2[Cumulative Sum Engine]
        P3[Root Finding Engine for IRR]
        P4[Ratio Engine for PI]
    end
    subgraph S3[Output Stage]
        O1[NPV in Rupees]
        O2[IRR in Percent]
        O3[PI as Ratio]
        O4[Payback in Years]
        O5[ARR in Percent]
    end
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    P1 --> O1
    P1 --> O2
    P1 --> O3
    P2 --> O4
    I2 --> P3
    P3 --> O2
    I1 --> P4
    P4 --> O3
```

### 4.4 Mutually Exclusive vs. Independent Project Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Pool] --> B{Project Type}
    B -- Independent --> C1[Evaluate Each on Stand-Alone Basis]
    C1 --> D1{NPV greater than 0}
    D1 -- Yes --> E1[Accept]
    D1 -- No --> F1[Reject]
    B -- Mutually Exclusive --> C2[Rank by NPV or PI]
    C2 --> D2{NPV highest}
    D2 -- Yes --> E2[Choose Highest NPV]
    D2 -- Conflict with IRR --> G2[Use Incremental IRR Test]
    G2 --> D3{Delta IRR greater than r}
    D3 -- Yes --> E2
    D3 -- No --> H2[Stick with Smaller Investment]
    B -- Capital Rationed --> C3[Rank by PI]
    C3 --> D4{Top N Projects within Budget}
    D4 -- Yes --> E3[Accept Selected Portfolio]
    D4 -- No --> F3[Reject All Others]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Net Present Value (NPV) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR). Which one is theoretically superior and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | NPV | IRR |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Present value of cash flows *minus* initial investment, at the cost of capital | Discount rate that makes NPV equal to zero |
| **Unit** | Rupees (absolute wealth) | Percentage (relative rate) |
| **Decision Rule** | $NPV \geq 0$ | $IRR \geq r$ |
| **Reinvestment Assumption** | Cash flows reinvested at cost of capital $r$ | Cash flows reinvested at IRR (often unrealistic) |
| **Scale Handling** | Directly accounts for project size | Misleading for projects of different sizes |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** NPV is theoretically superior because (i) it directly measures wealth creation in rupees, (ii) it uses the realistic reinvestment rate (cost of capital), and (iii) it does not suffer from multiple IRR or scaling problems.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain the concept of "Mutually Exclusive Projects." Why is Profitability Index (PI) used for ranking such projects under capital rationing?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Mutually Exclusive Projects (1 mark):** Two or more investment proposals are *mutually exclusive* if accepting one automatically rejects all others. The firm has a *single* investment slot and must choose the *best* alternative (e.g., buying Machine A *or* Machine B, not both).

**PI for Capital Rationing (2 marks):** When capital is scarce (rationed), NPV alone is insufficient because a high-NPV project may consume too much capital. **PI** measures *rupees of present value per rupee invested*, helping the firm select a portfolio that maximizes total NPV within the budget. KTU requires PI-based ranking combined with NPV checks (accept only if $PI \geq 1$).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

> A company is evaluating two mutually exclusive projects, **X** and **Y**, with a cost of capital of **12%**. The cash flows are:
>
> | Year | Project X (₹) | Project Y (₹) |
> |---|---|---|
> | 0 | -5,00,000 | -5,00,000 |
> | 1 | 1,80,000 | 1,00,000 |
> | 2 | 1,80,000 | 1,50,000 |
> | 3 | 1,80,000 | 2,00,000 |
> | 4 | 1,80,000 | 2,50,000 |
> | 5 | 80,000 | 3,00,000 |
>
> Required:
> **(a)** Compute the NPV and Profitability Index of both projects. Recommend the better project with justification. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compute the IRR of both projects using the interpolation method. If the IRR and NPV methods give conflicting rankings, apply the **Incremental IRR** technique to resolve the conflict. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Compute the PV factors at 12%**

$$\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= \frac{1}{1.12} = 0.8929 \\
PV_2 &= \frac{1}{1.12^2} = 0.7972 \\
PV_3 &= \frac{1}{1.12^3} = 0.7118 \\
PV_4 &= \frac{1}{1.12^4} = 0.6355 \\
PV_5 &= \frac{1}{1.12^5} = 0.5674
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: NPV of Project X**

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV_X &= -5{,}00{,}000 + (1{,}80{,}000 \times 0.8929) + (1{,}80{,}000 \times 0.7972) + (1{,}80{,}000 \times 0.7118) + (1{,}80{,}000 \times 0.6355) + (80{,}000 \times 0.5674) \\
&= -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}60{,}722 + 1{,}43{,}496 + 1{,}28{,}124 + 1{,}14{,}390 + 45{,}392 \\
&= +92{,}124 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: NPV of Project Y**

$$\begin{aligned}
NPV_Y &= -5{,}00{,}000 + (1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8929) + (1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.7972) + (2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7118) + (2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.6355) + (3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5674) \\
&= -5{,}00{,}000 + 89{,}290 + 1{,}19{,}580 + 1{,}42{,}360 + 1{,}58{,}875 + 1{,}70{,}220 \\
&= +1{,}80{,}325 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Profitability Index**

$$\begin{aligned}
PI_X &= \frac{5{,}92{,}124}{5{,}00{,}000} = 1.184 \\
PI_Y &= \frac{6{,}80{,}325}{5{,}00{,}000} = 1.361
\end{aligned}$$

**Recommendation (1 mark):** Since $NPV_Y > NPV_X$ and $PI_Y > PI_X$, **Project Y is recommended.** The NPV and PI rankings are consistent.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: IRR of Project X (Interpolation)**

Compute NPV at $r = 15\%$:

$$NPV_X(15\%) = -5{,}00{,}000 + (1{,}80{,}000 \times 0.8696 \times 4) + (80{,}000 \times 0.4972) = +9{,}700 \text{ ₹ (approx.)}$$

Compute NPV at $r = 16\%$:

$$NPV_X(16\%) \approx -16{,}200 \text{ ₹}$$

Interpolation:

$$IRR_X = 15\% + \frac{9{,}700}{9{,}700 + 16{,}200} \times 1\% = 15\% + 0.37\% \approx 15.37\%$$

**Step 2: IRR of Project Y (Interpolation)**

Compute NPV at $r = 20\%$:

$$NPV_Y(20\%) \approx +25{,}500 \text{ ₹}$$

Compute NPV at $r = 25\%$:

$$NPV_Y(25\%) \approx -42{,}300 \text{ ₹}$$

Interpolation:

$$IRR_Y = 20\% + \frac{25{,}500}{25{,}500 + 42{,}300} \times 5\% = 20\% + 1.88\% \approx 21.88\%$$

**Step 3: Apply Incremental IRR (Y – X) [2 marks]**

Incremental cash flows: $\Delta CF_t = CF_{Y,t} - CF_{X,t}$

| Year | $\Delta CF$ (₹) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0 |
| 1 | -80,000 |
| 2 | -30,000 |
| 3 | +20,000 |
| 4 | +70,000 |
| 5 | +2,20,000 |

At $r = 12\%$, $\Delta NPV = NPV_Y - NPV_X = +88{,}201$ ₹. Since $\Delta NPV > 0$, the additional investment in Y is justified.

$$\Delta IRR > 12\% \quad \Rightarrow \text{Accept the larger project (Y).}$$

**Final Recommendation (1 mark):** **Project Y** is selected because it dominates Project X on NPV, PI, IRR, and Incremental IRR grounds simultaneously — no conflict to resolve.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary June 2024]**

> M/s Kerala Engineering Ltd. has a capital rationing budget of **₹20,00,000** and a target return (cost of capital) of **10%**. The following independent projects are available:
>
> | Project | Initial Investment (₹) | NPV at 10% (₹) |
> |---|---|---|
> | P1 | 8,00,000 | 1,20,000 |
> | P2 | 7,00,000 | 1,05,000 |
> | P3 | 9,00,000 | 90,000 |
> | P4 | 6,00,000 | 80,000 |
> | P5 | 5,00,000 | 75,000 |
>
> Required:
> **(a)** Rank the projects using the Profitability Index (PI) and identify the optimal combination of projects within the ₹20,00,000 budget. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the limitations of the Payback Period method and compute the Payback Period (undiscounted) for the project with the highest PI. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Compute PI for each project (2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
PI_{P1} &= \frac{1{,}20{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = \frac{9{,}20{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = 1.150 \\
PI_{P2} &= \frac{1{,}05{,}000 + 7{,}00{,}000}{7{,}00{,}000} = \frac{8{,}05{,}000}{7{,}00{,}000} = 1.150 \\
PI_{P3} &= \frac{90{,}000 + 9{,}00{,}000}{9{,}00{,}000} = \frac{9{,}90{,}000}{9{,}00{,}000} = 1.100 \\
PI_{P4} &= \frac{80{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} = \frac{6{,}80{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} = 1.133 \\
PI_{P5} &= \frac{75{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = \frac{5{,}75{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 1.150
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Rank by PI (Descending) (1 mark)**

| Rank | Project | PI | Investment (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P1 | 1.150 | 8,00,000 |
| 2 | P2 | 1.150 | 7,00,000 |
| 3 | P5 | 1.150 | 5,00,000 |
| 4 | P4 | 1.133 | 6,00,000 |
| 5 | P3 | 1.100 | 9,00,000 |

**Step 3: Build Combinations under ₹20,00,000 (3 marks)**

| Combination | Projects | Total Investment (₹) | Total NPV (₹) | Within Budget? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | P1 + P2 + P5 | 20,00,000 | 3,00,000 | ✓ |
| B | P1 + P2 + P4 | 21,00,000 | 3,05,000 | ✗ |
| C | P1 + P5 + P4 | 19,00,000 | 2,75,000 | ✓ |
| D | P2 + P5 + P4 | 18,00,000 | 2,60,000 | ✓ |
| E | P1 + P2 + P5 + P4 | 26,00,000 | 3,80,000 | ✗ |

**Step 4: Select Optimal Portfolio (1 mark)**

The feasible combination with the **highest total NPV** that fits the budget is **Combination B logic** — but since B exceeds the budget, choose **Combination A: P1 + P2 + P5** with Total NPV = **₹3,00,000** using the full ₹20,00,000.

> *Alternative valid answer:* If fractional investments are allowed, Combination B (P1 + P2 + P4) may be chosen as marginal NPV per extra rupee is positive.

---

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Limitations of Payback Period Method (4 marks):**

1. **Ignores Time Value of Money:** Cash flows received in later years are treated equal to those received today.
2. **Ignores Cash Flows After Payback:** A project with strong long-term returns may be rejected simply because the initial investment is recouped slowly.
3. **Subjective Cut-off:** The "target payback period" is arbitrary and varies by management preference.
4. **Bias Against Long-Term, Wealth-Creating Projects:** Strategic projects (e.g., R&D, market entry) typically have long paybacks but high NPVs.
5. **Not a Measure of Profitability:** It only measures *liquidity recovery*, not *return*.

**Step 5: Payback Period of Project P1 (Highest PI) (3 marks)**

*Cash flow data for P1 (assumed):* CFs over 5 years = ₹2,40,000 per year (so that NPV ≈ ₹1,20,000 at 10%).

| Year | CF (₹) | Cumulative CF (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2,40,000 | 2,40,000 |
| 2 | 2,40,000 | 4,80,000 |
| 3 | 2,40,000 | 7,20,000 |
| 4 | 2,40,000 | 9,60,000 |
| 5 | 2,40,000 | 12,00,000 |

Initial Investment = ₹8,00,000. Recovery occurs between Year 3 and Year 4.

$$\text{Payback} = 3 + \frac{8{,}00{,}000 - 7{,}20{,}000}{2{,}40{,}000} = 3 + \frac{80{,}000}{2{,}40{,}000} = 3 + 0.33 = 3.33 \text{ years}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract Initial Investment:** Students often sum CFs without the $-I_0$ term, leading to a wrong NPV sign. KTU deducts 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **IRR Interpolation Linear Assumption:** If you don't state "assuming linear NPV profile between $R_L$ and $R_H$," the examiner may deduct 0.5 marks.
> 3. **NPV vs. IRR Conflict Ignored:** For mutually exclusive projects, comparing IRRs directly is *not* acceptable. Always use Incremental IRR.
> 4. **PI Numerator Error:** PI = (PV of inflows) / $I_0$. Some students wrongly compute it as (NPV / $I_0$) + 1, which is mathematically the same — but write it as $\frac{PV_{inflows}}{I_0}$ to avoid confusion.
> 5. **Capital Rationing — Integer vs. Fractional:** KTU generally expects *all-or-nothing* projects unless the question states divisible investments.
> 6. **ARR Denominator Error:** ARR uses *Average Investment* = $(I_0 + \text{Salvage})/2$, not $I_0$. Mistaking this loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Rapid Fire)**
>
> - **NPV** is the *gold standard* of capital budgeting; always the primary decision metric in KTU answers.
> - **NPV Decision Rule:** Accept if $NPV \geq 0$; Reject if $NPV < 0$.
> - **IRR Decision Rule:** Accept if $IRR \geq r$ (cost of capital).
> - **PI Decision Rule:** Accept if $PI \geq 1$. PI ranks projects under capital rationing.
> - **Payback Decision Rule:** Accept if Payback $\leq$ Target Period. Ignores TVM — treat as a *secondary* screening tool.
> - **ARR Decision Rule:** Accept if $ARR \geq$ Target Return. Uses accounting profit, not cash flow.
> - **Discounted Payback** fixes Payback's TVM flaw but still ignores post-payback cash flows.
> - **NPV vs. IRR Conflict:** Occurs due to (i) scale difference, (ii) timing difference, (iii) cash flow sign changes. Resolve using **Incremental IRR**.
> - **Fisher Rate (Intersection):** The discount rate at which two projects' NPV profiles cross. Below Fisher, NPV picks one; above, the other.
> - **Equivalent Annual Annuity (EAA):** Used when comparing projects of *unequal lives*. $EAA = \dfrac{NPV \times r}{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}$. Higher EAA wins.
> - **Capital Rationing:** Rank by PI; select top combinations within budget. Verify total NPV is maximized.
> - **Independent Projects:** Evaluate each separately; accept all with $NPV > 0$ (if no capital constraint).
> - **Mutually Exclusive Projects:** Choose the one with *highest NPV* (after Incremental IRR check).
> - **Multiple IRR Problem:** Occurs when cash flow sign changes more than once. Use NPV instead, or apply the **Modified IRR (MIRR)** technique.
> - **Real Options Consideration:** KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes that qualitative factors (strategic fit, environmental impact, employee morale) may override pure DCF results.

**Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** **"NIPP-DA"** →
- **N**PV (primary),
- **I**RR (intrinsic rate),
- **P**I (rationing),
- **P**ayback (screening),
- **D**iscounted Payback (TVM-corrected),
- **A**RR (accounting).

**Last-Mark Tactic:** Always conclude a 14-mark capital budgeting answer with a one-line statement: *"Therefore, based on the NPV criterion (the theoretically superior method), Project [X] is recommended as it maximizes shareholder wealth by ₹\_\_\_\_\_\_\_."* KTU valuators award the final 1 mark for this explicit conclusion.

# Module 4: Working Capital Management — Overview

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Capital (WC)** is the capital required by a firm to finance its **short-term assets** (current assets) and to meet **short-term obligations** (current liabilities) arising out of the day-to-day operations of a business.

In its **gross** sense, Working Capital refers to the **total investment in current assets** such as cash, receivables, inventories, and marketable securities.

In its **net** sense, Working Capital is defined as:

$$\text{Net Working Capital (NWC)} = \text{Current Assets (CA)} - \text{Current Liabilities (CL)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology:**
> - **Permanent Working Capital (PWC):** The minimum level of investment in current assets that a firm must maintain at all times to sustain operations (also called *Fixed Working Capital*).
> - **Variable Working Capital (VWC):** The additional working capital required to meet seasonal or cyclical fluctuations in demand.
> - **Operating Cycle (OC):** The total time duration between the acquisition of raw materials and the final realization of cash from the sale of finished goods.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Engine Oil" Analogy

Imagine a car engine. The engine is your **business**, and the **oil circulating inside** is your working capital.

- Without enough oil, the engine **seizes up** (liquidity crisis → insolvency).
- **Too much oil** reduces fuel efficiency (idle current assets reduce profitability).
- The oil must be **continuously circulated, filtered, and topped up** (working capital is dynamic, not static).
- A **longer road trip** (longer operating cycle) needs **more oil reserve** (more working capital).

Just as an engine needs a continuous flow of oil for smooth operation, a business needs adequate working capital to fund its **purchases → production → sales → collection** cycle without interruption.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world example:** A biotechnology firm manufacturing vaccines must maintain cold-chain inventory (raw materials + finished goods), offer credit to hospital buyers (receivables), and pay suppliers upfront (payables). The **gap** between these flows must be financed by working capital.

---

## 1.3 Why Working Capital Management Matters in KTU 2024 Context

Working capital management is one of the **most frequently tested modules** in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). It tests both **conceptual clarity** (types, factors) and **numerical estimation** (operating cycle method).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner's Note:** The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **applied problem-solving** — students are expected to not just *define* working capital but to **estimate** the quantum required using the **Operating Cycle Method**, which is the standard numerical tool.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 4 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Conceptual Views of Working Capital

### (a) Balance Sheet View
- **Gross Working Capital (GWC):** Total Current Assets
- **Net Working Capital (NWC):** Current Assets − Current Liabilities

### (b) Operating Cycle View (Functional View)
- Working capital is the **funds required to finance the operating cycle**.
- The longer the operating cycle, the higher the working capital requirement.

---

## 2.2 Types of Working Capital — Structured Classification

**1. Permanent (Fixed) Working Capital**
- Always retained in the business, irrespective of sales volume fluctuations.
- **Minimum level** needed to ensure uninterrupted operations.
- Part of long-term financing decisions (financed via equity, debentures, long-term loans).

**2. Variable (Fluctuating) Working Capital**
- Fluctuates with the **volume of business activity**.
- Increases during peak seasons, decreases during off-seasons.
- Financed through short-term sources (bank overdraft, commercial paper, trade credit).

**3. Seasonal Working Capital**
- Special category of variable WC required during **specific seasons** (e.g., ice-cream industry in summer, textile industry during festivals).

**4. Special Working Capital**
- Required for **special operations** like launching a new product, executing a major contract, or participating in a trade exhibition.

---

## 2.3 Factors Determining Working Capital Requirement

| **S.No.** | **Factor** | **Impact on WC** |
|------------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | Nature & Size of Business | Manufacturing firms need more WC than service firms |
| 2 | Length of Operating Cycle | Longer OC → Higher WC |
| 3 | Sales Volume & Growth | Higher sales → Higher WC |
| 4 | Credit Policy (Tight) | Strict credit terms → Lower receivables → Lower WC |
| 5 | Credit Policy (Liberal) | Easy credit → Higher receivables → Higher WC |
| 6 | Inventory Turnover Ratio | Lower turnover → Higher inventory → Higher WC |
| 7 | Availability of Credit from Suppliers | Higher payable days → Lower WC |
| 8 | Seasonal Variations | Increases variable WC |
| 9 | Business Cycle Phase | Expansion phase → Higher WC |
| 10 | Price Level Changes | Inflation → Higher WC (in rupee terms) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** "Length of Operating Cycle" and "Credit Policy" are the **two most common 7-mark questions** in KTU ESE.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 80% of Module 4 numerical questions.**

| **Formula** | **LaTeX Expression** | **Application** |
|--------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| Net Working Capital | $\text{NWC} = \text{CA} - \text{CL}$ | Standard NWC calculation |
| Operating Cycle (days) | $\text{OC} = R + W + F + D - C$ | Where R = RM storage, W = WIP holding, F = FG storage, D = Debtors, C = Creditors |
| Raw Material Storage Period | $R = \dfrac{\text{Avg. RM Inventory}}{\text{RM Consumed per Day}}$ | RM holding period |
| Work-in-Process Period | $W = \dfrac{\text{Avg. WIP Inventory}}{\text{Annual Production Cost per Day}}$ | Manufacturing cycle |
| Finished Goods Storage Period | $F = \dfrac{\text{Avg. FG Inventory}}{\text{Cost of Goods Sold per Day}}$ | Post-production storage |
| Debtors / Receivables Period | $D = \dfrac{\text{Avg. Receivables}}{\text{Credit Sales per Day}}$ | Collection efficiency |
| Creditors / Payables Period | $C = \dfrac{\text{Avg. Payables}}{\text{Credit Purchases per Day}}$ | Supplier credit utilization |
| Number of Operating Cycles per Year | $N = \dfrac{365}{\text{OC (in days)}}$ | Capital turnover |
| Estimated Working Capital | $\text{WC Required} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Operating Cost}}{N}$ | Final WC requirement |
| Working Capital as % of Sales | $\text{WC\%} = \dfrac{\text{WC}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Comparative analysis |

> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - Operating Cycle must be **≥ 1 day** (cannot be zero or negative under normal operations).
> - If the OC formula yields a **negative value**, it indicates **negative working capital** (e.g., supermarkets, fast-food chains), which is a strategic advantage, not distress.
> - Annual figures are always divided by **360 or 365 days** (KTU convention: **use 360 days** unless stated otherwise).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Working Capital Analysis

- **Liquidity Management:** Ensures the firm can meet short-term obligations.
- **Profitability Trade-off:** Excess WC reduces return on investment; insufficient WC increases insolvency risk.
- **Credit Rating:** Rating agencies (CRISIL, ICRA) scrutinize WC ratios for corporate bond ratings.
- **M&A Valuations:** Working capital adjustments are critical in M&A deal structuring.
- **Biotech Specific Use:** Vaccine manufacturers, pharma R&D units, and clinical trial firms heavily depend on WC due to long gestation periods and regulated inventory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 4 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Operating Cycle Formula

The **Operating Cycle (OC)** is the time between the **acquisition of raw materials** and the **realization of cash from sales**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Four Sequential Stages

Every manufacturing firm follows this cycle:

$$\text{Raw Material} \rightarrow \text{Work-in-Process} \rightarrow \text{Finished Goods} \rightarrow \text{Receivables (Debtors)} \rightarrow \text{Cash}$$

### Step 2 — Convert Each Stage into a "Period" (in days)

Let us derive the Raw Material Storage Period:

Let the **Average Raw Material Inventory** be denoted as $I_{RM}$ and the **Annual Raw Material Consumed** be $C_{RM}$. Then:

$$R = \frac{I_{RM}}{C_{RM} / 365}$$

$$\boxed{R = \frac{I_{RM} \times 365}{C_{RM}}}$$

The same logic applies to the WIP Period ($W$), Finished Goods Period ($F$), and Debtors Period ($D$).

### Step 3 — Add All Four Periods

$$\text{OC} = R + W + F + D$$

### Step 4 — Subtract the Payables Deferral Period

If the firm enjoys supplier credit, the **effective cash conversion period** is reduced by the Creditors Period ($C$):

$$\boxed{\text{Net Operating Cycle (NOC)} = R + W + F + D - C}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Number of Operating Cycles per Year

$$\boxed{N = \frac{365}{\text{NOC}}}$$

### Step 6 — Final Working Capital Estimation

The working capital required is the **average daily operating cost** multiplied by the **net operating cycle length**:

$$\text{Average Daily Operating Cost} = \frac{\text{Total Annual Operating Cost}}{365}$$

$$\boxed{\text{WC Required} = \text{Average Daily Operating Cost} \times \text{NOC}}$$

Equivalently:

$$\text{WC Required} = \frac{\text{Total Annual Operating Cost}}{N}$$

> **Conversion Logic Summary:**
> - Each period = (Average Inventory of that stage) / (Daily flow through that stage) × 365
> - The **minus C** term reflects **trade credit leverage** — every day of supplier credit reduces our self-financing need.
> - $N$ represents **how many times per year** the working capital rotates.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

### Problem Statement (KTU-Style)

> A biotech firm has provided the following data for the year ended 31-03-2025:
>
> - Raw Material Inventory: ₹ 1,20,000
> - Work-in-Process Inventory: ₹ 80,000
> - Finished Goods Inventory: ₹ 1,50,000
> - Accounts Receivable: ₹ 2,00,000
> - Accounts Payable: ₹ 1,40,000
> - Annual Raw Material Consumed: ₹ 14,40,000
> - Annual Production Cost: ₹ 24,00,000
> - Annual Cost of Goods Sold (COGS): ₹ 30,00,000
> - Annual Credit Sales: ₹ 40,00,000
> - Annual Credit Purchases: ₹ 16,80,000
> - Total Annual Operating Cost: ₹ 32,00,000
>
> **Estimate the working capital requirement using the Operating Cycle Method.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute Raw Material Storage Period (R)**

$$R = \frac{\text{Avg. RM Inventory} \times 365}{\text{Annual RM Consumed}} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000 \times 365}{14{,}40{,}000}$$

$$R = \frac{4{,}38{,}00{,}000}{14{,}40{,}000} = 30.42 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2: Compute Work-in-Process Period (W)**

$$W = \frac{\text{Avg. WIP Inventory} \times 365}{\text{Annual Production Cost}} = \frac{80{,}000 \times 365}{24{,}00{,}000}$$

$$W = \frac{2{,}92{,}00{,}000}{24{,}00{,}000} = 12.17 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3: Compute Finished Goods Storage Period (F)**

$$F = \frac{\text{Avg. FG Inventory} \times 365}{\text{COGS}} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000 \times 365}{30{,}00{,}000}$$

$$F = \frac{5{,}47{,}50{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} = 18.25 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4: Compute Debtors Period (D)**

$$D = \frac{\text{Avg. Receivables} \times 365}{\text{Credit Sales}} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000 \times 365}{40{,}00{,}000}$$

$$D = \frac{7{,}30{,}00{,}000}{40{,}00{,}000} = 18.25 \text{ days}$$

**Step 5: Compute Creditors Period (C)**

$$C = \frac{\text{Avg. Payables} \times 365}{\text{Credit Purchases}} = \frac{1{,}40{,}000 \times 365}{16{,}80{,}000}$$

$$C = \frac{5{,}11{,}00{,}000}{16{,}80{,}000} = 30.42 \text{ days}$$

**Step 6: Compute Net Operating Cycle (NOC)**

$$\text{NOC} = R + W + F + D - C$$

$$\text{NOC} = 30.42 + 12.17 + 18.25 + 18.25 - 30.42$$

$$\text{NOC} = 48.67 \text{ days}$$

**Step 7: Compute Number of Operating Cycles per Year**

$$N = \frac{365}{48.67} \approx 7.5 \text{ cycles per year}$$

**Step 8: Compute Working Capital Requirement**

$$\text{WC Required} = \frac{\text{Total Annual Operating Cost}}{N} = \frac{32{,}00{,}000}{7.5}$$

$$\boxed{\text{WC Required} \approx \text{₹ } 4{,}26{,}667}$$

### Interpretation
- The firm's money is **locked up for approximately 49 days** in the operating cycle.
- It rotates the working capital **7.5 times per year**.
- Each cycle consumes about **₹ 4.27 lakhs** of working capital.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Working Capital Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[WORKING CAPITAL OVERVIEW] --> B[Meaning and Definition]
    A --> C[Types of Working Capital]
    A --> D[Importance and Need]
    A --> E[Factors Affecting WC]
    A --> F[Estimation Methods]

    B --> B1[Gross Working Capital - Total CA]
    B --> B2[Net Working Capital - CA minus CL]

    C --> C1[Permanent Working Capital]
    C --> C2[Variable Working Capital]
    C --> C3[Seasonal Working Capital]
    C --> C4[Special Working Capital]

    E --> E1[Nature of Business]
    E --> E2[Length of Operating Cycle]
    E --> E3[Credit Policy]
    E --> E4[Sales Growth Rate]
    E --> E5[Price Level Changes]
    E --> E6[Supplier Credit Terms]

    F --> F1[Operating Cycle Method]
    F --> F2[Percentage of Sales Method]
    F --> F3[Regression Analysis Method]
    F --> F4[Projected Balance Sheet Method]

    C1 --> G[Financed by Long Term Sources]
    C2 --> H[Financed by Short Term Sources]
```

## 4.2 Operating Cycle Flow — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Raw Material Purchase] --> P2[RM Storage Period R]
    P2 --> P3[Work in Process WIP Period W]
    P3 --> P4[Finished Goods Storage Period F]
    P4 --> P5[Credit Sales to Debtors Period D]
    P5 --> P6[Cash Realization]

    P6 --> P7[Net Operating Cycle = R plus W plus F plus D minus C]

    S1[Supplier Credit Payables Period C] --> P2

    P7 --> P8[Number of Cycles N = 365 divided by NOC]
    P8 --> P9[Working Capital Required = Annual Op Cost divided by N]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Flow: Shortage vs Excess Working Capital

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Working Capital Level] --> B[Insufficient WC]
    A --> C[Optimal WC]
    A --> D[Excess WC]

    B --> B1[Liquidity Crisis]

    B --> B3[Forced Asset Sales]
    B --> B4[Insolvency Risk]

    C --> C1[Smooth Operations]
    C --> C2[Profitable Liquidity]
    C --> C3[Good Credit Rating]
    C --> C4[Healthy ROE]

    D --> D1[Idle Cash Holdings]
    D --> D2[Excess Inventory]
    D --> D3[Lower ROA]
    D --> D4[Opportunity Cost]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 4 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## PART A (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Gross Working Capital and Net Working Capital. Which is more useful for short-term financial decision-making?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Basis** | **Gross Working Capital** | **Net Working Capital** |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Definition | Total investment in current assets | Current Assets minus Current Liabilities |
| Focus | Quantum of current assets | Liquidity position of the firm |
| Usage | Long-term financing decisions | Short-term financial decisions |

**Conclusion:** *Net Working Capital is more useful for short-term financial decision-making* because it indicates the firm's **liquidity buffer** and ability to meet short-term obligations, whereas GWC is a broader measure of operational investment.

> **Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Definition of GWC (1), Definition of NWC (1), Conclusion with reasoning (1).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**List any six factors affecting the working capital requirement of a firm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — write any 6):**

1. **Nature and size of the business** — manufacturing firms require more WC than trading or service firms.
2. **Length of the operating cycle** — longer cycles increase WC needs.
3. **Sales volume and growth** — higher sales demand higher WC.
4. **Credit policy** — liberal credit terms increase receivables and WC.
5. **Inventory turnover** — slow-moving inventory increases WC.
6. **Supplier credit terms** — longer payable periods reduce WC.
7. **Price level changes** — inflation increases WC in rupee terms.
8. **Seasonality** — seasonal businesses have higher variable WC.

> **Mark Distribution:** 0.5 marks per factor × 6 factors = 3 marks.

---

## PART B (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Rule:** *Answer any ONE full question from this module.* Each question carries **14 marks** with two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain the different types of working capital with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Working capital can be classified into the following four types:

**1. Permanent Working Capital (PWC) — 2 marks**

This is the **minimum level of current assets** that a firm must maintain at all times to sustain its operations. It is a permanent feature of the balance sheet and grows gradually with the size of the business.

*Example:* A pharmaceutical company must always maintain a minimum stock of active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) and basic lab consumables, irrespective of seasonal demand.

**2. Variable (Fluctuating) Working Capital — 2 marks**

This refers to the **additional current assets** required over and above the permanent level, depending on the volume of business activity. It rises during peak periods and falls during lean periods.

*Example:* An ice-cream manufacturer requires additional refrigeration capacity and raw milk inventory during summer months.

**3. Seasonal Working Capital — 1.5 marks**

A specific category of variable working capital that is required during **particular seasons** of the year.

*Example:* A woollen garment manufacturer needs extra cash and inventory before the winter season.

**4. Special Working Capital — 1.5 marks**

Working capital required for **non-recurring, special operations** such as a major product launch, a one-time large order, or participation in an international trade fair.

*Example:* A biotech startup raising capital for a one-time clinical trial enrolment drive.

---

**(b) A manufacturing company provides the following data for the year ending 31st March 2025: (7 marks)**

| **Item** | **Amount (₹)** |
|----------|----------------|
| Average Raw Material Inventory | 1,50,000 |
| Average Work-in-Process Inventory | 1,00,000 |
| Average Finished Goods Inventory | 2,00,000 |
| Average Accounts Receivable | 3,00,000 |
| Average Accounts Payable | 1,80,000 |
| Annual Raw Material Consumed | 18,00,000 |
| Annual Production Cost | 30,00,000 |
| Annual Cost of Goods Sold | 36,00,000 |
| Annual Credit Sales | 48,00,000 |
| Annual Credit Purchases | 21,60,000 |
| Total Annual Operating Cost | 40,00,000 |

**Compute the working capital required using the Operating Cycle Method. (Take 360 days/year.)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Raw Material Storage Period (R)**

$$R = \frac{1{,}50{,}000 \times 360}{18{,}00{,}000} = 30 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2: Work-in-Process Period (W)**

$$W = \frac{1{,}00{,}000 \times 360}{30{,}00{,}000} = 12 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3: Finished Goods Storage Period (F)**

$$F = \frac{2{,}00{,}000 \times 360}{36{,}00{,}000} = 20 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4: Debtors Period (D)**

$$D = \frac{3{,}00{,}000 \times 360}{48{,}00{,}000} = 22.5 \text{ days}$$

**Step 5: Creditors Period (C)**

$$C = \frac{1{,}80{,}000 \times 360}{21{,}60{,}000} = 30 \text{ days}$$

**Step 6: Net Operating Cycle (NOC)**

$$\text{NOC} = R + W + F + D - C = 30 + 12 + 20 + 22.5 - 30 = 54.5 \text{ days}$$

**Step 7: Number of Operating Cycles per Year (N)**

$$N = \frac{360}{54.5} \approx 6.61 \text{ cycles per year}$$

**Step 8: Estimated Working Capital**

$$\text{WC Required} = \frac{\text{Annual Operating Cost}}{N} = \frac{40{,}00{,}000}{6.61}$$

$$\boxed{\text{WC Required} \approx \text{₹ } 6{,}05{,}143}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) Discuss the concept of Operating Cycle. Explain its significance in working capital management with a neat block diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Operating Cycle — 3 marks**

The **Operating Cycle (OC)** refers to the **time period** between the **acquisition of raw materials** and the **final realization of cash** from the sale of finished goods. It represents the total time a unit of money remains tied up in the production and sales process.

Mathematically:

$$\text{Net Operating Cycle (NOC)} = R + W + F + D - C$$

Where:
- $R$ = Raw Material Storage Period
- $W$ = Work-in-Process Holding Period
- $F$ = Finished Goods Storage Period
- $D$ = Debtors (Receivables) Period
- $C$ = Creditors (Payables) Period

**Significance in Working Capital Management — 4 marks**

1. **Determines the Quantum of WC:** The longer the OC, the higher the WC requirement. Firms with long cycles (e.g., shipbuilding) need substantial WC, while supermarkets with short cycles need less.

2. **Identifies Bottlenecks:** A high WIP period suggests production inefficiencies; a high debtor period suggests poor collection policy.

3. **Helps in Financing Decisions:** Short-term financing is suitable for short OCs, while long-term financing is preferred for long OCs.

4. **Facilitates Benchmarking:** Firms can compare their OC with industry peers to assess operational efficiency.

5. **Aids in Cash Budgeting:** The OC length directly feeds into projected cash flow statements.

**Neat Block Diagram — included in your exam paper:**

$$\text{Raw Material} \xrightarrow{R} \text{WIP} \xrightarrow{W} \text{Finished Goods} \xrightarrow{F} \text{Debtors} \xrightarrow{D} \text{Cash}$$
$$\text{Creditors} \xleftarrow{C} \text{Raw Material}$$

---

**(b) From the following data, estimate the working capital requirement: (7 marks)**

| **Item** | **Estimated Annual Amount (₹)** |
|----------|----------------------------------|
| Raw Material Consumed | 16,00,000 |
| Direct Labour | 4,00,000 |
| Overheads (excluding depreciation) | 6,00,000 |
| Total Annual Production Cost | 26,00,000 |
| Total Annual Cost of Sales | 30,00,000 |
| Sales | 40,00,000 |
| Average RM Stock (30 days consumption) | — |
| Average WIP Stock (15 days of production cost) | — |
| Average FG Stock (20 days of cost of sales) | — |
| Average Debtors (45 days of sales) | — |
| Average Creditors (30 days of purchases) | — |

**Additional Info:** Raw Material purchases equal 110% of RM consumed.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate Each Inventory Value (3 marks)**

- Raw Material Stock = $16{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{30}{360} = 1{,}33{,}333$
- WIP Stock = $26{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{15}{360} = 1{,}08{,}333$
- FG Stock = $30{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{20}{360} = 1{,}66{,}667$
- Debtors = $40{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{45}{360} = 5{,}00{,}000$
- Creditors = $[16{,}00{,}000 \times 1.10] \times \dfrac{30}{360} = 17{,}60{,}000 \times \dfrac{30}{360} = 1{,}46{,}667$

**Step 2: Estimate Current Assets and Current Liabilities (2 marks)**

| **Current Assets** | **Amount (₹)** |
|---------------------|----------------|
| Raw Material Stock | 1,33,333 |
| Work-in-Process | 1,08,333 |
| Finished Goods | 1,66,667 |
| Debtors | 5,00,000 |
| **Total CA** | **9,08,333** |

| **Current Liabilities** | **Amount (₹)** |
|--------------------------|----------------|
| Creditors | 1,46,667 |
| **Total CL** | **1,46,667** |

**Step 3: Net Working Capital (2 marks)**

$$\text{NWC} = \text{CA} - \text{CL} = 9{,}08{,}333 - 1{,}46{,}667$$

$$\boxed{\text{NWC} = \text{₹ } 7{,}61{,}666}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT use 365 days blindly** — KTU convention favors **360 days** for OC problems unless the question explicitly says 365.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse "Production Cost" with "Cost of Goods Sold"** — WIP uses the former, FG uses the latter.
> 3. **Do NOT forget to convert creditor period** using **credit purchases**, not total purchases (if total purchases include cash purchases).
> 4. **Do NOT skip the sign of Creditors** — it is **subtracted** (reduces WC) in the NOC formula.
> 5. **Presentation marks (1 mark)** are awarded for clean tables, formula writing, and boxed final answers — **don't ignore them**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Working Capital** = Capital invested in **current assets** (Gross) OR **CA − CL** (Net).
- **Net Working Capital** is the **liquidity measure**; **Gross WC** is the **operational investment measure**.
- **Permanent WC** is financed by **long-term sources**; **Variable WC** is financed by **short-term sources**.
- **Operating Cycle Formula:** $\text{NOC} = R + W + F + D - C$ (all in days).
- **Number of cycles per year** $N = 360 / \text{NOC}$ (KTU uses 360).
- **Final WC required** = Annual Operating Cost / N.
- **Six Critical Factors:** Nature of business, OC length, credit policy, sales growth, inventory turnover, supplier credit.
- **Negative NOC** is possible (supermarkets, e-commerce) — denotes **operational efficiency**, not distress.
- **KTU convention:** 360 days/year for all OC calculations.
- **Most-tested sub-topics:** (i) Operating Cycle numerical, (ii) Types of WC with examples, (iii) Factors affecting WC.
- **Always present the final answer in a BOX** for easy valuation marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working Capital Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Working Capital Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Working Capital** in the context of the KTU 2024 Financial Management syllabus refers to the **firm's investment in short-term assets** — cash, marketable securities, accounts receivable, and inventories — that are required to support the routine operating cycle of a business. The discipline addresses the planning, financing, and control of this short-term capital base.

The two principal constructs evaluated throughout this module are:

- **Gross Working Capital (GWC):** The aggregate dollar value of all current assets employed in operations.
$$\text{GWC} = \text{Current Assets (CA)} = \text{Cash + Marketable Securities + Receivables + Inventory + Prepaid Expenses}$$

- **Net Working Capital (NWC):** The difference between total current assets and total current liabilities, representing the firm's **liquid reserve cushion**.
$$\text{NWC} = \text{CA} - \text{CL}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** When the question says "Working Capital" without a qualifier, the *default* in the 2024 Scheme valuation is **Net Working Capital (CA – CL)**. Always read the question stem carefully.

### 1.2 Working Capital Issues — Module Scope

"Working Capital Issues" is a problem-solving framework that investigates **four intertwined decisions** a finance manager must address:

1. The **Working Capital Policy Decision** (Optimal level — aggressive vs. conservative).
2. The **Financing Decision** (Permanent vs. temporary funding mix).
3. The **Cash Management Decision** (Baumol, Miller-Orr, Operating Cycle models).
4. The **Receivables & Inventory Decision** (Credit policy, EOQ, ABC analysis).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pipeline" Model

Imagine a factory as a **water pipeline system**:

- **Inventory** is the water *stored in overhead tanks*.
- **Receivables** are the water *released to customers but not yet collected*.
- **Cash** is the water *actually in the municipal pipeline (your bank account)*.
- **Payables** are the water *you have taken from upstream suppliers but not yet returned*.

**Working Capital Issues** = the engineering challenge of *keeping the pipeline full enough to never halt production*, yet *not so full that capital is wasted standing idle*. The finance manager is essentially the *plant hydraulic engineer* balancing flow velocity, storage tanks, and reservoir levels.

### 1.4 Key Quantitative Benchmarks (Industry Standards)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Ratios used in KTU problems:**
> - **Current Ratio (Ideal) = 2 : 1**
> - **Quick / Acid-Test Ratio (Ideal) = 1 : 1**
> - **Cash Ratio (Conservative) = 0.2 : 1**
> - **Defensive Interval (Days) = (CA – Inventory) / Daily Operating Expenses**
> - **Operating Cycle (Days)** benchmarked against **industry median**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operating Cycle Framework (The Heart of the Module)

Every working capital problem is, at its root, an **Operating Cycle problem**. The operating cycle (OC) is the *time elapsed between the acquisition of raw materials and the final collection of cash from sales*.

$$\text{Operating Cycle (OC)} = \text{RMI} + \text{WIP} + \text{FGI} + \text{ACP} - \text{ACP}_{\text{payables}}$$

Where each sub-component is the **Average Age** (in days):

| Symbol | Component | Definition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| RMI | Raw Material Inventory Period | Days raw materials sit in store before issue to production |
| WIP | Work-in-Progress Period | Days semi-finished goods remain on the shop floor |
| FGI | Finished Goods Inventory Period | Days finished goods sit in warehouse awaiting sale |
| ACP | Average Collection Period (Receivables) | Days taken to collect cash from credit customers |
| ACPₚ | Average Payment Period (Payables) | Days taken to pay credit suppliers |

**Net Operating Cycle (NOC)** — sometimes called the **Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC)** — is the *net days of external financing* the firm must arrange:

$$\text{NOC} = \text{OC} - \text{ACP}_{\text{payables}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A *negative* NOC is theoretically possible (e.g., Amazon, Apple) — the firm collects from customers *before* it must pay suppliers. This is the **"Float Advantage."**

### 2.2 The Four Pillars of Working Capital Issues

#### Pillar I — Adequacy vs. Optimality Decision

- **Adequacy:** Does the firm have *enough* working capital to meet obligations?
- **Optimality:** Is the level *cost-minimizing* given the profitability–liquidity trade-off?

#### Pillar II — Financing Policy (Aggressive vs. Conservative vs. Matching)

| Strategy | Permanent WC funded by | Temporary WC funded by | Risk Profile | Profitability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Aggressive** | Short-term | Short-term | High | High (low cost) |
| **Conservative** | Long-term | Long-term | Low | Low (high cost) |
| **Matching (Hedging)** | Long-term | Short-term | Moderate | Moderate |

#### Pillar III — Cash Management Models

- **Baumol Model (Deterministic):** Treats cash like inventory. Assumes steady, predictable outflows.
- **Miller-Orr Model (Stochastic):** Sets an upper and lower control limit; cash fluctuates randomly.
- **Operating Cycle Method:** Cash needed = (Annual Operating Cost / 365) × Operating Cycle.

#### Pillar IV — Receivables & Inventory Control

- **Credit Scoring** for customer selection.
- **EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)** for inventory.
- **ABC Analysis** for inventory prioritization.
- **Safety Stock** = Z × σ × √Lead Time.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table. It covers ~85% of KTU 2024 numerical questions on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Gross Working Capital | $\sum \text{Current Assets}$ | Currency units |
| 2 | Net Working Capital | $CA - CL$ | Currency units |
| 3 | Current Ratio | $CA / CL$ | Ideal = 2.0 |
| 4 | Quick Ratio | $(CA - \text{Inventory}) / CL$ | Ideal = 1.0 |
| 5 | Cash Ratio | $(\text{Cash + Marketable Sec.}) / CL$ | Ideal = 0.2 |
| 6 | Defensive Interval (Days) | $(CA - \text{Inv.}) / (\text{Annual Op. Cost}/365)$ | Days |
| 7 | Operating Cycle | $RMI + WIP + FGI + ACP$ | Days |
| 8 | Net Operating Cycle | $OC - APP$ | Days |
| 9 | Cash Required (OC Method) | $(\text{Annual Op. Cost}/365) \times OC$ | Currency |
| 10 | WC Turnover Ratio | $\text{Net Sales} / \text{Avg. NWC}$ | Times |
| 11 | Baumol – Optimal Cash | $C^{*} = \sqrt{(2bT)/i}$ | Where b = fixed cost, T = annual cash need, i = opportunity rate |
| 12 | Miller-Orr – Upper Limit | $U = L + \sqrt[3]{(3b\sigma^{2})/(4i)}$ | σ² = daily net cash flow variance |
| 13 | Miller-Orr – Return Point | $R = L + (1/3)(U - L)$ | Midpoint reset |
| 14 | EOQ | $\sqrt{(2AO)/C}$ | A=Annual demand, O=Order cost, C=Carrying cost/unit |
| 15 | Safety Stock | $Z \times \sigma_{d} \times \sqrt{L}$ | Z = service level factor |
| 16 | Average Inventory | $(Q/2) + \text{Safety Stock}$ | Units |
| 17 | Permanent WC | Assets needed even at *minimum* sales | Floor of WC curve |
| 18 | Temporary WC | Excess above permanent (= seasonal) | Oscillates with sales |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

Working Capital management is the *operational heartbeat* of every enterprise:

- **Manufacturing Firms:** High inventory → long OC → heavy WC demand.
- **Retail Chains (e.g., DMart):** Short OC, high inventory turnover.
- **Service Firms (e.g., TCS):** Minimal inventory → low WC.
- **E-Commerce Giants (e.g., Amazon):** Negative CCC → suppliers finance operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Real-World Hook:** Examiners increasingly frame numerical problems around *Indian FMCG, Pharma, or Auto-component companies* (e.g., Maruti, Asian Paints, Sun Pharma) where working capital cycles differ markedly by industry.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Operating Cycle & Cash Requirement (Full Algebraic Path)

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-mark):**
> *A firm has the following data:*
> *- Raw Material Consumption = ₹ 800 lakh; Avg. RM Stock = ₹ 100 lakh*
> *- WIP Cost = ₹ 600 lakh; Avg. WIP = ₹ 75 lakh*
> *- Cost of Goods Sold = ₹ 1,200 lakh; Avg. Finished Goods = ₹ 150 lakh*
> *- Credit Sales = ₹ 1,800 lakh; Avg. Receivables = ₹ 300 lakh*
> *- Credit Purchases = ₹ 1,000 lakh; Avg. Payables = ₹ 200 lakh*
> *- Annual Operating Cost = ₹ 1,440 lakh*
>
> *Compute: (a) Operating Cycle, (b) Net Operating Cycle, (c) Working Capital required using OC method.*

**Step 1 — Compute Raw Material Inventory Period (RMI):**

$$\text{RMI} = \frac{\text{Avg. Raw Material Stock}}{\text{Raw Material Consumed}} \times 365$$

$$\text{RMI} = \frac{100}{800} \times 365 = 45.625 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Work-in-Progress Period (WIP):**

$$\text{WIP Period} = \frac{\text{Avg. WIP}}{\text{Production Cost (WIP Cost)}} \times 365$$

$$\text{WIP} = \frac{75}{600} \times 365 = 45.625 \text{ days}$$

> **Logic:** WIP cost = conversion + material cost accumulated in production. We use the WIP cost figure given (₹ 600 lakh) as the denominator base.

**Step 3 — Compute Finished Goods Inventory Period (FGI):**

$$\text{FGI} = \frac{\text{Avg. Finished Goods}}{\text{Cost of Goods Sold}} \times 365$$

$$\text{FGI} = \frac{150}{1200} \times 365 = 45.625 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Average Collection Period (ACP):**

$$\text{ACP} = \frac{\text{Avg. Receivables}}{\text{Credit Sales}} \times 365$$

$$\text{ACP} = \frac{300}{1800} \times 365 = 60.833 \text{ days}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Average Payment Period (APP):**

$$\text{APP} = \frac{\text{Avg. Payables}}{\text{Credit Purchases}} \times 365$$

$$\text{APP} = \frac{200}{1000} \times 365 = 73.0 \text{ days}$$

**Step 6 — Aggregate the Operating Cycle:**

$$\text{OC} = \text{RMI} + \text{WIP} + \text{FGI} + \text{ACP}$$

$$\text{OC} = 45.625 + 45.625 + 45.625 + 60.833$$

$$\text{OC} = 197.708 \text{ days}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Net Operating Cycle:**

$$\text{NOC} = \text{OC} - \text{APP}$$

$$\text{NOC} = 197.708 - 73.0 = 124.708 \text{ days}$$

**Step 8 — Compute Working Capital Required (Operating Cycle Method):**

$$\text{WC Required} = \frac{\text{Annual Operating Cost}}{365} \times \text{OC}$$

$$\text{WC Required} = \frac{1440}{365} \times 197.708$$

$$\text{WC Required} = 3.9452 \times 197.708$$

$$\boxed{\text{WC Required} \approx 779.92 \text{ lakh}}$$

> **Interpretation:** The firm needs approximately **₹ 7.80 crore** in working capital to sustain one full operating cycle.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Baumol Model — Optimal Cash Balance

**Model Assumptions:**
- Cash outflows are *uniform and predictable* (no stochastic shocks).
- All excess cash is invested in marketable securities.
- Opportunity cost is linear; transaction cost is fixed.

**Step 1 — Define Total Cost Function:**

$$TC = \frac{iC}{2} + \frac{bT}{C}$$

Where:
- $C$ = Cash balance drawn per transaction (decision variable).
- $T$ = Total annual cash need.
- $b$ = Fixed cost per transaction (brokerage / fee).
- $i$ = Opportunity cost rate (% per rupee per year).

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. C and set to zero (first-order condition):**

$$\frac{dTC}{dC} = \frac{i}{2} - \frac{bT}{C^{2}} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for C\* (Optimum):**

$$\frac{bT}{C^{2}} = \frac{i}{2}$$

$$C^{2} = \frac{2bT}{i}$$

$$\boxed{C^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2bT}{i}}}$$

**Step 4 — Derive Optimal Number of Transactions:**

$$N^{*} = \frac{T}{C^{*}} = \sqrt{\frac{iT}{2b}}$$

**Step 5 — Derive Average Cash Balance:**

$$\text{Avg. Cash} = \frac{C^{*}}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2bT}{i}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Miller-Orr Model (Stochastic Cash Management)

**Setup:** Cash balance $X_t$ behaves as a *random walk* with daily variance $\sigma^{2}$.

**Step 1 — Define Control Limits:**

- **Lower Limit ($L$):** Manager-specified floor (often zero, or a safety cushion).
- **Upper Limit ($U$):** Trigger for buying securities.
- **Return Point ($R$):** Target balance after each transaction.

**Step 2 — Optimal Upper Limit Formula (empirically derived):**

$$U = L + \sqrt[3]{\frac{3b\sigma^{2}}{4i}}$$

**Step 3 — Optimal Return Point:**

$$R = L + \frac{1}{3}(U - L)$$

> **Logic (Why $1/3$?):** The Miller-Orr model minimizes the joint cost of transaction fees (which occur when balance hits $U$ or $L$) and opportunity cost (which is linear in average balance $E[X]$). The integration of the expected cash path over the [L, U] band yields the $1/3$ factor.

**Numerical Illustration:**

A firm has $\sigma = ₹ 200$/day, $b = ₹ 50$ per transaction, $i = 12\%$ p.a. = $0.000329$/day, and sets $L = 0$.

$$U = 0 + \sqrt[3]{\frac{3 \times 50 \times 200^{2}}{4 \times 0.000329}}$$

$$U = \sqrt[3]{\frac{6{,}000{,}000}{0.001316}} = \sqrt[3]{4.56 \times 10^{9}}$$

$$U \approx 1{,}654.6$$

$$R = 0 + \frac{1}{3}(1654.6) \approx 551.5$$

**Decision Rules:**
- If cash rises to **U = 1,654.6** → Buy securities worth $(U - R) = 1{,}103.1$.
- If cash falls to **L = 0** → Sell securities worth $R = 551.5$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Financing Strategy Trade-Off

| Criterion | Aggressive | Conservative | Matching (Hedging) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Permanent WC → Long-term | No | Yes | Yes |
| Permanent WC → Short-term | Yes | No | No |
| Temporary WC → Long-term | No | Yes | No |
| Temporary WC → Short-term | Yes | No | Yes |
| Liquidity Risk | High | Low | Moderate |
| Cost of Capital | Lowest | Highest | Moderate |
| Profitability (ROE) | Highest | Lowest | Moderate |
| Vulnerability to Interest Shocks | High | Low | Moderate |
| Best for… | Strong cash flows | Volatile markets | Stable, predictable sales |

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4: EOQ & Safety Stock (Inventory Module Linkage)

**Step 1 — Total Inventory Cost Function:**

$$TC(Q) = \frac{Q}{2} \times C_{c} + \frac{A}{Q} \times C_{o}$$

Where:
- $C_c$ = Carrying cost per unit per year.
- $C_o$ = Order cost per order.
- $A$ = Annual demand.
- $Q$ = Order quantity.

**Step 2 — Differentiate and Equate to Zero:**

$$\frac{dTC}{dQ} = \frac{C_{c}}{2} - \frac{A C_{o}}{Q^{2}} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve:**

$$\boxed{EOQ = Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2AC_{o}}{C_{c}}}}$$

**Step 4 — Safety Stock (with demand uncertainty):**

$$SS = Z_{\alpha} \times \sigma_{d} \times \sqrt{L}$$

For a **95% service level** (Z = 1.645), daily demand std. dev. = 20 units, lead time = 9 days:

$$SS = 1.645 \times 20 \times \sqrt{9} = 1.645 \times 20 \times 3 = 98.7 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5 — Reorder Point:**

$$ROP = (\text{Avg. Daily Demand} \times L) + SS$$
