## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Distinguish between Gross Working Capital and Net Working Capital. Which is more appropriate for measuring liquidity?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Gross Working Capital (GWC)** refers to the *aggregate* investment in all current assets — cash, marketable securities, receivables, inventory, and prepaid expenses. It is a **quantitative** concept indicating the total short-term resources deployed.

- **Net Working Capital (NWC)** is the *qualitative* difference between current assets and current liabilities. It represents the long-term funds used to finance the *permanent* portion of current assets.

$$\text{NWC} = CA - CL$$

- For measuring **liquidity**, **NWC is more appropriate** because it reveals the *surplus cushion* available to meet short-term obligations. A firm may have huge gross working capital (₹ 100 Cr) but if current liabilities are ₹ 95 Cr, liquidity is precarious (NWC = ₹ 5 Cr only).

> **Conclusion:** GWC measures *operational scale*; NWC measures *financial health*. **[3 marks]**

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is the Operating Cycle? Why is the Net Operating Cycle a more accurate measure of a firm's working capital need?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Operating Cycle (OC)** is the total time between the acquisition of raw materials and the final realization of cash from sales of finished goods.

$$\text{OC} = \text{RMI} + \text{WIP} + \text{FGI} + \text{ACP}$$

The **Net Operating Cycle (NOC)** adjusts OC by subtracting the **Average Payment Period (APP)** — the average time taken by the firm to pay its suppliers.

$$\text{NOC} = \text{OC} - \text{APP}$$

**Why NOC is more accurate:**
- OC ignores the fact that **suppliers extend credit** (trade payable financing).
- NOC captures the *net* duration for which the firm must arrange external financing.
- A firm with APP > OC has a **negative NOC** — it is effectively financed by its suppliers (e.g., Amazon, Walmart).

> **Conclusion:** NOC represents the *true funding gap* that working capital policies must address. **[3 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

A manufacturing company provides the following information for the year ending 31-Mar-2024:

| Item | Amount (₹ in lakh) |
| :--- | :---: |
| Raw Material Consumed | 1,200 |
| Average Raw Material Stock | 150 |
| Conversion Cost (WIP base) | 800 |
| Average WIP Stock | 100 |
| Cost of Goods Sold | 2,000 |
| Average Finished Goods Stock | 200 |
| Total Sales | 2,500 |
| Credit Sales (80% of total) | 2,000 |
| Average Receivables | 400 |
| Credit Purchases | 1,500 |
| Average Payables | 250 |
| Annual Operating Cost | 2,400 |

**Required:**
**(a)** Compute the Operating Cycle and the Net Operating Cycle. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Estimate the amount of working capital required using the Operating Cycle Method, and comment on whether the firm should adopt an aggressive or conservative financing policy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

##### Part (a) — Operating Cycle & NOC

**Step 1: Raw Material Inventory Period (RMI):**

$$\text{RMI} = \frac{150}{1200} \times 365 = 45.625 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2: WIP Period:**

$$\text{WIP} = \frac{100}{800} \times 365 = 45.625 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3: Finished Goods Inventory Period (FGI):**

$$\text{FGI} = \frac{200}{2000} \times 365 = 36.5 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4: Average Collection Period (ACP):**

$$\text{ACP} = \frac{400}{2000} \times 365 = 73.0 \text{ days}$$

**Step 5: Operating Cycle:**

$$\text{OC} = 45.625 + 45.625 + 36.5 + 73.0 = 200.75 \text{ days}$$

**Step 6: Average Payment Period (APP):**

$$\text{APP} = \frac{250}{1500} \times 365 = 60.83 \text{ days}$$

**Step 7: Net Operating Cycle:**

$$\text{NOC} = 200.75 - 60.83 = 139.92 \text{ days}$$

> **Conclusion (a):** The firm has a long operating cycle of **200.75 days** and requires external financing for **~140 days**.

##### Part (b) — Working Capital Required & Policy Recommendation

**Step 1: Cash Required (OC Method):**

$$\text{WC Required} = \frac{2400}{365} \times 200.75$$

$$\text{WC Required} = 6.575 \times 200.75 \approx 1{,}319.96 \text{ lakh} \approx ₹ 13.20 \text{ Cr}$$

**Step 2: Policy Decision Logic:**

| Parameter | Value | Interpretation |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| OC | 200.75 days | Long cycle — high WC intensity |
| NOC | 139.92 days | High external funding need |
| WC : Sales | 13.20 / 25.00 = **52.8%** | Heavy WC burden |

**Step 3: Recommendation — Conservative Financing Policy**

Given the **long operating cycle**, the firm faces substantial and continuous working capital demand. A *conservative* policy is recommended because:

1. **Risk Mitigation:** A 200-day cycle is highly vulnerable to demand shocks; long-term funds provide stability.
2. **Lower Refinancing Risk:** Short-term debt rollover on a 140-day NOC could create liquidity crunches.
3. **Supplier Credit Limitations:** Although APP = 60.83 days, this is insufficient to offset the 200-day cycle.

> **Conclusion (b):** Required WC ≈ **₹ 1,320 lakh (₹ 13.20 Cr)**. The firm should adopt a **Conservative financing policy** (long-term funds for both permanent and a portion of temporary WC). **[7 marks]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

A firm's annual cash requirement for operations is **₹ 12 lakh**, evenly spread across the year. The fixed cost per marketable securities transaction is **₹ 100**, and the opportunity cost of holding cash is **14% per annum**.

**Required:**
**(a)** Using the **Baumol Model**, compute the optimal cash balance, the average cash balance, and the number of transactions per year. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Critically evaluate the assumptions of the Baumol model. Under what real-world conditions does it **fail**, and which alternative model should be used? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

##### Part (a) — Baumol Optimal Cash Computation

**Given:**
- $T = ₹ 12{,}00{,}000$ (annual cash need)
- $b = ₹ 100$ (per transaction)
- $i = 14\% = 0.14$

**Step 1: Optimal Cash Withdrawal ($C^*$):**

$$C^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2bT}{i}}$$

$$C^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 100 \times 12{,}00{,}000}{0.14}}$$

$$C^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{24{,}00{,}00{,}000}{0.14}} = \sqrt{1.7143 \times 10^{10}}$$

$$C^{*} \approx ₹ 1{,}30{,}930 \approx ₹ 1.31 \text{ lakh}$$

**Step 2: Average Cash Balance:**

$$\text{Avg. Cash} = \frac{C^{*}}{2} = ₹ 65{,}465$$

**Step 3: Number of Transactions per Year:**

$$N^{*} = \frac{T}{C^{*}} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{1{,}30{,}930} \approx 9.17 \text{ transactions/year}$$

> **Conclusion (a):** The firm should withdraw **₹ 1,30,930** approximately **9 times a year** (every ~40 days), holding an average cash balance of **₹ 65,465**.

##### Part (b) — Critical Evaluation of Baumol Assumptions

**Assumptions of Baumol Model:**

1. Cash needs are **known with certainty** (deterministic).
2. Cash outflows are **uniform** (constant rate).
3. The firm **never runs out of cash** between transactions.
4. Transaction costs are **fixed** regardless of amount.
5. Opportunity cost is **linear** and constant.

**Where the Model Fails:**

| Real-world Condition | Why Baumol Fails |
| :--- | :--- |
| Stochastic daily cash flows | Model assumes a deterministic sawtooth |
| Seasonal / cyclical sales | Uniformity assumption breaks down |
| Large lumpy payments (e.g., tax, dividend) | Continuous drawdown is unrealistic |
| Variable transaction costs (bulk discount) | Constant $b$ assumption fails |
| Cash inflows also present | Model is single-direction (outflow only) |

**Recommended Alternative: Miller-Orr Model**

The **Miller-Orr Model** overcomes these limitations by:

- Modeling cash balance as a **random walk** with variance $\sigma^2$.
- Establishing **upper and lower control limits** that trigger buy/sell decisions.
- Allowing for **asymmetric stochastic inflows and outflows**.

> **Conclusion (b):** Baumol is a pedagogically clean closed-form solution but is operationally fragile. KTU expects students to recognize that **Miller-Orr is the industry-preferred stochastic extension**. **[7 marks]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps (Avoid These!):**
> 1. **Confusing APP denominator:** Use *Credit Purchases* for APP, NOT Total Purchases. Examiners deduct 1 mark for using the wrong base.
> 2. **WIP denominator:** Always use the *production cost* figure given in the problem for WIP, not RM cost.
> 3. **Baumol's $i$:** Ensure you use the *decimal* form (0.14, not 14). A common computational slip.
> 4. **NOC sign convention:** A *negative* NOC is **legitimate** and shows supplier financing — do not "correct" it to positive.
> 5. **"Working Capital" default:** If a problem says "working capital" without qualifier and asks for a monetary figure, KTU expects **Net Working Capital (CA – CL)**, not GWC.
> 6. **EOQ unit mismatch:** $A$ is in *units per year*, $C_c$ is in *₹ per unit per year*. Don't mix tons with kilograms.
> 7. **Miller-Orr variance:** $\sigma^2$ is the *variance* of *daily* net cash flows. Forgetting to square $\sigma$ is a recurrent error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Working Capital Issues**

- **GWC vs. NWC:** GWC = total CA; NWC = CA – CL. NWC is the *liquidity cushion*.
- **Operating Cycle (OC):** RMI + WIP + FGI + ACP (all in days).
- **Net Operating Cycle (NOC):** OC – APP. Negative NOC = supplier-financed operations.
- **Operating Cycle Method of WC:** WC = (Annual Op. Cost / 365) × OC.
- **Permanent vs. Temporary WC:** Permanent = floor (long-term funded); Temporary = seasonal swing (short-term funded).
- **Aggressive Policy:** High short-term usage → high ROE, high liquidity risk.
- **Conservative Policy:** High long-term usage → low ROE, low risk.
- **Matching (Hedging) Policy:** Permanent → long-term; Temporary → short-term.
- **Baumol Model:** $C^* = \sqrt{2bT/i}$ — assumes deterministic, uniform flows.
- **Miller-Orr Model:** $U = L + \sqrt[3]{3b\sigma^2 / 4i}$ — stochastic with control limits.
- **EOQ:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2AO/C_c}$ — minimizes ordering + carrying cost.
- **Safety Stock:** $Z \times \sigma_d \times \sqrt{L}$ — buffer against demand uncertainty.
- **Industry Reality:** FMCG/Pharma firms have long OC; tech/service firms have short OC; e-commerce giants may have negative NOC.
- **Key Ratios:** Current = 2:1, Quick = 1:1, Cash = 0.2:1, WC Turnover = Sales / Avg. NWC.
- **KTU Favourite Topics:** OC method computation, Baumol derivation, Policy comparison, Credit policy 5 Cs (Character, Capacity, Capital, Collateral, Conditions).

> **End of Module 4 — Working Capital Issues Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme | PECBT631**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Financing Current Assets (Short Term and Long Term - Mix)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Financing Current Assets (Short Term and Long Term - Mix)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Financing of Current Assets (Short-Term and Long-Term Mix)** refers to the strategic financial decision-making process that determines the optimal proportion of **short-term** (e.g., trade credit, bank overdraft, commercial paper, short-term loans) and **long-term** (e.g., equity, debentures, term loans, retained earnings) sources of funds to finance a firm's **current assets** (cash, receivables, inventory, and marketable securities).

The current asset financing mix is decided based on:
- The **permanence** of current assets (Permanent Working Capital vs. Temporary Working Capital)
- The firm's **risk appetite** (liquidity risk vs. solvency risk)
- The firm's **profitability objectives** (cost of capital minimization)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Household Water Tank Analogy** 🏠
>
> Imagine a household has a **permanent water requirement** of 500 liters per day (drinking, cooking, bathing) and a **temporary extra requirement** of 300 liters during summer (watering plants, extra laundry).
>
> - **Matching Approach (Hedging)**: The 500L permanent tank is built with a long-term home loan (20-year EMI). The extra 300L in summer is fetched using a short-term water tanker (pay-as-you-go). → *Balanced*
> - **Conservative Approach**: Build a 1000L tank with a long-term loan, even filling the extra 500L. The house is safe, but EMI burden is heavy. → *Low Risk, Low Return*
> - **Aggressive Approach**: Take a small 200L permanent tank on loan, and use water tankers (high daily cost) for the remaining 600L daily. → *High Risk, High Return*
>
> The **firm's current assets = household water needs**. The **financing decision = choice between loan vs. tanker**. This is essentially the working capital financing problem.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Glossary)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Permanent Working Capital (PWC)** | The **minimum** level of current assets required to be maintained at all times to support ongoing business operations, irrespective of sales fluctuations. |
| **Temporary Working Capital (TWC)** | The **fluctuating** portion of current assets that rises and falls with the business cycle or seasonal demand patterns. Also called **Variable Working Capital**. |
| **Net Working Capital (NWC)** | Current Assets − Current Liabilities; represents the long-term funds permanently invested in current operations. |
| **Gross Working Capital** | Total investment in current assets. |
| **Hedging / Matching Approach** | Maturity-matching principle: finance assets with sources of identical maturity. |
| **Conservative Approach** | Using more long-term funds (even for temporary needs) to reduce liquidity risk. |
| **Aggressive Approach** | Using more short-term funds (even for permanent needs) to enhance profitability. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics in Bold**:
> - **Risk** in this context is measured by the **probability of insolvency** (inability to pay short-term obligations as they fall due).
> - **Return** is measured by **Net Profit After Tax (NPAT)** or **Return on Investment (ROI)**.
> - **Cost of Short-term Debt** is generally lower than the cost of long-term debt.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Theoretical Framework

The decision of financing current assets involves a fundamental **Risk-Return Trade-off**. The framework rests on three pillars:

#### Pillar 1: Classification of Current Assets

Current assets can be classified into two broad categories:

**A. Permanent (Fixed) Working Capital**
- Represents the irreducible minimum of current assets.
- Remains tied up in the business throughout the year.
- Analogous to the **"fixed floor"** of current assets.

**B. Temporary (Variable) Working Capital**
- The portion that fluctuates with the level of sales/operations.
- Includes seasonal build-ups of inventory and receivables.
- Analogous to the **"seasonal crests"** of current assets.

#### Pillar 2: Classification of Financing Sources

| Source Category | Examples | Typical Cost | Risk to Firm |
|-----------------|----------|--------------|--------------|
| **Spontaneous Sources** | Trade credit, accrued expenses | Lowest (often 0%) | Very Low |
| **Short-Term Bank Sources** | Cash credit, overdraft, short-term loans | Moderate | Low-Medium |
| **Short-Term Market Sources** | Commercial paper, certificates of deposit | Low-Moderate | Medium |
| **Long-Term Bank Sources** | Term loans | Moderate-High | Medium |
| **Long-Term Market Sources** | Equity, debentures, bonds | Highest (equity) / High (debt) | Lowest |

#### Pillar 3: The Three Financing Approaches

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Approaches — KTU High-Yield Classification**

**1. Hedging (Matching) Approach**
- **Principle**: Match the maturity of the financing source with the maturity of the asset.
- Permanent Current Assets → **Long-term financing** (Equity, Debentures, Term Loans)
- Temporary Current Assets → **Short-term financing** (Bank Overdraft, Trade Credit, CP)
- **Risk-Return Profile**: Moderate Risk, Moderate Return
- **Most Recommended by Theory** (matches cash flow timing)

**2. Conservative Approach**
- **Principle**: Use long-term funds to finance **all permanent** and **part of temporary** current assets. Use short-term funds only for the **residual** temporary needs.
- **Risk-Return Profile**: Low Risk, Low Return
- **Cost Implication**: Higher overall cost of capital (because long-term funds are costlier)
- **Benefit**: High liquidity cushion, lower insolvency risk

**3. Aggressive Approach**
- **Principle**: Use short-term funds to finance **all temporary** and **part of permanent** current assets. Use long-term funds only for **fixed assets** and a small part of permanent current assets.
- **Risk-Return Profile**: High Risk, High Return
- **Cost Implication**: Lower overall cost of capital
- **Risk**: Frequent refinancing, possible illiquidity crisis during downturns

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | **Net Working Capital** | $NWC = CA - CL$ | Long-term funds tied up in current operations |
| 2 | **Permanent Working Capital** | $PWC = \text{Minimum CA held throughout the year}$ | Floor of current assets |
| 3 | **Temporary Working Capital** | $TWC = \text{Total CA} - PWC$ | Variable portion |
| 4 | **Current Ratio** | $CR = \dfrac{CA}{CL}$ | Short-term solvency indicator; **Standard: 2:1** |
| 5 | **Quick (Acid-Test) Ratio** | $QR = \dfrac{CA - \text{Inventory}}{CL}$ | Excludes inventory; **Standard: 1:1** |
| 6 | **Risk Level Indicator** | $RL = \dfrac{\text{Short-term Financing}}{\text{Total Financing}}$ | Higher RL = Aggressive policy |
| 7 | **Cost of Conservative Policy** | Higher due to long-term debt's higher cost | Risk-Return trade-off |
| 8 | **Cost of Aggressive Policy** | Lower due to reliance on cheaper short-term funds | Profitability ↑ but Risk ↑ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert**: Students often confuse the **Current Ratio** with the **financing policy**. A firm with a high current ratio is NOT necessarily following a conservative policy — it might simply have excessive inventory. The financing policy refers to the **mix of liabilities** (debt structure), not the level of current assets.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

In **engineering and corporate finance**, the working capital financing decision is critical for:
- **Manufacturing firms** (high inventory levels → require substantial short-term financing)
- **IT/Tech firms** (low inventory, high receivables → need different mix)
- **Infrastructure projects** (long gestation periods → mostly long-term financing)
- **E-commerce firms** (cash conversion cycle variations → dynamic working capital needs)

The decision influences the firm's **credit rating**, **banking relationships**, **shareholder returns**, and **operational continuity**.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Working Capital Components

Let us derive the mathematical relationship between Permanent Working Capital, Temporary Working Capital, and Total Current Assets.

> **Step 1**: Define Total Current Assets as a function of time $t$.

Let $CA(t)$ represent the level of current assets at time $t$.

$$
CA(t) = PWC + TWC(t)
$$

> **Step 2**: Identify the Permanent Working Capital.

Permanent Working Capital ($PWC$) is the **time-invariant** minimum level:

$$
PWC = \min_{t \in [0,T]} CA(t)
$$

> **Step 3**: Identify the Temporary Working Capital.

Temporary Working Capital is the **time-varying** excess over the permanent level:

$$
TWC(t) = CA(t) - PWC
$$

> **Step 4**: Compute the Average Temporary Working Capital over period $[0, T]$.

$$
\overline{TWC} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} TWC(t) \, dt
$$

> **Step 5**: Compute the Average Total Current Assets.

$$
\overline{CA} = PWC + \overline{TWC}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Financing Approaches Comparison

| Parameter | Conservative | Matching (Hedging) | Aggressive |
|-----------|--------------|--------------------|-----------|
| **Permanent CA financed by** | Long-term (100%) | Long-term (100%) | Long-term (Partial) |
| **Temporary CA financed by** | Long-term (Partial) + Short-term (Residual) | Short-term (100%) | Short-term (100%) |
| **Risk of Illiquidity** | Very Low | Moderate | Very High |
| **Cost of Capital** | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Profitability (ROE)** | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Suitable for** | Firms with stable, high cash flows | Most firms (theoretical ideal) | High-growth firms with predictable short-term income |
| **Current Ratio Impact** | Very High (>2) | Moderate (~2) | Low (<2) |
| **Refinancing Frequency** | Low | Moderate | High |
| **EPS Volatility** | Low | Moderate | High |

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem**: A firm has the following current asset requirements across four quarters of a year:
>
> | Quarter | Current Assets (₹) |
> |---------|---------------------|
> | Q1 | 5,00,000 |
> | Q2 | 7,00,000 |
> | Q3 | 8,50,000 |
> | Q4 | 6,00,000 |
>
> **Required**:
> 1. Calculate Permanent Working Capital
> 2. Calculate Average Temporary Working Capital
> 3. Under the **Matching Approach**, how much should be financed by long-term and short-term sources?

**Solution:**

> **Step 1**: Identify the Minimum Current Asset level (this is PWC).

$$
PWC = \min(5,00,000,\ 7,00,000,\ 8,50,000,\ 6,00,000) = \text{₹} 5,00,000
$$

> **Step 2**: Calculate TWC for each quarter.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TWC_{Q1} &= 5,00,000 - 5,00,000 = \text{₹} 0 \\
TWC_{Q2} &= 7,00,000 - 5,00,000 = \text{₹} 2,00,000 \\
TWC_{Q3} &= 8,50,000 - 5,00,000 = \text{₹} 3,50,000 \\
TWC_{Q4} &= 6,00,000 - 5,00,000 = \text{₹} 1,00,000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 3**: Compute Average Temporary Working Capital.

$$
\overline{TWC} = \frac{0 + 2,00,000 + 3,50,000 + 1,00,000}{4}
$$

$$
\overline{TWC} = \frac{6,50,000}{4} = \text{₹} 1,62,500
$$

> **Step 4**: Apply the Matching Approach.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Long-term Financing} &= PWC = \text{₹} 5,00,000 \\
\text{Short-term Financing (Peak)} &= TWC_{\max} = \text{₹} 3,50,000 \text{ (Q3)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer**:
> - **Permanent Working Capital** = ₹ 5,00,000 (Financed by Long-term sources)
> - **Average Temporary Working Capital** = ₹ 1,62,500
> - **Peak Short-term Financing** = ₹ 3,50,000 (Financed by Short-term sources in Q3)

### 3.4 Cost Comparison Numerical (Aggressive vs Conservative)

> **Problem**: A firm needs total financing of ₹ 10,00,000 for current assets. Cost of long-term debt = **12% p.a.**, Cost of short-term debt = **9% p.a.**
>
> **Scenario A (Conservative)**: 80% Long-term, 20% Short-term
> **Scenario B (Aggressive)**: 30% Long-term, 70% Short-term
>
> Calculate the weighted cost of capital in each scenario.

**Solution:**

> **Scenario A — Conservative Approach**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Weighted Cost}_A &= (0.80 \times 12\%) + (0.20 \times 9\%) \\
&= 9.60\% + 1.80\% \\
&= \boxed{11.40\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Scenario B — Aggressive Approach**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Weighted Cost}_B &= (0.30 \times 12\%) + (0.70 \times 9\%) \\
&= 3.60\% + 6.30\% \\
&= \boxed{9.90\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion**: The aggressive approach saves **1.50%** in financing cost. However, the firm faces higher refinancing risk and potential liquidity crises. The decision is a **trade-off**, not a pure cost minimization problem.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

</br>

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"Distinguish between Permanent and Temporary Working Capital. Why is this classification important for financing decisions?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Permanent Working Capital (PWC)**: It refers to the **minimum level of current assets** that a firm must maintain at all times to support its regular business operations, irrespective of seasonal or cyclical fluctuations. It is a **permanent and continuous** investment.

> **Temporary Working Capital (TWC)**: It refers to the **additional current assets** required by a firm to meet **seasonal or cyclical variations** in sales or production. It fluctuates and is not permanently tied up.

> **Importance in Financing Decisions**:
> 1. PWC should ideally be financed by **long-term sources** to ensure availability and stability.
> 2. TWC should be financed by **short-term sources** due to its temporary and fluctuating nature.
> 3. This classification forms the basis of the **Matching (Hedging) Approach** of financing current assets.
> 4. It helps in **minimizing the cost of capital** by using appropriate sources for different asset maturities.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Briefly explain the Conservative Approach of financing current assets. Mention its risk-return implications."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Conservative Approach**: Under this approach, a firm finances **all of its permanent current assets** and a **part of its temporary current assets** through **long-term sources** of finance (equity, debentures, term loans). Only the **residual temporary working capital** is financed through short-term sources.

> **Risk-Return Implications**:
> 1. **Risk**: **Low** — the firm maintains a high current ratio, has abundant liquidity, and faces very low risk of insolvency.
> 2. **Return**: **Low** — because long-term funds are more expensive, the overall cost of capital is higher, leading to reduced profitability and lower Return on Equity (ROE).
> 3. **Trade-off**: It sacrifices profitability for safety and solvency.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**"Explain the three approaches of financing current assets — Hedging, Conservative, and Aggressive — with the help of a suitable diagram. Discuss the risk-return trade-off involved in each."**

**Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Explain the three approaches with a detailed comparative analysis.*

**Model Solution:**

> **1. Hedging (Matching) Approach**
> 
> Under this approach, the firm matches the **maturity of the financing source** with the **maturity of the asset** to be financed.
> 
> - **Permanent Current Assets** → Financed entirely by **Long-term Sources** (Equity, Debentures, Term Loans)
> - **Temporary Current Assets** → Financed entirely by **Short-term Sources** (Bank Overdraft, Trade Credit, Commercial Paper)
> 
> *This approach is also called the "Maturity Matching" principle. It is considered theoretically ideal as it synchronizes cash inflows with cash outflows.*

> **2. Conservative Approach**
> 
> A firm adopting this approach is **risk-averse** and prioritizes liquidity over profitability.
> 
> - **Permanent Current Assets** → Financed by **Long-term Sources** (100%)
> - **Part of Temporary Current Assets** → Also financed by **Long-term Sources**
> - **Residual Temporary Current Assets** → Financed by **Short-term Sources**
> 
> *Implication: The firm carries excess liquidity, resulting in a high current ratio (often > 2:1).*

> **3. Aggressive Approach**
> 
> A risk-taking firm adopts this approach to **maximize profitability** by minimizing financing costs.
> 
> - **Fixed Assets + Part of Permanent Current Assets** → Long-term Sources
> - **All Temporary Current Assets + Remaining Permanent Current Assets** → **Short-term Sources**
> 
> *Implication: The firm operates with low current ratio, high refinancing frequency, and substantial liquidity risk.*

> **Comparative Risk-Return Matrix:**
> 
> | Parameter | Hedging | Conservative | Aggressive |
> |-----------|---------|--------------|------------|
> | Risk | Moderate | Low | High |
> | Return | Moderate | Low | High |
> | Cost of Capital | Moderate | High | Low |
> | Current Ratio | ~2:1 | >2:1 | <2:1 |
> | Suitability | Most firms | Stable cash flows | High-growth firms |

**Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Discuss the risk-return trade-off in detail with a numerical example.*

**Model Solution:**

> **The Risk-Return Trade-off**
> 
> The financing decision is fundamentally a **trade-off** between **Risk** (probability of insolvency/liquidity crisis) and **Return** (profitability/ROE). There exists an **inverse relationship**:
> 
> - As the firm moves from **Aggressive → Matching → Conservative**, the **risk decreases** but the **return also decreases**.
> - Conversely, moving from **Conservative → Aggressive**, the firm earns **higher returns** but bears **higher risk**.

> **Numerical Illustration:**
> 
> A firm requires ₹ 20,00,000 in current asset financing. Cost of long-term funds = **14%**; Cost of short-term funds = **10%**.
> 
> | Policy | LT : ST Ratio | Weighted Cost | Risk Level | Expected ROE |
> |--------|---------------|---------------|------------|--------------|
> | Conservative | 80:20 | 10.8% | Low | 12% |
> | Matching | 60:40 | 11.6% | Moderate | 14% |
> | Aggressive | 30:70 | 11.2% | High | 17% |
> 
> **Calculation for Conservative:**
> $(0.80 \times 14\%) + (0.20 \times 10\%) = 11.2\% + 2.0\% = 13.2\%$ ✓
> 
> **Calculation for Matching:**
> $(0.60 \times 14\%) + (0.40 \times 10\%) = 8.4\% + 4.0\% = 12.4\%$
> 
> **Calculation for Aggressive:**
> $(0.30 \times 14\%) + (0.70 \times 10\%) = 4.2\% + 7.0\% = 11.2\%$
> 
> **Conclusion**: The firm must choose a policy that balances its **risk appetite** with its **profitability objectives**, considering external factors like interest rate volatility, business cycle, and credit availability.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"A manufacturing company has estimated its current asset requirements for the next four quarters as ₹ 8,00,000, ₹ 12,00,000, ₹ 15,00,000, and ₹ 10,00,000 respectively. Determine the Permanent Working Capital, Average Temporary Working Capital, and the financing required under (a) Matching Approach, and (b) Aggressive Approach. Comment on the risk-return implications."**

**Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Compute PWC, Average TWC, and the financing under the Matching Approach.*

**Model Solution:**

> **Step 1: Identify Permanent Working Capital**
> 
> $$
> PWC = \min(8,00,000,\ 12,00,000,\ 15,00,000,\ 10,00,000) = \text{₹ } 8,00,000
> $$

> **Step 2: Calculate TWC for each quarter**
> 
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> TWC_{Q1} &= 8,00,000 - 8,00,000 = \text{₹ } 0 \\
> TWC_{Q2} &= 12,00,000 - 8,00,000 = \text{₹ } 4,00,000 \\
> TWC_{Q3} &= 15,00,000 - 8,00,000 = \text{₹ } 7,00,000 \\
> TWC_{Q4} &= 10,00,000 - 8,00,000 = \text{₹ } 2,00,000
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Step 3: Average TWC**
> 
> $$
> \overline{TWC} = \frac{0 + 4,00,000 + 7,00,000 + 2,00,000}{4} = \frac{13,00,000}{4} = \text{₹ } 3,25,000
> $$

> **Step 4: Matching Approach Financing**
> 
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \text{Long-term Financing} &= PWC = \text{₹ } 8,00,000 \\
> \text{Peak Short-term Financing (Q3)} &= TWC_{\max} = \text{₹ } 7,00,000 \\
> \text{Average Short-term Financing} &= \text{₹ } 3,25,000
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Conclusion (Matching Approach)**: The firm uses ₹ 8,00,000 in long-term funds and may need up to ₹ 7,00,000 in short-term borrowing during Q3. This is the **theoretically ideal** policy.

**Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Determine financing under the Aggressive Approach and comment on risk-return.*

**Model Solution:**

> **Assumption for Aggressive Approach**: The firm finances the **entire permanent working capital and 50% of peak temporary working capital** through short-term sources (a common textbook assumption).

> **Step 1: Calculate Aggressive Financing**
> 
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \text{Short-term Financing (Permanent CA)} &= 8,00,000 \\
> \text{Short-term Financing (Temporary CA)} &= 7,00,000 \text{ (full TWC in Q3)} \\
> \text{Total Short-term Financing Required} &= 15,00,000 \text{ (in Q3)}
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> **Step 2: Long-term Financing in Aggressive Approach**
> 
> Under aggressive policy, long-term funds are used **only for fixed assets and a small portion of permanent CA**. Suppose ₹ 4,00,000 of PWC is financed by long-term sources:
> 
> $$
> \text{Long-term Financing} = \text{₹ } 4,00,000
> $$

> **Step 3: Risk-Return Implications**
> 
> | Aspect | Implication |
> |--------|-------------|
> | **Risk** | **HIGH** — Reliance on short-term funds requires frequent refinancing. Any tightening of credit can cause a liquidity crisis. |
> | **Return** | **HIGH** — Cost of capital is lower (short-term rates are typically 2-4% lower), boosting ROE and EPS. |
> | **Suitability** | Best for firms with **predictable, strong cash inflows** that can quickly repay short-term obligations. |
> | **Vulnerability** | High during **recessions or tight monetary conditions**; bankruptcy risk increases. |

> **Final Recommendation**: The aggressive approach is **not recommended for a manufacturing firm** with seasonal demand (as in the problem), because inventory build-up and receivables in Q2-Q3 require stable financing. The **Matching Approach** is the most suitable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> **Common mistakes students make on this topic** (and lose 2-3 marks each):
> 
> 1. **Confusing Permanent WC with Net WC** — Net WC = CA − CL; Permanent WC = minimum CA across the year. They are related but not the same.
> 2. **In numerical problems**, students often forget to calculate the **peak short-term financing requirement** (which is the maximum TWC, not the average). Always identify the highest TWC quarter.
> 3. **Mislabeling policies**: An "aggressive policy" does NOT mean "aggressive sales growth" — it means aggressive use of **short-term debt** for financing.
> 4. **Ignoring the cost calculation**: In Part B questions, examiners expect you to show weighted cost computations with at least one numerical illustration.
> 5. **Diagram quality**: Use **clear, labeled diagrams** (maturity matching chart) to score full marks on sub-parts.
> 6. **Forgetting qualitative discussion**: The risk-return commentary is mandatory; just numbers without interpretation will lose marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4**

✅ **Three Financing Approaches**:
- **Hedging/Matching**: Permanent → LT; Temporary → ST *(Ideal)*
- **Conservative**: Permanent → LT; Temporary → LT (partial) + ST (residual) *(Low Risk)*
- **Aggressive**: Permanent → LT (partial) + ST (partial); Temporary → ST *(High Return)*

✅ **Two Components of Working Capital**:
- **Permanent (Fixed) WC** = minimum CA across all periods
- **Temporary (Variable) WC** = Total CA − Permanent WC

✅ **Key Formulas** (must memorize):
- $NWC = CA - CL$
- $PWC = \min(CA_t)$ over time
- $TWC = CA_t - PWC$
- $\overline{TWC} = \dfrac{1}{T}\sum TWC_t$

✅ **Risk-Return Trade-off**:
- Conservative = Low Risk, Low Return, High Liquidity
- Matching = Moderate Risk, Moderate Return
- Aggressive = High Risk, High Return, Low Liquidity

✅ **Standard Liquidity Ratios**:
- Current Ratio = 2:1 *(norm)*
- Quick Ratio = 1:1 *(norm)*

✅ **Key Numerical Steps for KTU Problems**:
1. Identify PWC (lowest CA value)
2. Calculate TWC for each period
3. Compute average TWC
4. Apply chosen policy to determine LT vs. ST split
5. Always compute and compare **weighted cost of capital**
6. Discuss **risk-return implications** qualitatively

✅ **Practical Considerations** (often asked as 2-mark questions):
- Cost of ST debt is generally **lower** than LT debt
- Aggressive policy **saves cost** but increases **refinancing risk**
- Conservative policy is **safer** but reduces **ROE**
- The **Matching Approach** is theoretically the most efficient
</br>

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combining Liability Structures and Current Asset Decisions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Combining Liability Structures and Current Asset Decisions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Combining Liability Structures and Current Asset Decisions** refers to the integrated working-capital policy framework in which a firm simultaneously decides (a) the optimal level of investment in current assets and (b) the optimal mix of short-term and long-term sources of funds (liabilities) used to finance those current assets. The goal is to balance **liquidity** against **profitability** while minimising the firm's overall cost of capital and financial risk.

In KTU terminology, this is the **Working-Capital Financing Decision** — the joint optimisation of the *Asset-Side Decision* (current asset composition) and the *Liability-Side Decision* (maturity structure of financing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic sits at the intersection of **Module 3 (Current Assets)** and **Module 4 (Financing Decisions)**. It is treated as a *synthesis chapter* — students must demonstrate the ability to merge both halves of the balance sheet into one cohesive policy.

### The Two Halves of the Equation

| Decision | Asset Side | Liability Side |
|---|---|---|
| Question | *How much* to invest in current assets? | *How* to fund those assets? |
| Tool | Cash Conversion Cycle, Inventory & Receivables Policy | Spontaneous, Short-term, Long-term Sources |
| Objective | Maintain liquidity for operations | Minimise cost of capital |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tank" Model

Imagine a city water supply system:

- **Current Assets** are the **water in the overhead tank** (cash, receivables, inventory) that must always be available to serve the city's daily needs.
- **Liability Structures** are the **pipes** that refill the tank: small private pipes (short-term loans), municipal pipelines (long-term debt), and rainwater harvesting (spontaneous credit from suppliers).
- If you rely **entirely on small private pipes** (aggressive short-term borrowing), the tank may run dry the moment a pipe bursts — that is **liquidity risk**.
- If you build **only huge municipal pipelines** (long-term debt) for a small town, the fixed cost is enormous and the pipes sit idle — that is **excess cost of capital**.
- The **art of combining** is choosing the right *size* and *type* of pipes so the tank never empties AND the cost is optimal.

This is precisely what a financial manager does: he chooses how much *permanent capacity* (long-term finance) to install versus how much *on-demand top-up* (short-term finance) to use.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Think of *Swiggy/Zomato* during festival season — inventory (grocery stock) spikes temporarily. They use a mix of long-term bank credit lines (permanent) plus short-term invoice discounting (temporary). The same logic applies to manufacturing firms with seasonal demand.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology — Master Glossary

| Term | Definition | Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Permanent Current Assets** | Minimum level of current assets required regardless of sales cycle (e.g., safety stock, minimum cash balance) | Treated as **long-term** investment |
| **Temporary Current Assets** | Additional current assets needed only during peak/seasonal periods | Financed with **short-term** funds |
| **Hedging Approach (Matching)** | Maturity of financing matches maturity of asset being financed | Maturity-matching principle |
| **Conservative Approach** | All temporary + permanent current assets funded by long-term sources | Lowest risk, highest cost |
| **Aggressive Approach** | All temporary + part of permanent current assets funded by short-term sources | Highest risk, lowest cost |
| **Spontaneous Liabilities** | Sources that arise automatically from operations (trade credit, accrued expenses) | Cost-free or low-cost |
| **Net Working Capital** | Current Assets minus Current Liabilities | Liquidity cushion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pearl:** The *aggressive* policy is **NOT** a synonym for *risky mismanagement* — it is a deliberate, calculated strategy employed by firms confident about their cash flows (e.g., mature FMCG giants, large retailers).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Working-Capital Financing Policies

A firm's *liability structure* in the context of current-asset financing is essentially a choice among three policy archetypes. Each represents a different point on the **Risk–Return Trade-off Curve**.

### Policy 1 — The Hedging (Matching) Approach
- **Logic:** Every asset is financed with a source of the *same maturity*.
  - Permanent current assets + Fixed assets → **Long-term funds** (equity, debentures, term loans).
  - Temporary current assets → **Short-term funds** (bank overdraft, commercial paper, trade credit).
- **Mathematical Expression:**

$$
\text{Long-term Funds} = \text{Fixed Assets} + \text{Permanent Current Assets}
$$

$$
\text{Short-term Funds} = \text{Temporary Current Assets}
$$

- **Risk:** Moderate.
- **Cost:** Moderate.
- **Use Case:** The "textbook ideal" — most KTU numerical problems assume this unless stated otherwise.

### Policy 2 — The Conservative Approach
- **Logic:** Finance *everything* — including all temporary current assets — using long-term sources. Short-term debt is used only for emergencies.
- **Logic Equation:**

$$
\text{Long-term Funds} = \text{Fixed Assets} + \text{Permanent CA} + \text{Temporary CA}
$$

$$
\text{Short-term Funds} = 0 \quad \text{(normally)}
$$

- **Risk:** Lowest — no refinancing risk, no liquidity squeeze.
- **Cost:** Highest — long-term funds are costlier than short-term funds.
- **Side Effect:** Excessive cash pile-up, lower ROE (Return on Equity).

### Policy 3 — The Aggressive Approach
- **Logic:** Finance *all temporary current assets* AND *part of permanent current assets* using short-term funds.
- **Logic Equation:**

$$
\text{Short-term Funds} = \text{Temporary CA} + \text{Part of Permanent CA}
$$

$$
\text{Long-term Funds} = \text{Fixed Assets} + \text{Remaining Permanent CA}
$$

- **Risk:** Highest — rollover risk, interest-rate risk, liquidity crunch in downturn.
- **Cost:** Lowest — short-term interest rates are typically lower.
- **Side Effect:** Higher EPS in good times; potential bankruptcy in bad times.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Markdown Rule:** All absolute-value bars and such symbols inside the table below are written as `\vert` (in LaTeX-rendered display) to prevent breaking the table layout.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Variables & Units | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Net Working Capital | $NWC = CA - CL$ | CA, CL in ₹ crore | Must be positive for liquidity |
| 2 | Permanent Current Assets | $PCA = CA_{min}$ over the year | ₹ crore | Treated as fixed investment |
| 3 | Temporary Current Assets | $TCA = CA_{peak} - PCA$ | ₹ crore | Seasonal component |
| 4 | Total Assets | $TA = FA + CA$ | ₹ crore | Balance sheet identity |
| 5 | Conservative Long-term Fund Need | $L_{cons} = FA + PCA + TCA$ | ₹ crore | All assets long-term funded |
| 6 | Hedging Long-term Fund Need | $L_{hed} = FA + PCA$ | ₹ crore | Matches maturity |
| 7 | Aggressive Long-term Fund Need | $L_{agg} = FA + \alpha \cdot PCA$ where $0 \le \alpha < 1$ | ₹ crore | Only fraction $\alpha$ is permanent |
| 8 | Aggressive Short-term Fund Need | $S_{agg} = TCA + (1-\alpha) \cdot PCA$ | ₹ crore | Bulk is short-term |
| 9 | Effective Annual Rate (short-term rolled) | $EAR = \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{m}\right)^{m} - 1$ | $r$ nominal, $m$ compounding | Used to compare short vs long cost |
| 10 | Cost Trade-off Index | $CTI = \dfrac{\text{Saved Interest}}{\text{Increased Risk Premium}}$ | Ratio | CTI $> 1$ ⇒ Aggressive justified |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Financial Management

| Field of Application | Use of this Combined Decision |
|---|---|
| **Corporate Treasury** | Decides daily mix of cash, FDs, overdraft utilisation |
| **Investment Banking** | Structures working-capital lines in IPO prospectuses |
| **Credit Rating** | Rating agencies (CRISIL, ICRA) assess the *maturity mismatch* |
| **Supply-Chain Finance** | Determines vendor financing vs bank financing |
| **Banking & ALM** | Asset-Liability Management in banks uses the same matching principle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** A poorly matched liability structure was a major contributor to the 2008 financial crisis — banks funded long-term mortgages with overnight commercial paper. The same mistake at firm level can lead to insolvency.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Numerical Problem — Identifying the Policy and Computing the Funding Mix

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A firm has the following estimated current assets and fixed assets for the next year:
> - Fixed Assets = ₹ **200 crore**
> - Permanent Current Assets = ₹ **80 crore**
> - Temporary Current Assets (seasonal peak) = ₹ **60 crore**
> - Interest rate on Long-term debt = **12%** per annum
> - Interest rate on Short-term borrowings = **9%** per annum
>
> **Required:** Determine the financing mix (Long-term vs Short-term funds required) and the *total annual interest cost* under each of the three policies.

---

### Step 1 — Identify Asset Categories

The total current assets vary between a *trough* and a *peak*:
- **Trough Level of CA** = Permanent CA = ₹ 80 crore
- **Peak Level of CA** = Permanent CA + Temporary CA = 80 + 60 = ₹ 140 crore

Therefore:
- The **permanent** portion (always present) = ₹ 80 crore
- The **temporary** portion (only at peak) = ₹ 60 crore

---

### Step 2 — Apply the Three Policy Rules

#### Policy A — Hedging (Matching) Approach

$$
L_{hed} = FA + PCA = 200 + 80 = \text{₹ 280 crore}
$$

$$
S_{hed} = TCA = \text{₹ 60 crore}
$$

#### Policy B — Conservative Approach

$$
L_{cons} = FA + PCA + TCA = 200 + 80 + 60 = \text{₹ 340 crore}
$$

$$
S_{cons} = 0
$$

#### Policy C — Aggressive Approach

Assume the firm finances *all* temporary CA and *half* of permanent CA with short-term funds.

$$
L_{agg} = FA + 0.5 \times PCA = 200 + (0.5 \times 80) = \text{₹ 240 crore}
$$

$$
S_{agg} = TCA + 0.5 \times PCA = 60 + 40 = \text{₹ 100 crore}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute Annual Interest Cost (Assuming Average Utilisation)

For a more realistic calculation, recognise that the *temporary* short-term borrowing is only needed for the duration of the seasonal peak. Assuming the peak lasts **6 months** out of 12:

#### Hedging Approach

$$
\text{Interest}_{hed} = (L_{hed} \times 12\%) + \left(\frac{S_{hed}}{2} \times 9\%\right)
$$

$$
= (280 \times 0.12) + (30 \times 0.09)
$$

$$
= 33.60 + 2.70 = \text{₹ 36.30 crore}
$$

#### Conservative Approach

$$
\text{Interest}_{cons} = (L_{cons} \times 12\%) + 0
$$

$$
= 340 \times 0.12 = \text{₹ 40.80 crore}
$$

> Note: Although long-term funds of ₹ 340 crore are raised, the firm earns interest income on the surplus ₹ 60 crore parked in marketable securities. Assuming a 6% yield, that offsets some cost.

#### Aggressive Approach

$$
\text{Interest}_{agg} = (L_{agg} \times 12\%) + \left(\frac{S_{agg}}{2} \times 9\%\right)
$$

$$
= (240 \times 0.12) + (50 \times 0.09)
$$

$$
= 28.80 + 4.50 = \text{₹ 33.30 crore}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Comparative Summary Matrix

| Policy | Long-term Funds (₹ cr) | Short-term Funds (₹ cr) | Annual Interest (₹ cr) | Risk Level | ROE Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hedging** | 280 | 60 (peak only) | 36.30 | Moderate | Balanced |
| **Conservative** | 340 | 0 | 40.80 (gross) | Low | Lowest |
| **Aggressive** | 240 | 100 (peak only) | 33.30 | High | Highest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Aggressive policy saves ₹ 3 crore in interest versus Hedging, but at the cost of significantly higher refinancing and liquidity risk. The *optimal* choice depends on the firm's cash-flow predictability, credit rating, and management's risk appetite.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Framework — The Trade-off Equation

The combined decision can be reduced to a single inequality that the financial manager must satisfy:

$$
\boxed{
\underbrace{\text{Cost Savings from Short-term Finance}}_{\Delta C} \; > \; \underbrace{\text{Expected Cost of Financial Distress}}_{\Delta R}
}
$$

Where:

- $\Delta C$ = (Long-term rate − Short-term rate) × Short-term amount raised
- $\Delta R$ = Probability of distress × Cost of distress (bankruptcy, credit downgrade, lost sales)

### Decision Rule:
- If $\Delta C > \Delta R$ → Aggressive policy is justified
- If $\Delta C < \Delta R$ → Conservative policy is justified
- If $\Delta C \approx \Delta R$ → Hedging/Matching policy is optimal

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Optimal Short-term Debt Fraction

Let the firm's total assets be $TA = FA + CA$. Define $k$ as the fraction of CA financed with short-term debt. The expected cost of the policy can be modelled as:

$$
E[\text{Cost}] = k \cdot CA \cdot r_{st} + (1-k) \cdot CA \cdot r_{lt} + \lambda(k) \cdot D
$$

Where:
- $r_{st}$ = short-term interest rate
- $r_{lt}$ = long-term interest rate
- $\lambda(k)$ = probability of financial distress (increasing in $k$)
- $D$ = direct cost of distress

Differentiating with respect to $k$ and setting to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial E[\text{Cost}]}{\partial k} = CA \cdot (r_{st} - r_{lt}) + \lambda'(k) \cdot D = 0
$$

Solving for the *optimal* $k^*$:

$$
k^* = \lambda'^{-1} \left( \frac{CA \cdot (r_{lt} - r_{st})}{D} \right)
$$

This is the **theoretically optimal fraction of current assets to fund with short-term debt**. In practice, managers use a heuristic version: target a *current ratio* between 1.5 and 2.0 as a proxy for balanced policy.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Concept Map — The Combined Decision Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Firm Operations Start"] --> B["Identify Current Asset Need"]
    B --> C{"Separate Permanent vs Temporary CA"}
    C --> D["Permanent Current Assets PCA"]
    C --> E["Temporary Current Assets TCA"]
    D --> F["Apply Liability Choice"]
    E --> F
    F --> G1["Hedging Policy"]
    F --> G2["Conservative Policy"]
    F --> G3["Aggressive Policy"]
    G1 --> H1["LT Funds = FA plus PCA / ST Funds = TCA"]
    G2 --> H2["LT Funds = FA plus PCA plus TCA / ST Funds = 0"]
    G3 --> H3["LT Funds = FA plus part of PCA / ST Funds = TCA plus rest PCA"]
    H1 --> I["Risk Return Trade off Evaluation"]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J["Final Working Capital Policy Adopted"]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The firm first classifies its current assets, then routes the financing decision through one of three policy branches, and finally passes all three through the *Risk–Return Trade-off Filter* before selecting the optimal policy.

---

## 4.2 Detailed Liability–Asset Matching Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ASSETS["ASSET SIDE"]
        FA["Fixed Assets"]
        PCA["Permanent Current Assets"]
        TCA["Temporary Current Assets"]
    end
    subgraph LIAB["LIABILITY SIDE"]
        LTD["Long Term Debt and Equity"]
        STD["Short Term Borrowings"]
        SPON["Spontaneous Liabilities"]
    end
    HEDFA["Hedging: FA matched with LTD"]
    HEDPCA["Hedging: PCA matched with LTD"]
    HEDTCA["Hedging: TCA matched with STD plus SPON"]
    CONALL["Conservative: All assets matched with LTD"]
    AGGFA["Aggressive: FA matched with LTD"]
    AGGPCA["Aggressive: Partial PCA matched with STD"]
    AGGTCA["Aggressive: All TCA plus residual PCA matched with STD"]
    FA --> HEDFA
    PCA --> HEDPCA
    TCA --> HEDTCA
    FA --> CONALL
    PCA --> CONALL
    TCA --> CONALL
    FA --> AGGFA
    PCA --> AGGPCA
    TCA --> AGGTCA
```

---

## 4.3 Risk-Return Trade-off Curve (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["Cost of Capital Axis"] --> Y["Increasing Cost"]
    A1["Conservative Policy"] --- A2["Low Risk, High Cost"]
    B1["Hedging Policy"] --- B2["Balanced Risk and Cost"]
    C1["Aggressive Policy"] --- C2["High Risk, Low Cost"]
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    A2 --> B2
    B2 --> C2
```

> **Interpretation:** Moving from Conservative → Hedging → Aggressive, the cost of capital falls but the financial risk rises. The optimal point lies on the curve where the firm's marginal risk-tolerance equals the marginal cost-saving.

---

## 4.4 Spontaneous vs Negotiated Liability Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L["Liability Sources for Current Assets"] --> S["Spontaneous Free Sources"]
    L --> N["Negotiated Cost bearing Sources"]
    L --> LT["Long Term Sources"]
    S --> S1["Trade Credit from Suppliers"]
    S --> S2["Accrued Wages and Expenses"]
    S --> S3["Outstanding Tax Liability"]
    N --> N1["Bank Overdraft and Cash Credit"]
    N --> N2["Commercial Paper"]
    N --> N3["Short Term Loans"]
    N --> N4["Bills Discounting"]
    LT --> LT1["Term Loans from Banks"]
    LT --> LT2["Debentures and Bonds"]
    LT --> LT3["Preference and Equity Capital"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy Rule for KTU:** In an *Aggressive* policy, the firm exhausts Spontaneous → Negotiable short-term → Long-term sources (in that order). In a *Conservative* policy, the order is reversed.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define the "Hedging Approach" (or Matching Approach) of working capital financing. How does it differ from the Conservative Approach?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Hedging (Matching) Approach** is a working-capital financing policy in which the *maturity of the source of funds* is matched with the *maturity of the asset* being financed. Permanent current assets and fixed assets are financed using **long-term funds** (equity, debentures, term loans), while temporary current assets are financed using **short-term funds** (bank overdraft, commercial paper, trade credit).

| Feature | Hedging Approach | Conservative Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Long-term funds used for | Fixed Assets + Permanent CA | Fixed Assets + Permanent CA + Temporary CA |
| Short-term funds used for | Only Temporary CA | None (or negligible) |
| Risk level | Moderate | Very Low |
| Cost level | Moderate | High |
| Ideal for | Firms with stable cash flows | Firms with volatile/uncertain cash flows |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for difference + 1 for table-style comparison)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What are "Permanent Current Assets" and "Temporary Current Assets"? Why is the distinction important in combining liability structures with current-asset decisions?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Permanent Current Assets** refer to the *minimum level* of current assets that a firm must maintain *continuously* throughout the year, irrespective of seasonal variations. Examples: safety stock of inventory, minimum cash balance, core receivables from regular customers.

- **Temporary Current Assets** refer to the *additional* current assets required *only during peak seasons* or cyclical upturns. Examples: seasonal inventory build-up, festive-period receivables, peak-month cash buffer.

**Importance of the Distinction:**
1. It determines the **minimum long-term funding requirement** of the firm.
2. It enables the financial manager to decide *how much* of the working capital can be safely funded with cheaper short-term sources.
3. It is the foundation for choosing between the **Hedging, Conservative, and Aggressive** policies.
4. It directly impacts the firm's **risk-return profile** and the current ratio.

**(3 marks: 1 for definitions + 2 for importance points)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often confuse *Permanent Current Assets* with *Fixed Assets* — they are different. PCA is a CURRENT asset, not a fixed one.
> - In numerical problems, students forget to multiply short-term borrowing by the *fraction of the year* it is actually used.
> - Do NOT confuse the **Hedging Approach** with the **Conservative Approach**. Hedging funds *all* temporary CA with short-term debt; Conservative uses *zero* short-term debt.
> - Always show the financing-mix table in your answer — it carries 2-3 easy marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**A manufacturing firm provides the following data for the upcoming year:**

| Item | Amount (₹ Lakh) |
|---|---|
| Fixed Assets | 500 |
| Permanent Current Assets | 200 |
| Temporary Current Assets (peak) | 150 |
| Cost of Long-term Funds | 11% per annum |
| Cost of Short-term Funds | 8% per annum |

The temporary current assets are required for **5 months** in a year. The firm is considering three alternative working-capital financing policies.

**(a)** Compute the total annual interest cost under each of the three policies — Hedging, Conservative, and Aggressive (assume under Aggressive, the firm finances 50% of Permanent CA with short-term funds).

**(b)** Comment on the risk-return trade-off and recommend the most suitable policy. Justify your answer with reference to the firm's risk profile.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Classification of Assets**
- Fixed Assets = ₹ 500 Lakh (permanent, non-current)
- Permanent CA = ₹ 200 Lakh (always required)
- Temporary CA = ₹ 150 Lakh (only 5 months)

**Step 2: Hedging (Matching) Approach**

$$
L_{hed} = 500 + 200 = \text{₹ 700 Lakh}
$$

$$
S_{hed} = \text{₹ 150 Lakh (used for 5 months)}
$$

$$
\text{Average Short-term Balance} = 150 \times \frac{5}{12} = \text{₹ 62.5 Lakh}
$$

$$
\text{Interest}_{hed} = (700 \times 0.11) + (62.5 \times 0.08) = 77.00 + 5.00 = \text{₹ 82.00 Lakh}
$$

**Step 3: Conservative Approach**

$$
L_{cons} = 500 + 200 + 150 = \text{₹ 850 Lakh}
$$

$$
S_{cons} = 0
$$

$$
\text{Interest}_{cons} = 850 \times 0.11 = \text{₹ 93.50 Lakh}
$$

**Step 4: Aggressive Approach**

$$
L_{agg} = 500 + (0.5 \times 200) = \text{₹ 600 Lakh}
$$

$$
S_{agg} = 150 + (0.5 \times 200) = \text{₹ 250 Lakh (used for 5 months)}
$$

$$
\text{Average Short-term Balance} = 250 \times \frac{5}{12} = \text{₹ 104.17 Lakh}
$$

$$
\text{Interest}_{agg} = (600 \times 0.11) + (104.17 \times 0.08) = 66.00 + 8.33 = \text{₹ 74.33 Lakh}
$$

**Comparative Table (1 mark for clear presentation):**

| Policy | LT Funds (₹ L) | ST Funds (₹ L) | Annual Interest (₹ L) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hedging | 700 | 150 (5 m) | 82.00 |
| Conservative | 850 | 0 | 93.50 |
| Aggressive | 600 | 250 (5 m) | 74.33 |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

The Aggressive policy has the *lowest interest cost* (₹ 74.33 Lakh) but the *highest refinancing and liquidity risk* because the firm relies heavily on short-term borrowings that must be rolled over every few months. The Conservative policy has the *lowest risk* (no short-term debt) but the *highest cost* (₹ 93.50 Lakh), eroding profitability. The Hedging approach strikes a balance (₹ 82.00 Lakh, moderate risk).

**Recommendation:** The **Hedging (Matching) Approach** is recommended because:
1. It aligns the *maturity of the liability* with the *maturity of the asset* — the foundational principle of sound treasury management.
2. It avoids the bankruptcy risk of Aggressive policy and the cost-inefficiency of Conservative policy.
3. It maintains a healthy *current ratio* and *acid-test ratio*, which strengthens the firm's credit rating.
4. The ₹ 7.67 Lakh saving under Aggressive is *not worth* the incremental risk if cash flows are even slightly volatile.

> However, if the firm's cash inflows are *highly predictable* (e.g., a large FMCG company with monthly subscription revenue), the Aggressive policy could be adopted to maximise shareholder value.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**Distinguish between the three working-capital financing policies. Using a hypothetical balance sheet, illustrate how a firm would arrive at the optimal mix of short-term and long-term funds. Discuss the role of the Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC) in this decision.**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Distinguishing the Three Policies**

Already covered in detail in Section 2.1 above. Key distinguishing points to mention in the exam:

| Dimension | Hedging | Conservative | Aggressive |
|---|---|---|---|
| Long-term fund coverage | FA + PCA | FA + PCA + TCA | FA + part of PCA |
| Short-term fund coverage | TCA | Negligible | TCA + part of PCA |
| Liquidity position | Adequate | Excess (idle cash) | Tight |
| Risk of insolvency | Low | Very low | High |
| Profitability impact | Moderate | Lowest ROE | Highest ROE |
| Current Ratio level | ~ 2.0 | $> 2.5$ | $< 1.5$ |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Hypothetical Balance Sheet & Role of CCC**

**Hypothetical Balance Sheet of XYZ Ltd. (₹ Lakh):**

| Liabilities | ₹ | Assets | ₹ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equity Capital | 300 | Fixed Assets | 500 |
| Long-term Debt | 250 | Permanent CA | 200 |
| Short-term Debt | 200 | Temporary CA | 150 |
| Trade Credit (Spontaneous) | 100 | Cash & Bank | 50 |
| Other Current Liabilities | 50 | Receivables | 100 |
| **Total** | **900** | **Total** | **900** |

**Optimal Mix Derivation:**

- Permanent funding need = FA + PCA = 500 + 200 = ₹ 700 Lakh
- Available permanent funds = Equity (300) + LT Debt (250) = ₹ 550 Lakh
- **Funding Gap = ₹ 150 Lakh**, which the firm covers using short-term debt (200) and trade credit (100).
- This is essentially a **Hedging with a slight Aggressive tilt**.

**Role of Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC):**

$$
CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO
$$

Where:
- $DIO$ = Days Inventory Outstanding
- $DSO$ = Days Sales Outstanding
- $DPO$ = Days Payable Outstanding

**Implications for the Combined Decision:**

1. **Longer CCC → Larger current-asset need → More short-term financing required.** This pushes the firm toward Aggressive policy if cost is the priority, or toward Conservative if liquidity is the priority.
2. **Shorter CCC → Lower funding need → Firm can afford Conservative policy without much cost penalty.**
3. **Firms with volatile CCC** (e.g., agro-processing) should match more conservatively.
4. **Firms with stable, short CCC** (e.g., fast-food QSR chains like McDonald's) can safely use Aggressive policy.
5. CCC reduction through supply-chain optimisation *reduces* the financing burden, making all three policies cheaper.

**Conclusion:** The optimal combination of liability structure and current asset decisions is *CCC-driven*. A firm should first attempt to *shorten* the CCC (operational efficiency), and then choose the financing policy that minimises cost for the *residual* working capital need.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam!**

- [x] **Combining** means the *simultaneous optimisation* of current-asset levels and their financing mix.
- [x] **Permanent Current Assets (PCA)** = Minimum CA always present → Fund with **long-term sources**.
- [x] **Temporary Current Assets (TCA)** = Seasonal/peak CA → Can be funded with **short-term sources**.
- [x] **Hedging Approach:** LT = FA + PCA; ST = TCA. The "textbook balanced" answer.
- [x] **Conservative Approach:** LT = FA + PCA + TCA; ST = 0. Lowest risk, highest cost.
- [x] **Aggressive Approach:** LT = FA + part of PCA; ST = TCA + rest of PCA. Highest risk, lowest cost.
- [x] **Risk-Return Trade-off:** More short-term debt ⇒ Lower cost, Higher risk.
- [x] **Decision Rule:** $\Delta C > \Delta R$ ⇒ Aggressive justified.
- [x] **Spontaneous Liabilities** (trade credit, accruals) are *cost-free* and should be maximised first.
- [x] **Current Ratio heuristic:** Conservative > 2.5; Hedging ≈ 2.0; Aggressive < 1.5.
- [x] **CCC = DIO + DSO − DPO** — A longer CCC pushes the firm toward short-term funding.
- [x] The *optimal* policy is firm-specific — depends on cash-flow predictability, credit rating, and industry cyclicality.
- [x] **Aggressive ≠ Mismanagement** — it is a calculated strategy for firms with predictable cash flows.
- [x] **Conservative ≠ Safe always** — it destroys shareholder value through idle cash and low ROE.
- [x] In numerical problems, *always* adjust the short-term interest for the *duration of use* (e.g., 5/12 for 5 months).
- [x] The phrase "*matching maturity with asset life*" is the examiner's favourite — mention it at least once.

> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 examiners frequently frame Part (b) of a 14-mark question as "*Discuss the suitability of the policy in the Indian context*" or "*How would the policy change during a recession?*". Always include a 2-3 line application-oriented paragraph — it is the difference between a 12 and a 14.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Working Capital
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Working Capital

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Working Capital (WC)** refers to the capital required by a firm to finance its short-term assets and to meet its short-term obligations. In rigorous KTU 2024 Scheme terminology:

$$\text{Working Capital} = \text{Current Assets (CA)} - \text{Current Liabilities (CL)}$$

There are two distinct conceptual variants examiners routinely test:

| Concept | Definition | Nature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Gross Working Capital (GWC)** | Total investment in all current assets (Cash, Debtors, Inventory, Short-term Securities) | Permanent + Temporary |
| **Net Working Capital (NWC)** | The excess of Current Assets over Current Liabilities; represents the long-term funds used to finance current assets | Operating Liquidity Buffer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-4 Focus:** The unit deals with *Estimation* of Working Capital, which is the **quantitative forecasting of the quantum of funds** required to seamlessly run the **Operating Cycle** of a business. The Operating Cycle Method is the most heavily tested concept.

## 1.2 Types of Working Capital

**1. Permanent (Fixed) Working Capital** — The minimum level of current assets that a firm must always hold to continue its operations uninterruptedly. It grows gradually with the scale of business.

**2. Temporary (Variable) Working Capital** — The additional working capital required over and above the permanent level to meet seasonal demand, cyclical fluctuations, or unexpected requirements.

**3. Balance Sheet View vs. Operating Cycle View:**
- The *Balance Sheet View* is **static** (a point-in-time snapshot).
- The *Operating Cycle View* is **dynamic** (time-based flow of funds).

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Family Monthly Budget" Analogy** 🏠
> 
> Imagine a middle-class family. Their **salary (Long-term funds)** is fixed, but their **monthly expenses (groceries, milk, electricity — Current Assets)** fluctuate. They have **EMIs and credit card bills (Current Liabilities)** that must be paid on a *fixed date*, while their *salary comes on the 1st* and *rent is due on the 5th*.
> 
> The gap between "salary inflow date" and "rent outflow date" creates a **Working Capital Gap**. **Estimation of Working Capital is exactly this** — calculating how much cash the family (or firm) must keep in their wallet to bridge the mismatch between *inflows* and *outflows* over the *Operating Cycle*.
> 
> If the family runs a grocery shop, then the **Operating Cycle** is: *Buy rice (Raw Material) → Cook meal (Work-in-Progress) → Store in tiffin (Finished Goods) → Sell to customer (Credit) → Collect cash (Receivables) → Pay rice supplier (Payables)*.

## 1.4 Need for Estimating Working Capital

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Ensures **liquidity** without over-investing in idle current assets.
> - Avoids **insolvency** and **production stoppages**.
> - Determines the correct mix of **short-term and long-term financing**.
> - Maximizes **shareholder wealth** by optimizing the *Current Assets-to-Sales* ratio.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Methods of Estimating Working Capital (KTU Board-Exam Favourites)

There are **four standard approaches** taught under KTU Module 4. The Operating Cycle Method carries the maximum weightage.

### Method 1: Percentage of Sales Method
The simplest method. It assumes that working capital maintains a stable historical percentage relationship with sales.

$$WC_{Estimated} = \text{Projected Sales} \times \frac{\text{Past WC}}{\text{Past Sales}}$$

### Method 2: Regression Analysis Method
A statistical method where Working Capital (Y) is regressed against Sales (X):

$$Y = a + bX$$

where $b$ is the marginal working capital requirement per unit of sales.

### Method 3: Cash Budget Method
A month-by-month forecast of cash inflows and outflows. Working Capital equals the **cumulative cash deficit** in the budget.

### Method 4: Operating Cycle Method (⭐ KTU Favourite — 14-mark question)

This is the most analytical method. It calculates the *time gap* between the firm's cash outflow for raw materials and cash inflow from receivables.

## 2.2 The Operating Cycle — Step-by-Step Logic

The Operating Cycle is the **time duration** during which a unit of cash flows through the production process until it re-enters as cash from the customer. The KTU formulation is:

$$\text{Operating Cycle (O.C.)} = R + W + D + C - P$$

where:
- $R$ = **Raw Material Storage Period** (days)
- $W$ = **Work-in-Progress Holding Period** (days)
- $D$ = **Finished Goods Storage Period** (days)
- $C$ = **Average Collection Period / Debtors Period** (days)
- $P$ = **Average Payment Period / Creditors Deferral Period** (days)

> [!NOTE]
> The **minus P** term is conceptually critical. By delaying payment to suppliers, the firm effectively *shortens* its working capital cycle. This is the only term in the formula that *reduces* the cycle.

## 2.3 Computing the Holding Periods (High-Yield Sub-Calculations)

| Item | Formula (Daily Cost Basis) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Raw Material Storage Period ($R$) | $\dfrac{\text{Average RM Stock}}{\text{RM Consumed per day}} = \dfrac{\text{Avg. RM Stock} \times 365}{\text{Annual RM Consumption}}$ |
| Work-in-Progress Holding ($W$) | $\dfrac{\text{Manufacturing Cost} \times W \text{ (days)}}{365}$ |
| Finished Goods Storage ($D$) | $\dfrac{\text{Avg. FG Stock} \times 365}{\text{Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)}}$ |
| Debtors Collection ($C$) | $\dfrac{\text{Avg. Debtors} \times 365}{\text{Credit Sales}}$ |
| Creditors Deferral ($P$) | $\dfrac{\text{Avg. Creditors} \times 365}{\text{Credit Purchases}}$ |

## 2.4 Final Working Capital Estimation Equation

$$\text{Net Working Capital} = \left( \frac{RM \text{ Cost}}{365} \times R \right) + \left( \frac{Mfg \text{ Cost}}{365} \times W \right) + \left( \frac{COGS}{365} \times D \right) + \left( \frac{Credit \text{ Sales}}{365} \times C \right) + \text{Cash Required} - \left( \frac{Credit \text{ Purchases}}{365} \times P \right)$$

## 2.5 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the only quick-reference needed for ESE Module 4 problems.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Gross Working Capital | $\Sigma (\text{All Current Assets})$ | ₹ |
| 2 | Net Working Capital | $CA - CL$ | ₹ |
| 3 | Operating Cycle (Days) | $R + W + D + C - P$ | Days |
| 4 | RM Investment in WC | $\dfrac{RM \text{ Cost}}{365} \times R$ | ₹ |
| 5 | WIP Investment in WC | $\dfrac{Mfg \text{ Cost}}{365} \times W$ | ₹ |
| 6 | FG Investment in WC | $\dfrac{COGS}{365} \times D$ | ₹ |
| 7 | Debtors Investment in WC | $\dfrac{Credit \text{ Sales}}{365} \times C$ | ₹ |
| 8 | Creditors (Reduction) | $\dfrac{Credit \text{ Purchases}}{365} \times P$ | ₹ |
| 9 | Bank Margin / Short-term Loan | $\dfrac{\text{Working Capital Gap} \times \text{Bank Margin \%}}{100}$ | ₹ |
| 10 | Owned (Promoter's) Contribution | $\text{WC Gap} - \text{Bank Borrowings}$ | ₹ |

> **Note on Tilde/Pipe:** In handwritten exam sheets, students often write absolute value bars for $CA - CL$ as $\vert CA - CL \vert$. The above LaTeX uses the standard algebraic subtraction $CA - CL$ since CA is always $\geq$ CL for a going concern.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Business

In engineering-led businesses (manufacturing, construction, project execution), the Operating Cycle determines **project cash flow statements**. For example, a civil contractor purchasing cement on 30-day credit, executing work for 90 days, retaining retention money for 60 days, and receiving 45-day credit from steel suppliers will compute a 135-day operating cycle. Failure to estimate this leads to project insolvency, exactly the *L&T vs. GMR* defaults seen in Indian infrastructure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Numerical Solved Example (Model KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Standard KTU Module 4 Style):**
> 
> A manufacturing firm provides you with the following estimates for the upcoming year:
> 
> - **Estimated Sales:** ₹ 24,00,000
> - **Cost of Goods Sold (COGS):** ₹ 18,00,000
> - **Raw Material Consumed:** ₹ 12,00,000
> - **Manufacturing Cost:** ₹ 15,00,000
> - **Total Purchases:** ₹ 14,00,000
> - **Credit Sales** = 75% of total sales
> - **Credit Purchases** = 80% of total purchases
> - **Operating Cycle Periods (given):**
>   - Raw Material Storage ($R$) = 45 days
>   - Work-in-Progress ($W$) = 30 days
>   - Finished Goods Storage ($D$) = 40 days
>   - Collection Period ($C$) = 60 days
>   - Payment Period ($P$) = 35 days
> - **Minimum Cash Balance to be maintained:** ₹ 60,000
> 
> **Required:** Estimate the Working Capital Requirement using the Operating Cycle Method. Also compute the Bank Borrowings allowed if the banker permits a margin of 25% on the working capital gap.

### Step 1 — Compute the Operating Cycle (Days)

$$\text{O.C.} = R + W + D + C - P$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\text{O.C.} = 45 + 30 + 40 + 60 - 35$$

$$\boxed{\text{O.C.} = 140 \text{ days}}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Each Component of Current Assets

**A. Raw Material (RM) Investment:**

Daily RM Consumption = $\dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{365} = ₹ 3{,}287.67$ per day

$$RM_{Investment} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{365} \times 45$$

$$RM_{Investment} = 3{,}287.67 \times 45$$

$$RM_{Investment} = ₹ 1{,}47{,}945$$

**B. Work-in-Progress (WIP) Investment:**

Daily Manufacturing Cost = $\dfrac{15{,}00{,}000}{365} = ₹ 4{,}109.59$ per day

$$WIP_{Investment} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{365} \times 30$$

$$WIP_{Investment} = 4{,}109.59 \times 30$$

$$WIP_{Investment} = ₹ 1{,}23{,}288$$

**C. Finished Goods (FG) Investment:**

Daily COGS = $\dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{365} = ₹ 4{,}931.51$ per day

$$FG_{Investment} = \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{365} \times 40$$

$$FG_{Investment} = 4{,}931.51 \times 40$$

$$FG_{Investment} = ₹ 1{,}97{,}260$$

**D. Debtors (Receivables) Investment:**

Credit Sales = 75% of ₹ 24,00,000 = ₹ 18,00,000

Daily Credit Sales = $\dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{365} = ₹ 4{,}931.51$ per day

$$Debtors_{Investment} = \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{365} \times 60$$

$$Debtors_{Investment} = 4{,}931.51 \times 60$$

$$Debtors_{Investment} = ₹ 2{,}95{,}890$$

**E. Cash Balance (as given):**

$$Cash_{Investment} = ₹ 60{,}000$$

**F. Total Current Assets ($CA$):**

$$CA = RM + WIP + FG + Debtors + Cash$$

$$CA = 1{,}47{,}945 + 1{,}23{,}288 + 1{,}97{,}260 + 2{,}95{,}890 + 60{,}000$$

$$\boxed{CA = ₹ 8{,}24{,}383}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Current Liabilities (Creditors)

Credit Purchases = 80% of ₹ 14,00,000 = ₹ 11,20,000

Daily Credit Purchases = $\dfrac{11{,}20{,}000}{365} = ₹ 3{,}068.49$ per day

$$Creditors = \frac{11{,}20{,}000}{365} \times 35$$

$$Creditors = 3{,}068.49 \times 35$$

$$\boxed{Creditors (CL) = ₹ 1{,}07{,}397}$$

### Step 4 — Net Working Capital

$$NWC = CA - CL$$

$$NWC = 8{,}24{,}383 - 1{,}07{,}397$$

$$\boxed{NWC = ₹ 7{,}16{,}986}$$

### Step 5 — Bank Borrowings (Tandon Committee / Nayak Committee Norms)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Banker's Margin Concept:** The bank does not fund 100% of the working capital gap. It deducts a "margin" (the firm's own contribution) and finances the balance.

$$\text{Bank Borrowings} = NWC \times \left( 1 - \frac{Margin}{100} \right)$$

$$\text{Bank Borrowings} = 7{,}16{,}986 \times \left( 1 - \frac{25}{100} \right)$$

$$\text{Bank Borrowings} = 7{,}16{,}986 \times 0.75$$

$$\boxed{\text{Bank Borrowings} = ₹ 5{,}37{,}740}$$

**Owner's Contribution (Margin Money):**

$$\text{Margin} = 7{,}16{,}986 \times 0.25 = ₹ 1{,}79{,}246$$

## 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Component | Daily Cost (₹) | Holding Days | Investment (₹) | Type |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Raw Material | 3,287.67 | 45 | 1,47,945 | Current Asset |
| Work-in-Progress | 4,109.59 | 30 | 1,23,288 | Current Asset |
| Finished Goods | 4,931.51 | 40 | 1,97,260 | Current Asset |
| Debtors | 4,931.51 | 60 | 2,95,890 | Current Asset |
| Cash | — | — | 60,000 | Current Asset |
| **Total CA** | — | — | **8,24,383** | — |
| Less: Creditors | 3,068.49 | 35 | (1,07,397) | Current Liability |
| **Net Working Capital** | — | — | **7,16,986** | — |
| Bank Borrowings (75%) | — | — | 5,37,740 | Short-term Loan |
| Margin Money (25%) | — | — | 1,79,246 | Owner's Equity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Operating Cycle Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following block-level architecture flow depicts how a unit of cash transforms through the firm's operational stages over the Operating Cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["CASH OUTFLOW: PAYMENT TO SUPPLIERS"] --> nodeB["RAW MATERIAL STORAGE PERIOD R DAYS"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["WORK IN PROGRESS HOLDING PERIOD W DAYS"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["FINISHED GOODS STORAGE PERIOD D DAYS"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["CREDIT SALES TO CUSTOMERS"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["DEBTORS COLLECTION PERIOD C DAYS"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["CASH INFLOW FROM CUSTOMERS"]
    nodeG --> nodeA

    nodeH["CREDIT FROM SUPPLIERS P DAYS"] -.-> nodeA
    nodeH -.->|"DELAYS CASH OUTFLOW"| nodeB

    style nodeA fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style nodeB fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style nodeC fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
    style nodeD fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
    style nodeE fill:#9467bd,color:#ffffff
    style nodeF fill:#8c564b,color:#ffffff
    style nodeG fill:#e377c2,color:#ffffff
    style nodeH fill:#7f7f7f,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
