topics.title: Outsourcing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Outsourcing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Outsourcing** is a strategic managerial and financial decision in which a firm contracts an external third-party vendor to perform activities, produce components, or deliver services that have traditionally been executed internally (in-house). From a **Financial Management** perspective, outsourcing is fundamentally treated as a **"Make-or-Buy Decision"** — a capital budgeting and cost-volume-profit problem where management must compare the **relevant costs of manufacturing** a product/component internally versus the **relevant costs of purchasing** it from an outside supplier.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Outsourcing (or Make-or-Buy Decision) is a managerial decision that determines whether a particular good or service should be produced within the organization (Make) or purchased from an external supplier (Buy). It is evaluated using the principle of **Relevant Costing** — considering only future costs that differ between the two alternatives.*

The key financial term here is **Relevant Cost**. These are the costs that:
- Will occur in the **future**, and
- **Differ** between the make and buy alternatives.

Relevant costs include:
- Variable manufacturing costs (direct material, direct labour, direct expenses, variable overheads).
- Avoidable fixed costs (specific fixed costs that disappear if production is stopped).
- Purchase price of the bought-out item.
- Opportunity cost of resources freed (if any).

**Irrelevant costs (sunk costs, allocated fixed overheads, book values)** are ignored.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Tiffin Lunch" Example**
> Imagine a college student (a firm) deciding whether to carry a home-cooked lunch to college (Make) or order a meal from a canteen outside (Buy/Outsource).
> - If she cooks, she spends on raw vegetables, gas, electricity, her own time, and cleaning the vessels. Some of these are *avoidable* if she decides not to cook tomorrow.
> - If she buys from the canteen, she simply pays the meal price. The canteen offers bulk discount if she orders daily.
> - She will compare only those costs that *change* — gas, vegetables, vessel depreciation, time cost — versus the canteen price. The cost of the lunchbox itself (already bought) is *sunk* and irrelevant.
> - The financial manager of a firm does exactly the same with products, components, and services.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Relevant Cost (RC):** Future cost that differs between alternatives.
- **Avoidable Fixed Cost (AFC):** Fixed cost that can be eliminated by outsourcing.
- **Unavoidable Fixed Cost (UFC):** Fixed cost that continues even if outsourced (e.g., rent of factory).
- **Opportunity Cost (OC):** Benefit foregone by using internal resources elsewhere.
- **Break-Even Volume (BEV):** The production quantity at which "Make" cost equals "Buy" cost.
- **Total Relevant Cost of Making (TRCM) = Variable Cost + Avoidable Fixed Cost + Opportunity Cost.**
- **Total Relevant Cost of Buying (TRCB) = Purchase Price + Additional Buying Costs.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Framework — The Make-or-Buy Decision

The outsourcing decision is evaluated in **five structured steps**:

1. **Step 1 — Identify the Component/Service:** Pinpoint the exact item, process, or service under consideration.
2. **Step 2 — Determine Relevant Costs of Making:** Sum up only those future costs that disappear if production stops — variable manufacturing cost + avoidable fixed cost + opportunity cost of freed capacity.
3. **Step 3 — Determine Relevant Costs of Buying:** Sum up the purchase price + freight + inspection + receiving costs − any savings on avoidable fixed costs.
4. **Step 4 — Compare and Decide:** If TRCM > TRCB → **Buy (Outsource)**. If TRCM < TRCB → **Make (In-house)**. If equal → Indifferent (consider qualitative factors).
5. **Step 5 — Qualitative Considerations:** Quality, reliability, secrecy, employee morale, supplier dependence, long-term strategic implications.

## 2.2 Cost Classification for Outsourcing Decisions

| Cost Category | Examples | Behaviour with Make | Behaviour with Buy | Relevant? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Variable Manufacturing Cost (VC) | Direct Material, Direct Labour, Direct Expenses, Variable OH | Incurred | Not incurred | ✅ Yes |
| Avoidable Fixed Cost (AFC) | Specific supervisor salary, special machine depreciation | Incurred | Saved | ✅ Yes |
| Unavoidable Fixed Cost (UFC) | Factory rent, common admin salaries | Incurred | Incurred (still paid) | ❌ No |
| Sunk Cost | Past R&D, historical design cost | Already incurred | Already incurred | ❌ No |
| Allocated Overhead | Share of corporate overhead | Allocated regardless | Allocated regardless | ❌ No |
| Purchase Price | Quoted price of supplier | Not incurred | Incurred | ✅ Yes |
| Additional Buying Cost | Freight, inspection, testing | Not incurred | Incurred | ✅ Yes |
| Opportunity Cost | Profit from released capacity | Foregone | Earned (if capacity used) | ✅ Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why & How" Matters in KTU Exams:**
> Examiners award marks not just for the final answer, but for the *classification logic*. Always show a *Cost Statement* separating relevant from irrelevant costs. This is a **5-mark scoring zone** in Part B questions.

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TRCM = VC + AFC + OC$ | Total Relevant Cost of Making |
| 2 | $TRCB = PP + ABC$ | Total Relevant Cost of Buying (Purchase Price + Additional Buying Cost) |
| 3 | $\text{Differential Cost} = TRCM - TRCB$ | Positive ⇒ Buy; Negative ⇒ Make |
| 4 | $BEV = \dfrac{AFC}{(PP - VC) \text{ per unit}}$ | Break-Even Volume — units where Make = Buy |
| 5 | $Q^* = \dfrac{AFC - (PP - VC) \times Q_0}{PP - VC}$ | Indifference Quantity (alternate form) |
| 6 | $\text{Profit} = \text{Sales} - \text{TRC}$ | Used in capacity-utilisation problems |
| 7 | $OC = \text{Contribution Lost} - \text{FC Saved}$ | Opportunity Cost via released capacity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM RULE:** Never use the pipe symbol `|` inside a table cell — it breaks the Markdown renderer. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. (Already followed above.)

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Outsourcing decisions are used in:
- **IT Services** (cloud computing, software development, BPO).
- **Manufacturing** (subcontracting of components like gears, panels, electronic chips).
- **Logistics** (third-party logistics — 3PL providers like DHL, FedEx).
- **HR Functions** (payroll, recruitment process outsourcing — RPO).
- **Finance & Accounting** (KPO — Knowledge Process Outsourcing).

In each case, the financial manager applies the **relevant costing framework** to ensure that outsourcing genuinely reduces cost — not just shifts it.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Break-Even Volume (BEV) Formula

The **break-even volume** in a make-or-buy decision is the production quantity at which the total cost of making equals the total cost of buying. Below is the rigorous derivation that KTU expects in 14-mark problems.

### Let:
- $VC$ = Variable cost per unit to make
- $AFC$ = Total avoidable fixed cost (per period)
- $PP$ = Purchase price per unit (from supplier)
- $Q$ = Annual quantity (units)
- $TRCM$ = Total Relevant Cost of Making
- $TRCB$ = Total Relevant Cost of Buying

### Derivation:

Step 1 — Express TRCM as a linear function of quantity:

$$TRCM(Q) = (VC \times Q) + AFC$$

Step 2 — Express TRCB as a linear function of quantity:

$$TRCB(Q) = (PP \times Q)$$

Step 3 — At break-even, $TRCM(Q) = TRCB(Q)$:

$$(VC \times Q_{BE}) + AFC = (PP \times Q_{BE})$$

Step 4 — Collect Q-terms on one side:

$$AFC = (PP \times Q_{BE}) - (VC \times Q_{BE})$$

Step 5 — Factor out $Q_{BE}$:

$$AFC = Q_{BE} \times (PP - VC)$$

Step 6 — Solve for $Q_{BE}$:

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{AFC}{PP - VC}$$

This is the **Break-Even Volume Formula** in outsourcing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If annual demand $Q > Q_{BE}$, the **Make** option is cheaper. If $Q < Q_{BE}$, the **Buy** option is cheaper. This is a direct analogue of BEP in CVP analysis.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

**Problem Statement:**

> *XYZ Ltd. currently manufactures a component at a variable cost of ₹60 per unit. Annual demand is 20,000 units. Fixed manufacturing cost allocated to the component is ₹5,00,000 per year, of which ₹2,00,000 is avoidable if outsourced. A supplier offers to supply the component at ₹80 per unit.*
> *Should XYZ outsource? What is the break-even volume?*

### Solution — Cost Statement Method:

| Cost Element | Make (₹) | Buy (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Variable Cost (20,000 × 60) | 12,00,000 | 0 |
| Avoidable Fixed Cost | 2,00,000 | 0 |
| Purchase Cost (20,000 × 80) | 0 | 16,00,000 |
| **Total Relevant Cost** | **14,00,000** | **16,00,000** |

**Decision:** Since TRCM (₹14,00,000) < TRCB (₹16,00,000), **XYZ should Make** (continue in-house production). Savings = ₹2,00,000.

### Break-Even Volume:

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{AFC}{PP - VC} = \frac{2,00,000}{80 - 60} = \frac{2,00,000}{20} = 10,000 \text{ units}$$

Since demand (20,000) > BEV (10,000), the **Make** decision is confirmed.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Qualitative Factors

| Qualitative Factor | Favours Make | Favours Buy |
|---|---|---|
| Confidentiality of process / technology | ✅ | |
| Quality control & assurance | ✅ | |
| Supplier reliability & capacity | | ✅ |
| Long-term strategic independence | ✅ | |
| Cost of monitoring supplier | | ✅ |
| Employee morale / layoff concerns | ✅ | |
| Flexibility to scale production | | ✅ |
| Core competency of the firm | ✅ (if core) | ✅ (if non-core) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When a numerical problem is silent on qualitative issues, write 2-3 lines saying "Qualitative factors should also be considered before a final decision is made." This fetches **2-3 bonus marks**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Outsourcing Decision Flowchart

The following Mermaid diagram captures the complete decision-tree for a Make-or-Buy analysis as prescribed in the KTU 2024 scheme.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Identify Component or Service] --> B[List All Costs of Make]
    B --> C[List All Costs of Buy]
    C --> D{Classify Each Cost}
    D --> E[Relevant Costs]
    D --> F[Irrelevant Costs Ignore]
    E --> G[Compute TRCM and TRCB]
    G --> H{TRCM vs TRCB}
    H --> I[TRCM greater than TRCB]
    H --> J[TRCM less than TRCB]
    H --> K[TRCM equals TRCB]
    I --> L[BUY Outsource]
    J --> M[MAKE In House]
    K --> N[Indifferent Zone]
    L --> O[Apply Qualitative Factors]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Final Strategic Decision]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Outsourcing Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Data Input Layer]
        A1[Annual Demand Q]
        A2[Variable Cost per unit VC]
        A3[Avoidable Fixed Cost AFC]
        A4[Purchase Price PP]
    end

    subgraph Compute[Computation Engine]
        B1[TRCM equal VC times Q plus AFC]
        B2[TRCB equal PP times Q]
        B3[Differential Cost equal TRCM minus TRCB]
        B4[Break Even Volume equal AFC divided by PP minus VC]
    end

    subgraph Output[Decision Output Layer]
        C1{Differential Sign}
        C2[Positive implies Buy]
        C3[Negative implies Make]
        C4[Qualitative Overlay Layer]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A4 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    A3 --> B4
    A4 --> B4
    A2 --> B4
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C4 --> C2
    C4 --> C3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Architecture?**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises *system-level thinking*. The above block diagram shows how inputs (cost data) flow through the computation engine (relevant cost analysis) into a final output (decision). This is the preferred diagram style in KTU board exams for management topics.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define "Outsourcing" in the context of Financial Management. State any two advantages of outsourcing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Outsourcing is a strategic financial decision in which a firm contracts an external vendor to produce a component or deliver a service that could otherwise be produced in-house. It is essentially a **Make-or-Buy** decision evaluated using **Relevant Costing**.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Cost Reduction:** Outsourcing converts fixed costs into variable costs, providing flexibility.
2. **Focus on Core Competency:** Management can concentrate on strategic, high-value activities while non-core work is delegated to specialists.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark each for two advantages = 3 marks)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is "Relevant Cost"? Why are sunk costs considered irrelevant in an outsourcing decision?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **relevant cost** is a future cost that differs between the available alternatives. Sunk costs are costs already incurred and unrecoverable; they remain the same whether the firm makes or buys, and therefore **do not influence** the outsourcing decision. Only future avoidable costs matter.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark explanation of relevant cost + 1 mark on sunk cost = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Statement:**
*ABM Industries manufactures a sub-component 'X' at a variable cost of ₹45 per unit. The annual demand is 30,000 units. The fixed manufacturing cost allocated is ₹6,00,000, out of which ₹3,50,000 is specifically avoidable if outsourced. A vendor offers to supply the same at ₹70 per unit, with an additional freight cost of ₹5 per unit. ABM can also rent out its freed production capacity for ₹2,50,000 per year.*

*Required:*
- *(a) Prepare a Comparative Cost Statement and advise whether to make or buy.* **(7 marks)**
- *(b) Calculate the break-even volume of production at which the firm would be indifferent between making and buying.* **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (A):**

**Part (a) — Comparative Cost Statement**

| Cost Element | Make (₹) | Buy (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Variable Cost (30,000 × ₹45) | 13,50,000 | 0 |
| Avoidable Fixed Cost | 3,50,000 | 0 |
| Opportunity Cost (rent lost) | 2,50,000 | 0 |
| Purchase Cost (30,000 × ₹70) | 0 | 21,00,000 |
| Freight Cost (30,000 × ₹5) | 0 | 1,50,000 |
| Rent Earned from Freed Capacity | 0 | (2,50,000) |
| **Total Relevant Cost** | **19,50,000** | **20,00,000** |

**Decision:** Since TRCM (₹19,50,000) < TRCB (₹20,00,000), ABM should **MAKE in-house** and save ₹50,000 per year.

**Part (b) — Break-Even Volume Calculation**

The opportunity cost of ₹2,50,000 is treated as an additional avoidable cost for the Make alternative (since it is foregone by making). Adjusted AFC = ₹3,50,000 + ₹2,50,000 = ₹6,00,000.

Effective variable cost of buying = ₹70 + ₹5 = ₹75 per unit.

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{AFC_{adjusted}}{PP_{effective} - VC_{make}} = \frac{6,00,000}{75 - 45} = \frac{6,00,000}{30} = 20,000 \text{ units}$$

Since demand (30,000 units) > BEV (20,000 units), the **Make** decision is reconfirmed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> A common error students make is *forgetting to include the opportunity cost of lost rent* on the Make side, or *failing to deduct the rent earned* on the Buy side. Both treatments yield the same result, but only one consistent method should be applied. Marks are deducted for **inconsistent treatment**. Also, never include allocated fixed costs (the remaining ₹2,50,000 out of ₹6,00,000) — they are *unavoidable and irrelevant*.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Statement:**
*Premier Pvt. Ltd. uses 10,000 units of a chemical annually. The company can manufacture it in-house at ₹30 per unit (variable) plus ₹2,00,000 fixed cost which is fully avoidable if outsourced. An external supplier quotes ₹55 per unit. Additionally, if outsourced, the company can deploy its released labour to earn an additional contribution of ₹80,000 per year.*

*Required:*
- *(a) Should the company make or buy? Show calculations.* **(7 marks)**
- *(b) If the demand is expected to fall to 6,000 units, what would be your decision? Support with the indifference point.* **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (B):**

**Part (a) — Make or Buy Decision (at 10,000 units)**

| Cost Element | Make (₹) | Buy (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Variable Cost (10,000 × ₹30) | 3,00,000 | 0 |
| Avoidable Fixed Cost | 2,00,000 | 0 |
| Opportunity Cost (lost contribution) | 80,000 | 0 |
| Purchase Cost (10,000 × ₹55) | 0 | 5,50,000 |
| Additional Contribution Earned | 0 | (80,000) |
| **Total Relevant Cost** | **5,80,000** | **4,70,000** |

**Decision:** TRCB (₹4,70,000) < TRCM (₹5,80,000), so the company should **BUY** (Outsource) and save ₹1,10,000 per year.

**Part (b) — Decision at 6,000 units + Indifference Point**

*Step 1:* Compute Total Cost at 6,000 units:

- Make: $(6,000 \times 30) + 2,00,000 + 80,000 = 1,80,000 + 2,80,000 = ₹4,60,000$
- Buy: $(6,000 \times 55) - 80,000 = 3,30,000 - 80,000 = ₹2,50,000$

At 6,000 units, **Buy** remains cheaper (saves ₹2,10,000).

*Step 2:* Compute Indifference Point. Adjusted AFC = ₹2,00,000 + ₹80,000 = ₹2,80,000.

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{2,80,000}{55 - 30} = \frac{2,80,000}{25} = 11,200 \text{ units}$$

Since demand (6,000 or 10,000) is **below 11,200 units**, the **Buy** decision is justified at both demand levels.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Students often forget to **add opportunity cost to the Make side** and **subtract it from the Buy side**. This creates asymmetry and is a **3-mark deduction zone**.
> 2. For the indifference point, students sometimes use only the avoidable fixed cost and ignore the opportunity cost. The opportunity cost must be added to the numerator.
> 3. The question may not ask for break-even — read the wording carefully. Here, it explicitly asks for the indifference point, which is the same as break-even volume.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Outsourcing = Make-or-Buy Decision** — always frame it this way in exams.
- ✅ Use the **Relevant Costing** principle — only future, differing costs.
- ✅ Build a **Comparative Cost Statement** with two columns (Make vs Buy).
- ✅ Always classify fixed costs into **Avoidable (relevant)** and **Unavoidable (irrelevant)**.
- ✅ **Sunk costs and allocated overheads are ALWAYS irrelevant** — never include them.
- ✅ **Opportunity cost** is added to the Make side (or subtracted from the Buy side — be consistent).
- ✅ **Break-Even / Indifference Volume** = $\dfrac{AFC_{total}}{PP - VC}$. Demand > BEV → Make; Demand < BEV → Buy.
- ✅ Always end with a **qualitative paragraph** (2-3 lines) covering quality, secrecy, supplier reliability, employee morale.
- ✅ In multi-period problems, do not forget to consider **escalation clauses, freight, inspection, and import duties** as additional buying costs.
- ✅ KTU examiners award **1-2 bonus marks** for a clean cost table and a clear final recommendation sentence.
- ✅ Avoid writing "depends" as the final answer — commit to a recommendation with justification.
- ✅ The opportunity cost of freed capacity is the **most commonly missed item** in KTU answer sheets — practice this concept thoroughly.

---

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 4: OUTSOURCING (PECBT631 — FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT, KTU 2024 SCHEME)**
