<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question 1]**
> **Q:** *Define the "5 C's of Credit" and explain their role in setting credit standards.*
> **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
The **5 C's of Credit** are the qualitative framework used by firms to evaluate a borrower's creditworthiness:
1. **Character** — The customer's willingness to pay (judged from past payment history and references).
2. **Capacity** — The customer's ability to pay from operating cash flows (judged from financial statements and ratios).
3. **Capital** — The customer's financial strength and own equity stake in the business.
4. **Collateral** — The security / asset backing offered by the customer against default.
5. **Conditions** — The macroeconomic and industry environment affecting repayment.

**Role in Credit Standards:** Together, the 5 C's form the **filter** that determines who qualifies for credit. A customer scoring well on all 5 C's is granted standard credit terms; weak on 1-2 → reduced credit limit or collateral demand; weak on 3+ → rejection.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question 2]**
> **Q:** *Distinguish between Credit Policy and Collection Policy. Why are they complementary?*
> **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Credit Policy | Collection Policy |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Rules for *granting* credit | Rules for *recovering* credit |
| **Stage** | Pre-sale decision | Post-sale action |
| **Focus** | Customer selection, terms | Overdue follow-up |
| **Tool** | 5 C's, credit scoring | Reminders, legal action |

**Complementarity:** A lenient **credit policy** creates more AR and consequently more *delinquent* accounts. Without an aggressive **collection policy**, those delinquencies snowball into bad debts. Conversely, an over-aggressive collection policy damages customer relationships, undermining the sales objective of the credit policy. The two must be **balanced** to optimise firm value.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** A firm currently sells on terms *net 30*. It is considering a switch to *2/10, net 30*. Variable cost ratio is 70%, and the cost of capital is 14% p.a. Currently, **60%** of customers pay on day 30, and **40%** pay on day 40. Under the proposed terms, **30%** will pay on day 10 (avail discount), **50%** on day 30, and **20%** on day 40. Current credit sales = **₹ 600 lakhs p.a.**

**Compute the net benefit (or cost) of the proposed policy switch.** **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **Aging Schedule** as a receivables monitoring tool. Construct an aging schedule for the following data and comment on the collection efficiency. Credit terms = *net 45*. **[7 Marks]**

| Age (days) | Amount (₹ lakhs) |
|---|---|
| 0 – 30 | 18 |
| 31 – 45 | 10 |
| 46 – 60 | 6 |
| 61 – 90 | 4 |
| 90 + | 2 |

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (a)**

**Step 1:** Compute current ACP (weighted by customer mix).

$$\text{ACP}_{\text{old}} = (0.60 \times 30) + (0.40 \times 40) = 18 + 16 = 34 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2:** Compute proposed ACP.

$$\text{ACP}_{\text{new}} = (0.30 \times 10) + (0.50 \times 30) + (0.20 \times 40) = 3 + 15 + 8 = 26 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3:** Reduction in ACP.

$$\Delta \text{ACP} = 34 - 26 = 8 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4:** Reduction in AR investment.

$$\Delta I_{AR} = \frac{\Delta \text{ACP}}{360} \times \text{Credit Sales} = \frac{8}{360} \times 600 = \text{₹ 13.33 lakhs}$$

**Step 5:** Cost of capital saved.

$$\Delta K_c = 13.33 \times 0.70 \times 0.14 = \text{₹ 1.307 lakhs}$$

**Step 6:** Cost of cash discount.

$$D_c = 0.30 \times 0.02 \times 600 = \text{₹ 3.60 lakhs}$$

**Step 7:** Net benefit.

$$\text{Net Benefit} = 1.307 - 3.60 = \text{−₹ 2.293 lakhs}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verdict:** Net Benefit is **negative** (₹ 2.29 lakhs loss). The cash discount cost outweighs the carrying cost saving. The firm should **retain net 30** terms.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (b)**

**Aging Schedule with Midpoints:**

| Bucket | Midpoint (days) | Amount (₹ lakhs) | Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 – 30 | 15 | 18 | 270 |
| 31 – 45 | 38 | 10 | 380 |
| 46 – 60 | 53 | 6 | 318 |
| 61 – 90 | 75 | 4 | 300 |
| 90 + | 105 | 2 | 210 |
| **Total** | — | **40** | **1,478** |

**Computed ACP:**

$$\text{ACP} = \frac{1478}{40} = 36.95 \approx 37 \text{ days}$$

**Comparison with credit period of 45 days:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** Overall ACP (37 days) is below the credit period (45 days), so the collection efficiency is **acceptable on average**. However, **₹ 6 lakhs (15%)** of receivables are 60+ days overdue, and **₹ 2 lakhs (5%)** are 90+ days — these need **immediate escalation** through the collection policy. The firm should initiate **legal notices** for the 90+ bucket and **personal visits** for the 60–90 bucket.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Define **Average Collection Period (ACP)**. The following data is extracted from the books of a firm:

- Credit Sales = ₹ 720 lakhs
- Opening AR = ₹ 80 lakhs
- Closing AR = ₹ 100 lakhs
- Bad Debts written off = ₹ 12 lakhs

Compute the **ACP, AR Turnover, and the Bad Debt Loss Rate**. Comment on each. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** What are the **costs of maintaining receivables**? Explain any three with formulas. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (a)**

**Step 1:** Average AR.

$$\text{Avg. AR} = \frac{80 + 100}{2} = \text{₹ 90 lakhs}$$

**Step 2:** AR Turnover.

$$\text{ART} = \frac{720}{90} = 8 \text{ times p.a.}$$

**Step 3:** Average Collection Period.

$$\text{ACP} = \frac{360}{8} = 45 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4:** Bad Debt Loss Rate.

$$\text{BDLR} = \frac{12}{720} \times 100 = 1.67\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comments:**
> - **ACP of 45 days** is on the higher side; industry benchmark ~30 days. **Tighten** collection efforts.
> - **AR Turnover of 8×** indicates receivables are turned over ~1.5 months — moderate.
> - **BDLR of 1.67%** is high; healthy firms target ≤ 1%. **Tighten** credit standards.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (b)**

**Three Costs of Maintaining Receivables:**

> **1. Opportunity Cost of Capital Locked in AR**

$$K_c = I_{AR} \times v \times k$$
Where $I_{AR} = \dfrac{\text{ACP}}{360} \times \text{Credit Sales}$.

> **2. Administrative / Collection Cost**
> Includes salaries of credit & collection staff, stationery, postage, telephone, legal fees, and the cost of credit investigation. Empirical rule: **0.5% to 2%** of credit sales.

> **3. Bad Debt Loss**
> The unrecoverable portion of credit sales:

$$\text{BD Loss} = \text{Credit Sales} \times \text{Bad Debt Rate}$$

> *(Other valid costs include Delinquency Cost — the cost of capital locked in *overdue* (but not yet written-off) receivables, and the **Default Probability Premium**.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Using full sales instead of credit sales** in ACP formula — Examiner deducts full 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the VC ratio** multiplier when computing carrying cost — the cost is on *variable cost locked*, not on gross receivables.
> 3. **In the forgone discount formula**, students often write $\dfrac{d}{N - d_p}$ instead of $\dfrac{d}{1-d} \times \dfrac{360}{N - d_p}$. The $1-d$ denominator is *mandatory*.
> 4. **Aging schedule without midpoints** — examiner deducts 0.5 marks; always use the bucket midpoint.
> 5. **Net benefit negative** → students wrongly conclude "adopt the policy". The correct rule is: adopt only if **Net Benefit > 0**.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Credit & Collection Policies)**

- [x] **AR Management** is the deliberate balancing of profitability vs. liquidity risk in trade credit.
- [x] **Credit Policy** has three components: *Credit Standards, Credit Terms, Collection Efforts*.
- [x] **5 C's of Credit** = Character, Capacity, Capital, Collateral, Conditions.
- [x] **Credit Terms Notation** = "d / d_p, net N" — discount %, discount period, full credit period.
- [x] **ACP** = (AR / Credit Sales) × 360.
- [x] **Cost of Forgone Discount** = $\left( \dfrac{d}{1 - d} \right) \times \left( \dfrac{360}{N - d_p} \right) \times 100$.
- [x] **Carrying Cost of AR** = AR × VC ratio × Cost of Capital.
- [x] **Optimal Credit Policy** = Point where MBC = MCC (or Net Incremental Benefit is maximised).
- [x] **Net Incremental Benefit** = ΔContribution − ΔBad Debt − ΔCarrying Cost.
- [x] **Aging Schedule** classifies receivables by age — use *midpoints* to compute ACP.
- [x] **Collection Policy** escalates: reminder → phone → visit → legal notice → litigation → write-off.
- [x] **BDLR** = (Bad Debts / Credit Sales) × 100. Healthy benchmark: ≤ 1%.
- [x] **Decision Rule:** Accept a credit switch only if **Net Benefit > 0**; reject otherwise.
- [x] **Tighten** standards if ACP > Credit Period OR if BDLR > industry norm.
- [x] **Cash discount** is justified only if the *saving on carrying cost* exceeds the *cost of the discount*.
- [x] **Variable cost ratio (v)** is critical — fixed costs are sunk in short-run receivables decision.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Credit and Collection Policies**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analyzing the Credit Applicant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analyzing the Credit Applicant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Credit Applicant Analysis** is a systematic, multi-dimensional evaluation process undertaken by a lender (commercial bank, financial institution, or trade creditor) to assess the **creditworthiness**, **repayment ability**, and **default risk** of a prospective borrower before the sanction, renewal, or revision of a credit facility. It is the financial equivalent of a "due diligence audit" applied to a loan request.

Under the **NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework of KTU, this topic maps directly to:
- **CO 4 (Module 4):** *Evaluate the credit risk of applicants using quantitative and qualitative credit scoring frameworks.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Memorize This):**
> *"Credit applicant analysis is the structured process of gathering, verifying, and interpreting financial and non-financial information about a prospective borrower to determine the probability of default and to decide upon the appropriate terms of credit, including the credit limit, interest rate, security, and repayment schedule."*

### 1.2 The Five C's of Credit — The Core Framework

The international standard for analyzing any credit applicant is encapsulated in the **"Five C's of Credit"**:

| C | Dimension | What It Measures |
|---|-----------|------------------|
| **Character** | Integrity & Willingness | The borrower's reputation, honesty, and intent to repay |
| **Capacity** | Ability to Repay | Cash flow generation power relative to existing obligations |
| **Capital** | Financial Strength | Net worth, owner's equity, and personal financial stake |
| **Collateral** | Security | Assets pledged by the borrower as a secondary repayment source |
| **Conditions** | External Environment | Industry outlook, macroeconomic factors, purpose of loan |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic (Exam Tip):** Think **"C-Cubed-CC"** → *Character-Capacity-Capital* (the borrower's side) and *Collateral-Conditions* (the lender's side and external side). In KTU answers, always present the 5 C's in a **structured bullet format** for easy mark allocation.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are lending **₹50,000** to a friend. Before giving the money, you would instinctively ask:
1. *"Is this person trustworthy and known to repay?"* → **Character**
2. *"Does this person earn enough monthly to return ₹5,000?"* → **Capacity**
3. *"Does this person have savings or assets to fall back on?"* → **Capital**
4. *"Can I hold their bike as security?"* → **Collateral**
5. *"Is the reason for the loan (medical emergency vs. gambling) reasonable?"* → **Conditions**

A bank does **exactly the same**, but using **financial statements, ratios, credit scores, and structured models** instead of intuition. That entire process is **Analyzing the Credit Applicant**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why This Topic Matters in KTU Exams:**
> A 14-mark question in Module 4 almost always combines **qualitative (5 C's)** with **quantitative (Altman's Z-Score or Numerical Credit Scoring System)**. Students who only write theory lose up to **5–7 marks** because they skip the numerical model.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of the Five C's of Credit

#### 2.1.1 Character (The "Will" to Repay)
- **Definition:** The borrower's reputation, integrity, and track record in honoring financial obligations.
- **How to Assess:**
  - Credit history with existing lenders (CIBIL, Experian, Equifax in India)
  - Past defaults, lawsuits, bounced cheques
  - Reputation in trade circles
  - Quality of management and corporate governance
- **Why It Matters:** A borrower with strong capacity but weak character is a **high-risk credit** because they may *choose* to default even when they *can* repay.

#### 2.1.2 Capacity (The "Power" to Repay)
- **Definition:** The borrower's ability to generate sufficient cash flows to service the proposed debt along with existing obligations.
- **How to Assess (Quantitative):**
  - **Debt-Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR):**
    $$\text{DSCR} = \frac{\text{Net Operating Income}}{\text{Total Debt Service (Principal + Interest)}}$$
  - A DSCR **greater than 1.5** is generally considered acceptable; below **1.0** indicates inability to service debt.
  - **Interest Coverage Ratio (ICR):**
    $$\text{ICR} = \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Interest Expense}}$$
- **KTU Rule of Thumb:** A lender typically prefers DSCR ≥ **1.5** and ICR ≥ **3.0** for industrial borrowers.

#### 2.1.3 Capital (The Borrower's "Skin in the Game")
- **Definition:** The borrower's own financial stake in the venture, measured by net worth and equity contribution.
- **How to Assess:**
  - **Debt-to-Equity Ratio (D/E):**
    $$\text{D/E Ratio} = \frac{\text{Total Debt}}{\text{Total Equity}}$$
  - Higher equity contribution → Lower financial risk → Higher creditworthiness.
  - **Promoter's Contribution** in project finance (usually **25%–40%** of project cost in India).

#### 2.1.4 Collateral (The "Safety Net")
- **Definition:** Specific assets pledged by the borrower that the lender can seize and liquidate in case of default.
- **Types:**
  - **Primary Security:** Asset financed (e.g., the machinery or vehicle)
  - **Collateral Security:** Additional assets (land, building, marketable securities)
- **Concept:** **Loan-to-Value (LTV) Ratio**
  $$\text{LTV} = \frac{\text{Loan Amount}}{\text{Value of Collateral}} \times 100$$
- A lower LTV means a larger safety cushion for the lender.

#### 2.1.5 Conditions (The "External Context")
- **Definition:** External economic, industry, and regulatory conditions affecting the borrower's business.
- **Examples:**
  - Industry cyclicality, demand outlook
  - Government policy (tax holidays, subsidies)
  - Competition and barriers to entry
  - Interest rate environment

### 2.2 Quantitative Models for Credit Applicant Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Numerical Credit Scoring System

In a numerical credit scoring system, the analyst assigns **weights** to various financial and non-financial factors. The total score determines whether credit is granted.

**Procedure:**
1. Identify key factors (e.g., profitability, leverage, liquidity, character).
2. Assign a weight to each factor based on importance (total = **100**).
3. Score each factor on a scale (typically **0 to 10**).
4. Compute **Weighted Score = Weight × Score** for each factor.
5. Sum the weighted scores → **Total Credit Score**.
6. Compare with cutoff (e.g., **≥ 6.0 = Approve**, **< 4.0 = Reject**, between = Review).

#### 2.2.2 Altman's Z-Score Model (Bankruptcy Prediction)

Developed by **Edward I. Altman** in **1968** as a multivariate model to predict corporate bankruptcy. It is one of the most heavily tested quantitative models in KTU Financial Management exams.

**The Z-Score Equation (For Public Manufacturing Firms):**

$$Z = 1.2 X_1 + 1.4 X_2 + 3.3 X_3 + 0.6 X_4 + 0.999 X_5$$

Where each ratio is defined in the table below.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Symbol | Variable | Formula | Meaning | Weight |
|---|--------|----------|---------|---------|--------|
| 1 | $X_1$ | Working Capital / Total Assets | $\dfrac{WC}{TA}$ | Short-term liquidity strength | **1.2** |
| 2 | $X_2$ | Retained Earnings / Total Assets | $\dfrac{RE}{TA}$ | Cumulative profitability & age | **1.4** |
| 3 | $X_3$ | EBIT / Total Assets | $\dfrac{EBIT}{TA}$ | Operating efficiency | **3.3** (highest) |
| 4 | $X_4$ | Market Value of Equity / Book Value of Total Liabilities | $\dfrac{MVE}{TL}$ | Market's view of solvency | **0.6** |
| 5 | $X_5$ | Sales / Total Assets | $\dfrac{Sales}{TA}$ | Asset turnover efficiency | **0.999** |

**Z-Score Interpretation Zones:**

| Zone | Z-Score Range | Interpretation | Lender's Action |
|------|---------------|----------------|------------------|
| **Safe Zone** | $Z > 2.99$ | Financially healthy, low default risk | Approve credit |
| **Grey Zone** | $1.81 < Z < 2.99$ | Caution; moderate risk | Detailed review needed |
| **Distress Zone** | $Z < 1.81$ | High probability of bankruptcy | Reject / Demand extra security |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Hint:** The **Z-Score for Private Firms** uses a slightly different formula:
> $$Z' = 0.717 X_1 + 0.847 X_2 + 3.107 X_3 + 0.420 X_4 + 0.998 X_5$$
> with **Safe Zone: Z' > 2.90**. However, KTU questions 90% of the time use the **original public-firm model**. If a problem doesn't specify, default to the **original Altman formula**.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Management Fields

- **Banks & NBFCs:** Use Z-Score variants as inputs into automated loan-approval engines.
- **Supply Chain Finance:** Trade creditors use the 5 C's to set buyer credit limits.
- **Credit Rating Agencies (CRISIL, ICRA, Moody's):** Build their internal scoring on similar multivariate models.
- **Project Finance:** Capacity (DSCR) and Capital (D/E) are the two most heavily weighted factors in term-loan sanction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Numerical Credit Scoring — Complete Worked Example

**Problem (KTU Style):**
A commercial bank uses the following **weighted credit scoring system** to evaluate loan applicants. A company applies for a working capital loan. Compute the total credit score and recommend whether to approve, reject, or review the application.

| Factor | Weight | Applicant's Score (out of 10) |
|--------|--------|-------------------------------|
| Profitability (ROCE) | **30** | 7 |
| Leverage (D/E Ratio) | **20** | 6 |
| Liquidity (Current Ratio) | **15** | 8 |
| Credit History | **20** | 9 |
| Management Quality | **15** | 7 |
| **Total** | **100** | — |

**Cutoff Rule:** Score ≥ 6.5 → **Approve**; Score between 4.5 and 6.5 → **Review**; Score < 4.5 → **Reject**.

#### Step 1: Compute Weighted Score for Each Factor

$$\text{Weighted Score}_i = \text{Weight}_i \times \text{Raw Score}_i$$

- Profitability: $30 \times 7 = 210$
- Leverage: $20 \times 6 = 120$
- Liquidity: $15 \times 8 = 120$
- Credit History: $20 \times 9 = 180$
- Management Quality: $15 \times 7 = 105$

#### Step 2: Sum Total Weighted Score

$$\text{Total} = 210 + 120 + 120 + 180 + 105 = 735$$

#### Step 3: Compute the Final Credit Score (Out of 10)

$$\text{Credit Score} = \frac{\text{Total Weighted Score}}{\text{Total Weight}} = \frac{735}{100} = 7.35$$

#### Step 4: Decision

Since $7.35 > 6.5$, the application **falls in the Approval Zone**.

> **Recommendation:** Approve the working capital loan. The strongest dimension is **Credit History (9/10)** and weakest is **Leverage (6/10)** — suggest the bank add a covenant requiring debt reduction within 18 months.

### 3.2 Altman's Z-Score — Complete Worked Example

**Problem (KTU Style, 14-Mark Format):**
From the following data of **M/s Kerala Engineering Ltd.**, compute the **Altman Z-Score** and comment on the creditworthiness of the company.

| Item | Amount (₹ in Lakhs) |
|------|---------------------|
| Working Capital | 200 |
| Total Assets | 1,000 |
| Retained Earnings | 300 |
| EBIT | 180 |
| Market Value of Equity | 800 |
| Book Value of Total Liabilities | 1,200 |
| Sales | 1,500 |

#### Step 1: Compute the Five Ratios ($X_1$ to $X_5$)

$$
X_1 = \frac{\text{Working Capital}}{\text{Total Assets}} = \frac{200}{1000} = 0.20
$$

$$
X_2 = \frac{\text{Retained Earnings}}{\text{Total Assets}} = \frac{300}{1000} = 0.30
$$

$$
X_3 = \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Total Assets}} = \frac{180}{1000} = 0.18
$$

$$
X_4 = \frac{\text{Market Value of Equity}}{\text{Book Value of Total Liabilities}} = \frac{800}{1200} = 0.6667
$$

$$
X_5 = \frac{\text{Sales}}{\text{Total Assets}} = \frac{1500}{1000} = 1.50
$$

#### Step 2: Apply the Altman Z-Score Equation

$$
Z = 1.2(0.20) + 1.4(0.30) + 3.3(0.18) + 0.6(0.6667) + 0.999(1.50)
$$

**Step-by-step expansion of each term:**

$$
1.2 \times 0.20 = 0.2400
$$

$$
1.4 \times 0.30 = 0.4200
$$

$$
3.3 \times 0.18 = 0.5940
$$

$$
0.6 \times 0.6667 = 0.4000 \;\;(\text{rounded})
$$

$$
0.999 \times 1.50 = 1.4985
$$

**Final summation:**

$$
Z = 0.2400 + 0.4200 + 0.5940 + 0.4000 + 1.4985
$$

$$
Z = 3.1525
$$

#### Step 3: Interpretation

$$
Z = 3.1525 > 2.99 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{SAFE ZONE}
$$

> **Conclusion:** M/s Kerala Engineering Ltd. is in the **Safe Zone** with a low probability of bankruptcy. **Credit may be approved** at standard terms.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Z-Score Sensitivity Analysis

The following table shows how each $X$ ratio individually influences the Z-Score using the data above. This is a high-yield KTU exam technique to display **analytical depth**.

| If $X_i$ Changes By | Impact on Z | Reasoning |
|---------------------|-------------|-----------|
| $X_1$ ↑ 0.1 | Z ↑ **0.12** | Working capital strengthens liquidity cushion |
| $X_2$ ↑ 0.1 | Z ↑ **0.14** | More retained earnings → financial maturity |
| $X_3$ ↑ 0.1 | Z ↑ **0.33** | Operating efficiency has the **highest weight** (3.3) |
| $X_4$ ↑ 0.1 | Z ↑ **0.06** | Market confidence (lowest weight) |
| $X_5$ ↑ 0.1 | Z ↑ **0.0999** | Asset turnover efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategic Insight for KTU Answers:** The variable **$X_3$ (EBIT/TA)** carries the highest weight (**3.3**) in the model. Therefore, **improving operating efficiency is the single most powerful lever** to raise the Z-Score — a fact often asked in KTU viva or as a 2-mark Part A question.

### 3.4 Comparison: 5 C's vs. Numerical Models

| Aspect | Qualitative (5 C's) | Quantitative (Z-Score / Scoring) |
|--------|---------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Data Type** | Subjective, judgment-based | Objective, ratio-based |
| **Best Used For** | Initial screening, relationship banking | Final approval, large corporates |
| **Weakness** | Bias, inconsistency | Ignores qualitative factors |
| **Strength** | Captures character & management | Reproducible, defensible |
| **KTU Exam Tip** | Write in bullet/tabular form | Always show full calculation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow — Steps in Credit Applicant Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Loan Application Received] --> B[Gather Information]
    B --> B1[Financial Statements]
    B --> B2[Credit Bureau Reports - CIBIL]
    B --> B3[Bank Statements]
    B --> B4[Industry Data]
    B1 --> C[Evaluate Five Cs of Credit]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> C1[Character]
    C --> C2[Capacity]
    C --> C3[Capital]
    C --> C4[Collateral]
    C --> C5[Conditions]
    C1 --> D[Apply Quantitative Models]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> D1[Altman Z-Score]
    D --> D2[Numerical Credit Scoring]
    D --> D3[DSCR and D/E Ratios]
    D1 --> E{Z-Score Zone}
    E -->|Z > 2.99| F1[SAFE - Approve]
    E -->|1.81 to 2.99| F2[GREY - Detailed Review]
    E -->|Z less than 1.81| F3[DISTRESS - Reject or Extra Security]
    D2 --> G{Weighted Score}
    G -->|Score >= 6.5| H1[Approve]
    G -->|4.5 to 6.5| H2[Review]
    G -->|Score less than 4.5| H3[Reject]
    F1 --> I[Sanction Credit with Terms]
    F2 --> I
    F3 --> J[Communicate Decision to Applicant]
    H1 --> I
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> J
    I --> K[Monitor and Review Periodically]
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 The Five C's Framework — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BORROWER[Borrower Profile]
        CH[Character - Willingness to Repay]
        CA[Capacity - Cash Flow Ability]
        CP[Capital - Net Worth and Equity]
    end
    subgraph LENDER[Lender Protection]
        CO[Collateral - Security Pledge]
    end
    subgraph EXTERNAL[External Environment]
        CN[Conditions - Industry and Macro]
    end
    BORROWER --> OUT[Overall Credit Decision]
    LENDER --> OUT
    EXTERNAL --> OUT
    CH --> CH1[Credit History - CIBIL]
    CH --> CH2[Management Reputation]
    CA --> CA1[DSCR Calculation]
    CA --> CA2[Interest Coverage]
    CA --> CA3[Cash Flow Analysis]
    CP --> CP1[Debt to Equity Ratio]
    CP --> CP2[Promoters Contribution]
    CO --> CO1[Primary Security]
    CO --> CO2[Collateral Security]
    CO --> CO3[Loan to Value Ratio]
    CN --> CN1[Industry Outlook]
    CN --> CN2[Regulatory Environment]
    CN --> CN3[Interest Rate Cycle]
```

### 4.3 Z-Score Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Compute Z-Score from 5 Ratios] --> CALC[Substitute into Altman Equation]
    CALC --> DECISION{Z-Score Value}
    DECISION -->|Z greater than 2.99| SAFE[SAFE ZONE - Low Default Risk - APPROVE]
    DECISION -->|Z between 1.81 and 2.99| GREY[GREY ZONE - Moderate Risk - DETAILED REVIEW]
    DECISION -->|Z less than 1.81| DIST[DISTRESS ZONE - High Risk - REJECT or ADD COLLATERAL]
    SAFE --> MON1[Standard Monitoring]
    GREY --> MON2[Quarterly Review and Covenants]
    DIST --> MON3[Rejection or Aggressive Recovery Terms]
```

### 4.4 Information Sources for Credit Analysis

```mermaid

    INFO[Information Sources] --> PRIMARY[Primary Sources]
    INFO --> SECONDARY[Secondary Sources]

    PRIMARY --> P4[Site Visit and Plant Inspection]
    SECONDARY --> S1[Credit Bureau - CIBIL Experian Equifax]
    SECONDARY --> S2[Industry and Trade Reports]
    SECONDARY --> S3[Tax Returns and GST Filings]
    SECONDARY --> S4[Court and Legal Records]

    P3 --> OUT

    S4 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Note for Print:** If the diagram fails to render in your study material, sketch a **3-layer pyramid** with the borrower at the top (Character, Capacity, Capital), lender protection in the middle (Collateral), and external conditions at the base.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
> *State and explain any **three** C's of credit that a banker considers while analyzing a credit applicant.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A banker evaluates the creditworthiness of an applicant using the **Five C's of Credit**. Any three may be explained:

1. **Character:** Refers to the borrower's integrity, reputation, and willingness to repay debts. It is judged by the borrower's credit history, CIBIL score, and past track record. A borrower with strong character will repay even in difficult times.

2. **Capacity:** Refers to the borrower's ability to repay the loan from operating cash flows. It is measured using the **Debt-Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR)** and the **Interest Coverage Ratio**. A DSCR greater than 1.5 indicates adequate capacity.

3. **Capital:** Refers to the borrower's own financial stake in the business, measured by net worth and the **Debt-to-Equity Ratio**. A higher capital contribution reduces the lender's risk and signals the borrower's confidence in the venture.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark per C with a clear one-line definition and one-line example. Total 3 marks.)*

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> *Briefly explain the **Altman Z-Score model** for credit applicant analysis.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Altman Z-Score model**, developed by Edward I. Altman in 1968, is a multivariate model that predicts the probability of corporate bankruptcy. It uses five financial ratios combined with specific weights:

$$Z = 1.2 X_1 + 1.4 X_2 + 3.3 X_3 + 0.6 X_4 + 0.999 X_5$$

The interpretation zones are:
- **Z > 2.99** → Safe Zone (low bankruptcy risk)
- **1.81 < Z < 2.99** → Grey Zone (moderate risk)
- **Z < 1.81** → Distress Zone (high bankruptcy risk)

The model is widely used by banks and credit rating agencies to assess the creditworthiness of corporate borrowers.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q.** From the following data of **M/s Bharat Electronics Ltd.**, compute the **Altman Z-Score** and comment on the creditworthiness of the company. Explain the **Five C's of Credit** as a qualitative supplement to this analysis.

| Item | Amount (₹ in Crores) |
|------|----------------------|
| Working Capital | 400 |
| Total Assets | 2,000 |
| Retained Earnings | 600 |
| EBIT | 360 |
| Market Value of Equity | 1,800 |
| Book Value of Total Liabilities | 2,400 |
| Sales | 3,000 |

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Altman Z-Score Calculation [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Compute the five ratios** [2 marks for setup]

$$
X_1 = \frac{400}{2000} = 0.20
$$

$$
X_2 = \frac{600}{2000} = 0.30
$$

$$
X_3 = \frac{360}{2000} = 0.18
$$

$$
X_4 = \frac{1800}{2400} = 0.75
$$

$$
X_5 = \frac{3000}{2000} = 1.50
$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the Altman Equation** [3 marks]

$$
Z = 1.2(0.20) + 1.4(0.30) + 3.3(0.18) + 0.6(0.75) + 0.999(1.50)
$$

**Step 3: Compute each term** [1 mark]

$$
1.2 \times 0.20 = 0.2400
$$

$$
1.4 \times 0.30 = 0.4200
$$

$$
3.3 \times 0.18 = 0.5940
$$

$$
0.6 \times 0.75 = 0.4500
$$

$$
0.999 \times 1.50 = 1.4985
$$

**Step 4: Sum the terms** [1 mark]

$$
Z = 0.2400 + 0.4200 + 0.5940 + 0.4500 + 1.4985 = 3.2025
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $Z = 3.2025 > 2.99$, the company lies in the **Safe Zone** with low bankruptcy risk. **Credit may be approved** at standard terms.

#### Part (b) — Five C's of Credit as Qualitative Supplement [7 Marks]

The **Five C's of Credit** provide a qualitative framework that complements the quantitative Z-Score analysis. [Allocate 1 mark per C and 2 marks for integration/table]

1. **Character:** Evaluate the promoter group's reputation, integrity, and CIBIL history. A good Z-Score plus a strong character yields a high-confidence credit decision.

2. **Capacity:** Assess the company's ability to generate cash flows via DSCR. For Bharat Electronics, a Z-Score of 3.20 suggests strong operating capacity, which should be verified by checking cash flow statements and projected EBIT growth.

3. **Capital:** Examine the Debt-to-Equity ratio. A high Z-Score ($X_4 = 0.75$) indicates that the market values equity close to total liabilities, implying a healthy capital structure.

4. **Collateral:** Identify the assets offered as security (machinery, land, inventory). Even in a Safe Zone, prudent lenders take collateral to cover systemic shocks.

5. **Conditions:** Consider the electronics and defence manufacturing industry outlook, government policies (Make in India, defence procurement), and macroeconomic environment.

> **Final Recommendation:** The combination of a Safe Z-Score (3.2025) and a positive qualitative profile (defence-sector company with strong promoter track record) supports a **strong approval recommendation** with **standard covenants** and a **quarterly review clause**.

---

> **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q.** A commercial bank uses a **numerical credit scoring system** to evaluate loan applications. The system uses the following weighted factors. **ABC Steels Ltd.** has applied for a term loan of ₹50 crores. Evaluate the application and recommend the bank's decision.

| Factor | Weight | ABC Steels Score (out of 10) |
|--------|--------|------------------------------|
| Profitability (ROCE) | **25** | 6 |
| Leverage (D/E Ratio) | **20** | 4 |
| Liquidity (Current Ratio) | **15** | 7 |
| Credit History (CIBIL) | **25** | 8 |
| Management Quality | **15** | 5 |
| **Total** | **100** | — |

**Cutoff Rule:** Score ≥ 6.0 → **Approve**; Score 4.5 to 6.0 → **Review with Conditions**; Score < 4.5 → **Reject**.

Also explain the **role of quantitative and qualitative tools** in credit applicant analysis.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Numerical Credit Scoring [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Compute Weighted Scores** [3 marks]

$$
\text{Profitability: } 25 \times 6 = 150
$$

$$
\text{Leverage: } 20 \times 4 = 80
$$

$$
\text{Liquidity: } 15 \times 7 = 105
$$

$$
\text{Credit History: } 25 \times 8 = 200
$$

$$
\text{Management: } 15 \times 5 = 75
$$

**Step 2: Total Weighted Score** [2 marks]

$$
\text{Total} = 150 + 80 + 105 + 200 + 75 = 610
$$

**Step 3: Final Credit Score (out of 10)** [2 marks]

$$
\text{Credit Score} = \frac{610}{100} = 6.10
$$

**Decision:** Since $6.10 \geq 6.0$, the application is in the **Approval Zone**. However, the weak score in **Leverage (4/10)** and **Management Quality (5/10)** warrants **conditional approval** with a covenant to reduce D/E ratio within 24 months.

#### Part (b) — Role of Quantitative and Qualitative Tools [7 Marks]

1. **Quantitative Tools Provide Objectivity (2 marks):** Ratios, Z-Score, and scoring systems produce reproducible, defensible numbers. They reduce human bias in large-scale lending.

2. **Qualitative Tools Provide Context (2 marks):** The 5 C's capture management intent, industry conditions, and character — dimensions that ratios cannot measure. A company with high Z-Score but poor character is still risky.

3. **Complementary Use (1.5 marks):** Best practice is to use both. Quantitative models screen and rank applicants; qualitative judgment refines the final decision and sets terms.

4. **Limitations of Each (1.5 marks):**
   - Quantitative models are based on **historical data** and may fail in sudden downturns.
   - Qualitative judgments can be **subjective** and may vary between analysts.
   - Combining both methods reduces the weakness of either approach.

> **Final Recommendation:** Approve the loan of ₹50 crores for ABC Steels Ltd. with **conditional covenants**: (i) reduction of D/E ratio from current to ≤ 1.5 within 24 months, (ii) quarterly review of leverage and management quality, and (iii) collateral coverage of at least 1.25× the loan amount.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Units in $X_4$** — A very common error is using **Book Value of Equity** instead of **Market Value of Equity** in $X_4$. The correct numerator is **Market Value of Equity (MVE)**, and the denominator is **Book Value of Total Liabilities** — *not* the other way around. Losing 1 mark here is normal, but losing 2–3 is a disaster in a 14-mark question.
>
> 2. **Mixing Up Zone Boundaries** — Many students write "$Z > 2.99$ is Grey Zone". The correct answer is **$Z > 2.99$ is SAFE**, **$1.81 < Z < 2.99$ is GREY**, **$Z < 1.81$ is DISTRESS**. Misremembering this loses **2 marks instantly**.
>
> 3. **Skipping the Qualitative Part** — In a 14-mark question with sub-parts (a) and (b), if part (a) is the Z-Score (7 marks) and part (b) asks for the 5 C's, students who only write the 5 C's as a single paragraph without a **table or structured bullets** lose 1–2 marks on presentation.
>
> 4. **Skipping the Final Recommendation** — KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **conclusion-oriented answers**. A 14-mark answer without a final decision sentence ("Credit may be approved / rejected / reviewed") will typically lose **1 mark** for lacking closure.
>
> 5. **Arithmetic Errors in Weighted Scores** — In numerical scoring, students often divide by the wrong total. Always check: $\dfrac{\text{Sum of weighted scores}}{\text{Sum of weights (100)}}$, not the number of factors (5).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Analyzing the Credit Applicant"**

- [ ] **Five C's of Credit:** Character, Capacity, Capital, Collateral, Conditions — always present in a **structured table or bullet** format.
- [ ] **Character** = willingness to repay; assessed via **CIBIL score, credit history, management reputation**.
- [ ] **Capacity** = ability to repay; assessed via **DSCR (≥ 1.5 ideal)** and **Interest Coverage Ratio (≥ 3.0 ideal)**.
- [ ] **Capital** = borrower's own stake; assessed via **Debt-to-Equity Ratio** and promoter contribution.
- [ ] **Collateral** = safety net; assessed via **Loan-to-Value (LTV) ratio** — lower LTV = safer.
- [ ] **Conditions** = external environment: industry outlook, regulatory climate, interest rate cycle.
- [ ] **Altman Z-Score Equation** (Public Manufacturing Firms):
  $$Z = 1.2 X_1 + 1.4 X_2 + 3.3 X_3 + 0.6 X_4 + 0.999 X_5$$
- [ ] **Z-Score Zones (Memorize Exactly):** Safe > 2.99 | Grey 1.81 to 2.99 | Distress < 1.81.
- [ ] **$X_3$ (EBIT/TA) has the highest weight (3.3)** — operating efficiency is the most powerful predictor.
- [ ] **$X_4$ uses Market Value of Equity** (not book value) in the numerator.
- [ ] **Numerical Credit Scoring** uses **weights × raw scores**, summed, then divided by total weight to give a final score on a 0–10 scale.
- [ ] **Typical Decision Cutoffs:** ≥ 6.5 = Approve; 4.5 to 6.5 = Review; < 4.5 = Reject.
- [ ] **Information Sources:** Primary (financials, bank statements, site visit) + Secondary (CIBIL, industry reports, tax returns).
- [ ] **Combine both qualitative and quantitative tools** for best results — never rely on Z-Score alone.
- [ ] **End every KTU answer with a clear recommendation sentence** — this avoids losing the "conclusion" mark.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Viva Tip:** If asked *"Which C is most important?"*, answer: **"Capacity"** — because a borrower must be able to repay, regardless of intention. If asked *"Why is the Z-Score not used for service firms?"*, answer: **"The original Altman model was calibrated on manufacturing firms; service firms have different asset structures (mostly intangible), so Altman later developed variants for non-manufacturers and emerging markets."**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Credit References
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Credit References

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Credit References** are the formal and informal sources of information that a credit manager consults to evaluate the **creditworthiness**, **repayment capacity**, and **character** of a prospective borrower before extending trade credit, bank loans, or any other form of short-term/long-term financing. They form the empirical backbone of the **credit investigation** process in working capital and receivables management.

In the KTU 2024 framework, credit references are positioned within the broader domain of **Receivables Management and Credit Policy**, where the firm must decide *to whom* to extend credit, *how much* to extend, and *on what terms*. The reference mechanism is what allows the firm to convert subjective judgement into an objective, defensible credit decision.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE] **The Landlord Analogy**
> Imagine you are a landlord deciding whether to rent your flat to a stranger. Before handing over the keys, you would naturally:
> 1. **Ask previous landlords** (Trade Reference) — *"Did they pay rent on time?"*
> 2. **Call their bank** (Banking Reference) — *"Do they have a stable salary account?"*
> 3. **Talk to their employer or friends** (Personal Reference) — *"Are they trustworthy?"*
> 4. **Check the CIBIL / police record** (Credit Bureau Reference) — *"Any defaults or pending cases?"*
> 5. **Look at their salary slips and ITR** (Financial Statement Reference) — *"Can they actually afford the rent?"*
>
> Each of these inquiries is a **credit reference**. Collectively, they form the **5 C's of Credit** that any prudent lender — be it a bank, an NBFC, or a B2B seller offering trade credit — must examine.

## 1.3 The 5 C's of Credit — The Analytical Lens

Every credit reference ultimately feeds into one of five analytical buckets. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests the **5 C's framework**:

| # | The "C" | What the Reference Tells Us | Reference Source |
|---|---------|----------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Character** | Will the borrower *want* to repay? | Personal references, past payment track record |
| 2 | **Capacity** | Is the borrower *able* to repay? | Financial statements, cash flow projections |
| 3 | **Capital** | How much skin-in-the-game does the borrower have? | Net worth, owner's equity, balance sheet |
| 4 | **Collateral** | What can be seized if default occurs? | Asset statements, security offered |
| 5 | **Conditions** | What is the external environment? | Industry outlook, macroeconomic conditions |

> [!NOTE] **Key Constant for KTU 2024**
> The **acceptable minimum CIBIL / Credit Score** in India is conventionally cited as **750 (out of 900)**. A score below 600 is considered **high-risk** and typically triggers either rejection or a heavily collateralized lending decision.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Credit References

Credit references can be classified along two dimensions — **source** and **formality**.

### A. Classification by Source

1. **Personal References**
   - Provided by friends, relatives, employers, professional acquaintances.
   - Useful for assessing *Character*.
   - Weakest form of reference; highly subjective.

2. **Trade References**
   - Provided by suppliers/manufacturers with whom the applicant has prior credit history.
   - The most important reference in **B2B trade credit**.
   - Reveals payment habits, average days to pay, returned cheques, etc.

3. **Banking References**
   - Provided by the applicant's banker(s) — both current and previous.
   - Reveals account conduct, average balance, overdraft usage, cheque bounces.
   - Most reliable for assessing *Capacity* and *Character*.

4. **Credit Bureau References**
   - Provided by agencies like **CIBIL (TransUnion)**, **Experian**, **Equifax**, **CRIF High Mark**.
   - Provide a **numeric Credit Score** and a full credit history (loans, defaults, enquiries).
   - Mandatory for institutional lending above prescribed thresholds.

5. **Financial Statement References**
   - Audited Profit & Loss, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow Statement.
   - Tax returns (ITR), GST returns.
   - Critical for assessing *Capacity* and *Capital*.

### B. Classification by Formality

- **Formal References** — written, documented, legally admissible (e.g., audited financials, CIBIL report).
- **Informal References** — verbal, subjective, used as supplementary inputs (e.g., personal references, trade gossip).

## 2.2 The Credit Investigation Process — Operational Steps

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Favourite — Steps of Credit Investigation**
> 1. **Receipt of Credit Application** — borrower submits request with basic KYC.
> 2. **Preliminary Screening** — eliminate obviously ineligible applicants.
> 3. **Collection of References** — gather trade, bank, personal, bureau references.
> 4. **Analysis using 5 C's** — score each C using the weighted model.
> 5. **Decision** — Approve / Reject / Refer to senior authority.
> 6. **Communication of Terms** — credit limit, payment period, discount terms, interest on overdue.
> 7. **Post-Sanction Monitoring** — periodic review using fresh references.

## 2.3 Quantitative Credit Scoring Model (The 5-C Weighted Model)

The most commonly used analytical tool in KTU numerical problems is the **Weighted Credit Scoring Model**, where each of the 5 C's is assigned a **weight** (reflecting its importance) and a **score** (reflecting the applicant's performance on that parameter).

### KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Boundary |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Weighted Credit Score** | $S = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_{i} \cdot s_{i}$ | Score is unitless; range 0–100 |
| 2 | **Weight Constraint** | $\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_{i} = 1$ | Weights are pure numbers (or %) |
| 3 | **CIBIL Score Range (India)** | $300 \le \text{CIBIL} \le 900$ | Higher is better |
| 4 | **CIBIL Decision Bands** | 750–900 Excellent; 700–749 Good; 650–699 Fair; 600–649 Poor; $\le 599$ Very Poor | CIBIL = Credit Information Bureau (India) Ltd. |
| 5 | **Default Probability Proxy** | $P_{D} \approx 1 - \dfrac{S}{100}$ | Unitless probability 0–1 |
| 6 | **Norms / Cut-off Score** | $S \ge S_{\text{cut-off}}$ for approval | $S_{\text{cut-off}}$ is firm-specific, typically **70/100** |
| 7 | **Trade Credit Cost (for context)** | $K_{TC} = \dfrac{d}{1-d} \times \dfrac{360}{N}$ | Where *d* = discount %, *N* = net period beyond discount |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Syllabus Highlight**
> In KTU 2024 ESE, problems typically give the weights of each C and ask the student to compute the **composite score**, then take a **grant/reject decision**. Always show the weight constraint check.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

The credit reference mechanism is the silent engine behind:
- **Banks & NBFCs** sanctioning personal loans, home loans, and MSME credit.
- **B2B trade credit** decisions in supply chains (e.g., Tata Steel extending credit to Maruti).
- **Credit card issuance** by HDFC, ICICI, etc.
- **Export credit** decisions by ECGC and EXIM Bank.
- **Bond rating** by CRISIL, ICRA, CARE, India Ratings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Solved Numerical — The Classic KTU 5-C Weighted Scoring Problem

### Problem Statement

> **Mr. Rajesh**, a prospective credit customer of M/s Kerala Traders Pvt. Ltd., has applied for a trade credit limit of ₹10,00,000. The credit manager has collected the following information and wants to apply the **5-C Weighted Credit Scoring Model**.
>
> **Weights assigned by the firm:**
> - Character = 0.30
> - Capacity = 0.25
> - Capital = 0.20
> - Collateral = 0.15
> - Conditions = 0.10
>
> **Scores awarded (out of 100) based on references gathered:**
> - Character = 80
> - Capacity = 70
> - Capital = 75
> - Collateral = 85
> - Conditions = 90
>
> The firm's **cut-off score** for approval is **70**. Determine whether the credit should be sanctioned.

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Verify the Weight Constraint**

We must first check that the weights sum to 1, as required by the formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_{i} &= 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 \\
&= 0.55 + 0.35 + 0.10 \\
&= 1.00 \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The weight constraint is satisfied. This is the **first mark-bearing checkpoint** in KTU valuation.

**Step 2 — Identify the Formula to be Applied**

The composite weighted credit score is given by:

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_{i} \cdot s_{i}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= (0.30 \times 80) + (0.25 \times 70) + (0.20 \times 75) + (0.15 \times 85) + (0.10 \times 90)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Each Term Individually**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Character contribution} &= 0.30 \times 80 = 24.00 \\
\text{Capacity contribution} &= 0.25 \times 70 = 17.50 \\
\text{Capital contribution} &= 0.20 \times 75 = 15.00 \\
\text{Collateral contribution} &= 0.15 \times 85 = 12.75 \\
\text{Conditions contribution} &= 0.10 \times 90 = 9.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum the Contributions**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 24.00 + 17.50 + 15.00 + 12.75 + 9.00 \\
  &= 41.50 + 15.00 + 12.75 + 9.00 \\
  &= 56.50 + 12.75 + 9.00 \\
  &= 69.25 + 9.00 \\
  &= 78.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with Cut-off and Take Decision**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 78.25 \\
S_{\text{cut-off}} &= 70.00 \\
S &\ge S_{\text{cut-off}} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{APPROVE THE CREDIT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Inferential Commentary**

Since the score exceeds the cut-off by **8.25 points (≈ 11.79 % cushion)**, the credit is **low-to-moderate risk**. The credit manager may sanction the limit with **standard terms** (e.g., Net 30, no cash discount, with a personal guarantee).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Grid for the Same Problem

| Score Range | Risk Class | Decision | Suggested Action |
|-------------|-----------|----------|------------------|
| 85 – 100 | Very Low Risk | Approve unconditionally | Best terms, higher credit limit |
| 70 – 84.99 | Low-Moderate Risk | **Approve (our case: 78.25)** | Standard terms, monitor periodically |
| 55 – 69.99 | Moderate-High Risk | Refer to senior / conditional | Reduce limit, demand collateral |
| 40 – 54.99 | High Risk | Reject or heavily collateralize | Only with 100 % security |
| < 40 | Very High Risk | Reject outright | Decline application |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Credit Reference Gathering Flow

The following block-level functional architecture flow maps how a credit application moves through the various credit reference channels and converges into the 5-C decision matrix.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Applies for Credit] --> B[Preliminary KYC and Screening]
    B --> C[Collection of Credit References]
    C --> D1[Personal Reference]
    C --> D2[Trade Reference]
    C --> D3[Banking Reference]
    C --> D4[Credit Bureau Reference CIBIL Experian Equifax]
    C --> D5[Financial Statement Reference]
    D1 --> E[5 C Scoring Engine]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F{Score vs Cut off 70}
    F -->|Score greater than or equal 70| G[APPROVE Credit]
    F -->|Score less than 70| H[REJECT or Refer]
    G --> I[Sanction Letter and Terms]
    I --> J[Periodic Post Sanction Monitoring]
    J --> K[Fresh Reference Review Annually]
```

## 4.2 The 5 C's — Input-Process-Output Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Character Input Personal and Trade Refs] --> M1[Weight 0.30]
    P2[Capacity Input Bank and Financial Refs] --> M2[Weight 0.25]
    P3[Capital Input Balance Sheet Refs] --> M3[Weight 0.20]
    P4[Collateral Input Asset and Security Refs] --> M4[Weight 0.15]
    P5[Conditions Input Industry and Macro Refs] --> M5[Weight 0.10]
    M1 --> S[Composite Credit Score]
    M2 --> S
    M3 --> S
    M4 --> S
    M5 --> S
    S --> D[Decision Approve Reject Conditional]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Reference Reliability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Most Reliable Credit Bureau CIBIL Score 750 plus] --> R2[Bank Reference with written confirmation]
    R2 --> R3[Audited Financial Statements]
    R3 --> R4[Trade Reference from Established Supplier]
    R4 --> R5[Personal Reference from Employer]
    R5 --> R6[Least Reliable Casual Acquaintance Reference]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define the term "Credit References" and briefly explain any THREE types of credit references used in trade credit decisions.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Credit References** are the sources of information that a credit manager relies upon to assess the creditworthiness, repayment ability, and trustworthiness of a prospective borrower before extending credit. They convert subjective judgement into an objective, data-driven decision.

The three principal types are:

1. **Trade References** — Information collected from suppliers and manufacturers with whom the applicant has a prior credit relationship. They reveal the applicant's actual payment behaviour, average payment period, and any history of bounced cheques.

2. **Banking References** — Information gathered from the applicant's banks (current and previous). They indicate account conduct, average balance maintained, frequency of cheque returns, and use of overdraft facilities.

3. **Credit Bureau References** — Reports obtained from agencies such as CIBIL, Experian, or CRIF High Mark. They provide a standardized **Credit Score (300–900 in India)** and a comprehensive history of loans, defaults, and credit enquiries.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: List and briefly explain the FIVE C's of Credit as a framework for evaluating credit references.**

**Model Answer:**

The **5 C's of Credit** are the analytical lens through which all credit references are interpreted:

1. **Character** — The borrower's integrity and willingness to repay. Assessed via personal and trade references. It is the most subjective of the five.

2. **Capacity** — The borrower's ability to generate sufficient cash flow to service the debt. Assessed via financial statements and banking references.

3. **Capital** — The borrower's own stake in the business (net worth, equity). Assessed via the balance sheet. Higher capital signals lower moral hazard.

4. **Collateral** — The security offered against the credit. Assessed via asset statements and valuation reports. Acts as a fallback in case of default.

5. **Conditions** — The external environment — industry outlook, macroeconomic climate, purpose of the loan. Assessed via market research and economic reports.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model ESE Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)
**Q: Explain in detail the various sources/types of credit references used by a credit manager. How do these references feed into the 5-C framework of credit evaluation? Discuss with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Sources of Credit References [7 Marks]**

A credit manager has access to the following sources:

| Sl. | Reference Source | What It Reveals | Reliability |
|-----|------------------|-----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Personal References** | Character, social standing | Low |
| 2 | **Trade References** | Payment track record | High |
| 3 | **Banking References** | Cash flow, account conduct | Very High |
| 4 | **Credit Bureau (CIBIL)** | Composite credit score (300–900) | Highest |
| 5 | **Audited Financials** | Capacity, Capital, Leverage | Very High |
| 6 | **Tax Returns (ITR, GST)** | Real income, business turnover | High |
| 7 | **Public Records / Court Records** | Litigation, insolvency history | Medium |

**(b) Mapping References to the 5-C Framework [7 Marks]**

The mapping is non-exclusive — a single reference can feed multiple C's. For example, an audited financial statement informs both **Capacity** (via P&L and CF) and **Capital** (via Balance Sheet equity).

- **Character** ← Personal + Trade references
- **Capacity** ← Financial statements + Banking references
- **Capital** ← Financial statements (net worth)
- **Collateral** ← Asset statements + Valuation reports
- **Conditions** ← Industry reports + Macro indicators

**Example:** When M/s Infosys evaluates extending credit to a new B2B client, it pulls the client's CIBIL report (→ Character + historical defaults), audited financials (→ Capacity + Capital), banker's opinion (→ Capacity + Character), and reviews the IT sector outlook (→ Conditions). The composite picture drives the decision.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**Q: ABC Industries Ltd. is evaluating a credit application from Mr. Sharma for a credit limit of ₹25,00,000. The credit manager has used the 5-C Weighted Scoring Model with the following data:**

| Parameter | Weight | Score (out of 100) |
|-----------|--------|--------------------|
| Character | 0.25 | 90 |
| Capacity | 0.30 | 60 |
| Capital | 0.20 | 70 |
| Collateral | 0.15 | 80 |
| Conditions | 0.10 | 50 |

**The cut-off score for approval is 70. Calculate the composite credit score and advise whether the credit should be sanctioned. If sanctioned, what risk-control measures would you recommend?**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Calculation of Composite Credit Score [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Check the weight constraint:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum w_{i} &= 0.25 + 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 \\
&= 0.55 + 0.45 \\
&= 1.00 \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula** $S = \sum w_{i} \cdot s_{i}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= (0.25 \times 90) + (0.30 \times 60) + (0.20 \times 70) + (0.15 \times 80) + (0.10 \times 50) \\
  &= 22.50 + 18.00 + 14.00 + 12.00 + 5.00 \\
  &= 40.50 + 14.00 + 12.00 + 5.00 \\
  &= 54.50 + 12.00 + 5.00 \\
  &= 66.50 + 5.00 \\
  &= 71.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**

$$
S = 71.50 \;\; \ge \;\; S_{\text{cut-off}} = 70 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{APPROVE (Marginal)}
$$

The score exceeds the cut-off by only **1.5 points (≈ 2.1 % cushion)**, indicating a **borderline case** that warrants **risk-control measures**.

**(b) Recommended Risk-Control Measures [7 Marks]**

Given the marginal score and the weak areas (**Capacity = 60** and **Conditions = 50**), the following safeguards are recommended:

1. **Reduce the Credit Limit** — Sanction ₹15,00,000 instead of the requested ₹25,00,000 until a clean repayment track record is established.
2. **Shorten the Credit Period** — Offer Net 15 instead of the usual Net 30, to accelerate cash recovery.
3. **Demand Additional Collateral** — Secure the balance ₹10,00,000 with a personal guarantee and post-dated cheques.
4. **Offer a Cash Discount** — e.g., 2/10 Net 15, to incentivize early payment and reduce exposure.
5. **Tighten Monitoring Frequency** — Review account conduct **monthly** for the first six months, then quarterly.
6. **Tie-up with a Credit Insurance Agency** — Insure the receivables against default.
7. **Revisit CIBIL** — Periodically re-pull the bureau report to detect emerging stress signals.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - **Always verify** that $\sum w_{i} = 1$ before substitution. Examiners award a separate mark for this **constraint check**; skipping it costs you **1 mark** even if the rest of the computation is correct.
> - **Do not** confuse the **CIBIL score** (300–900) with the **5-C composite score** (0–100). They are different scales measuring related but distinct constructs.
> - **Always show units-free numbers** in the weighted model; using ₹ or % symbols inside the formula is a common KTU valuation error.
> - **For 14-mark questions**, ensure your answer has **two clear sub-parts** (7 + 7). Examiners are instructed to award sub-part marks independently.
> - **Mention the cut-off comparison explicitly** in the final line; a missing decision is the single most common reason for losing 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE] **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Memorize These**

- ☐ **Credit References** = sources of information used to evaluate borrower creditworthiness.
- ☐ The **5 C's** = **Character, Capacity, Capital, Collateral, Conditions** (in that order of weightage typically).
- ☐ **Personal Reference** → weakest; **CIBIL Report** → strongest.
- ☐ **CIBIL Score Range in India** = **300 to 900**; **750+ = Excellent**.
- ☐ **Weighted Credit Score Formula** = $S = \sum w_{i} \cdot s_{i}$; with $\sum w_{i} = 1$.
- ☐ **Always verify the weight constraint first** — earns a separate mark.
- ☐ **Cut-off comparison** is the decision point: $S \ge S_{\text{cut-off}} \Rightarrow$ Approve.
- ☐ **Risk-Control Measures** for marginal cases: reduce limit, shorten period, demand collateral, monitor frequently, take credit insurance.
- ☐ **Three reliable references** = Banking, Trade, and Credit Bureau.
- ☐ **Trade Credit Cost Formula** (often tested alongside) = $K_{TC} = \dfrac{d}{1-d} \times \dfrac{360}{N}$.
- ☐ **Steps of Credit Investigation**: Application → Screening → Reference Collection → 5-C Scoring → Decision → Terms → Monitoring.
- ☐ **Examiner's hot-button words** to use in answers: *creditworthiness, repayment capacity, weighted average, cut-off, composite score, risk-control measures, post-sanction monitoring*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Selecting the Optimum Credit Period
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT631 - FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Selecting the Optimum Credit Period

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Optimum Credit Period** is the specific length of the credit period (in days) at which the firm's profitability from credit sales is maximized. It is the equilibrium point where the **incremental revenue (incremental contribution from additional sales)** generated by relaxing/strengthening the credit terms is exactly equal to the **incremental cost** (cost of additional receivables, bad debts, administrative expenses, and cash discounts) associated with that change.

Mathematically, the Optimum Credit Period corresponds to the point on the **Total Credit Cost Curve** (or the Net Profit Curve) at which the firm's **Net Profit from Credit Sales is maximized** and the marginal incremental benefit equals marginal incremental cost (i.e., $\frac{dB}{dP} = \frac{dC}{dP}$, where $B$ is incremental benefit and $C$ is incremental cost).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 4 – Working Capital Management / Receivables Management):** Selection of the optimum credit period is a *strategic* decision under **Receivables Management**, distinguished from tactical decisions like average collection period monitoring. The focus is on inter-temporal **cost-benefit comparison of alternative credit policies (P0, P1, P2, …).**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you own a small grocery shop in Kerala. You have two choices:

- **Policy A (No Credit):** You sell only for cash. Your daily revenue is fixed at ₹10,000, and you have no bad debts and no money stuck in bills receivable.
- **Policy B (Offer 30-day Credit):** You offer credit to nearby shops. Now your daily revenue rises to ₹15,000 because more people buy from you, but you must wait a month to collect money, some customers default, and you need to hire a clerk to track bills.

The *incremental revenue* is ₹5,000/day. The *incremental cost* is the cost of the ₹5,000 extra tied up in receivables + clerk salary + bad debts. If the extra revenue exceeds the extra cost, Policy B is profitable. Now, what if you offer **60-day credit**? Some customers pay, some don't, but revenue climbs to ₹17,000. The *additional* ₹2,000 may not justify the *additional* delay, defaults, and capital cost.

👉 The **Optimum Credit Period** is the sweet spot — not too tight (lost sales) and not too loose (high cost of credit) — where the **net incremental gain is at its absolute maximum**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics in Receivables Management:**
> - **ACP (Average Collection Period)** = Average number of days it takes to collect receivables. Industry benchmark: **30–60 days** for B2B.
> - **Bad Debt Loss Rate** = Uncollectible receivables as a % of credit sales. Typical range: **1%–5%** depending on industry risk.
> - **Required Rate of Return (K)** = Opportunity cost of funds tied up in receivables. Typical range: **12%–18% p.a.** in Indian corporates.

## 1.3 Components Influencing Optimum Credit Period

A credit policy has **four interacting variables** that jointly determine the optimum:

1. **Credit Period (P)** — the duration (in days) for which credit is extended.
2. **Cash Discount (d)** — the incentive offered for early payment (e.g., "2/10 net 30" means 2% discount if paid within 10 days, otherwise full payment due in 30 days).
3. **Credit Standards (S)** — the minimum financial strength required of a customer to qualify for credit.
4. **Collection Effort (E)** — the intensity and cost of follow-up activities on overdue accounts.

Selecting the optimum credit period is essentially finding the value of **P** that maximizes the **Net Profit of Credit Sales**, holding the other three variables constant (or evaluating combinations of all four).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — How Optimum Credit Period is Determined

The decision is made by evaluating **alternative credit policies** (typically three: current policy P₀, relaxed policy P₁, more relaxed P₂) and selecting the one that yields the **maximum net incremental benefit** over the existing policy.

The analytical procedure follows a **4-step logical flow**:

### Step 1 — Estimate the Incremental Sales Volume
When the credit period is relaxed (lengthened or opened to more customers), total sales volume increases. Estimate $\Delta S$ = (New Sales Level – Old Sales Level).

### Step 2 — Compute Incremental Contribution / Gross Profit
Compute $\Delta \text{GP}$ = $\Delta S \times \text{Contribution Margin Ratio}$.

> The contribution margin is the relevant revenue measure (not net profit) because fixed costs are presumed unchanged in the short run. Only variable costs vary with sales.

### Step 3 — Quantify Incremental Costs of Carrying Additional Receivables
The costs that rise when credit is relaxed are:
- **Cost of Investment in Receivables** (= Average Receivables × Required Rate of Return, K).
- **Bad Debt Losses** (typically expressed as a % of credit sales).
- **Credit Administration Cost** (clerical, record-keeping, monitoring).
- **Cash Discount Expense** (if discount terms are tightened alongside).

### Step 4 — Compute Net Incremental Benefit and Choose the Best Policy
$$
\text{Net Incremental Benefit} = \Delta \text{Gross Profit} - \Delta \text{Cost of Receivables} - \Delta \text{Bad Debts} - \Delta \text{Admin Costs} - \Delta \text{Discount Costs}
$$

> The **Optimum Credit Period** is the one whose incremental net benefit is the **maximum positive value** (or the least negative if all policies are unprofitable).

## 2.2 The Total Cost–Benefit Diagram (Conceptual)

The **Total Credit Cost Curve** is U-shaped (or the **Net Profit Curve** is inverted-U). Three regions exist:

- **Region I (Very Tight Credit):** Low sales, low default, but **high opportunity cost of foregone profit**.
- **Region II (Optimum Credit Period):** Marginal benefit of an extra day of credit = marginal cost. Net profit peaks here.
- **Region III (Very Loose Credit):** Additional credit length brings diminishing sales but accelerating cost of capital, defaults, and administration. Net profit falls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not simply "Maximize Sales"?** Because beyond the optimum, every additional day of credit *destroys* shareholder value. This is a classic example of the **finance principle of marginalism** applied to working capital.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use the table below as the master reference for all numerical problems on this topic in **KTU 2024 ESE and Internal examinations.**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Average Investment in Receivables (AIR) | $\text{AIR} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Credit Sales}}{360} \times \text{Average Collection Period (ACP)}$ | Currency (₹). Sometimes ACP is expressed as fraction of year. |
| 2 | Cost of Investment in Receivables | $\text{CIR} = \text{AIR} \times K$ where $K$ = required rate of return | Currency (₹) per year |
| 3 | Total Cost of Credit Sales | $\text{TCC} = \text{CIR} + \text{Bad Debts} + \text{Admin Cost} + \text{Discount Cost}$ | Currency (₹) per year |
| 4 | Net Benefit of a Credit Policy | $\text{NB} = \text{Gross Profit on Credit Sales} - \text{TCC}$ | Currency (₹) per year |
| 5 | Marginal Rule of Optimum | $\dfrac{d(\text{Net Benefit})}{dP} = 0 \;\; \text{or} \;\; \Delta \text{Benefit} = \Delta \text{Cost}$ | Differential calculus form (theoretical) |
| 6 | Practical Rule (KTU Board Exam) | Compare all candidate policies; select the one with the **maximum Net Benefit** | Used in numericals |
| 7 | Bad Debt Loss (BDL) | $\text{BDL} = \text{Bad Debt \%} \times \text{Credit Sales}$ | Currency (₹) per year |
| 8 | Cash Discount Cost | $\text{CDC} = \text{Discount \%} \times \text{\% of Customers Availing} \times \text{Credit Sales}$ | Currency (₹) per year |
| 9 | Incremental Gross Profit | $\Delta \text{GP} = (\text{New Sales} - \text{Old Sales}) \times \text{CM Ratio}$ | Currency (₹) per year |
| 10 | Incremental Cost of Receivables | $\Delta \text{CIR} = \Delta \text{AIR} \times K$ | Currency (₹) per year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the markdown table above, the division bar has been kept on the same line and the vertical pipe `$\vert$` is used to denote absolute value (per KTU formatting safeguard). For 360-day year convention, ALWAYS use 360 unless the problem explicitly says 365.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Business Fields

- **Manufacturing Firms** (e.g., Kerala-based spice processing units): Optimum credit decisions balance the cost of capital tied up in receivables against the volume leverage credit offers in export markets.
- **Construction & EPC Companies** (Civil Engineering firms): Long credit cycles to government PSUs are evaluated using the same framework — the "credit period" is often 90–180 days, making the cost of receivables huge.
- **IT and Service Firms** (CS-related industries): Since services are typically billed on milestones, receivables are large. Firms use the same incremental analysis to decide payment terms in client contracts.
- **Retail & FMCG Companies**: Cash discounts (e.g., "2/15 net 45") are a critical lever in this analysis. The KTU formula framework is used to decide the discount percentage and the discount period.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Average Investment in Receivables (AIR)

The derivation begins with the steady-state assumption: receivables are uniformly distributed across the credit period.

Let:
- $S$ = Total annual credit sales (in ₹).
- $P$ = Credit period in days.
- $T$ = 360 (days in a year for the KTU convention).

**Logic Step 1 — Daily Credit Sales:**
The total credit sales of $S$ rupees occur uniformly over $T$ days, so the daily credit sales is:
$$
\text{Daily Credit Sales} = \frac{S}{T}
$$

**Logic Step 2 — Outstanding Receivables at any Instant:**
Since receivables are continuously being created and continuously being collected, at any random instant during the year, the receivables outstanding have an **average age** of $P/2$ days. Therefore, the amount of money tied up at any instant is:
$$
\text{Average Receivables} = \text{Daily Credit Sales} \times \text{Average Age}
$$
$$
\text{AIR} = \frac{S}{T} \times \frac{P}{2} = \frac{S \cdot P}{2T}
$$

**Logic Step 3 — Verification with the Alternative Form:**
We can verify this using the standard textbook convention:
$$
\text{AIR} = \frac{S}{T} \times \text{ACP}
$$
where $\text{ACP} = P$ (when all customers pay exactly on the due date) or $\text{ACP} = \frac{P}{2}$ (when customers pay uniformly over the period). The KTU convention is to use $\text{AIR} = \dfrac{S \cdot P}{360}$ when the credit period $P$ itself is the ACP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When the problem says "customers pay uniformly over the credit period", use $\text{AIR} = \dfrac{S \cdot P}{2 \times 360}$. When it says "all customers pay on the last day", use $\text{AIR} = \dfrac{S \cdot P}{360}$. This is a **very common 2–3 mark trap** in KTU exams.

## 3.2 Cost of Investment in Receivables (CIR)

Once AIR is known, multiplying by the required rate of return (K) gives the annual cost of carrying receivables. The rate $K$ represents the firm's opportunity cost of capital — i.e., the return the firm could have earned if the same funds were invested in its own business.

$$
\text{CIR} = \text{AIR} \times K = \frac{S \cdot P}{2T} \times K
$$

**Numerical Worked Example — Step-by-Step Evaluation**

Consider a firm evaluating **three credit policies** (P₀, P₁, P₂) with the following data:

| Item | P₀ (Current) | P₁ (Relaxed) | P₂ (More Relaxed) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Credit Period (days) | 30 | 45 | 60 |
| Annual Credit Sales (₹) | 24,00,000 | 28,00,000 | 30,00,000 |
| Bad Debt % of Sales | 2% | 3% | 4% |
| Credit Admin Cost (₹) | 30,000 | 40,000 | 50,000 |
| Required Rate of Return (K) | 15% | 15% | 15% |
| CM Ratio | 25% | 25% | 25% |

**Step 1 — Compute AIR for each policy** (assuming uniform payment over credit period):
$$
\text{AIR}_{P_0} = \frac{24{,}00{,}000 \times 30}{2 \times 360} = \frac{72{,}00{,}000}{720} = 10{,}000
$$

$$
\text{AIR}_{P_1} = \frac{28{,}00{,}000 \times 45}{2 \times 360} = \frac{1{,}26{,}00{,}000}{720} = 1{,}75{,}000
$$

$$
\text{AIR}_{P_2} = \frac{30{,}00{,}000 \times 60}{2 \times 360} = \frac{1{,}80{,}00{,}000}{720} = 2{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute CIR for each policy** (CIR = AIR × K):
$$
\text{CIR}_{P_0} = 10{,}000 \times 0.15 = 1{,}500
$$
$$
\text{CIR}_{P_1} = 1{,}75{,}000 \times 0.15 = 26{,}250
$$
$$
\text{CIR}_{P_2} = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.15 = 37{,}500
$$

> Wait — there is a unit inconsistency. Let me re-check the formula scaling. AIR computed in step 1 is in ₹, but here we used a truncated "10,000" which is incorrect. Let me recompute:

$$
\text{AIR}_{P_0} = \frac{24{,}00{,}000 \times 30}{720} = 1{,}00{,}000 \;\; \text{(₹)}
$$
$$
\text{AIR}_{P_1} = \frac{28{,}00{,}000 \times 45}{720} = 1{,}75{,}000 \;\; \text{(₹)}
$$
$$
\text{AIR}_{P_2} = \frac{30{,}00{,}000 \times 60}{720} = 2{,}50{,}000 \;\; \text{(₹)}
$$

$$
\text{CIR}_{P_0} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.15 = 15{,}000 \;\; \text{(₹)}
$$
$$
\text{CIR}_{P_1} = 1{,}75{,}000 \times 0.15 = 26{,}250 \;\; \text{(₹)}
$$
$$
\text{CIR}_{P_2} = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.15 = 37{,}500 \;\; \text{(₹)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Bad Debt Loss (BDL) for each policy:**
$$
\text{BDL}_{P_0} = 24{,}00{,}000 \times 0.02 = 48{,}000
$$
$$
\text{BDL}_{P_1} = 28{,}00{,}000 \times 0.03 = 84{,}000
$$
$$
\text{BDL}_{P_2} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.04 = 1{,}20{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Total Cost of Credit Sales (TCC):**
$$
\text{TCC}_{P_0} = 15{,}000 + 48{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 93{,}000
$$
$$
\text{TCC}_{P_1} = 26{,}250 + 84{,}000 + 40{,}000 = 1{,}50{,}250
$$
$$
\text{TCC}_{P_2} = 37{,}500 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 2{,}07{,}500
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Gross Profit (GP) from Credit Sales:**
$$
\text{GP}_{P_0} = 24{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = 6{,}00{,}000
$$
$$
\text{GP}_{P_1} = 28{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = 7{,}00{,}000
$$
$$
\text{GP}_{P_2} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = 7{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Net Benefit (NB) = GP − TCC:**
$$
\text{NB}_{P_0} = 6{,}00{,}000 - 93{,}000 = 5{,}07{,}000
$$
$$
\text{NB}_{P_1} = 7{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}250 = 5{,}49{,}750
$$
$$
\text{NB}_{P_2} = 7{,}50{,}000 - 2{,}07{,}500 = 5{,}42{,}500
$$

**Step 7 — Comparative Decision Table:**

| Policy | Credit Period | GP (₹) | TCC (₹) | Net Benefit (₹) | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P₀ | 30 days | 6,00,000 | 93,000 | 5,07,000 | III |
| P₁ | 45 days | 7,00,000 | 1,50,250 | **5,49,750** | **I (Optimum)** |
| P₂ | 60 days | 7,50,000 | 2,07,500 | 5,42,500 | II |

**Conclusion:** The **Optimum Credit Period is 45 days (Policy P₁)** because it yields the **maximum Net Benefit of ₹5,49,750**.

## 3.3 Incremental (Marginal) Analysis — Step-by-Step

A more powerful approach used in **advanced KTU problems** is to compare each policy *incrementally* against the current one, rather than absolutely.

**Logic Step A — Incremental Gross Profit from P₀ to P₁:**
$$
\Delta \text{GP}_{0 \to 1} = (28{,}00{,}000 - 24{,}00{,}000) \times 0.25 = 1{,}00{,}000
$$

**Logic Step B — Incremental Cost from P₀ to P₁:**
$$
\Delta \text{TCC}_{0 \to 1} = 1{,}50{,}250 - 93{,}000 = 57{,}250
$$

**Logic Step C — Incremental Net Benefit:**
$$
\Delta \text{NB}_{0 \to 1} = 1{,}00{,}000 - 57{,}250 = 42{,}750 \;\; \text{(Positive ⇒ Accept)}
$$

**Logic Step D — Incremental Net Benefit from P₁ to P₂:**
$$
\Delta \text{GP}_{1 \to 2} = (30{,}00{,}000 - 28{,}00{,}000) \times 0.25 = 50{,}000
$$
$$
\Delta \text{TCC}_{1 \to 2} = 2{,}07{,}500 - 1{,}50{,}250 = 57{,}250
$$
$$
\Delta \text{NB}_{1 \to 2} = 50{,}000 - 57{,}250 = -7{,}250 \;\; \text{(Negative ⇒ Reject)}
$$

**Logic Step E — Decision Rule:**
Since shifting from P₁ to P₂ destroys ₹7,250 of value, P₁ is the **final optimum** — exactly the point at which the next incremental move becomes unprofitable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalization:** The optimum is the policy at which the **next incremental step yields a negative or zero incremental net benefit**. This is the **discrete-form of the marginal rule** $\frac{dB}{dP} = 0$.

## 3.4 Role of Cash Discounts in the Optimum Decision

A common KTU twist is to bundle a **cash discount** with the credit period. For example, Policy P₃ = "45 days credit with a 2% discount if paid within 15 days" might replace plain "45 days credit".

The relevant additional cost is the **Cash Discount Expense (CDE)**:
$$
\text{CDE} = \text{Credit Sales} \times \text{\% of customers using discount} \times \text{Discount \%}
$$

The expected **acceleration in collections** (new ACP) reduces AIR and CIR, potentially offsetting the discount cost. The optimum combination balances:
- Discount cost (CDE)
- Savings in CIR (lower AIR × K)
- Reduction in bad debt losses (early-paying customers rarely default)

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Pattern (KTU):** When the problem specifies that 50% of customers take the cash discount, the formula is: $\text{CDE} = S \times 0.50 \times d$, where $d$ is the discount rate. **Many students forget the 0.50 multiplier and lose 1–2 marks.**
