# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Variables Affecting the Optimum Credit Period

The diagram below maps the **four interacting levers** of a credit policy and their downstream cost/profit channels, leading to the **Optimum Credit Period** decision node.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Credit Policy Decision Variables] --> B[Credit Period P]
    A --> C[Cash Discount d]
    A --> D[Credit Standards S]
    A --> E[Collection Effort E]

    B --> F[Incremental Sales Volume]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F

    F --> G[Incremental Gross Profit GP]
    G --> H[Net Benefit Calculation]

    B --> I[Average Investment in Receivables AIR]
    C --> I
    E --> I

    I --> J[Cost of Capital Tied Up CIR]
    D --> K[Bad Debt Loss BDL]
    E --> K
    C --> L[Cash Discount Expense CDE]
    B --> M[Credit Administration Cost CAC]

    J --> N[Total Cost of Credit Sales TCC]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N

    N --> H
    H --> O{Maximum Net Benefit Achieved?}
    O -->|Yes| P[OPTIMUM CREDIT PERIOD SELECTED]
    O -->|No| A
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Iterative Search for Optimum Policy

The following diagram represents the **branching search procedure** that a finance manager (or a KTU student in a numerical problem) follows to identify the best policy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start with Current Policy P0]) --> EvalP0[Compute Net Benefit for P0]
    EvalP0 --> ListAlt[List Alternative Policies P1 P2 P3]
    ListAlt --> LoopPolicy{For Each Policy Pi}
    LoopPolicy --> ComputeGP[Compute Incremental Gross Profit]
    ComputeGP --> ComputeCost[Compute Incremental Cost of Receivables, Bad Debts, Admin, Discount]
    ComputeCost --> ComputeNB[Compute Net Incremental Benefit]
    ComputeNB --> Compare{Net Incremental Benefit > 0 ?}
    Compare -->|Yes| AcceptT[Add Pi to Candidate List]
    Compare -->|No| RejectT[Discard Pi]
    AcceptT --> NextPolicy[Move to Next Policy]
    RejectT --> NextPolicy
    NextPolicy --> LoopPolicy
    LoopPolicy -->|All Policies Evaluated| SelectMax[Select Policy with Maximum Net Benefit]
    SelectMax --> OptimumOut([OPTIMUM CREDIT PERIOD IDENTIFIED])
```

## 4.3 Cost–Benefit Tradeoff Schematic

The block diagram below shows how the **Total Credit Cost Curve** and the **Incremental Benefit Curve** interact to produce the **Net Profit Curve** with its peak at the optimum credit period.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        Q1[Sales Volume]
        Q2[Average Collection Period ACP]
        Q3[Required Rate of Return K]
        Q4[Bad Debt Rate]
    end

    subgraph Costs
        R1[Cost of Receivables CIR = AIR x K]
        R2[Bad Debt Loss BDL = Rate x Sales]
        R3[Admin Cost CAC]
        R4[Discount Cost CDE]
    end

    subgraph Benefits
        S1[Gross Profit on Credit Sales GP = Sales x CM Ratio]
    end

    subgraph Output
        T1[Net Benefit NB = GP - CIR - BDL - CAC - CDE]
    end

    Q1 --> R1
    Q1 --> R2
    Q1 --> S1
    Q2 --> R1
    Q3 --> R1
    Q4 --> R2

    R1 --> T1
    R2 --> T1
    R3 --> T1
    R4 --> T1
    S1 --> T1

    T1 --> U1{Maximize NB over choice of P}
    U1 --> V1[Optimum Credit Period P-star]
```

## 4.4 Mapping of Cost–Benefit Curves (Conceptual)

Since Mermaid cannot directly draw mathematical curves, the following block schematic represents the **inverted-U shape of the Net Benefit curve** and the **U-shape of the Total Credit Cost curve** as a decision-mapping flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Region I: Very Tight Credit P very small] --> B[Low Sales, Low Defaults]
    B --> C[High Opportunity Cost of Foregone Sales]
    C --> D[Net Benefit is LOW]

    D --> E[Region II: Moderate Credit P moderate]
    E --> F[Balanced Sales and Cost Structure]
    F --> G[Net Benefit is MAXIMUM = OPTIMUM]
    G --> H[Optimum Credit Period P-star]

    H --> I[Region III: Very Loose Credit P very large]
    I --> J[Diminishing Sales, Rising Costs]
    J --> K[Net Benefit FALLS]
    K --> L[Firm Value Destruction Beyond P-star]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define the term "Optimum Credit Period" and state the two key conditions that must be satisfied at the optimum.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Optimum Credit Period** is the length of the credit period (in days) that maximizes the net profit of a firm from its credit sales. At this period, the firm achieves the **maximum possible Net Benefit** from its receivables policy.

The two key conditions that must be satisfied at the optimum are:

1. **Marginal Benefit = Marginal Cost:** The additional (incremental) gross profit earned by extending credit for one more day must equal the additional (incremental) cost incurred (cost of capital, bad debts, administration, and discount) for that extra day. Mathematically, $\frac{dB}{dP} = \frac{dC}{dP} = 0$ at the optimum.

2. **Net Benefit is Maximum (Second-Order Condition):** The Net Benefit function is at its peak, meaning any further relaxation OR tightening of the credit period reduces the Net Benefit. Hence $\frac{d^2 B}{dP^2} < 0$ at the optimum.

In practice (and in KTU problems), since credit periods are discrete choices, the optimum is the **policy with the highest positive net benefit** among the alternatives evaluated.

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: List and briefly explain the four variables that influence a firm's credit policy and affect the determination of the optimum credit period.**

**Model Answer:**

The four variables of a firm's credit policy that influence the optimum credit period are:

1. **Credit Period (P):** The number of days a customer is allowed to delay payment after purchase. A longer period increases sales but also increases the cost of receivables, bad debts, and administration.

2. **Cash Discount (d):** The percentage reduction in the invoice amount offered to customers as an incentive for early payment (e.g., "2/10 net 45" means 2% discount if paid within 10 days, else full amount due in 45 days). A well-designed discount accelerates collections and reduces bad debts.

3. **Credit Standards (S):** The minimum financial strength (creditworthiness) a customer must demonstrate to qualify for credit. Tighter standards reduce bad debt losses but may also reduce sales volume.

4. **Collection Effort (E):** The intensity and expense of follow-up activities on overdue accounts. A more aggressive collection effort shortens ACP and reduces bad debts but increases administrative cost.

The **optimum combination** of these four variables is the one that maximizes the **Net Benefit** of credit sales.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B — Set 1: Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: M/s Trident Tools Ltd. is evaluating three alternative credit policies for its industrial customers. The following data has been compiled by the finance manager:**

| Particulars | Policy P₀ (Current) | Policy P₁ (Relaxed) | Policy P₂ (More Relaxed) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Credit Period (days) | 30 | 45 | 60 |
| Annual Credit Sales (₹) | 36,00,000 | 42,00,000 | 48,00,000 |
| Bad Debt % of Sales | 2% | 3% | 4% |
| Credit Admin Cost (₹ p.a.) | 40,000 | 50,000 | 60,000 |
| Required Rate of Return (K) | 16% | 16% | 16% |
| Variable Cost Ratio | 70% | 70% | 70% |
| Customers pay uniformly over the credit period. | | | |

**Required:**
**(a)** Compute the Average Investment in Receivables (AIR) and the Cost of Investment in Receivables (CIR) for each policy.
**(b)** Compute the Net Benefit of each policy and determine the **Optimum Credit Period** with reasoning.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Computation of AIR and CIR**

Using the formula:
$$
\text{AIR} = \frac{\text{Annual Credit Sales} \times \text{Credit Period}}{2 \times 360}
$$
$$
\text{CIR} = \text{AIR} \times K
$$

**For Policy P₀ (30 days, ₹36,00,000 sales):**
$$
\text{AIR}_{P_0} = \frac{36{,}00{,}000 \times 30}{720} = \frac{10{,}80{,}00{,}000}{720} = 1{,}50{,}000
$$
$$
\text{CIR}_{P_0} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.16 = 24{,}000
$$

**For Policy P₁ (45 days, ₹42,00,000 sales):**
$$
\text{AIR}_{P_1} = \frac{42{,}00{,}000 \times 45}{720} = \frac{18{,}90{,}00{,}000}{720} = 2{,}62{,}500
$$
$$
\text{CIR}_{P_1} = 2{,}62{,}500 \times 0.16 = 42{,}000
$$

**For Policy P₂ (60 days, ₹48,00,000 sales):**
$$
\text{AIR}_{P_2} = \frac{48{,}00{,}000 \times 60}{720} = \frac{28{,}80{,}00{,}000}{720} = 4{,}00{,}000
$$
$$
\text{CIR}_{P_2} = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.16 = 64{,}000
$$

**Summary Table — Part (a):**

| Policy | AIR (₹) | CIR (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| P₀ | 1,50,000 | 24,000 |
| P₁ | 2,62,500 | 42,000 |
| P₂ | 4,00,000 | 64,000 |

---

**Part (b) — Computation of Net Benefit and Optimum Decision**

**Step 1 — Gross Profit (GP) = Credit Sales × (1 − Variable Cost Ratio)**
$$
\text{GP}_{P_0} = 36{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30 = 10{,}80{,}000
$$
$$
\text{GP}_{P_1} = 42{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30 = 12{,}60{,}000
$$
$$
\text{GP}_{P_2} = 48{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30 = 14{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Bad Debt Loss (BDL) = Credit Sales × Bad Debt %**
$$
\text{BDL}_{P_0} = 36{,}00{,}000 \times 0.02 = 72{,}000
$$
$$
\text{BDL}_{P_1} = 42{,}00{,}000 \times 0.03 = 1{,}26{,}000
$$
$$
\text{BDL}_{P_2} = 48{,}00{,}000 \times 0.04 = 1{,}92{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Total Cost of Credit (TCC) = CIR + BDL + Admin Cost**
$$
\text{TCC}_{P_0} = 24{,}000 + 72{,}000 + 40{,}000 = 1{,}36{,}000
$$
$$
\text{TCC}_{P_1} = 42{,}000 + 1{,}26{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 2{,}18{,}000
$$
$$
\text{TCC}_{P_2} = 64{,}000 + 1{,}92{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 3{,}16{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Net Benefit (NB) = GP − TCC**
$$
\text{NB}_{P_0} = 10{,}80{,}000 - 1{,}36{,}000 = 9{,}44{,}000
$$
$$
\text{NB}_{P_1} = 12{,}60{,}000 - 2{,}18{,}000 = 10{,}42{,}000
$$
$$
\text{NB}_{P_2} = 14{,}40{,}000 - 3{,}16{,}000 = 11{,}24{,}000
$$

**Summary Table — Part (b):**

| Policy | Credit Period | GP (₹) | CIR (₹) | BDL (₹) | Admin (₹) | TCC (₹) | NB (₹) | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P₀ | 30 | 10,80,000 | 24,000 | 72,000 | 40,000 | 1,36,000 | 9,44,000 | III |
| P₁ | 45 | 12,60,000 | 42,000 | 1,26,000 | 50,000 | 2,18,000 | 10,42,000 | II |
| P₂ | 60 | 14,40,000 | 64,000 | 1,92,000 | 60,000 | 3,16,000 | **11,24,000** | **I (Optimum)** |

**Conclusion:** Policy **P₂ (60-day credit period)** is the **Optimum Credit Period** because it yields the **highest Net Benefit of ₹11,24,000**. While P₂ has the highest cost, the additional gross profit from increased sales (₹14,40,000) more than compensates, indicating that in this specific problem the firm benefits from the most relaxed credit policy among the options.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Notice that the *ranking of policies is data-dependent*. In some problems, the optimum may be the middle policy (P₁), while in others, the most relaxed (P₂) wins. The student must compute NB for **all** alternatives and pick the maximum — never assume P₀ is optimal.

---

### Question B — Set 1: Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Surya Spices Pvt. Ltd. sells its products on credit. The management is considering modifying the credit policy by introducing a cash discount. Evaluate the following two options and recommend the optimum one:**

| Particulars | Existing Policy | Proposed Policy |
|---|---|---|
| Credit Period | 30 days | 30 days |
| Cash Discount | None | 2% if paid within 10 days |
| Annual Credit Sales (₹) | 20,00,000 | 22,00,000 |
| Bad Debt % | 4% | 2% |
| Collection Cost (₹ p.a.) | 30,000 | 35,000 |
| Required Rate of Return (K) | 15% | 15% |
| Variable Cost Ratio | 75% | 75% |
| % of customers availing discount | — | 50% |
| Average Collection Period (days) | 30 | 18 |

**Required:**
**(a)** Compute the Average Investment in Receivables and the Cost of Receivables for both policies.
**(b)** Compute the Net Benefit of both policies and recommend the **optimum credit policy** with justification.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — AIR and CIR for Both Policies**

Using the formula:
$$
\text{AIR} = \frac{\text{Annual Credit Sales} \times \text{ACP}}{360}
$$
$$
\text{CIR} = \text{AIR} \times K
$$

**Existing Policy (ACP = 30 days):**
$$
\text{AIR}_{\text{Existing}} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000 \times 30}{360} = \frac{60{,}00{,}00{,}000}{360} = 1{,}66{,}667
$$
$$
\text{CIR}_{\text{Existing}} = 1{,}66{,}667 \times 0.15 = 25{,}000
$$

**Proposed Policy (ACP = 18 days):**
$$
\text{AIR}_{\text{Proposed}} = \frac{22{,}00{,}000 \times 18}{360} = \frac{39{,}60{,}00{,}000}{360} = 1{,}10{,}000
$$
$$
\text{CIR}_{\text{Proposed}} = 1{,}10{,}000 \times 0.15 = 16{,}500
$$

**Summary Table — Part (a):**

| Policy | ACP (days) | Sales (₹) | AIR (₹) | CIR (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Existing | 30 | 20,00,000 | 1,66,667 | 25,000 |
| Proposed | 18 | 22,00,000 | 1,10,000 | 16,500 |

---

**Part (b) — Net Benefit Comparison and Optimum Recommendation**

**Step 1 — Gross Profit (GP) = Credit Sales × (1 − VC Ratio)**
$$
\text{GP}_{\text{Existing}} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = 5{,}00{,}000
$$
$$
\text{GP}_{\text{Proposed}} = 22{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = 5{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Bad Debt Loss (BDL)**
$$
\text{BDL}_{\text{Existing}} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.04 = 80{,}000
$$
$$
\text{BDL}_{\text{Proposed}} = 22{,}00{,}000 \times 0.02 = 44{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Cash Discount Expense (CDE) — only for proposed policy:**
$$
\text{CDE}_{\text{Proposed}} = 22{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50 \times 0.02 = 22{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Total Cost of Credit (TCC) = CIR + BDL + Admin + CDE**
$$
\text{TCC}_{\text{Existing}} = 25{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 0 = 1{,}35{,}000
$$
$$
\text{TCC}_{\text{Proposed}} = 16{,}500 + 44{,}000 + 35{,}000 + 22{,}000 = 1{,}17{,}500
$$

**Step 5 — Net Benefit (NB) = GP − TCC**
$$
\text{NB}_{\text{Existing}} = 5{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}35{,}000 = 3{,}65{,}000
$$
$$
\text{NB}_{\text{Proposed}} = 5{,}50{,}000 - 1{,}17{,}500 = 4{,}32{,}500
$$

**Summary Table — Part (b):**

| Item | Existing Policy (₹) | Proposed Policy (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Credit Sales | 20,00,000 | 22,00,000 |
| Gross Profit | 5,00,000 | 5,50,000 |
| CIR | 25,000 | 16,500 |
| Bad Debt Loss | 80,000 | 44,000 |
| Admin Cost | 30,000 | 35,000 |
| Cash Discount Expense | 0 | 22,000 |
| Total Cost | 1,35,000 | 1,17,500 |
| **Net Benefit** | 3,65,000 | **4,32,500** |

**Recommendation:** The **Proposed Policy (30 days credit with 2% cash discount if paid within 10 days)** is the **Optimum Credit Policy** because it yields a **Net Benefit of ₹4,32,500**, which is ₹67,500 higher than the existing policy. The cash discount, despite its cost, accelerates collections (ACP reduces from 30 to 18 days), reduces bad debt losses (from 4% to 2%), and increases sales — all of which combine to enhance profitability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **FORGOT CDE**: Many students forget to include the Cash Discount Expense for the proposed policy. This causes a 2–3 mark deduction. Always check: *Does the new policy have a discount? If yes, compute CDE separately.*
> 2. **USED WRONG ACP**: In the proposed policy, the ACP is **18 days** (not 30), because the discount accelerates payments. Using 30 days for the proposed policy gives a wrong AIR and wrong CIR.
> 3. **DOUBLE-COUNTED**: The bad debt loss and CIR are *separate* costs. Do not merge them.
> 4. **WRONG SIGN**: When a question asks "should we switch?", explicitly state the **incremental net benefit** with the correct sign. Negative = Reject. Positive = Accept.
> 5. **VARIABLE COST RATIO vs CONTRIBUTION MARGIN**: The KTU board uses both terms interchangeably. (1 − VC Ratio) = CM Ratio. Always convert correctly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist for last-day revision before KTU ESE / Internal Assessment.**

- **Optimum Credit Period** = the credit period that **maximizes Net Benefit** from credit sales.
- **Net Benefit** = Gross Profit on Credit Sales − (CIR + Bad Debt Loss + Admin Cost + Cash Discount Cost).
- **Average Investment in Receivables (AIR):**
  - If customers pay **uniformly** over credit period → $\text{AIR} = \dfrac{S \times P}{2 \times 360}$.
  - If customers pay **on the last day** → $\text{AIR} = \dfrac{S \times P}{360}$.
  - If ACP is **given explicitly** (e.g., 18 days with discount) → $\text{AIR} = \dfrac{S \times \text{ACP}}{360}$.
- **Cost of Investment in Receivables (CIR)** = AIR × K, where K is the required rate of return (opportunity cost of capital).
- **Bad Debt Loss (BDL)** = Bad Debt % × Credit Sales.
- **Cash Discount Expense (CDE)** = Credit Sales × % of customers availing × Discount %.
- **Decision Rule:** Select the policy with the **highest Net Benefit** (absolute method) OR the policy beyond which the next incremental move has a **negative incremental Net Benefit** (incremental method).
- **KTU Convention:** Always use **360 days** per year unless specified otherwise.
- **Contribution Margin Ratio** = 1 − Variable Cost Ratio. Use CM for incremental gross profit calculations.
- **Order of Cost Increase with Relaxed Credit:** Cost of Capital (CIR) ↑, Bad Debts ↑, Admin Cost ↑, Discount Cost ↑ (if discount added).
- **Cash Discount Tradeoff:** Cash discount cost is *acceptable* if the savings in CIR (from shorter ACP) + reduction in bad debts > CDE.
- **Real-World Indicators:** If a firm observes declining ACP, rising bad debts, or stretched DSO beyond the industry average, it must re-evaluate its credit period.
- **The four credit policy variables** (P, d, S, E) **must be analyzed jointly** in advanced problems — but the KTU exam typically asks you to vary P and d.
- **Incremental Net Benefit = Incremental GP − Incremental Cost**. A negative result means the next step is value-destructive.
- **Valuation Pitfalls:** Watch the divisor (360 vs 365), the uniform-payment factor (1/2 vs 1), and the sign of incremental net benefit.
